AI.conclusion: Adaptive multiresolution operators like LiNO represent a promising direction for scientific machine learning, enabling effective multiscale operator learning that captures both global and fine-scale features simultaneously.

title: Relational Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Pricing in High-Speed Railway Markets
summary: In liberalised railway systems, operators must set prices dynamically in an environment with partial observability, as they retain private information about their objectives and performance, where regulatory constraints prohibit communication or direct information exchange between competitors to prevent explicit collusion. Consequently, agents must learn to infer strategic interactions only from observable market data which presents a significant challenge for multi-agent reinforcement learning, where standard approaches typically treat observations as unstructured vectors, ignoring the underlying market topology that governs strategic interactions. To address this, an entity graph modelling approach is proposed, which represents the environment as a graph of operational units, rather than decision-making agents or static infrastructure, encoding competition, coordination, and connectivity relations between entities. Then, an extension of the multi-agent twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient algorithm with graph-based representation learning processes the features of the entities through a multi-layer relational graph convolutional network and aggregates them via a learnt attention mechanism. Experimental results in a rail pricing reinforcement learning environment show that this novel framework achieves higher revenue and stability in two different settings of increasing market complexity compared to a representative selection of relational and non-relational baselines. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/Kinrre/RelationalRailPricing-RL

AI.tldr: A graph-based multi-agent reinforcement learning approach for dynamic pricing in liberalized railway systems that models operational entities as a graph and uses relational graph convolutional networks to learn competitive strategies without explicit communication between operators.
AI.motivation: In liberalized railway systems, operators need to set prices dynamically but face partial observability and regulatory constraints that prohibit communication between competitors to prevent collusion. This creates a challenging multi-agent reinforcement learning environment where standard approaches ignore the underlying market topology that governs strategic interactions.
AI.method: The authors propose an entity graph modelling approach representing the environment as a graph of operational units rather than decision-making agents or static infrastructure. They extend the multi-agent twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient algorithm with graph-based representation learning, using a multi-layer relational graph convolutional network to process entity features and aggregating them via a learnt attention mechanism.
AI.result: Experimental results in a rail pricing reinforcement learning environment show that the proposed framework achieves higher revenue and stability in two different settings of increasing market complexity compared to a representative selection of relational and non-relational baselines.
AI.conclusion: The graph-based approach effectively captures the competition, coordination, and connectivity relations between operational entities in railway systems, enabling better strategic decision-making in partially observable environments with communication constraints, demonstrating superiority over standard multi-agent reinforcement learning methods that treat observations as unstructured vectors.

title: VERITAS: Towards a General-Purpose Replication Tool for Scientific Research
summary: AI tools are accelerating scientific publication while the systems that review it struggle to keep up, and independent verification of published research has become both harder and more important. As manual replication is slow and expensive, a growing line of work uses coding agents to automate parts of the process. Existing efforts are largely packaged as benchmarks with companion agents that only run inside the benchmark's own pipeline, and no general-purpose replication tool exists. We present VERITAS, a domain-agnostic replication framework built around CLI coding agents. Given a paper, a code repository, or both, VERITAS extracts the paper's claims, runs the methodology while resolving issues as they arise, and judges each claim against the evidence from experiment runs. The pipeline returns an importance-weighted Replication Score, a severity-rated log of every fix applied, and the patched codebase. We evaluate VERITAS on CORE-Bench and ReplicationBench, 65 papers spanning computer science, social science, medicine, and astrophysics. Against two strong Claude Code baselines on the same model and host environment, VERITAS achieves state-of-the-art performance and leads on every metric on both benchmarks.

AI.tldr: VERITAS is an automated replication framework that uses CLI coding agents to verify research papers by extracting claims, running methodologies, resolving issues, and judging claims against experimental evidence.
AI.motivation: AI tools are accelerating scientific publication while review systems struggle to keep up, making independent verification both harder and more important. Manual replication is slow and expensive, and existing automated replication tools are limited to specific benchmarks without general-purpose solutions.
AI.method: VERITAS is a domain-agnostic replication framework built around CLI coding agents. It takes a paper, code repository, or both, extracts claims, runs the methodology while resolving issues, judges claims against experimental evidence, and outputs a Replication Score, fix logs, and patched codebase.
AI.result: VERITAS achieves state-of-the-art performance on CORE-Bench and ReplicationBench (65 papers across computer science, social science, medicine, and astrophysics), outperforming two strong Claude Code baselines on every metric.
AI.conclusion: VERITAS provides an effective automated solution for scientific replication that works across domains, offering a practical tool to address the growing challenge of verifying accelerated scientific publications.

title: Weighted Conformal Prediction for Lab-to-Track Thermal Transfer in EV Motorsport Powertrains
comment: 8 pages, 3 tables, github like for the codes and dataset:https://github.com/nearpot/Weighted-Conformal-Thermal-Transfer
summary: Predicting thermal volatility in high-performance EV powertrains is difficult as internal temperatures are rarely observable outside the lab, and models calibrated on lab drive cycles fail when deployed against real-world loads. We study this lab-to-track transfer problem using conformal prediction, offering distribution-free uncertainty bounds. We implement Ensemble Batch Prediction Intervals (EnbPI; Xu & Xie, 2021), a leave-one-out bootstrap-ensemble conformal method for autocorrelated time series, and calibrate it on real CALCE lithium-ion cycler data (A123 SP20 cells, FUDS profile). We evaluate it under a genuine, measured covariate shift: a second real CALCE test condition (US06 Highway Driving Schedule at 45°C). The unweighted EnbPI bound, achieving its nominal 95% coverage in-distribution (measured: 95.00%), degrades to 70.13% empirical coverage under this real shift. We introduce a weighted EnbPI procedure combining EnbPI's ensemble residuals with density-ratio weighting (Tibshirani et al., 2019), estimating the density ratio via a probabilistic domain classifier. This recovers coverage to 72.42%, a modest, honestly-reported improvement, not a complete fix. We additionally apply the calibrated model to real 2023 Formula 1 telemetry (Monza and Silverstone, driver VER) as an unsupervised out-of-distribution diagnostic. Because no internal thermal channel exists in public trackside telemetry, we report only unsupervised flag rates (65.6% at Monza, 58.0% at Silverstone, well above the 5% in-distribution base rate) and note inconsistent associations between flags and braking/DRS zones. We conclude that conformal domain adaptation is a promising but only partially solved tool for this problem, detailing exactly where it falls short.

AI.tldr: Conformal prediction with density-ratio weighting improves thermal volatility prediction transfer from lab to real-world EV powertrains, but coverage remains incomplete under significant covariate shifts.
AI.motivation: Predicting thermal volatility in EV powertrains is challenging because internal temperatures are rarely observable outside labs, and lab-calibrated models fail when deployed against real-world loads due to distribution shifts.
AI.method: Implemented Ensemble Batch Prediction Intervals (EnbPI) for autocorrelated time series on lithium-ion battery data, then applied density-ratio weighting via probabilistic domain classifier to adapt to covariate shift from lab to real conditions.
AI.result: Standard EnbPI achieved 95% coverage in-distribution but degraded to 70.13% under real shift; weighted EnbPI improved coverage to 72.42%. Application to F1 telemetry showed high unsupervised flag rates (65.6% at Monza, 58.0% at Silverstone) versus 5% base rate.
AI.conclusion: Conformal domain adaptation is promising but only partially solved for thermal volatility prediction; the method shows modest improvement under real covariate shift but substantial room remains for better transfer techniques.

title: OptiAgent: End-to-End Optimization Modeling via Multi-Agent Iterative Refinement
summary: We propose OptiAgent, a multi-agent framework that, given a natural language description of an Operations Research problem, is able to output a solver-ready mathematical formulation as well as executable code. Our architecture prioritizes the mathematical modeling step, where dedicated agents extract structures, such as decision variables and constraints, enabling iterative self-correction. We introduce a novel multi-loop validation architecture with four specialized feedback mechanisms, each targeting a distinct failure mode such as misinterpretation, structural defects, mathematical inconsistencies, validation failures, and code errors. Alongside accuracy, our modular design improves the process of solving optimization problems by improving transparency, as each agent exposes its reasoning and feedback, making the full modeling process auditable. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3 out of 4 benchmarks across LP, MILP, and Nonlinear Programming tasks, while remaining highly competitive on the remaining dataset.

AI.tldr: OptiAgent is a multi-agent framework that automatically converts natural language descriptions of Operations Research problems into solver-ready mathematical formulations and executable code, featuring specialized agents for mathematical modeling and multi-loop validation with targeted feedback mechanisms.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of automatically converting natural language problem descriptions into formal mathematical formulations for Operations Research problems, which traditionally requires significant expertise and manual effort. There's a need for systems that can not only generate accurate formulations but also provide transparency in the modeling process.
AI.method: The framework employs a multi-agent architecture with dedicated agents for extracting problem structures (decision variables, constraints). It introduces a novel multi-loop validation system with four specialized feedback mechanisms that target distinct failure modes: misinterpretation, structural defects, mathematical inconsistencies, validation failures, and code errors. The design prioritizes mathematical modeling with iterative self-correction capabilities.
AI.result: OptiAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3 out of 4 benchmarks across Linear Programming (LP), Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP), and Nonlinear Programming tasks, while remaining highly competitive on the remaining dataset. The modular design improves transparency by exposing each agent's reasoning and feedback, making the full modeling process auditable.
AI.conclusion: The proposed OptiAgent framework successfully bridges the gap between natural language problem descriptions and formal mathematical formulations, offering both high accuracy and improved transparency through its multi-agent architecture and specialized validation mechanisms. The system represents an advancement in automated Operations Research problem formulation while maintaining auditability of the modeling process.

title: A Sliding-Window-Based Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Assembly Flow Shop Scheduling with Multi-Product Delivery
summary: Multi-product kitting delivery imposes significant challenges for real-time scheduling in hybrid manufacturing systems that integrate processing and assembly, as dynamic order arrivals simultaneously alter supply dependencies and the set of feasible job-machine assignments. This paper proposes a sliding-window-based reinforcement learning (SWRL) framework for end-to-end online scheduling in the flexible assembly flow shop scheduling problem with complex kitting constraints. The problem is formulated as a heterogeneous graph-based Markov decision process that captures the dual-layer kitting structure and the tail-product bottleneck dynamics that produce a sparse reward landscape. To address the resulting challenges, SWRL integrates a sliding-window filtering mechanism that filters inactive nodes and prioritizes kitting-critical operations, a spatiotemporal graph encoding network that tracks bottleneck shifts across consecutive decision states, and a dynamic action mapping module with a constrained waiting strategy that adapts to the changing action space under variable topologies. Experiments on real-world instances from a home appliance manufacturer demonstrate that SWRL achieves consistent tardiness reductions over classical dispatching rules and existing deep reinforcement learning methods, and exhibits robust performance across varying resource configurations, order loads, and arrival concentrations.

AI.tldr: A sliding-window reinforcement learning framework for online scheduling in flexible assembly flow shops with complex kitting constraints, achieving better tardiness reduction than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Multi-product kitting delivery in hybrid manufacturing systems creates real-time scheduling challenges due to dynamic order arrivals that simultaneously change supply dependencies and feasible job-machine assignments, requiring an intelligent online scheduling solution.
AI.method: Proposes SWRL (Sliding-Window-based Reinforcement Learning) framework with: 1) sliding-window filtering mechanism for inactive nodes and kitting-critical operations, 2) spatiotemporal graph encoding network for bottleneck tracking, 3) dynamic action mapping module with constrained waiting strategy for variable topologies.
AI.result: SWRL achieves consistent tardiness reductions over classical dispatching rules and existing deep reinforcement learning methods, with robust performance across varying resource configurations, order loads, and arrival concentrations on real-world home appliance manufacturing instances.
AI.conclusion: The SWRL framework effectively addresses online scheduling challenges in complex kitting-constrained manufacturing systems through its integrated approach of filtering, bottleneck tracking, and adaptive action mapping, demonstrating practical value for real-world industrial applications.

title: Out-of-Distribution Generalization of Risk Aversion in Language Models
comment: ICML 2026 Agents in the Wild workshop
summary: Training AIs to be risk-averse in resources could offer a failsafe in the event that AIs turn out misaligned. Misaligned but risk-averse AIs would tend to prefer low-risk, low-reward strategies like cooperation over high-risk, high-reward strategies like rebellion, limiting the downsides of any misalignment. But we can only feasibly train AIs to be risk-averse on low-stakes gambles, and we will only be safe if their risk aversion generalizes to astronomically-high-stakes gambles. Will it? To shed light on this question, we introduce RiskAverseOOD: a benchmark for measuring how well risk aversion generalizes out of distribution. We then offer some initial results. Using a variety of methods to make Qwen3-8B choose risk-aversely when the stakes are low, we find that we can induce substantial risk aversion when the stakes are astronomically high. Our models' learned risk aversion generalizes at least partially across 98 orders of magnitude. From a baseline 2% rate of choosing a safe `Cooperate' option, we see rates around 70% (SFT and tie training), 52% (DPO), and 39% (activation steering). In another experiment, our fine-tuned reward model reliably scores risk-averse reasoning above risk-neutral or excessively risk-averse alternatives (99.6% pairwise accuracy). We replicate these effects at different scales (Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-14B) and across model families (Gemma-3-12B-IT and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). Overall, we find that risk aversion learned at low stakes can generalize OOD to astronomically high stakes, though not yet consistently enough to serve as a reliable failsafe. Achieving that level of consistency is an open problem.

AI.tldr: Risk aversion trained on low-stakes scenarios can partially generalize to astronomically high-stakes situations, but not yet reliably enough for failsafe AI alignment.
AI.motivation: To create a safety mechanism where risk-averse AIs would prefer low-risk strategies over high-risk rebellion even if misaligned, requiring generalization from low-stakes to high-stakes scenarios.
AI.method: Introduced RiskAverseOOD benchmark to measure risk aversion generalization. Trained models (Qwen3-8B) with various methods (SFT, tie training, DPO, activation steering) on low-stakes gambles and tested generalization to high-stakes scenarios.
AI.result: Risk aversion learned at low stakes generalized partially across 98 orders of magnitude. Safe 'Cooperate' rates increased from 2% baseline to ~70% (SFT/tie training), 52% (DPO), and 39% (activation steering). Reward models scored risk-averse reasoning above alternatives with 99.6% pairwise accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Risk aversion learned at low stakes can generalize out-of-distribution to high stakes, though not yet consistently enough for reliable failsafe. Achieving consistent generalization remains an open problem.

title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework
summary: Scaling pre-training, post-training, and test-time compute have become the central paradigms for improving the capabilities of LLMs. In this work, we identify verification, the ability to determine the correctness of a solution, as a new scaling axis. To unlock this and demonstrate its effectiveness, we introduce LLM-as-a-Verifier, a general-purpose verification framework that provides fine-grained feedback for agentic tasks without requiring additional training. Unlike standard LM judges that prompt LLMs to produce discrete scores for candidate solutions, LLM-as-a-Verifier computes the expectation over the distribution of scoring token logits to generate continuous scores. This probabilistic formulation enables verification to scale along multiple dimensions: (1) score granularity, (2) repeated evaluation, and (3) criteria decomposition. In particular, we show that scaling the scoring granularity leads to better separation between positive and negative solutions, resulting in more calibrated comparisons. Moreover, scaling repeated evaluation and criteria decomposition consistently lead to additional gains in verification accuracy through variance and complexity reduction. We further introduce a cost-efficient ranking algorithm for selecting the best solution among candidates using the verifier's continuous scores. LLM-as-a-Verifier achieves state-of-the-art performance on Terminal-Bench V2 (86.5%), SWE-Bench Verified (78.2%), RoboRewardBench (87.4%), and MedAgentBench (73.3%). Beyond verification, the fine-grained signals from LLM-as-a-Verifier can also serve as a proxy for estimating task progress. We build an extension for Claude Code, enabling developers to monitor and improve their own agentic systems. Finally, we show that LLM-as-a-Verifier can provide dense feedback for RL, improving the sample efficiency of SAC and GRPO on robotics and mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: LLM-as-a-Verifier is a probabilistic verification framework that uses continuous scoring from token logits to enable fine-grained feedback for agentic tasks, achieving SOTA results across multiple benchmarks and applications.
AI.motivation: Current scaling paradigms (pre-training, post-training, test-time compute) are insufficient; verification (determining solution correctness) is identified as a new scaling axis that can improve LLM capabilities without requiring additional training.
AI.method: Introduces LLM-as-a-Verifier framework that computes expectation over scoring token logits to generate continuous scores (instead of discrete LM judge scores). This enables scaling along three dimensions: score granularity, repeated evaluation, and criteria decomposition. Includes a cost-efficient ranking algorithm for selecting best solutions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on Terminal-Bench V2 (86.5%), SWE-Bench Verified (78.2%), RoboRewardBench (87.4%), and MedAgentBench (73.3%). Fine-grained signals enable task progress estimation and dense RL feedback, improving SAC and GRPO sample efficiency on robotics/math benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Verification is a novel and effective scaling axis for LLMs. LLM-as-a-Verifier's probabilistic continuous scoring enables fine-grained feedback that improves agentic task performance, verification accuracy, and enables new applications like RL training and agentic system monitoring.

title: Evaluating Generative Agents with Actions Grounded in Socially Distributed Task Environments using Incognita
summary: Effective agency in social environments depends on when an agent seeks knowledge, when it acts, and whether its actions are justified by acquired information. Existing grounded benchmarks provide executable actions, persistent state, and verifiable outcomes, while social simulation environments provide rich interaction among language agents. We study an evaluation setting that combines these requirements. We define socially distributed task environments as interactive environments where task-relevant knowledge is partitioned across role-isolated participants and consequential actions are accessible only through them. Communication serves as exploration over role-partitioned knowledge, while grounded action serves as exploitation over environment state. We introduce Incognita, a Concordia-based framework that separates social interaction from grounded execution. The evaluated agent routes messages to a user or specialist entities; specialists mediate admissible operations; a deterministic sub-environment executes accepted operations over a canonical state; and an offline evaluator scores outcomes with inherited rewards. Incognita-Retail transforms tau-bench retail into a multi-entity environment while preserving final-state reward semantics. We evaluate three generative agent models on 18 tasks stratified by social breadth, with 540 trials. Progress appears in reward and behavior: success rises from 0 percent to 8.9 percent and 17.2 percent, while premature finalization falls from 100 percent to 87 percent and 58 percent. Stronger models elicit more hidden knowledge, contact more entities, and attempt more grounded writes, yet reliability remains low. These findings show that socially distributed task environments expose behavior before reliable success, including knowledge elicitation, source selection, grounded action attempts, and premature completion belief.

AI.tldr: A framework called Incognita combines social simulation with grounded task environments to evaluate agents on socially distributed tasks where knowledge is partitioned across role-isolated participants.
AI.motivation: Existing grounded benchmarks provide executable actions and verifiable outcomes, while social simulations provide rich agent interactions, but there's a need to evaluate agents in settings that combine both - where task-relevant knowledge is distributed across different participants and meaningful actions require social coordination.
AI.method: Introduces Incognita, a Concordia-based framework that separates social interaction from grounded execution. It features message routing to users/specialists, specialist mediation of operations, deterministic sub-environment execution, and offline evaluation with inherited rewards. Tests on Incognita-Retail (adapted tau-bench retail) with three generative agent models across 18 tasks.
AI.result: Success rates improved from 0% to 8.9% and 17.2% across models, while premature finalization dropped from 100% to 87% and 58%. Stronger models elicited more hidden knowledge, contacted more entities, and attempted more grounded actions, though overall reliability remained low.
AI.conclusion: Socially distributed task environments reveal important agent behaviors (knowledge elicitation, source selection, action attempts, completion beliefs) before achieving reliable success, providing valuable evaluation insights for social AI systems.

title: Safe Inference-Time Alignment via Lagrangian Reward Augmentation
summary: Inference-time alignment steers a frozen language model during decoding using auxiliary reward signals, avoiding the cost of repeated weight updates. However, existing inference-time alignment methods typically optimize a single scalar score, so explicit safety constraints must either be ignored or encoded through manually tuned penalties. We propose Lagrangian Reward Augmentation (LARA), a general inference-time alignment framework under safety constraints. Starting from a KL-regularized constrained objective with a reward model and a cost model, LARA dualizes the constraint and reduces the optimization problem to a one-dimensional convex problem over a nonnegative dual variable. Estimated on a small calibration set, this dual variable defines an augmented reward that can be used as a drop-in scoring signal within existing inference-time alignment methods. For sequence-level sampling methods, such as Best-of-N reranking, the calibrated dual variable corresponds to the solution of the expected-cost constrained problem. For token-level reward-guided decoding methods, the same construction yields a principled dual-calibrated heuristic rather than an exact constrained-policy guarantee. We evaluate LARA on both sequence-level and token-level inference-time alignment methods, and find that LARA improves the helpfulness-harmlessness tradeoff, with Best-of-N achieving the best performance among inference-time methods, approaching finetuning-based direct alignment baselines.

AI.tldr: LARA is a Lagrangian-based inference-time alignment framework that optimizes language models under safety constraints using calibrated dual variables, achieving safety-alignment without weight updates.
AI.motivation: Existing inference-time alignment methods only optimize single scalar scores, forcing safety constraints to be ignored or manually tuned, lacking principled constrained optimization.
AI.method: LARA dualizes KL-regularized constrained objectives with reward and cost models, reducing optimization to a 1D convex problem over nonnegative dual variables calibrated on small datasets.
AI.result: LARA improves helpfulness-harmlessness tradeoffs for both sequence-level (Best-of-N) and token-level methods, with Best-of-N approaching finetuning-based alignment performance.
AI.conclusion: LARA provides a general framework for principled constrained alignment at inference time, enabling safety constraints without manual tuning and bridging performance gaps with finetuning methods.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Evidence-Seeking Diagnostic Reasoning with Large Language Models
summary: Recent reasoning-centric Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant strides, yet they predominantly operate on a passive-inference pattern that assumes complete information. In contrast, real-world clinical intelligence is inherently an iterative investigative process requiring strategic evidence acquisition. To bridge this gap, we formalize medical diagnosis as an Iterative Evidence-Seeking Task. We leverage Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to elicit intrinsic reasoning within a closed-loop environment, guided by a novel suite of rewards that enforce diagnostic precision and examination consistency. To facilitate this, we introduce the Retrieval-Augmented Generation-based Examination Simulator (RAGES), a high-fidelity clinical oracle that provides realistic, knowledge-grounded follow-up evidence. Empirical results across diverse datasets demonstrate that our framework enables LLMs to transition from passive responders to autonomous assistants. Notably, our model demonstrates comparable performance to larger and reasoning-enhanced baselines, while RAGES proves superior to vanilla LLMs in generating biologically plausible clinical feedback.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a framework that transforms LLMs from passive responders to active evidence-seeking agents for medical diagnosis, using RL with verifiable rewards and a high-fidelity clinical simulator.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning-centric LLMs operate on a passive-inference pattern assuming complete information, while real-world clinical intelligence requires iterative investigative processes with strategic evidence acquisition. There's a gap between passive LLM inference and active clinical decision-making.
AI.method: The authors formalize medical diagnosis as an Iterative Evidence-Seeking Task and use Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to elicit intrinsic reasoning. They introduce Retrieval-Augmented Generation-based Examination Simulator (RAGES) as a high-fidelity clinical oracle for realistic follow-up evidence generation.
AI.result: Empirical results show the framework enables LLMs to transition from passive responders to autonomous assistants. The model achieves comparable performance to larger reasoning-enhanced baselines, while RAGES outperforms vanilla LLMs in generating biologically plausible clinical feedback.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully bridges the gap between passive LLM inference and active clinical intelligence, demonstrating that LLMs can be transformed into autonomous evidence-seeking agents for medical diagnosis through RL with verifiable rewards and realistic clinical simulation.

title: Induction Heads Interpolate N-Grams
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICML 2026. OpenReview: https://openreview.net/forum?id=BSY7jhBxM1
summary: Induction heads are attention circuits believed to underlie in-context learning in transformers, yet a precise characterization of the estimators they implement remains elusive. We study transformers trained on order-$k$ Markov chains and identify two complementary smoothing mechanisms. First, at finite attention-weight scale, the circuit implements a soft context-matching estimator: it aggregates contributions from exact and partial context matches, weighted exponentially by their overlap, and induces a data-dependent interpolation across context orders analogous to Jelinek-Mercer smoothing. Second, a beginning-of-sequence (BOS) token induces additive pseudo-counts, recovering Dirichlet-style smoothing. We construct a disentangled transformer implementing both mechanisms and show that trained transformers recover the predicted attention patterns. Across settings where pseudo-count smoothing is optimal or lower-order contexts provide structured evidence, trained transformers match or outperform classical count-based baselines. Our results bridge mechanistic interpretability of induction heads with classical statistical smoothing, revealing that transformers learn to regularize in-context estimation rather than simply count.

AI.tldr: Transformers learn induction heads that implement statistical smoothing mechanisms for in-context learning, combining soft context-matching with pseudo-count smoothing rather than simply counting patterns.
AI.motivation: While induction heads are known to underlie in-context learning in transformers, their exact implementation and the statistical estimators they learn remain poorly characterized. The paper aims to bridge mechanistic interpretability with classical statistical theory.
AI.method: Study transformers trained on order-k Markov chains to identify smoothing mechanisms. Analyze attention circuits at finite attention-weight scale, construct disentangled transformers implementing identified mechanisms, and compare trained transformers with classical count-based baselines.
AI.result: Identified two complementary smoothing mechanisms: 1) soft context-matching estimator that aggregates contributions from exact/partial matches with exponential weighting, analogous to Jelinek-Mercer smoothing, and 2) BOS token inducing additive pseudo-counts like Dirichlet smoothing. Trained transformers recover predicted attention patterns and match/outperform count-based baselines when smoothing is beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Transformers learn to implement statistical smoothing mechanisms for in-context estimation rather than simply counting patterns, bridging mechanistic interpretability with classical statistical theory of language modeling regularization.

title: Beyond Forecasting: The Belief-to-Trade Layer in Prediction-Market Agents
summary: Forecasting future events has attracted growing attention as a testbed for general-purpose AI. A natural way to ground this evaluation is let the models trade in the prediction markets. Trading, however, requires more than forecasting. Moreover, recent benchmarks report a substantial gap between calibrated probability scores and the trading results. We propose Raven-Agent, to the best of our knowledge, the first autonomous trading agent for prediction markets. On a controlled replay over an archived decision set, our architecture achieves the only positive return and the only positive risk-adjusted return among all tested policies. We have released our code in https://github.com/Alchemist-X/predict-raven .

AI.tldr: Raven-Agent is the first autonomous trading agent for prediction markets that successfully translates calibrated probability forecasts into profitable trading results, achieving positive returns where other methods fail.
AI.motivation: There's a growing interest in using forecasting future events as a testbed for AI, with prediction markets providing a natural evaluation ground. However, current approaches show a significant gap between calibrated probability scores and actual trading performance, indicating that forecasting alone isn't sufficient for successful market participation.
AI.method: The authors developed Raven-Agent, an autonomous trading agent specifically designed for prediction markets. They tested their architecture using a controlled replay approach over an archived decision set, comparing it against various other trading policies.
AI.result: Raven-Agent achieved the only positive return and the only positive risk-adjusted return among all tested policies in the controlled replay experiments. This demonstrates that their approach successfully bridges the gap between probability forecasting and profitable trading.
AI.conclusion: The Raven-Agent represents a significant advancement in AI for prediction markets, showing that autonomous trading agents can successfully translate probability forecasts into profitable trading strategies where other approaches fail, and provides a promising direction for AI evaluation through market mechanisms.

title: Training Hybrid Block Diffusion Language Models with Partial Bidirectionality
summary: High-throughput long-context generation is one of the central challenges for large language models. Generation is typically memory-bandwidth-bound rather than compute-bound: each decoding step must stream the accumulated key/value (KV) cache from memory, so bandwidth demand grows with context length while only one token is emitted. Two parallel approaches have therefore emerged: reducing memory access with efficient attention variants and linear-time mixers such as Mamba, or increasing parallel computation by generating blocks of tokens at once. However, technical challenges arise when combining these two ideas. Earlier hybrid diffusion models such as DiffuMamba use bidirectional Mamba mixing, including a reverse-direction scan relative to causal generation. This reverse scan needs to scan the entire sequence, so its states are not prefix-only and cannot be precisely reused as a cache even when diffusion is performed block by block. We propose a BDLM Mamba--attention hybrid that addresses this challenge by restricting the reverse Mamba scan to the active denoising block, which enables exact caching across blocks. In an 87M-parameter DCLM sweep, BDLM Mamba-H achieves the best C4-en validation perplexity compared to BDLM attention and full-sequence baselines. At 350M parameters, it remains competitive with BDLM attention. For long-context inference, BDLM Mamba-H reaches 19.7x the throughput of full-sequence DiffuMamba-H at 65K tokens and 3.7x the throughput of BDLM attention at 262K, showing that Mamba hybrids are a potential long-context diffusion architecture.

AI.tldr: A BDLM Mamba-attention hybrid architecture that enables exact caching across blocks for efficient long-context generation by restricting reverse Mamba scans to active denoising blocks.
AI.motivation: High-throughput long-context generation is challenging because current approaches face memory bandwidth limitations (KV cache streaming) and parallel computation trade-offs. Existing hybrid approaches like DiffuMamba have technical issues with reverse scans that prevent efficient caching when generating blocks incrementally.
AI.method: Proposes BDLM Mamba-H hybrid architecture that combines Mamba (linear-time mixers) with attention mechanisms, specifically restricting reverse Mamba scans to only the active denoising block instead of scanning the entire sequence, enabling exact caching across blocks.
AI.result: BDLM Mamba-H achieves best C4-en validation perplexity in 87M-parameter DCLM sweep compared to BDLM attention and full-sequence baselines. At 350M parameters, remains competitive with BDLM attention. For long-context inference, reaches 19.7x throughput of full-sequence DiffuMamba-H at 65K tokens and 3.7x throughput of BDLM attention at 262K tokens.
AI.conclusion: The BDLM Mamba-attention hybrid architecture successfully addresses caching challenges in hybrid diffusion models, demonstrating that Mamba hybrids are a viable and efficient architecture for long-context diffusion with significant throughput improvements.

title: Less Tokens, Better Forecasts: Sparse Residual Routing for Efficient Weather Prediction
summary: Existing ViT-based weather forecasting models apply uniform computation across all spatial tokens, even though nearby atmospheric grid points often contain similar values and large regions evolve smoothly over time. This makes much of the intermediate per-token computation redundant. Standard token-efficiency methods, such as pruning or merging, reduce cost by removing or fusing tokens. However, weather forecasting is a spatiotemporal dense prediction problem in which a history of atmospheric states must be mapped to future values on the original latitude-longitude grid. Thus, every grid cell must retain a physically meaningful representation, especially under autoregressive rollout. We introduce Sparse-Reslim, a parameter-free plug-in routing module that makes sparse token processing compatible with this fixed-grid requirement. Sparse-Reslim routes only 25% of spatial tokens through the expensive middle transformer blocks and treats those blocks as residual updates: it computes the change produced for the routed tokens and scatters only this delta back to the full sequence. Unselected tokens keep their pre-routing representations exactly, so no grid cell is dropped or replaced by a mask token, and no fusion layer or additional parameters are introduced. Across ERA5 resolutions up to the operational 0.25\textdegree{} standard and two model families, a deterministic Transformer and a diffusion model, Sparse-Reslim improves forecast accuracy on every evaluated variable while substantially reducing cost: training is about 2.5x faster in the main settings and reaches 3.18x speedup at 0.25\textdegree{}, with over 2.2x lower peak memory. A controlled decomposition shows that the accuracy gain comes primarily from sparse routing itself, while random token selection provides an additional regularization benefit without selector overhead.

AI.tldr: Sparse-Reslim: A parameter-free routing module for ViT-based weather forecasting that processes only 25% of spatial tokens through expensive transformer blocks while maintaining full-grid accuracy via residual updates.
AI.motivation: Standard ViT-based weather forecasting models apply uniform computation across all spatial tokens, but atmospheric grid points often contain similar values with smooth temporal evolution, making much per-token computation redundant. Existing token-efficiency methods (pruning/merging) are incompatible with weather forecasting's fixed-grid requirements where every grid cell must retain physically meaningful representations, especially under autoregressive rollout.
AI.method: Sparse-Reslim is a parameter-free plug-in routing module that routes only 25% of spatial tokens through expensive middle transformer blocks, treating those blocks as residual updates. It computes changes only for routed tokens and scatters these delta updates back to the full sequence. Unselected tokens keep their pre-routing representations exactly, ensuring no grid cells are dropped or replaced with mask tokens, and no fusion layers or additional parameters are introduced.
AI.result: Across ERA5 resolutions up to operational 0.25° standard and two model families (deterministic Transformer and diffusion model), Sparse-Reslim improves forecast accuracy on every evaluated variable while substantially reducing costs: ~2.5x faster training in main settings (up to 3.18x speedup at 0.25°) with over 2.2x lower peak memory. Analysis shows accuracy gains primarily from sparse routing itself, with random token selection providing additional regularization benefit.
AI.conclusion: Sparse-Reslim successfully enables efficient sparse token processing for weather forecasting while maintaining fixed-grid accuracy requirements, achieving both improved forecast accuracy and significant computational savings without introducing additional parameters or compromising physical representation integrity.

title: On the Design Space of Discrete Diffusion Online Adaptation for Molecular Optimization
comment: Submitted to The Fortieth Annual Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems
summary: Molecular optimization often starts from a pretrained generative model that captures a broad prior over valid molecular structures. At test time, however, the goal is not to sample from this prior, but to use a limited oracle budget to shift generation toward task-specific high-reward molecules. We study this adaptation problem for discrete diffusion models. Each online round couples several choices. The loop must decide which candidates to evaluate, how rewards become model updates, which feedback to reuse, and how far to move beyond the pretrained prior. These choices have mostly been studied in isolation, leaving open whether they complement one another, become redundant, or interfere inside a full online adaptation loop. We conduct controlled studies across six small-molecule binding-affinity tasks and three protein-fitness tasks. We find that acquisition, reward shaping, and model debiasing provide complementary routes to higher reward, especially for small molecules. Replay further stabilizes learning, while validity penalties keep small-molecule exploration on the valid molecular manifold. Together, these findings point to a practical recipe for feedback-efficient molecular optimization: online fine-tuning with acquisition, reward shaping, debiasing, replay, and validity control. This recipe outperforms offline fine-tuning and inference-time search baselines under matched oracle-call budgets and GPU-hour accounting. The gains are largest when high-reward candidates require larger shifts from the pretrained prior.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to adapt pretrained discrete diffusion models for molecular optimization using limited oracle budgets, identifying complementary techniques for online fine-tuning that outperform offline methods.
AI.motivation: In molecular optimization, pretrained generative models capture broad molecular priors, but at test time the goal shifts to using limited oracle budgets to generate task-specific high-reward molecules. While various adaptation techniques exist (acquisition strategies, reward shaping, model updates, feedback reuse, exploration boundaries), they have been studied in isolation, leaving open questions about their interactions and complementarity in a complete online adaptation loop.
AI.method: The authors conduct controlled studies across six small-molecule binding-affinity tasks and three protein-fitness tasks, systematically examining how different adaptation choices interact. They study acquisition strategies (which candidates to evaluate), reward shaping (how rewards become model updates), model debiasing, feedback reuse through replay, and validity control for exploration boundaries.
AI.result: The study finds that acquisition, reward shaping, and model debiasing provide complementary routes to higher rewards, especially for small molecules. Replay stabilizes learning, while validity penalties keep small-molecule exploration on the valid molecular manifold. These techniques work best when combined, outperforming offline fine-tuning and inference-time search baselines under matched oracle-call budgets and GPU-hour accounting, particularly when high-reward candidates require larger shifts from the pretrained prior.
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes a practical recipe for feedback-efficient molecular optimization: online fine-tuning with acquisition, reward shaping, debiasing, replay, and validity control. This combined approach enables more effective adaptation of pretrained diffusion models to task-specific objectives while respecting real-world computational and evaluation constraints.

title: APeB: Benchmarking Personalization Ability of Large Language Model Agents
summary: LLM-powered agents struggle with personalization when users issue raw, underspecified queries. In this setting, agents must infer latent intent, extract preferences from noisy interaction histories, and select among competing alternatives. Existing benchmarks rarely test this capability, as they often rely on user-refined queries or simplified histories. We introduce personalized product search (PPS), a testbed for agentic personalization under raw queries and diverse histories. We construct Agent Personalized Benchmark (APeB) from action logs, pairing underspecified intents with rich histories and user-viewed candidate items. Evaluating state-of-the-art LLMs with multi-step agent workflows, we find that models handle explicit queries well but struggle with early-stage queries requiring intent and preference discovery. Rubric analysis attributes this gap mainly to ineffective history use. A simple history-aware query-refinement pipeline, VQRA, yields consistent gains, highlighting the need for dedicated history-utilization modules in personalized agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Agent Personalized Benchmark (APeB) for testing LLM agents on personalization with raw queries, finding they struggle with intent discovery from histories, and proposes a history-aware query refinement method (VQRA) that improves performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-powered agents struggle with personalization when users provide raw, underspecified queries, as agents must infer latent intent and extract preferences from noisy interaction histories. Existing benchmarks rarely test this capability as they often use refined queries or simplified histories.
AI.method: The paper introduces personalized product search (PPS) as a testbed for agentic personalization under raw queries and diverse histories. They construct Agent Personalized Benchmark (APeB) from action logs, pairing underspecified intents with rich histories and user-viewed candidate items. They evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs with multi-step agent workflows and analyze performance gaps through rubric analysis. They also propose VQRA, a simple history-aware query-refinement pipeline.
AI.result: Models handle explicit queries well but struggle with early-stage queries requiring intent and preference discovery. Rubric analysis attributes this gap mainly to ineffective history use. The proposed VQRA (history-aware query-refinement pipeline) yields consistent gains, highlighting the need for dedicated history-utilization modules.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents need better history utilization for personalization with raw queries. Dedicated history-utilization modules are necessary for effective personalized agents, as demonstrated by the improvements from VQRA. The APeB benchmark provides a realistic testbed for evaluating agentic personalization capabilities.

title: Labeled-Data-Free Meta-Learning: Efficient Task Generation Using Pre-trained Models and Unlabeled Data
summary: Meta-learning without labeled data is crucial for real-world applications, where obtaining labeled datasets can be expensive or restricted due to privacy concerns. Data-Free Meta-Learning (DFML) addresses this challenge by leveraging pre-trained models without access to training data. However, existing DFML methods rely on model inversion to generate training data, a process that is generally difficult and computationally expensive due to the need to generate high-dimensional data matching the original distribution. To address this limitation, we propose a novel meta-learning setting that avoids model inversion by jointly leveraging pre-trained models and unlabeled data. Our method generates meta-training tasks by assigning soft labels from pre-trained models to unlabeled data. Since the quality of these tasks can vary, we introduce a task-weighting mechanism based on task confidence and class distribution balance to ensure effective meta-learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach substantially reduces computational cost and improves generalization, achieving up to 104-fold speedup and 8.4 percent to 36.4 percent improvements in few-shot classification accuracy compared to state-of-the-art DFML methods.

AI.tldr: Data-Free Meta-Learning (DFML) without labeled data using pre-trained models and unlabeled data instead of computationally expensive model inversion.
AI.motivation: Labeled data acquisition is expensive and restricted due to privacy concerns in real-world applications. Existing DFML methods rely on difficult and computationally expensive model inversion to generate training data.
AI.method: Proposes a novel meta-learning setting that avoids model inversion by jointly using pre-trained models and unlabeled data. Generates meta-training tasks by assigning soft labels from pre-trained models to unlabeled data, with a task-weighting mechanism based on task confidence and class distribution balance.
AI.result: Achieves substantial computational cost reduction (up to 104-fold speedup) and improved generalization (8.4% to 36.4% improvements in few-shot classification accuracy) compared to state-of-the-art DFML methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully addresses the computational challenges of DFML by eliminating model inversion, making data-free meta-learning more practical and efficient for real-world applications.

title: Trading Confidence: Comprehensive Uncertainty Estimation in Algorithmic Trading
comment: Accepted at the Pacific Asia Conference on Information Systems (PACIS 2025). 17 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful approach in financial trading, enabling agents to learn optimal strategies through direct market interaction. However, financial markets are highly uncertain, with price fluctuations driven by stochastic volatility, model limitations, and regime shifts. Traditional RL models struggle in dynamic environments, often failing to adapt to sudden market disruptions, leading to suboptimal trading decisions. To address this challenge, we propose an uncertainty-aware RL framework that integrates distributional, epistemic, and aleatoric uncertainty estimations. Our approach enhances uncertainty estimation using SHAP-weighted reconstruction uncertainty, MC Dropout, and an LSTM-based technical indicator consensus mechanism. Experimental results on five major U.S. stock indices demonstrate that RL agents equipped with uncertainty estimation significantly outperform traditional models in return and risk management. This study advances uncertainty estimation in RL-based financial trading, with future research extending its application to other asset classes and alternative RL architectures for greater adaptability.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware RL framework improves financial trading by integrating three types of uncertainty estimation (distributional, epistemic, aleatoric), outperforming traditional RL models in stock market trading.
AI.motivation: Financial markets are highly uncertain with stochastic volatility, model limitations, and regime shifts. Traditional RL models struggle in dynamic environments and fail to adapt to sudden market disruptions, leading to suboptimal trading decisions.
AI.method: Proposes an uncertainty-aware RL framework integrating distributional, epistemic, and aleatoric uncertainty estimations. Uses SHAP-weighted reconstruction uncertainty, MC Dropout, and an LSTM-based technical indicator consensus mechanism for enhanced uncertainty estimation.
AI.result: Experimental results on five major U.S. stock indices show that RL agents with uncertainty estimation significantly outperform traditional models in both return and risk management.
AI.conclusion: This study advances uncertainty estimation in RL-based financial trading and suggests future research for application to other asset classes and alternative RL architectures for greater adaptability.

title: Embodied Operators and Benchmarking: Toward Reusable and Deployable Embodied Intelligence Systems
summary: Embodied intelligence systems require not only end-to-end policy models, but also reusable functional modules that transform multimodal observations, robot states, human demonstrations, and task contexts into structured representations, decisions, trajectories, control references, and system services. This work defines these modules as embodied operators and studies them as independent yet composable units in embodied intelligence pipelines. We clarify their definition boundary, emphasizing task semantics, standardized input-output contracts, deployability, reusability, and multi-layer optimizability. We further construct a taxonomy covering five categories: detection and segmentation, spatial localization and 3D understanding, hand motion recovery, embodied foundation models and task-decision operators, and planning, control, and system support operators. For each category, we summarize representative functions, technical paradigms, application roles, and practical limitations. Beyond taxonomy, we propose a multi-dimensional benchmark framework that evaluates embodied operators in terms of correctness, end-to-end efficiency, resource usage, temporal stability, portability, interface compatibility, deployment reliability, and downstream task utility. We also discuss workflow-level operator acceleration and open challenges in operator composition, data standardization, world models, VLA safety, edge deployment, and real-world application value. Overall, this work argues that embodied operators should be optimized and evaluated as holistic deployable components, providing a foundation for reusable, scalable, and verifiable embodied intelligence systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes "embodied operators" as standardized, reusable modules for building embodied intelligence systems, provides a taxonomy and benchmarking framework, and discusses challenges for scalable robot AI.
AI.motivation: Current embodied intelligence systems lack standardized, reusable functional modules. The paper aims to address this gap by defining embodied operators as independent, composable units that can transform multimodal observations, robot states, and task contexts into structured representations for scalable robot AI systems.
AI.method: 1. Define embodied operators with clear boundaries (task semantics, standardized I/O contracts, deployability, reusability, multi-layer optimizability). 2. Construct taxonomy covering five categories of operators. 3. Propose multi-dimensional benchmark framework for evaluation. 4. Discuss workflow-level acceleration and open challenges.
AI.result: Provides: 1) Clear definition of embodied operators, 2) Comprehensive taxonomy covering detection/segmentation, spatial localization, hand motion recovery, embodied foundation models, and planning/control operators, 3) Multi-dimensional benchmarking framework evaluating correctness, efficiency, resource usage, stability, portability, compatibility, reliability, and utility.
AI.conclusion: Embodied operators should be optimized and evaluated as holistic deployable components, providing foundation for reusable, scalable, and verifiable embodied intelligence systems. The framework addresses key challenges in operator composition, data standardization, world models, safety, deployment, and real-world application value.

title: Reward Granularity in RLVR: Comparing Process and Outcome Reward Structures for Mathematical Reasoning in Small Language Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for improving mathematical reasoning in language models. Yet most RLVR work rewards only the final answer (outcome-based rewards), leaving the impact of step-level process supervision (process rewards) underexplored especially for small models that lack the capacity to self-correct under sparse feedback. We systematically compare five reward conditions applied to Qwen2.5-0.5B fine-tuned with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on GSM8K: a no-RL baseline, process-only, outcome-only, and three hybrid weightings ($λ\in \{0.9, 0.5, 0.1\}$ process weight). Process-only supervision achieves 63.73% test accuracy versus 53.75% for outcome-only, a nearly 10-percentage point gap while yielding reasoning traces with higher step validity and lower deviation from ground-truth chain length. Hybrid rewards generally correlate positively with process weight, with one notable anomaly: the low-process / high-outcome configuration ($λ=0.1$) underperforms pure outcome supervision, suggesting conflicting optimization signals. Error analysis using GPT-4o as a judge reveals distinct failure mode distributions: process models generate structurally inconsistent but arithmetically grounded traces, while outcome models produce concise but derivation-error-prone chains. Our results demonstrate that reward granularity is a first-order design decision for RLVR, with process-level supervision substantially improving both accuracy and trace fidelity in small language models.

AI.tldr: Process-level rewards significantly outperform outcome-only rewards in RLVR for small language models, improving mathematical reasoning accuracy by nearly 10 percentage points while producing more valid and structured reasoning traces.
AI.motivation: Most RLVR work focuses on rewarding only final answers (outcome-based rewards), leaving step-level process supervision (process rewards) underexplored, especially for small models that lack self-correction capacity under sparse feedback.
AI.method: Systematically compare five reward conditions applied to Qwen2.5-0.5B fine-tuned with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on GSM8K: no-RL baseline, process-only, outcome-only, and three hybrid weightings (λ∈{0.9, 0.5, 0.1} process weight).
AI.result: Process-only supervision achieves 63.73% test accuracy vs. 53.75% for outcome-only (nearly 10 percentage point gap), producing reasoning traces with higher step validity and lower deviation from ground-truth chain length. Hybrid rewards generally correlate positively with process weight, except λ=0.1 underperforms pure outcome supervision.
AI.conclusion: Reward granularity is a first-order design decision for RLVR, with process-level supervision substantially improving both accuracy and trace fidelity in small language models, and models exhibit different failure modes depending on reward type.

title: Reflective Dialogue or Prompt Refinement? Effects of Tutor Scaffolding on Students' Independent LLM Use for Programming
comment: Best paper award, Published in AIED 2026: The 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) can provide personalized support in learning, several studies have raised concerns regarding their use in education. Importantly, learning depends on how students engage with LLMs. This study examined how two types of LLM-based tutors shape students' prompting practices, learning, and subsequent LLM-use: a Socratic-Guidance (SG) tutor, which structures interaction through dialogic questioning, and a Prompt-Refinement (PR) tutor that guides the formulation of effective prompts. We conducted a two-phase study in a graduate-level mobile robotics course: 66 students used either the SG or PR tutor during a 6-week intervention, followed by 52 students using an unconstrained LLM during a 3-week course project. Results show that while the SG- and PR tutors led to similar task performance and prompting patterns during guided use, they differ in learning outcomes and later LLM-use. SG-students, relative to PR-student, achieved higher learning gains in later sessions, and were more likely to adopt understanding-driven prompting strategies, which are predictive of higher understanding, when using an unconstrained LLM. Although learners perceived the SG tutor as less efficient, the findings suggest that Socratic guidance supports the development of students' capacity to learn with LLMs over time, highlighting its importance for LLM tutor design.

AI.tldr: LLM tutors with Socratic-Guidance (question-based) vs Prompt-Refinement approaches show similar short-term task performance but different long-term outcomes: Socratic-Guidance leads to better learning gains and more understanding-driven prompting strategies when students later use unconstrained LLMs.
AI.motivation: While LLMs offer personalized learning support, concerns exist about their educational use. The study aims to understand how different LLM tutor designs shape students' prompting practices, learning outcomes, and future LLM usage patterns in educational contexts.
AI.method: Two-phase study in a graduate mobile robotics course: Phase 1 involved 66 students using either Socratic-Guidance (dialogic questioning) or Prompt-Refinement (prompt formulation guidance) tutors during a 6-week intervention. Phase 2 involved 52 students using unconstrained LLMs during a 3-week course project to examine subsequent LLM-use patterns.
AI.result: SG and PR tutors led to similar task performance and prompting patterns during guided use. However, SG students achieved higher learning gains in later sessions and were more likely to adopt understanding-driven prompting strategies (predictive of higher understanding) when using unconstrained LLMs. SG was perceived as less efficient but fostered better long-term learning capacity.
AI.conclusion: Socratic guidance in LLM tutor design supports the development of students' capacity to learn with LLMs over time, promoting more effective prompting strategies and better learning outcomes despite being perceived as less efficient. This highlights the importance of pedagogical approaches in LLM tutor design for sustainable educational benefits.

title: Poisson-Gamma Modeling of Inter-Relational Dependencies in Dynamic Knowledge Graphs
comment: Accepted at the 42nd Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2026). 20 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables
summary: Dynamic knowledge graphs are ubiquitous in today's AI applications, as we represent molecular structures, social relationships, and language information using these graph models. As knowledge graphs evolve over time and are often noisy and incomplete, modeling their temporal and relational dependencies becomes crucial for downstream tasks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes PGRE (Poisson-Gamma Relational Evolution), a probabilistic model for modeling inter-relational dependencies in dynamic knowledge graphs. PGRE represents multi-relational temporal links via a Poisson-Bernoulli formulation. It introduces Gamma-distributed latent variables to capture entity-factor associations and cross-relation dependencies mediated by shared latent communities. A Gamma Markov process further models the temporal evolution of these latent variables, enabling principled characterization of relational dynamics. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that PGRE achieves competitive performance in link prediction, particularly in sparse settings, while revealing meaningful relational evolution patterns in dynamic knowledge graphs.

AI.tldr: PGRE: A Poisson-Gamma probabilistic model for dynamic knowledge graphs that captures inter-relational dependencies and temporal evolution through Gamma-distributed latent variables.
AI.motivation: Dynamic knowledge graphs are widely used but evolve over time and are often noisy/incomplete, making modeling their temporal and relational dependencies crucial for downstream tasks.
AI.method: Proposes PGRE (Poisson-Gamma Relational Evolution) using Poisson-Bernoulli formulation for temporal links, Gamma-distributed latent variables for entity-factor associations and cross-relation dependencies via shared latent communities, and Gamma Markov process for temporal evolution.
AI.result: PGRE achieves competitive link prediction performance on benchmark datasets, especially in sparse settings, while revealing meaningful relational evolution patterns in dynamic knowledge graphs.
AI.conclusion: PGRE provides an effective probabilistic framework for modeling inter-relational dependencies and temporal evolution in dynamic knowledge graphs, with practical benefits for link prediction and pattern discovery.

title: When Aggregate Alignment Misleads: Auditing Policy Repair Without Per-State Expert Actions
summary: Agentic AI systems are increasingly used to edit, refine, and repair decision policies, but evaluating these edits is difficult when per-state expert action labels are unavailable. We study this problem in a hotel-pricing simulator where an agentic policy editor receives only region-level diagnostic feedback: summaries of how its price distribution differs from a benchmark policy across time, inventory, and market regions. The editor cannot observe benchmark actions, benchmark source code, reward numbers, or held-out outcomes, and may only propose constrained edits to a target-action table. On 5,000 held-out episodes, a multi-restart LLM editor reaches RevPAR 108.47 (95% CI 107.61 - 109.34), close to the benchmark policy's 108.75 (107.81 - 109.68), with paired gap (LLM minus benchmark) -0.276 and 95% CI [-0.692, 0.146]. A cheap diagnostic projection already recovers much of the revenue (107.90), so the LLM editor's distinctive gain is not raw revenue lift alone: it also reduces episode composition distance from 1.153 to 0.609. This is the strongest non-benchmark repair result. This profile is not explained by restart search alone: non-semantic proposers with up to 2,500 evaluations fall 8.77 - 14.57 RevPAR points short. Nor is it explained by plausible prompt format: a shuffled-diagnostic control breaks region-error correspondence and falls to RevPAR 94.30. The match is genuine but partial. A tree editor achieves stronger pooled alignment, 0.214 versus 0.266, and stronger reference-state D1, 0.328 versus 1.197, yet revenue falls to 98.91. These results show that agentic policy repair should be evaluated by whether diagnostic feedback becomes reliable closed-loop outcome, not by a single behavioral distance.

AI.tldr: Agentic AI policy editors can effectively repair hotel pricing policies using only region-level diagnostic feedback without expert action labels, achieving competitive revenue while reducing distributional distance from benchmark.
AI.motivation: Evaluating agentic AI policy edits is challenging when per-state expert action labels are unavailable, requiring methods to assess policy repairs using only diagnostic feedback.
AI.method: Multi-restart LLM editor proposing constrained edits to target-action table using only region-level diagnostic feedback (summaries of price distribution differences from benchmark across regions), with comparison to non-semantic proposers and shuffled-diagnostic controls.
AI.result: LLM editor achieved RevPAR 108.47 (close to benchmark's 108.75) and reduced episode composition distance from 1.153 to 0.609, showing genuine diagnostic learning; non-semantic proposers fell 8.77-14.57 RevPAR points short, and shuffled controls performed poorly.
AI.conclusion: Agentic policy repair should be evaluated by whether diagnostic feedback becomes reliable closed-loop outcome rather than single behavioral distance metrics, as different editors excel in different dimensions.

title: Dynamic Regret for Non-Stationary Linear Bandits via Misspecification Reductions
summary: Many online decision-making problems involve both round-specific feasible actions and drifting reward models: eligible ad impressions, feasible prices, and available treatments can change over time, while user preferences, demand curves, and patient responses may evolve. Motivated by these applications, we study non-stationary linear bandits with round-specific feasible decision sets. Existing methods that obtain the optimal \(\widetilde O(T^{2/3}P_T^{1/3})\) dependence, where \(P_T\) is the path length of the reward-parameter sequence, impose an orthogonal-structure assumption on round-specific decision sets, which can be restrictive in contextual applications. We address this gap through a unified misspecification-reduction viewpoint: after partitioning the horizon into blocks, we relate each block's dynamic regret to regret against a fixed-parameter linear bandit benchmark, with the within-block parameter drift entering as bounded misspecification. Restarting algorithms with misspecification-dependent regret guarantees then yields the optimal \(T^{2/3}P_T^{1/3}\) dynamic-regret dependence for both linear bandits with general compact decision sets and \(K\)-armed contextual linear bandits.

AI.tldr: A novel framework for non-stationary linear bandits with time-varying feasible decision sets achieves optimal dynamic regret bound without restrictive orthogonal structure assumptions.
AI.motivation: Many real-world online decision problems involve both changing feasible actions (eligible ads, prices, treatments) and drifting reward models (evolving user preferences, demand curves, patient responses). Existing optimal methods require restrictive orthogonal-structure assumptions on decision sets.
AI.method: A unified misspecification-reduction approach: partition horizon into blocks, relate each block's dynamic regret to fixed-parameter linear bandit benchmark with parameter drift as bounded misspecification, then apply restarting algorithms with misspecification-dependent guarantees.
AI.result: Achieves optimal $\widetilde O(T^{2/3}P_T^{1/3})$ dynamic regret dependence for both linear bandits with general compact decision sets and $K$-armed contextual linear bandits, removing previous orthogonal-structure requirements.
AI.conclusion: The proposed misspecification-reduction viewpoint successfully bridges theoretical optimality with practical applicability by eliminating restrictive assumptions while maintaining optimal dynamic regret bounds in non-stationary bandit settings with round-specific feasible sets.

title: From Mobile Data to Business Insights: An End-to-End Analytics Framework for Large-Scale Urban Mobility Analysis and Decision Support
summary: Real time location data derived from mobile applications is a powerful tool for addressing various urban challenges, including tourism planning, parking management, bus route optimization, and resource allocation. Besides, it offers invaluable insights for shaping strategic decisions in commercial domains such as location based services, market share analysis, and behavioral profiling. In this expansive study, we aim to address all of the aforementioned challenges by investigating the behaviors and patterns of smartphone users within urban environments, particularly in the domains of tourism, transportation, and retail. Our approach encompasses the development of a sophisticated data platform from inception to implementation, which includes the formulation of use cases, architectural design, and implementation of modules. We employ state of the art techniques and technologies, including data anonymization, ETL pipelines, and utilizing Google BigQuery and Vertex AI for data processing and machine learning model development. A modular architecture based on reusable analytical building blocks was developed to generate data products that support multiple stakeholder driven use cases. Additionally, we apply interactive data visualization techniques via Power BI to facilitate the effective interpretation of analytical findings by stakeholders. The developed models address a wide range of mobility analytics tasks, including mobility profiling, frequent trajectory mining, area of influence analysis, traffic anomaly detection, and origin destination pattern analysis. The results demonstrate the framework's ability to capture user mobility dynamics at fine spatial and temporal resolutions, providing actionable insights for urban planning and strategic business decision making.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive framework for analyzing real-time mobile location data to address urban planning and business challenges through modular analytics platform and machine learning models.
AI.motivation: Real-time mobile location data has significant potential for solving urban challenges (tourism planning, parking management, bus route optimization) and providing business insights (location-based services, market analysis, behavioral profiling), but requires sophisticated systems to process and analyze this data effectively.
AI.method: Developed an end-to-end data platform from use case formulation to implementation, using data anonymization, ETL pipelines, Google BigQuery and Vertex AI for processing/ML, modular architecture with reusable analytical blocks, and Power BI for interactive visualization.
AI.result: The framework successfully captures user mobility dynamics at fine spatial/temporal resolutions, generating data products that support stakeholder use cases and address mobility analytics tasks including profiling, trajectory mining, influence analysis, anomaly detection, and origin-destination patterns.
AI.conclusion: The developed system demonstrates effective application of mobile location data analytics for actionable urban planning insights and strategic business decision-making, showing the practical value of sophisticated data platforms in addressing real-world mobility challenges.

title: Variable Bit-width Quantization: Learning Per-Group Precision for "Bigger-but-Smaller" Language Models
summary: Low-bit quantization shrinks language models but treats precision as a single global hyper-parameter: every weight uses the same bit-width. We introduce Variable Bit-width Quantization (VBQ), a training-time method in which each contiguous group of 64 weights learns its own resolution from {1,2,4,8} bits via a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation, trained jointly by an alternating optimization that gives the precision logits a clean, task-aligned signal. VBQ discovers a consistent, strongly heterogeneous allocation within individual projection types, not merely across layers, impossible to express with per-layer methods: 69% of groups collapse to 1 bit, the LM head averages 1.09 bits, while the first MLP block keeps ~2.5 bits. This pattern is stable enough to freeze into a fixed recipe and reuse without further search. The recipe yields a "bigger-but-smaller" regime: a 131M model at 1.82 mean bits reaches perplexity 4.2 on TinyStories, beating a 55M FP16 model (PPL 4.4) at 3.8x less storage, and lets a 1.46B model on FineWeb-Edu match a 593M FP16 control at ~3.7x less storage with 2.5x more parameters. As quality-per-byte, VBQ is 3.9-8.4x more efficient than FP16. The recipe maps directly to packed low-bit storage, so it also accelerates inference: with custom fused dequantize-and-multiply kernels, memory-bandwidth-bound autoregressive decode is faster at equal output, and the speedup grows with scale (parity at 131M, 1.9x at 1.0B, 4.7x at 9B on Apple silicon). A distributional analysis (KL divergence and argmax-flip rate) reveals a striking mechanism: deeper layers progressively self-heal the quantization error injected by early layers. The win is a from-scratch, train-time phenomenon; scaling the search economically beyond 1.5B parameters remains open. VBQ reframes precision as a learnable, non-uniform resource and shows that spending a fixed bit budget unevenly beats spending it uniformly.

AI.tldr: VBQ is a training-time quantization method that learns variable bit-widths for contiguous weight groups, achieving heterogeneous precision allocation that outperforms uniform quantization for LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current low-bit quantization treats precision as a single global hyperparameter where all weights use the same bit-width, which may not be optimal. The paper aims to learn non-uniform bit allocation for better efficiency.
AI.method: VBQ trains each contiguous group of 64 weights to learn its own resolution from {1,2,4,8} bits via Gumbel-Softmax relaxation. Uses alternating optimization with clean task-aligned signals for precision logits.
AI.result: Discovers consistent heterogeneous allocation: 69% of groups collapse to 1 bit, LM head averages 1.09 bits, first MLP block keeps ~2.5 bits. Achieves 3.9-8.4x more efficiency than FP16. Enables inference acceleration with custom kernels (1.9x at 1B, 4.7x at 9B). Shows deeper layers self-heal quantization error from early layers.
