AI.conclusion: VBQ reframes precision as a learnable, non-uniform resource where spending a fixed bit budget unevenly beats uniform allocation. Enables "bigger-but-smaller" regime with better quality-per-byte and inference acceleration. The discovered patterns are stable enough for fixed recipes.

title: Efficient bias mitigation in T2I diffusion models using Concept Graphs
summary: Text-to-Image diffusion models often propagate harmful bias inherited from the training data. Existing bias mitigation techniques typically intervene only at the text encoder or provide inference-time guidance, often leading to generations that collapse into semantically incoherent outputs. To address these limitations, we introduce CO-ALIGN (Concept Ontology Alignment), a novel bias mitigation approach based on concept-graph alignment that operates on the model's internal concept ontology. By aligning concepts within the text encoder and denoiser, CO-ALIGN achieves substantial bias reduction while preserving generative integrity. We demonstrate the effectiveness of concept-graph alignment across three paradigms: text-encoders, denoisers and joint text-denoiser ontology alignment. CO-ALIGN outperforms the state of the art, improving fairness by $30\%$, $ΔFID=11.4$ in image quality, $2.8\%$ in image fidelity, all while reducing semantically incoherent outputs by $88\%$. Beyond bias mitigation, we show that CO-ALIGN benefits other downstream tasks as well. In particular, our experiments demonstrate that better-aligned internal ontologies enhance concept unlearning robustness across multiple unlearning techniques.

AI.tldr: CO-ALIGN is a novel bias mitigation approach for text-to-image diffusion models that aligns concepts in the model's internal ontology to reduce harmful bias while maintaining generation quality.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models often propagate harmful bias from training data, and existing mitigation techniques cause problems: they typically only intervene at the text encoder or provide inference-time guidance, often leading to semantically incoherent outputs.
AI.method: CO-ALIGN (Concept Ontology Alignment) uses concept-graph alignment operating on the model's internal concept ontology. It aligns concepts within both the text encoder and denoiser components, implemented across three paradigms: text-encoder alignment, denoiser alignment, and joint text-denoiser ontology alignment.
AI.result: CO-ALIGN outperforms state-of-the-art methods with: 30% fairness improvement, ΔFID=11.4 improvement in image quality, 2.8% improvement in image fidelity, and 88% reduction in semantically incoherent outputs. It also enhances concept unlearning robustness across multiple unlearning techniques.
AI.conclusion: Concept-graph alignment effectively reduces bias in text-to-image diffusion models while preserving generative integrity. The approach demonstrates that better-aligned internal ontologies not only mitigate bias but also benefit downstream tasks like concept unlearning.

title: Bootstrap Flow-Map Tree Sampling Enables Online Feedback Driven Search
summary: In many scientific and engineering domains, maximizing discovery within a limited sampling budget demands strategic, observation-guided exploration. While generative models have enabled training-free reward alignment, current methods typically excel in local searches within narrow regions of the underlying distribution. These approaches struggle when preferences are unknown a priori and only revealed through sequential feedback-a scenario demanding broad exploration to uncover high-utility regions. To address this, we introduce Bootstrap Flow-Map-Tree (a.k.a BFMT), a novel computationally efficient sampling framework designed for history-aware global search and alignment under sampling budget constraints. BFMT enables full tree-path construction from any tree depth using a single function evaluation, drastically reducing computational overhead while providing critical foresight for sequential sampling. By enabling dynamic transition time steps scheduling, BFMT efficiently allocates its sampling budget, smoothly transitioning from broad global exploration to fine-grained local refinement of high-utility modes discovered through exploration. Extensive experiments and ablations across diverse search and alignment tasks demonstrate that BFMT substantially outperforms baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: BFMT (Bootstrap Flow-Map-Tree) is a new computationally efficient sampling framework that enables history-aware global search and alignment under budget constraints, outperforming existing methods in diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Current generative model approaches for reward alignment struggle when preferences are unknown and only revealed through sequential feedback. They excel at local searches but fail at broad exploration needed to uncover high-utility regions, which is critical for maximizing discovery within limited sampling budgets.
AI.method: BFMT enables full tree-path construction from any depth using a single function evaluation, drastically reducing computational overhead. It incorporates dynamic transition time steps scheduling to efficiently allocate sampling budget, transitioning smoothly from broad global exploration to fine-grained local refinement of discovered high-utility modes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and ablations across diverse search and alignment tasks demonstrate that BFMT substantially outperforms baseline approaches in terms of discovery efficiency and alignment performance under budget constraints.
AI.conclusion: BFMT provides an effective solution for strategic, observation-guided exploration in scientific and engineering domains where maximizing discovery under limited sampling budgets requires both broad exploration and precise local refinement.

title: Personalized Causal Recourse: A Human-In-The-Loop Approach
comment: Accepted at the 4th World Conference on eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI 2026)
summary: Algorithmic recourse addresses the challenge of providing tailored recommendations to users affected by unfavorable machine learning decisions, in potentially high-stakes scenarios. Traditional approaches to recourse often rely on the closest counterfactual explanations or assume a priori knowledge of a user's causal structure, resulting in interventions that overlook individual contexts and specific feature interactions. To overcome these limitations, we study a human-in-the-loop framework that iteratively approximates the user's structural causal model through interactive queries via Bayesian inference before producing recourse recommendations. This framework exploits humans' feedback to improve the identification of causal effects, allowing personalized recourse that is plausible, cost-effective, and aligned with the actual causal dependencies of each user. As a proof of concept, we evaluate this framework through simulated human responses. Our simulations across linear and non-linear causal models show promising results, though challenges remain in capturing complex, non-linear structures, emphasizing the importance of accurate approximations and robust noise distribution modeling.

AI.tldr: A human-in-the-loop framework that iteratively learns users' causal models through Bayesian inference for personalized algorithmic recourse, showing promise but facing challenges with complex non-linear structures.
AI.motivation: Traditional recourse approaches rely on closest counterfactual explanations or assume a priori knowledge of user's causal structure, resulting in interventions that overlook individual contexts and specific feature interactions. There's a need for more personalized recourse that accounts for actual causal dependencies of each user.
AI.method: A human-in-the-loop framework that iteratively approximates user's structural causal model through interactive queries using Bayesian inference before generating recourse recommendations. The method leverages human feedback to improve causal effect identification and was evaluated through simulated human responses across linear and non-linear causal models.
AI.result: Simulations across linear and non-linear causal models show promising results for generating plausible, cost-effective, and personalized recourse recommendations. However, challenges remain in capturing complex non-linear structures, emphasizing the importance of accurate approximations and robust noise distribution modeling.
AI.conclusion: The human-in-the-loop framework shows promise for personalized algorithmic recourse through interactive causal model approximation, though challenges remain in handling complex non-linear structures. The approach demonstrates the importance of accurate causal approximations and robust noise modeling for effective recourse recommendations.

title: CoFEND: A Cross-Modal Fusion End-to-End Network for Cold-Start Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction
summary: Cold-start drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction for new drugs is critical for minimizing unexpected adverse drug reactions. The key challenge is to capture similarity between new and known drugs. However, such similarity is closely associated with complex relationships and mechanisms among drugs, enzymes, transporters, molecular structures, and other biomedical entities. Existing methods have three limitations in capturing such similarity: (1) only partial relationships and mechanisms are considered, which overlooks cross-modal information and yields incomplete or biased similarity modeling; (2) similarity computation between new and known drugs is conducted separately across modalities and performed offline for cold-start DDI prediction, leading to misalignment between similarity computation and DDI prediction; and (3) existing interpretability analyses are typically single-modality and focus primarily on key determinants of the perpetrator drug, while the underlying causes of susceptibility for the victim drug are seldom investigated. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Cross-Modal-Fused End-to-End Learning Network (CMF-ELN) with three components. First, diverse multimodal information is leveraged to construct four types of drug-centered knowledge graphs, enabling comprehensive similarity modeling under reconstruction-based supervision. Second, a four-channel graph autoencoder is designed to fuse cross-modal similarity within an end-to-end learning framework. Finally, a two-stage interpretability scheme is devised to precisely localize key factors for both perpetrator and victim drugs. Extensive experiments on two real datasets demonstrate that CMF-ELN achieves significantly higher prediction accuracy and more comprehensive interpretability of mechanisms than its peers.

AI.tldr: A new cross-modal fusion framework called CMF-ELN improves cold-start DDI prediction by comprehensively modeling drug similarity through multiple knowledge graphs and providing better interpretability for both perpetrator and victim drugs.
AI.motivation: Cold-start DDI prediction for new drugs is critical for minimizing adverse drug reactions. Existing methods have limitations: (1) only partial relationships considered, overlooking cross-modal information; (2) similarity computation is separated from DDI prediction; (3) interpretability analyses are single-modality and focus only on perpetrator drugs, ignoring victim drug susceptibility.
AI.method: Proposes CMF-ELN with three components: (1) constructs four types of drug-centered knowledge graphs using diverse multimodal information for comprehensive similarity modeling; (2) designs a four-channel graph autoencoder to fuse cross-modal similarity within an end-to-end learning framework; (3) devises a two-stage interpretability scheme to localize key factors for both perpetrator and victim drugs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two real datasets demonstrate that CMF-ELN achieves significantly higher prediction accuracy and more comprehensive interpretability of mechanisms compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: CMF-ELN successfully addresses the limitations of existing methods by providing a comprehensive cross-modal fusion framework that improves both prediction accuracy and interpretability for cold-start DDI prediction, with particular focus on understanding both perpetrator and victim drug mechanisms.

title: Demonstrating Generalization Failures via Mixtures of Conditional Policies
summary: Post-training of frontier language models is conducted on curated task suites, and inevitably leaves a distribution shift between training and deployment environments. This exposes developers to generalization failures, which are relatively poorly understood. To better understand such generalization failures, we believe the community should construct clean demonstrations under simplified conditions. To facilitate this, we propose a simple and flexible way to construct language models which fail to generalize in controllable ways when subsequently trained with Reinforcement Learning (RL) on a given distribution of training tasks. Our construction uses Supervised Fine-Tuning on a dataset of a mixture of transcripts corresponding to a collection of 'conditional policies', which can each independently be assigned certain behaviors on each different task distribution, to obtain a model that is then well approximated as a 'mixture of conditional policies.' We observe that RL training then selects for policies that obtain the highest reward on the training distribution. This can produce striking behaviors: in a controlled setting with two distributions containing identical questions prepended with two different 'trigger strings', RL training on either distribution actively degrades performance on the other to zero, even though the underlying task is identical. We also use our construction to illustrate two novel ways in which generalization may fail in future language models, corresponding to distribution shifts of task coverage and temporal context respectively. While our construction is deliberately simple and may not closely resemble 'natural' generalization failures, the resulting 'model organisms' are of interest for alignment stress-testing and generalization science, and can be used as existence proofs that training success and generalization can come apart in structured ways.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to construct language models that fail to generalize in controllable ways after RL training, creating "model organisms" for studying generalization failures and alignment stress-testing.
AI.motivation: There's a distribution shift between training and deployment environments in language model post-training, leading to poorly understood generalization failures. The community needs clean demonstrations under simplified conditions to better understand these failures.
AI.method: Use Supervised Fine-Tuning on a mixture of transcripts corresponding to 'conditional policies' (each with assigned behaviors on different task distributions) to create a model approximated as a 'mixture of conditional policies'. Then apply Reinforcement Learning training which selects policies with highest reward on the training distribution.
AI.result: In controlled experiments with identical questions but different trigger strings, RL training on one distribution completely degrades performance on the other to zero. The construction illustrates novel generalization failures for task coverage and temporal context distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: While deliberately simple and not resembling natural generalization failures, these "model organisms" are valuable for alignment stress-testing and generalization science, serving as existence proofs that training success and generalization can diverge in structured ways.

title: In-span learning: adapting reduced-order models using their own predictions
summary: Reduced-order models compress high-dimensional dynamics into low-dimensional representations that can be evaluated rapidly, but they lose accuracy when online dynamics drift beyond the training data. Adaptive methods address this by updating the subspace online with external, out-of-span information, such as full-order corrections or sensor snapshots. We discovered that a complementary and previously unexploited in-span adaptation channel exists within the current reduced subspace. By streaming the model's own predictions through an incremental singular-value decomposition with forgetting, we obtain a trajectory-informed spectral preconditioner, in which the subspace is unchanged but the basis is reweighted and realigned toward the modes visited by the dynamics. This enables the model to absorb future out-of-span corrections more effectively. We expose aspects of this mechanism on a three-dimensional spiral and confirm it on viscous Burgers and Fisher-KPP dynamics. We also discuss how in-span learning can be viewed as a dynamical-systems analogue of in-context learning. More broadly, in-span learning suggests a new principle for computational science, revealing that model-generated trajectories contain more usable information than previously recognized.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "in-span learning," a novel adaptive method that uses a model's own predictions to improve reduced-order models by realigning and reweighting the basis within the existing subspace, enabling better absorption of future out-of-span corrections.
AI.motivation: Reduced-order models lose accuracy when online dynamics drift beyond training data, and existing adaptive methods rely on external out-of-span information. The authors recognized an unexploited opportunity: using the model's own predictions (in-span information) within the current reduced subspace for adaptation.
AI.method: They stream the model's predictions through an incremental singular-value decomposition with forgetting to create a trajectory-informed spectral preconditioner. This keeps the subspace unchanged but reweights and realigns the basis toward the modes visited by the dynamics, essentially learning from the model's own trajectory.
AI.result: The method enables reduced-order models to absorb future out-of-span corrections more effectively. It was validated on three systems: a 3D spiral, viscous Burgers equation, and Fisher-KPP dynamics. The in-span learning approach reveals that model-generated trajectories contain more usable information than previously recognized.
AI.conclusion: In-span learning represents a new principle for computational science and a dynamical-systems analogue of in-context learning, showing that models can self-improve by leveraging their own predictions within their existing representation space.

title: MentalThink: Shaping Thoughts in Mental SVG World
summary: We introduce MentalThink, a visual-symbolic reasoning paradigm that equips Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) with an executable mechanism for "mental" visualization. The core of MentalThink is a think-with-SVG pipeline, where the model learns to generate, render, and interpret scalable vector graphics (SVG) code as an intermediate visual representation for multi-turn reasoning. By creating structured vector sketches, the model can externalize spatial hypotheses, inspect them through deterministic rendering, and reason within a constrained geometric space, effectively mimicking the human process of mental imagery. We instantiate this paradigm through a two-stage training framework, combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for SVG syntactic alignment with multi-turn Reinforcement Learning (RL) to encourage iterative inspection, revision, and refinement of intermediate visual hypotheses. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that MentalThink achieves superior performance on spatial understanding and reasoning benchmarks (e.g., 55.1% on VSIBench, 76.0% on MindCube), showing that executable vector graphics provide a verifiable visual workspace for dynamic perspective taking, visual reflection, and compositional scene construction.

AI.tldr: MentalThink introduces a visual-symbolic reasoning paradigm where MLLMs generate and execute SVG code as an intermediate visual representation, enabling mental visualization through structured vector sketches and iterative refinement.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for multimodal LLMs to have an executable mechanism for "mental" visualization, mimicking human mental imagery processes. Current MLLMs lack structured visual reasoning capabilities for spatial understanding, perspective taking, and dynamic scene construction.
AI.method: MentalThink uses a think-with-SVG pipeline where MLLMs generate, render, and interpret SVG code as intermediate visual representations. It employs a two-stage training framework: 1) Supervised Fine-Tuning for SVG syntactic alignment, and 2) multi-turn Reinforcement Learning to encourage iterative inspection, revision, and refinement of visual hypotheses.
AI.result: MentalThink achieves superior performance on spatial reasoning benchmarks: 55.1% on VSIBench and 76.0% on MindCube. The approach demonstrates that executable vector graphics provide a verifiable visual workspace for dynamic perspective taking, visual reflection, and compositional scene construction.
AI.conclusion: The MentalThink paradigm successfully equips MLLMs with executable mental visualization capabilities through structured vector graphics, enabling them to externalize spatial hypotheses and reason in constrained geometric spaces, effectively mimicking human mental imagery processes.

title: Missingness as Signal: Channel-Independent Spectrogram Learning for Clinical Time Series Prediction
summary: Clinical time series prediction in intensive care units remains challenging due to heterogeneous physiological variables and informative missingness. The presence or absence of a measurement can reflect clinical decisions and patient severity, and thus missingness can serve as a predictive signal rather than a simple data artifact. This work presents CISM, a Channel-Independent Spectrogram framework with a Missingness stream for clinical multivariate time series prediction. CISM converts each clinical variable into a variable-wise time-frequency spectrogram, preserves variable identity through variable-aligned encoding, and aligns an explicit missingness stream with the spectrogram representation. Experiments on an in-hospital mortality task derived from MIMIC-IV show that CISM achieves the highest mean AUROC (0.7225), AUPRC (0.3308), and F1 (0.3808) among the compared time series, missingness-aware, vision, and time-frequency baselines. Ablation studies further show that observation patterns provide a meaningful informative signal. Pixel-level mask injection improves performance over plain spectrogram inputs and recovers much of this predictive value. The aligned missingness stream contributes a further, complementary gain in both AUROC and AUPRC. These results highlight the importance of modeling observation patterns as structured signals in clinical time series prediction.

AI.tldr: CISM is a channel-independent spectrogram framework with missingness stream that achieves state-of-the-art performance for clinical time series prediction by modeling missingness as informative signals.
AI.motivation: Clinical time series prediction is challenging due to heterogeneous variables and informative missingness. Missing measurements can reflect clinical decisions and patient severity, serving as predictive signals rather than data artifacts.
AI.method: CISM converts each clinical variable into variable-wise time-frequency spectrograms, preserves variable identity through variable-aligned encoding, and aligns an explicit missingness stream with the spectrogram representation using pixel-level mask injection.
AI.result: On MIMIC-IV in-hospital mortality task, CISM achieves highest mean AUROC (0.7225), AUPRC (0.3308), and F1 (0.3808) among compared baselines. Ablation studies show observation patterns provide meaningful signal, and aligned missingness stream provides complementary performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Modeling observation patterns as structured signals is crucial for clinical time series prediction. The proposed CISM framework effectively leverages missingness information to improve prediction performance.

title: Applying Answer Set Programming with Fuzzy Membership Functions: a Case Study
comment: A shorter version of this paper has been accepted at the 24th International Workshop on Nonmonotonic Reasoning (July 17-19, 2026, Lisbon, Portugal)
summary: Human reasoning often operates through qualitative concepts expressed by linguistic labels such as high, low, expensive, or cheap, whose interpretation depends on context and is usually vague, despite being rooted in numerical data. This paper explores a novel fuzzy-logic-based qualitative extension of Answer Set Programming (ASP) to bridge numerical information and qualitative reasoning. The underlying language, formally introduced in a separate work, provides a principled framework that avoids rigid thresholds and supports robust reasoning under vagueness. Focusing on a representative use case, we illustrate how the framework integrates numerically grounded inputs (such as outputs of machine learning models) with symbolic reasoning over qualitative labels. Key features, including learning-based membership functions and semantically enriched predicates, enable the combination of expert knowledge, contextual factors, and subjective interpretations within a unified declarative setting.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a fuzzy-logic-based qualitative extension of Answer Set Programming (ASP) that bridges numerical data with qualitative reasoning using linguistic labels, enabling robust reasoning under vagueness without rigid thresholds.
AI.motivation: Human reasoning often uses qualitative concepts (like "high" or "low") that are context-dependent and vague, yet rooted in numerical data. There's a need to bridge numerical information with qualitative reasoning in a principled way that avoids rigid thresholds and supports robust reasoning under vagueness.
AI.method: The paper develops a novel fuzzy-logic-based qualitative extension of Answer Set Programming (ASP) with a formally introduced language. The framework integrates numerically grounded inputs (like ML model outputs) with symbolic reasoning over qualitative labels, featuring learning-based membership functions and semantically enriched predicates.
AI.result: The framework provides a principled approach that combines expert knowledge, contextual factors, and subjective interpretations within a unified declarative setting. It demonstrates how to integrate numerical inputs with symbolic qualitative reasoning through a representative use case.
AI.conclusion: The proposed fuzzy-logic-based qualitative ASP extension successfully bridges numerical information and qualitative reasoning, offering a robust framework for handling vagueness and context-dependency while avoiding rigid thresholds, enabling practical applications that combine machine learning outputs with symbolic reasoning.

title: A Precedent-Guided Co-Scientist for Side-Effect-Aware Drug Redesign
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on AI for Science
summary: We propose PRECEDE, a precedent-guided co-scientist for side-effect-aware drug redesign that revises a parent compound to mitigate a specified side effect while preserving therapeutic function. Rather than isolated molecular generation, PRECEDE frames redesign as evidence-grounded reasoning over drug--side-effect associations, biomedical knowledge graphs, and precedents of safety-driven optimization, coordinated by an LLM orchestrator with explicit policies and human-review checkpoints. We position PRECEDE as a human-supervised AI-for-science workflow in which hypotheses remain auditable, falsifiable, and bounded by prior pharmacology.

AI.tldr: PRECEDE is an AI system that redesigns drugs to reduce side effects while maintaining therapeutic function, using biomedical knowledge graphs and precedent-guided reasoning with human oversight.
AI.motivation: Current drug redesign approaches often treat molecular generation in isolation without considering biomedical evidence and safety precedents. There's a need for more evidence-grounded, safety-focused drug optimization that maintains therapeutic effectiveness while mitigating specific side effects.
AI.method: PRECEDE frames drug redesign as evidence-grounded reasoning over drug-side-effect associations, biomedical knowledge graphs, and safety optimization precedents. It uses an LLM orchestrator with explicit policies and human-review checkpoints, creating an auditable workflow where hypotheses remain falsifiable and bounded by prior pharmacology.
AI.result: The paper proposes a human-supervised AI-for-science workflow (PRECEDE) that coordinates multiple knowledge sources to systematically revise parent compounds, maintaining both therapeutic function and improved safety profiles through precedent-guided optimization.
AI.conclusion: PRECEDE represents a shift from isolated molecular generation to evidence-based, safety-aware drug redesign with human oversight, ensuring pharmacological hypotheses remain testable and grounded in existing biomedical knowledge.

title: How to Avoid Debate: Scalable AI Safety via Doubly-Efficient Interactive Proofs
summary:
As AI models continue to develop powerful capabilities, it becomes critical that we are able to verify that their output is aligned with our intentions. A recent line of work focuses on verification via debate, a model of interactive proofs where two competing powerful provers, or AI models, debate each other to convince a weak verifier, or a human, of the correctness of their claim. However, debate assumes that the two AI models possess equal abilities and that one of them is truthful, which may not be realistic.
  In this work, we show \emph{how to avoid debate}: we initiate the study of \emph{single-prover} interactive proofs for AI safety. Prior results in single-prover interactive proofs do not immediately carry over to the AI safety setting: for example, they do not work when the computation has access to an oracle, such as to human judgment or an external database such as the web. We present doubly-efficient single-prover interactive proofs and arguments for oracle-aided computations (also known as relativizing proofs), in the settings where (1) the computation is robust, in the sense that the output does not change if at most a small fraction of the answers to oracle queries are incorrect, or (2) the oracle is a low-degree polynomial. These results suggest that interactive verification is possible even without debate, under structured or noise-tolerant oracle access.

AI.tldr: Single-prover interactive proofs can verify AI outputs without needing debate between competing models, working with oracle-aided computations like human judgment or web access under specific conditions (robustness or polynomial oracles).
AI.motivation: Debate-based verification assumes equal abilities between competing AI models and that one is truthful, which may not be realistic. There's a need for verification methods that don't require debate, especially when dealing with AI systems that access external oracles like human judgment or databases.
AI.method: Proposes single-prover interactive proofs (as opposed to debate with multiple provers) for oracle-aided computations. Develops doubly-efficient interactive proofs and arguments that work when: (1) computations are robust (output stable under small fractions of incorrect oracle answers), or (2) the oracle is a low-degree polynomial function.
AI.result: Demonstrates that interactive verification is possible without debate under structured or noise-tolerant oracle access. Provides theoretical foundations for single-prover interactive proofs that can handle relativizing computations (those with oracle access), overcoming limitations of prior single-prover methods.
AI.conclusion: Single-prover interactive proofs offer a viable alternative to debate-based verification for AI safety, particularly when dealing with robust computations or structured oracles. This suggests interactive verification can work without the unrealistic assumptions of debate-based approaches.

title: Individual Parameters in Weight-Sparse Transformers Appear Interpretable
summary: A central goal of mechanistic interpretability is to understand how neural networks work and what each individual component does. Dominant circuit-finding approaches focus on a specific behavior and reverse-engineer the role of components on the associated sub-distribution. However, past work has shown that components can have different functions that are active on different subsets of the input distribution. In this work we ask whether a single weight can be understood globally across the full training distribution by characterizing when it matters (the inputs on which ablating it changes the model's predictions). We introduce an automated LLM pipeline that writes a short, human-readable description of when a weight matters and verifies it on held-out text, crediting a weight only if its description generalizes. Across two sparse and two dense transformers, the fraction of weights that are interpretable (in this sense) is higher in sparse transformers than in dense ones, a gap that widens once unreliable descriptions are discarded. Our results show that a meaningful fraction of a sparse transformer model's weights can be interpreted: 12 to 31% of weights have a single short description that identifies what the weight is used for.

AI.tldr: Automated LLM pipeline generates human-readable descriptions of when individual weights matter in transformers, finding sparse models have more interpretable weights than dense ones.
AI.motivation: Current mechanistic interpretability approaches focus on specific behaviors on sub-distributions, but weights can have different functions across different input subsets. The authors want to understand whether single weights can be interpreted globally across the full training distribution by characterizing when they matter.
AI.method: Developed an automated LLM pipeline that: 1) writes short human-readable descriptions of when a weight matters (when ablating it changes predictions), and 2) verifies these descriptions on held-out text, only crediting a weight if its description generalizes beyond training examples.
AI.result: Across two sparse and two dense transformers, sparse models have higher fractions of interpretable weights compared to dense ones. After discarding unreliable descriptions, 12-31% of sparse transformer weights have a single short description that identifies what the weight is used for.
AI.conclusion: A meaningful fraction of sparse transformer weights can be globally interpreted across the full training distribution using automated description generation and verification methods. Sparse architectures appear more amenable to this type of mechanistic interpretability than dense ones.

title: The Role of Rigor in Artificial Intelligence
comment: To appear in D. Rickles and T. Thebault (eds.) Philosophy of Rigor (Routledge, 2026)
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) has achieved extraordinary capabilities despite lacking many of the conceptual and scientific foundations associated with mature disciplines. Unlike traditional sciences, where reliable technology typically emerges from theoretical understanding, modern AI has progressed largely through performance-driven iteration and "alchemical" experimentation. This tension motivates a systematic analysis of AI through the lens of rigor. We introduce a three-part framework consisting of conceptual rigor (clarifying foundational concepts), epistemic rigor (establishing scientific understanding), and operational rigor (ensuring reliable performance and deployment). Using this framework, we analyze competing conceptions of intelligence and understanding, the strengths and limitations of the empirical approach to deep learning, the power and pitfalls of benchmarks, and the obstacles to theory development posed by modern AI systems. We argue that the distinctive trajectory of AI arises from how forms of rigor interact across paradigms, resulting in the primacy of operational rigor in modern deep learning. This perspective helps explain both AI's rapid advances and its persistent uncertainties, while clarifying the challenges involved in transforming AI into a mature science and reliable technology.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a three-part rigor framework to analyze AI's unusual development path, explaining why AI advances rapidly through operational rigor despite lacking conceptual and epistemic foundations.
AI.motivation: AI has achieved extraordinary capabilities without following the traditional scientific pattern where theoretical understanding precedes reliable technology. The tension between AI's "alchemical" experimental progress and lack of foundational understanding motivates a systematic analysis through rigor.
AI.method: Develops a three-part framework of conceptual rigor (clarifying foundational concepts), epistemic rigor (establishing scientific understanding), and operational rigor (ensuring reliable performance). Applies this framework to analyze competing conceptions of intelligence, empirical approaches to deep learning, benchmark strengths/limitations, and obstacles to theory development.
AI.result: Finds that AI's distinctive trajectory arises from how different forms of rigor interact across paradigms, resulting in operational rigor taking primacy in modern deep learning over conceptual and epistemic rigor.
AI.conclusion: This rigor perspective explains both AI's rapid advances and persistent uncertainties, while clarifying challenges in transforming AI into a mature science and reliable technology through more balanced integration of all three rigor types.

title: Rank-Order N-of-M Codes for Sparse Distributed Memory: Disentangling Representation and Learning Effects in Noise Robustness Against Contemporary Neuromorphic Architectures
comment: 14 pages, 8 tables, 2 figures. Revisits rank-order sparse distributed memory for continual learning and memory-augmented AI. Includes comparisons with CALM and SpikingMamba, statistical evaluation, and real embedding experiments
summary: Large language models remain limited as continual learning systems, motivating renewed interest in Sparse Distributed Memory (SDM) as an explicit online episodic memory. CALM (Nechesov and Ruponen, 2025) identifies its threshold-binary encoder as an open design question. This paper evaluates rank-order N-of-M encoding (Furber et al., 2007) as an alternative. We make three contributions. First, a faithful reimplementation validates the published architecture by confirming exact equivalence between WheelSDM and RankOrderSDM (cosine similarity 1.0000 across 10 seeds) and reproducing the documented divergence of RDLIF neurons under interference. Second, multi-seed capacity experiments show RankOrderSDM outperforming StandardSDM by 13.4 percentage points at saturation in the scaled configuration and by 0.8 percentage points at the published architecture scale. Third, BER robustness experiments disentangle representation and learning effects, showing that the large robustness gain arises primarily from the interaction of rank-order encoding with MAX-Hebbian learning, while the encoder alone provides only a small advantage under matched learning conditions. Experiments on GloVe-100 embeddings confirm this small but consistent encoding benefit on real structured data, whereas sentence embeddings exhibit a ceiling effect at low memory load. A secondary analysis shows that idealized rank-order encoding requires half the component-level encoding energy of SpikingMamba's SI-LIF neurons at four-bit precision, although decoder costs dominate overall system energy. These results identify which components of the original rank-order SDM architecture provide measurable benefits for contemporary memory-augmented AI systems, offering practical guidance for architectures such as CALM.

AI.tldr: Rank-order N-of-M encoding outperforms standard Sparse Distributed Memory in capacity and robustness, with benefits primarily from encoder-learning interaction rather than encoding alone.
AI.motivation: Large language models have limitations as continual learning systems, motivating renewed interest in Sparse Distributed Memory (SDM) for explicit online episodic memory. CALM identified the threshold-binary encoder as an open design question, leading to evaluation of rank-order encoding as an alternative.
AI.method: The paper uses a faithful reimplementation of published architectures, multi-seed capacity experiments comparing RankOrderSDM vs StandardSDM, BER robustness experiments to disentangle representation and learning effects, experiments on GloVe-100 embeddings and sentence embeddings, and secondary energy analysis comparing to SpikingMamba's SI-LIF neurons.
AI.result: RankOrderSDM outperforms StandardSDM by 13.4 percentage points at saturation in scaled configuration and 0.8 points at published scale. Robustness gains come primarily from rank-order encoding with MAX-Hebbian learning interaction, while encoder alone provides only small advantage. Experiments confirm encoding benefits on GloVe-100 data, with ceiling effect on sentence embeddings. Rank-order encoding requires half the energy of SpikingMamba's neurons at 4-bit precision.
AI.conclusion: The results identify which components of rank-order SDM architecture provide measurable benefits for contemporary memory-augmented AI systems, offering practical guidance for architectures like CALM by showing where the performance improvements actually come from.

title: Robust Feasible Route Construction through Collaborative Partition Optimization
summary: Large-scale Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problems (CVRPs) are commonly solved by partitioning customers into smaller routing problems that can be optimized independently. While this substantially reduces computational complexity, independently constructed routing solutions may leave some customer demand unserved even when sufficient resources exist elsewhere in the fleet. We present Collaborative Routing Constructors (CoRC), a routing framework that enables independently solved subproblems to exchange customers and vehicles during optimization rather than relying solely on a fixed partition or a subsequent global re-optimization stage. Computational experiments on AGS benchmark instances and synthetic instances containing up to 200,000 customers compare CoRC against independent routing, post-routing global re-optimization, and state-of-the-art, end-to-end routing frameworks. Across all evaluated partitioning strategies, CoRC consistently constructs feasible routing solutions where competing partition-based methods do not. Furthermore, it remains effective on problem instances for which the evaluated end-to-end routing frameworks did not produce solutions under the same computational budget. These results demonstrate that collaboration between routing subproblems provides a robust and scalable approach for feasible large-scale route construction.

AI.tldr: CoRC (Collaborative Routing Constructors) is a routing framework that enables independently solved routing subproblems to exchange customers and vehicles during optimization, improving solution feasibility and scalability for large-scale CVRPs.
AI.motivation: When solving large-scale CVRPs by partitioning customers into smaller subproblems, independently constructed routing solutions may fail to serve some customer demand even when sufficient fleet resources exist elsewhere. Traditional approaches using fixed partitions or subsequent global re-optimization stages have limitations in achieving feasible solutions efficiently.
AI.method: CoRC allows routing subproblems to collaborate during optimization by exchanging customers and vehicles rather than being isolated or relying on post-optimization merging. This enables dynamic resource allocation between partitions while maintaining independent optimization of subproblems.
AI.result: CoRC consistently constructs feasible routing solutions where competing partition-based methods fail, and remains effective on large instances (up to 200k customers) where end-to-end routing frameworks couldn't produce solutions under the same computational budget. It outperforms independent routing, global re-optimization, and state-of-the-art end-to-end frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Collaboration between routing subproblems provides a robust and scalable approach for constructing feasible large-scale routing solutions, demonstrating that dynamic resource exchange during optimization is more effective than static partitioning or post-processing methods.

title: MABLE: Masked Autoencoding with Bi-Lipschitz Decoding for Embeddings and Graph Metric Learning
summary: We propose MABLE (Masked Autoencoding with Bi-Lipschitz Decoding for Embeddings and Graph Metric Learning), a self-supervised framework for learning node and graph embeddings from large, heterogeneous graphs, demonstrated here on geospatial mineral-exploration data. MABLE combines masked reconstruction with fixed cosine-similarity losses that align matched augmented views while keeping unpaired embeddings well spread. A bi-Lipschitz feature decoder ties a low-dimensional reconstruction component of each node embedding to feature similarity, while matched-node consistency shapes the remaining context used by graph pooling. Lipschitz-controlled pooling helps stabilize graph-level representations under perturbations of retained node embeddings, while augmentation alignment trains robustness to masking, node dropping, and sampling variation. Across local copper and regional Arabian Shield studies, MABLE embeddings provide complementary downstream signal and produce coherent embedding-derived layers for hypothesis generation without learned discriminators or hard-negative selection.

AI.tldr: MABLE is a self-supervised framework for learning node and graph embeddings from large, heterogeneous graphs using masked autoencoding with bi-Lipschitz decoding and graph metric learning, demonstrated on geospatial mineral-exploration data.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for learning meaningful embeddings from large, heterogeneous graphs (specifically geospatial mineral-exploration data) without relying on labeled data, learned discriminators, or hard-negative mining techniques. It aims to create robust embeddings that can serve both node-level and graph-level tasks while being stable under perturbations.
AI.method: MABLE combines masked reconstruction with fixed cosine-similarity losses for aligned augmented views. It uses a bi-Lipschitz feature decoder to connect low-dimensional reconstruction components to feature similarity. The method includes: 1) matched-node consistency for graph pooling context, 2) Lipschitz-controlled pooling to stabilize graph-level representations, and 3) augmentation alignment for robustness to masking, node dropping, and sampling variations.
AI.result: Applied to local copper and regional Arabian Shield mineral-exploration studies, MABLE embeddings provide complementary downstream signal and produce coherent embedding-derived layers for hypothesis generation. The approach works without requiring learned discriminators or hard-negative selection.
AI.conclusion: MABLE is an effective self-supervised framework for learning both node and graph embeddings from heterogeneous graphs that offers robustness to various perturbations and produces useful representations for downstream tasks in geospatial mineral exploration and potentially other domains.

title: Agent Reinforcement Learning via Pivotal-Aware Self-Feedback Retry
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents have shown strong decision-making capabilities in long-horizon interactive tasks, yet they still struggle to effectively leverage failed trajectories: full retries incur high interaction costs, while experience retrieval tends to dilute critical experience signals. To address this, we propose PivoARL, a self-feedback retry framework for experience exploitation in LLM agents. PivoARL identifies the pivotal erroneous turn through structured reflection and performs local retry only from the corresponding pivotal state, thereby reusing the correct prefix and reducing redundant interactions. From an information-gain perspective, we further show that pivotal retry concentrates useful experience signals near the error boundary, mitigating the signal dilution caused by state-agnostic experience utilization. Based on this insight, we design a pivotal-aware credit assignment mechanism that rewards correct prefixes while isolating erroneous suffixes, and optimize reflection quality through implicit reflection returns. We conduct a systematic evaluation on 4 agent tasks and 7 search-based QA benchmarks. Results show that PivoARL achieves significant improvements on Pass@2/3 across all tasks, with an average gain of about 11.5\% over MetaRL. Moreover, benefiting from contrastive preference signals induced by pivotal turns, PivoARL also consistently improves Pass@1 on over 80\% of the tasks. On Minesweeper environment, PivoARL improves over GiGPO by more than 45\% and reduces interaction turns by about 42\% on average compared with full-retry methods. Code is available at https://github.com/yuki-younai/PivoARL.

AI.tldr: PivoARL is a self-feedback retry framework for LLM agents that identifies pivotal error turns, performs local retries from those states, and uses credit assignment to improve decision-making efficiency.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle to effectively leverage failed trajectories - full retries are expensive while experience retrieval dilutes critical signals. There's a need for more efficient error recovery and experience exploitation.
AI.method: PivoARL uses structured reflection to identify pivotal erroneous turns, performs local retries only from pivotal states (reusing correct prefixes), implements pivotal-aware credit assignment to reward correct prefixes while isolating errors, and optimizes reflection through implicit reflection returns.
AI.result: Achieves ~11.5% average improvement in Pass@2/3 over MetaRL across all tasks, consistently improves Pass@1 on 80%+ tasks, shows 45%+ improvement over GiGPO on Minesweeper, and reduces interaction turns by ~42% compared to full-retry methods.
AI.conclusion: PivoARL effectively addresses experience exploitation in LLM agents by concentrating useful signals near error boundaries, reducing redundant interactions while improving decision-making performance across diverse agent tasks.

title: Transfer Learning in High-dimensional Ising Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICML 2026
summary: In high-dimensional Ising model estimation, target sample sizes are often limited, and effectively using auxiliary binary datasets of unknown relevance remains challenging. To address this, we propose Trans-Ising, a transfer learning method that combines a loss-based source screening rule with a two-stage estimation procedure. The method first identifies informative auxiliary sources using held-out target pseudolikelihood to prevent negative transfer. It then computes an initial estimator via pooled nodewise $\ell_1$-regularized logistic regression, followed by a target-only correction step using a folded-concave penalty. Theoretically, we establish fixed-node $\ell_2$ and $\ell_1$ error bounds, exact graph selection consistency, and the conditional consistency of the screening rule. Through extensive simulations and real-data analyses, we demonstrate that Trans-Ising achieves lower estimation errors than both target-only estimation and naive data pooling.

AI.tldr: Transfer learning method for high-dimensional Ising models that screens auxiliary binary datasets for relevance and uses a two-stage estimation procedure to prevent negative transfer while improving estimation accuracy.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional Ising model estimation often suffers from limited target sample sizes, and existing methods struggle to effectively utilize auxiliary binary datasets of unknown relevance without causing negative transfer.
AI.method: Two-stage transfer learning approach: 1) A loss-based source screening rule using held-out target pseudolikelihood to identify informative auxiliary sources, 2) Initial estimator via pooled nodewise ℓ1-regularized logistic regression followed by target-only correction with folded-concave penalty.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees include fixed-node ℓ2 and ℓ1 error bounds, exact graph selection consistency, and conditional consistency of the screening rule. Empirical results show Trans-Ising achieves lower estimation errors than target-only estimation and naive data pooling.
AI.conclusion: Trans-Ising provides an effective transfer learning framework for high-dimensional Ising models that safely leverages auxiliary data while avoiding negative transfer, offering theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements.

title: Explainable Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Traffic Signal Control
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for adaptive traffic signal control. However, in safety-critical infrastructure like traffic control, the opaque, black-box nature of deep RL models poses challenges for transportation agency acceptance, regulatory compliance, operational trust, troubleshooting, and fine-tuning. To bridge this gap between high-performance optimization and human-comprehensible interpretability, this effort introduces a novel, explainable entity centric RL framework for safe and transparent traffic signal control. Rather than processing traffic states through monolithic, flat vectors, the proposed architecture disaggregates real-time intersection observations into distinct, high-dimensional lane entities and phase temporal configurations to inherently preserve the structural topology and geometric configurations of the intersection. Relational dependencies and inter-lane conflicts are dynamically extracted via a dual-stage attention network featuring sequential multi-head cross-attention and self-attention blocks. This design yields a real time affinity matrix that quantifies the direct influence of signal phases on specific approach volumes and queues, providing full visual and analytical interpretability. To ensure strict operational reliability, a deterministic action-masking interface is integrated directly into the Proximal Policy Optimization pipeline, explicitly blocking invalid phase transitions to guarantee absolute compliance with established signal timing and safety constraints. Evaluated in a microscopic simulation environment, outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in delay minimization. More importantly, the emergent attention weights align precisely with established traffic engineering principles, offering an auditable, trust-enabling, and deployable architecture for next-generation adaptive traffic control systems.

AI.tldr: A novel explainable RL framework for traffic signal control that uses entity-centric modeling and attention mechanisms for interpretability while ensuring safety through action masking.
AI.motivation: The black-box nature of deep RL models in safety-critical traffic control systems creates challenges for agency acceptance, regulatory compliance, operational trust, and troubleshooting, necessitating a more interpretable approach.
AI.method: Proposes an explainable entity-centric RL framework that disaggregates intersection observations into lane entities and phase configurations, uses dual-stage attention networks (cross-attention and self-attention) to extract relational dependencies, and integrates deterministic action masking in Proximal Policy Optimization to enforce safety constraints.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in delay minimization in microscopic simulation, with attention weights that align with established traffic engineering principles, providing full visual and analytical interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers an auditable, trust-enabling, and deployable architecture for next-generation adaptive traffic control systems by bridging high-performance optimization with human-comprehensible interpretability while ensuring safety compliance.

title: Back to Basics: Improving Molecular Understanding in LLMs via SMILES-Graph Translation
summary: Recent advances in molecular large language models have led to strong performance on molecular understanding and generation tasks, yet these gains often come without reliable structural grounding. In particular, existing approaches conflict with the chemistry principle that structure determines function: despite their downstream success, current molecular LLMs perform poorly on basic structure recognition, suggesting that they fail to capture molecular graphs from canonical SMILES. To remedy this, we propose MolBasic, a structure-first framework that strengthens structural comprehension via SMILES-Graph translation. MolBasic is built around a multi-level structure perception benchmark, where bidirectional SMILES-Graph conversion serves as the core task to align sequential and topological representations. On top of this foundation, we employ a progressive learning scheme with a standardized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to steer models from structure acquisition toward higher-level molecular reasoning. Experiments show that MolBasic substantially improves structural understanding and yields robust gains on downstream tasks, including property prediction and objective optimization, supporting our structure-first paradigm.

AI.tldr: MolBasic is a structure-first framework for molecular LLMs that improves structural comprehension through bidirectional SMILES-Graph translation and progressive learning with Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current molecular LLMs achieve strong performance on understanding/generation tasks but lack structural grounding. They conflict with the fundamental chemistry principle that "structure determines function" and perform poorly on basic structure recognition, failing to capture molecular graphs from canonical SMILES.
AI.method: MolBasic uses a structure-first framework with bidirectional SMILES-Graph conversion as the core task to align sequential (SMILES) and topological (graph) representations. It employs a progressive learning scheme with standardized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to guide models from structure acquisition to higher-level molecular reasoning.
AI.result: MolBasic substantially improves structural understanding and yields robust gains on downstream tasks including property prediction and objective optimization, supporting the structure-first paradigm.
AI.conclusion: The structure-first paradigm through MolBasic effectively bridges the gap between sequential SMILES representations and molecular graph structures, leading to better structural comprehension and improved performance on molecular reasoning tasks.

title: Can Conversational Temporal Dynamics Improve Depression Detection in Dyads? A Preliminary Investigation in Multi-Modality Perspectives
summary: Automatic depression detection from clinical interviews typically models the semantic content and acoustic characteristics of participant speech. However, the interactional timing between the clinician and participant remains comparatively under-modeled. We investigate conversational temporal dynamics, specifically dyadic turn-pair timing, as a primary modality fused with self-supervised encoders. Evaluated on the DAIC-WOZ dataset, we compare a compact 24-dimensional timing module against frozen WavLM-large and RoBERTa-large baseline detectors. This temporal module achieves the highest single-modality performance on the development set. Furthermore, a convex-weighted late fusion strategy improves overall performance to 0.804 and 0.669 macro-F1 on the development and test sets, respectively. The learned fusion effectively assigns zero weight to acoustics, demonstrating that conversational timing serves as a lightweight, interpretable complement for dyadic depression screening.

AI.tldr: Investigating conversational timing as a novel modality for automatic depression detection, showing that dyadic turn-pair timing combined with speech and text features outperforms existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Most depression detection systems focus on semantic content and acoustic characteristics of speech, but the interactional timing between clinician and participant remains under-explored. There's a need to investigate conversational temporal dynamics as a complementary modality for improved depression screening.
AI.method: Proposes modeling dyadic turn-pair timing as a primary modality, using a compact 24-dimensional timing module. Combines this with self-supervised encoders (frozen WavLM-large for acoustics and RoBERTa-large for text) and employs a convex-weighted late fusion strategy to integrate the three modalities.
AI.result: The temporal module achieves the highest single-modality performance on the development set. Late fusion improves performance to 0.804 and 0.669 macro-F1 on development and test sets respectively. The learned fusion assigns zero weight to acoustics, showing conversational timing serves as a lightweight, interpretable complement for depression screening.
AI.conclusion: Conversational timing provides a valuable, lightweight, and interpretable modality for dyadic depression screening. It effectively complements existing approaches and can potentially replace acoustic features in some contexts, offering new insights into the temporal dynamics of clinical interactions.

title: Do ECG Foundation Models Transfer to Rare Cardiac Diseases? Evidence from Brugada Syndrome Detection
summary: Background: Foundation models (FMs) trained on large-scale unlabeled physiological data have emerged as a promising paradigm for medical artificial intelligence. Their ability to capture clinically meaningful, transferable representations for rare diseases remains largely unproven. This study investigates whether FM pre-training provides genuine clinical generalization benefits beyond improved optimization for rare electrocardiographic (ECG) phenotypes. Methods: We systematically evaluated nine publicly available ECG FMs for Brugada syndrome detection on the BrSwiss cohort (294 patients, 87 cases) and the independent external HUCA cohort (363 patients, 76 cases), under three strategies (from-scratch training, linear probing, full fine-tuning) across several configurations, including a 3% data ablation and zero-shot cross-site transfers. Results: Pre-training was necessary for high-capacity architectures unable to converge from scratch (AUC gain up to 0.411, p < 0.05), but gave no significant gain for compact architectures already converged on labeled data alone. On full BrSwiss, the best fine-tuned FM (ECG-CPC, AUC = 0.962) only marginally exceeded the strongest supervised baseline (ECG-CPC from scratch, AUC = 0.932; p = 0.091). At matched training-set size, the data-efficiency advantage on BrSwiss-3% (AUC gain = 0.055, p < 0.01) did not replicate on HUCA. Under zero-shot cross-site transfer, FM-based pipelines did not generalize better than supervised baselines, all approaching chance-level performance. Conclusion: For Brugada syndrome detection, FM pre-training is mechanical rather than semantic, providing optimization stability rather than transferable clinical knowledge. These findings challenge the assumption that large-scale pre-training inherently encodes clinically meaningful representations, highlighting the central role of model architecture and data-domain alignment.

AI.tldr: Foundation models for ECG provide optimization stability for large architectures but don't offer meaningful clinical knowledge transfer for rare Brugada syndrome detection beyond what supervised baselines achieve.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether foundation model pre-training on large-scale physiological data provides genuine clinical generalization benefits beyond improved optimization for rare ECG phenotypes like Brugada syndrome.
AI.method: Systematically evaluated 9 publicly available ECG foundation models for Brugada detection using two cohorts (BrSwiss: 294 patients, HUCA: 363 patients) under three strategies: from-scratch training, linear probing, and full fine-tuning, including data ablation (3%) and zero-shot cross-site transfers.
AI.result: Pre-training helped high-capacity architectures converge (AUC gain up to 0.411) but gave no significant gain for compact architectures. Best fine-tuned FM (ECG-CPC, AUC=0.962) only marginally exceeded supervised baseline (AUC=0.932). Data-efficiency advantage on small datasets didn't replicate across sites. Zero-shot cross-site transfers performed at chance level.
AI.conclusion: FM pre-training is mechanical rather than semantic for Brugada detection, providing optimization stability rather than transferable clinical knowledge. Challenges assumption that large-scale pre-training inherently encodes clinically meaningful representations.

title: Bridging Interleaved Multi-Modal Reasoning as a Unified Decision Process
summary: Unified multi-modal models (UMMs) have shown promising interleaved text-image reasoning capabilities, yet effectively optimizing such multi-turn generation via reinforcement learning (RL) remains an open challenge. Existing approaches apply RL exclusively to text steps, relegating image generation to supervised surrogates, preventing policy gradients from propagating through the full interleaved trajectory across heterogeneous modalities. This leaves the potential of RL for UMMs largely untapped. In the paper, we introduce \textbf{BRAID} (\textbf{B}ridging inte\textbf{R}le\textbf{A}ved mult\textbf{I}-modal reasoning as a unified \textbf{D}ecision process), a simple framework that casts multi-turn text-image-text reasoning as a unified Markov decision process (MDP), enabling joint optimization of textual and visual generation via a single, principled RL objective. BRAID computes a shared trajectory-level advantage and propagates it coherently into both text tokens and image denoising paths, each optimized through its modality-native policy gradient mechanism. To further address long-horizon credit assignment, BRAID employs a vision-language model (VLM) judge that scores each intermediate image on its reasoning utility, supplying dense turn-level feedback to sharpen learning at critical visual branches. Experiments on spatial reasoning and visual perception benchmarks show that BRAID consistently outperforms various baselines, confirming that a unified MDP formulation with vision-thinking guidance is essential for effective multi-modal reasoning.

AI.tldr: BRAID is a novel RL framework that unifies multi-turn text-image-text reasoning as a single MDP, enabling joint optimization of both textual and visual generation with a shared trajectory-level advantage signal and vision-language feedback.
AI.motivation: Current unified multi-modal models (UMMs) have limitations in reinforcement learning optimization - existing approaches only apply RL to text steps while treating image generation with supervised learning. This prevents policy gradients from flowing through the complete interleaved trajectory across different modalities, leaving RL's potential for UMMs largely untapped.
AI.method: BRAID casts multi-turn text-image-text reasoning as a unified Markov decision process (MDP). It computes a shared trajectory-level advantage and propagates it coherently into both text tokens and image denoising paths, each optimized through modality-specific policy gradient mechanisms. The framework also employs a vision-language model (VLM) judge to score intermediate images on their reasoning utility, providing dense turn-level feedback to address long-horizon credit assignment.
AI.result: Experiments on spatial reasoning and visual perception benchmarks show that BRAID consistently outperforms various baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of the unified MDP formulation with vision-thinking guidance for multi-modal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: A unified MDP formulation with vision-thinking guidance is essential for effective multi-modal reasoning optimization via reinforcement learning, enabling joint optimization of textual and visual generation through a single principled RL objective.

title: Can Model Merging Improve Aggregation in DiLoCo?
summary: Model merging techniques, which aggregate independently finetuned models into one to combine their capabilities, have become a topic of significant interest in recent years, with a broad array of methods having been proposed to tackle this problem. Simultaneously, an emerging trend in distributed learning has been the use of methods such as local SGD and DiLoCo, which greatly reduce communication costs by periodically aggregating the independently trained local models. However, these communication-efficient methods have been shown to degrade in performance relative to the FLOP-matched data-parallel gold standard as the number of independent local models grows and as the number of local training steps before global communication is increased. In this work, we draw an explicit analogy between the pseudo-gradient aggregation step in local SGD/DiLoCo and task arithmetic-based model merging, establishing a straightforward way to utilize merging methods in the context of distributed optimization. We then evaluate multiple state-of-the-art model merging methods in this setting and identify one method in particular, Iso-C, as a promising approach for improving DiLoCo. We find that DiLoCo SGD with Iso-C aggregation outperforms not only simple pseudo-gradient averaging but even the momentum-based DiLoCo, despite lacking a momentum mechanism itself. Building on this finding, we propose IsoLoCo, which adapts Iso-C for distributed training by equipping it with Nesterov momentum. Our empirical evaluations on language model pre-training across varying numbers of local workers show that IsoLoCo significantly outperforms DiLoCo, with the gap between them widening as the number of workers increases. This advantage remains present across model sizes and inner step counts, confirming that merging-inspired aggregation is an effective strategy for low-communication distributed training.

AI.tldr: IsoLoCo improves distributed training by applying model merging techniques (specifically Iso-C) to aggregate local models instead of using traditional pseudo-gradient averaging, outperforming existing low-communication methods like DiLoCo.
AI.motivation: There's a growing need for communication-efficient distributed training methods (like local SGD and DiLoCo), but these methods degrade in performance as more local models are trained independently and communication intervals increase. Model merging techniques have been developed to combine independently finetuned models, but haven't been systematically applied to distributed optimization.
AI.method: The authors draw an explicit analogy between pseudo-gradient aggregation in distributed methods and model merging via task arithmetic. They evaluate multiple state-of-the-art model merging methods in distributed settings, identify Iso-C as the most promising, and propose IsoLoCo which adapts Iso-C with Nesterov momentum for distributed training.
AI.result: IsoLoCo significantly outperforms DiLoCo in language model pre-training, with the performance gap widening as the number of workers increases. The advantage persists across different model sizes and inner step counts. Notably, DiLoCo SGD with Iso-C aggregation even outperforms momentum-based DiLoCo without using momentum itself.
AI.conclusion: Model merging-inspired aggregation is an effective strategy for low-communication distributed training. The proposed IsoLoCo method demonstrates that advanced model merging techniques can substantially improve distributed optimization performance compared to traditional pseudo-gradient averaging methods.

title: Folding, Reasoning, and Scaling with Open-source Drug Discovery Engine
summary: Accurately modeling biomolecular interactions is a central bottleneck in biology and therapeutic discovery. Here, we introduce Open Drug Discovery Engine (OpenDDE), an open-source, all-atom biomolecular foundation model that uses co-folding as the entry point to a scalable AI-driven drug discovery engine. Rather than treating structure prediction as an isolated endpoint, OpenDDE is designed as a shared structural reasoning layer for modeling sequence-structure-function relationships across biomolecular complexes, enabling complex structure prediction today while providing a foundation for de novo design, affinity estimation, structure-conditioned optimization, and more. OpenDDE integrates advances in all-atom architecture, atomic latent reasoning, inference optimization, and large-scale data processing to achieve IsoDDE-level co-folding accuracy within a reproducible and openly accessible framework. We also identify two scaling-law directions for co-folding models, revealing practical routes for continued improvement through data, model, inference, and training scaling. By releasing training code, inference pipelines, checkpoints, and benchmarks, OpenDDE aims to democratize access to frontier biomolecular intelligence, accelerate global collaboration, and lay an open foundation for next-generation drug discovery systems that can move from predicting molecular structures toward designing, scoring, and optimizing therapeutic candidates for human health.

AI.tldr: OpenDDE is an open-source, all-atom biomolecular foundation model that uses co-folding as a structural reasoning layer for scalable AI-driven drug discovery, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy while democratizing access to biomolecular intelligence.
AI.motivation: Accurately modeling biomolecular interactions is a central bottleneck in biology and therapeutic discovery. There's a need for accessible, scalable AI systems that can move beyond isolated structure prediction toward comprehensive drug discovery workflows.
AI.method: OpenDDE integrates advances in all-atom architecture, atomic latent reasoning, inference optimization, and large-scale data processing. It uses co-folding as the entry point to create a shared structural reasoning layer for modeling sequence-structure-function relationships across biomolecular complexes.
AI.result: OpenDDE achieves IsoDDE-level co-folding accuracy within a reproducible and openly accessible framework. The authors identify two scaling-law directions for co-folding models, revealing practical routes for continued improvement through data, model, inference, and training scaling.
AI.conclusion: OpenDDE aims to democratize access to frontier biomolecular intelligence, accelerate global collaboration, and lay an open foundation for next-generation drug discovery systems that can move from predicting molecular structures toward designing, scoring, and optimizing therapeutic candidates for human health.

title: Out-of-distribution Neural Inference in Dynamical Ising Models
summary: Neural networks are increasingly used to infer hidden physical structure from dynamical observations, yet it remains unclear whether their out-of-distribution performance reflects transferable physical rule learning. We address this question in a controlled inverse problem: reconstructing interaction graphs of a kinetic Ising model from Glauber magnetization trajectories. Across convolutional, graph, Transformer, and hybrid architectures, we find that data-driven training produces distinct and reproducible statistical strategies under topology and temperature shifts. Edge-population diagnostics reveal that Transformer-based models tend to preserve the link density of the training ensemble, whereas convolutional models can collapse toward sparse- or no-link predictions that appear out-of-distribution stable by exploiting the majority no-link class. Thus, high in-distribution accuracy and apparent out-of-distribution robustness do not necessarily imply a learned dynamics-to-structure rule. Instead, neural reconstruction can be governed by architecture-dependent statistical priors. Our results identify a concrete failure mode of standard data-driven learning in physical inverse problems and motivate rule-guided principles for machine-learning-assisted scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: Neural networks show architecture-dependent statistical strategies rather than learning transferable physical rules when reconstructing interaction graphs from dynamical observations, revealing a failure mode in data-driven scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: To understand whether neural networks truly learn transferable physical rules or just exploit statistical patterns when inferring hidden physical structure from dynamical observations, specifically examining their out-of-distribution performance.
AI.method: Tested various neural architectures (convolutional, graph, Transformer, and hybrid) on the controlled inverse problem of reconstructing interaction graphs of a kinetic Ising model from Glauber magnetization trajectories. Analyzed performance under topology and temperature shifts using edge-population diagnostics.
AI.result: Different architectures developed distinct and reproducible statistical strategies. Transformers preserved link density of training ensemble, while convolutional models collapsed toward sparse- or no-link predictions. High in-distribution accuracy and apparent out-of-distribution robustness didn't indicate learned dynamics-to-structure rules.
AI.conclusion: Neural reconstruction can be governed by architecture-dependent statistical priors rather than learned physical rules, identifying a concrete failure mode of standard data-driven learning in physical inverse problems and motivating rule-guided principles for scientific ML.

title: Evaluating LLM Uncertainty in Long-Form Generation Using Deterministic Ground Truth
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). Code available at https://github.com/IdoAmit198/SALT
summary: As LLMs generate increasingly long outputs, effective uncertainty estimation must identify errors at fine-grained levels rather than discard entire responses. While such methods exist, evaluating uncertainty at any resolution (token to an entire generation) is challenging and highly sensitive to label imperfections, making zero-noise benchmarks essential; yet, long-form generation benchmarks tend to rely on fallible labels rather than deterministic ground truth. We introduce Single-answer Atomic Long-form Target (SALT), a benchmark of six procedurally generated tasks with single deterministic long textual ground truths, enabling unit-level evaluation of correctness, calibration, and ranking without external judges. Equipped with SALT, our analysis of 50+ LLMs reveals key insights: We identify which confidence functions dominate each uncertainty aspect and show that confidence ranking largely breaks at atomic resolution, even when clearer separability emerges at coarser line-level units. SALT further enables controlled atom-level interventions throughout generation, revealing two separable drivers of future errors: propagation from corrupted prefixes, dominated by global context correctness, and bounded degradation from increasing answer-context length.
