Finally, we demonstrate that reasoning, via Chain-of-Thought prompting or internalized through training, introduces a trade-off, improving accuracy while degrading confidence ranking. These findings directly impact risk-critical applications requiring reliable error identification and mitigation.

AI.tldr: SALT benchmark enables fine-grained uncertainty evaluation in LLMs using deterministic ground truths, revealing confidence ranking breaks at atomic levels and insights into error propagation vs. context length degradation.
AI.motivation: Current uncertainty estimation methods for LLMs struggle with fine-grained error identification and are highly sensitive to label imperfections in long-form generation benchmarks. Existing benchmarks rely on fallible labels rather than deterministic ground truth, making reliable evaluation at any resolution (token to entire generation) challenging.
AI.method: The authors introduce Single-answer Atomic Long-form Target (SALT), a benchmark of six procedurally generated tasks with single deterministic long textual ground truths. This enables unit-level evaluation of correctness, calibration, and ranking without external judges. They analyze 50+ LLMs and perform controlled atom-level interventions throughout generation.
AI.result: Analysis reveals: 1) Confidence ranking largely breaks at atomic resolution but clearer separability emerges at coarser line-level units. 2) Two separable drivers of future errors: propagation from corrupted prefixes (dominated by global context correctness) and bounded degradation from increasing answer-context length. 3) Reasoning (Chain-of-Thought or internalized through training) improves accuracy but degrades confidence ranking, creating a trade-off.
AI.conclusion: SALT provides essential zero-noise benchmarks for fine-grained uncertainty estimation in LLMs. The findings reveal fundamental limitations in current confidence measures at atomic levels and identify separable error mechanisms, with important implications for risk-critical applications requiring reliable error identification and mitigation. The benchmark enables controlled evaluation without external judges.

title: OmniFocus: Query-Guided Modality-Balanced Token Compression for Omni-Modal Large Language Models
summary: Omni modal large language models (OmniLLMs) have attracted wide attention for their ability to jointly process audio and video, but they generate large token sequences under audio-visual inputs, leading to substantial inference cost. Existing audio-visual token compression methods often rely on unimodal guidance, overlooking the temporal locality of query-relevant evidence in audio-visual inputs and implicitly assuming that the two modalities share a temporally aligned information density distribution. We propose \textbf{OmniFocus}, a training-free query-guided token compression method for OmniLLMs that performs independent importance estimation for video and audio, enabling a modality-symmetric compression design that preserves modality-specific salient evidence while maintaining audio-visual alignment, thereby mitigating the modality bias issue that can arise from unimodal-guided compression. Experiments on the Qwen2.5-Omni model family across four audio-visual benchmarks show that OmniFocus maintains strong compressed performance at low token retention ratios and outperforms existing baselines on several major benchmark scores at 25\% token retention. On DailyOmni with Qwen2.5-Omni-7B at 25\% token retention, OmniFocus maintains 59.40 accuracy while delivering up to 1.38$\times$ prefill speedup relative to the full-token baseline, highlighting a favorable practical accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

AI.tldr: OmniFocus is a training-free query-guided token compression method for multimodal LLMs that independently estimates importance for video and audio modalities, enabling efficient compression while maintaining performance at low token retention ratios.
AI.motivation: Omni-modal LLMs processing audio-visual inputs generate large token sequences leading to high inference costs. Existing compression methods rely on unimodal guidance and overlook temporal locality of query-relevant evidence, while assuming audio and video share temporally aligned information density distributions.
AI.method: Proposes OmniFocus, a training-free query-guided token compression method that performs independent importance estimation for video and audio, enabling modality-symmetric compression design. This preserves modality-specific salient evidence while maintaining audio-visual alignment, mitigating modality bias from unimodal-guided compression.
AI.result: Experiments on Qwen2.5-Omni model family across four audio-visual benchmarks show OmniFocus maintains strong performance at low token retention ratios. At 25% token retention, it outperforms baselines on several major benchmark scores. On DailyOmni with Qwen2.5-Omni-7B at 25% retention, maintains 59.40 accuracy with up to 1.38× prefill speedup relative to full-token baseline.
AI.conclusion: OmniFocus provides an effective training-free approach for compressing audio-visual tokens in multimodal LLMs, achieving favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off by addressing modality bias through independent importance estimation across modalities while preserving audio-visual alignment.

title: Harness-Aware Self-Evolving: Co-Evolving Model Weights, Harness, and Task Solutions
summary: Self-evolving frameworks usually optimize task solutions while treating the surrounding harness as fixed. We introduce Harness-Aware Self-Evolving (HASE), an agentic reinforcement-learning framework in which a single model can generate task solutions or edit selected harness components in a multi-turn action space. HASE enables a single Qwen3-8B model to match the text-classification performance of a GPT-OSS-120B model that uses Claude Code as the harness proposer. In alpha factor mining, HASE outperforms the reported GPT-OSS-120B baseline. HASE also repairs imperfect evaluation components and converges to state-of-the-art performance in circle-packing algorithm discovery. These results show that HASE improves the harness and the solution through one unified agentic process.

AI.tldr: HASE is a self-evolving RL framework where a single model can both generate task solutions and edit harness components, achieving SOTA performance across multiple domains.
AI.motivation: Current self-evolving frameworks typically optimize task solutions while treating the surrounding harness (infrastructure, evaluation components, etc.) as fixed, limiting overall system improvement.
AI.method: Harness-Aware Self-Evolving (HASE) uses an agentic reinforcement learning framework where a single model operates in a multi-turn action space - it can either generate task solutions or edit selected harness components, allowing simultaneous optimization of both.
AI.result: HASE enables a Qwen3-8B model to match GPT-OSS-120B's text classification performance; outperforms GPT-OSS-120B baseline in alpha factor mining; repairs imperfect evaluation components; and converges to SOTA performance in circle-packing algorithm discovery.
AI.conclusion: HASE demonstrates that jointly optimizing both task solutions and harness components through a unified agentic process leads to superior performance across diverse domains compared to traditional fixed-harness approaches.

title: SHiPPO: Recurrent Memory with Transported Polynomial Projections
summary: HiPPO gives recurrent states memory semantics as coefficients of online polynomial projections, but in fixed channel coordinates. Modern selective SSMs, by contrast, rely on token-dependent control and channel interaction. We introduce SHiPPO (Sylvester HiPPO), a transported projection-memory prior that lifts HiPPO coefficient memories into a moving channel frame. For any fixed or realized right-transport path, SHiPPO transports the approximation family and channel metric together; conditional on that path, the state is ordinary HiPPO in a tied moving frame and follows Sylvester coefficient dynamics, preserving the left online-memory operator while adding right-action transport. For selective-SSM execution, we derive a restricted group-local realization with controller-compatible right actions, exponential-adjusted updates, exact block-affine scan, and recurrent decoding. We also give a simultaneous-reducibility criterion identifying when right transports collapse to static mixing plus independent scalar or blockwise banks. Controlled diagnostics show that larger current-token write rank improves ordinary prediction error but cannot recover order-sensitive changes to already-written memory; transported-memory variants recover this signal, which disappears when the transport pathway is removed. A finite-field associative-recall diagnostic with interleaved bindings, operations, and queries provides complementary autoregressive evidence while leaving the preferred right-action realization open. Taken together, these results support SHiPPO as a mechanistically grounded transported-memory prior, with evidence focused on memory mechanisms rather than broad sequence-modeling dominance.

AI.tldr: SHiPPO extends HiPPO by adding channel-frame transport to coefficient memories, enabling Sylvester dynamics for selective SSMs with moving-frame memory while preserving online-memory operations.
AI.motivation: HiPPO's fixed channel coordinates limit memory semantics for modern selective SSMs that rely on token-dependent control and channel interaction. There's a need for a transported projection-memory prior that can lift coefficient memories into moving channel frames.
AI.method: SHiPPO transports both approximation family and channel metric along any right-transport path, creating a tied moving frame where state follows Sylvester coefficient dynamics. For selective-SSM execution, derived restricted group-local realization with controller-compatible right actions, exponential-adjusted updates, exact block-affine scan, and recurrent decoding.
AI.result: Larger current-token write rank improves prediction error but can't recover order-sensitive memory changes; transported-memory variants recover this signal. Finite-field associative-recall diagnostic provides complementary autoregressive evidence. Results support SHiPPO as mechanistically grounded transported-memory prior.
AI.conclusion: SHiPPO provides a principled extension to HiPPO with moving-frame memory mechanisms, offering evidence for transported-memory prior focused on memory mechanisms rather than broad sequence-modeling dominance, leaving preferred right-action realization open for future work.

title: Online Linear Programming for Multi-Objective Routing in LLM Serving
summary: We study the online routing problem in large language model serving, where requests arrive sequentially and must be dispatched to parallel decode workers under tight batch-size and KV-cache constraints. Unlike widely used routing heuristics that are not tied to explicit service-level objectives (SLOs) and offer limited control over latency-throughput trade-offs, we introduce a multi-objective optimization framework that formulates routing as an online linear programming with interpretable decision rewards. We apply an efficient bid-price control policy based on the online linear programming that admits requests when their SLO-weighted benefit exceeds their shadow prices. To meet millisecond decision requirements, we develop a warm-started, projected first-order updates that track the evolving dual shadow prices online with predictable runtime. We integrate our router into the Vidur simulator and demonstrate substantial improvements over standard baselines across multiple SLO regimes, including end-to-end latency, time-to-first-token, throughput, and tail performance. A big picture from our result: a science-based approach outperforms others based on heuristics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an online optimization-based routing framework for LLM serving that outperforms heuristic approaches by balancing latency-throughput trade-offs through shadow price-based request admission.
AI.motivation: Current routing heuristics in LLM serving lack explicit connection to service-level objectives (SLOs) and offer limited control over latency-throughput trade-offs. There's a need for a more systematic, science-based approach to routing that can optimize multiple objectives simultaneously.
AI.method: Formulates routing as online linear programming with interpretable decision rewards. Uses bid-price control policy based on shadow prices. Develops warm-started, projected first-order updates to track evolving dual shadow prices online with predictable runtime for millisecond decisions.
AI.result: Integrated into Vidur simulator and demonstrates substantial improvements over standard baselines across multiple SLO regimes including end-to-end latency, time-to-first-token, throughput, and tail performance.
AI.conclusion: A science-based optimization approach to LLM serving routing significantly outperforms heuristic-based methods, providing better control over latency-throughput trade-offs and SLO compliance.

title: LACE-SVD: Loss-Aware SVD with Cumulative Error Correction for LLM Compression
summary: The rapid growth in the parameter scale of large language models (LLMs) has created a strong demand for efficient compression techniques. As a hardware-agnostic and highly compatible approach, low-rank compression has been widely adopted to reduce both memory footprint and computational cost. However, existing SVD-based methods are still largely driven by local reconstruction objectives, overlooking two critical limitations: rank budgets are often allocated without explicitly considering layer-wise loss sensitivity, and local approximation errors can propagate and accumulate through the residual stream, leading to amplified global deviations from the original model. To address these issues, we propose LACE-SVD, a Loss-Aware SVD framework with Cumulative Error correction for LLM compression. LACE-SVD first estimates the calibration negative-log-likelihood increase induced by candidate layer-wise compression ratios and solves a budget-constrained allocation problem to assign rank budgets. It then refines the compressed model with closed-form local updates and introduces a propagation-aware correction for residual-stream output modules, reducing layer-output discrepancy as a proxy for cumulative error propagation. Experimental results demonstrate that at a high compression ratio (0.6), the WikiText-2 PPL of our method on LLaMA-7B (32.57) is significantly better than that of Dobi-SVD (46.18).

AI.tldr: LACE-SVD is a novel low-rank compression method for LLMs that addresses cumulative error propagation through loss-aware rank allocation and propagation-aware correction, achieving better performance than existing methods at high compression ratios.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth in LLM parameter sizes creates demand for efficient compression. Existing SVD-based methods have two critical limitations: 1) rank budgets are allocated without considering layer-wise loss sensitivity, and 2) local approximation errors propagate and accumulate through the residual stream, causing amplified global deviations from the original model.
AI.method: LACE-SVD first estimates calibration negative-log-likelihood increase for candidate compression ratios and solves a budget-constrained allocation problem to assign rank budgets per layer. It then refines the compressed model with closed-form local updates and introduces propagation-aware correction for residual-stream output modules to reduce layer-output discrepancy as a proxy for cumulative error propagation.
AI.result: At high compression ratio (0.6), LACE-SVD achieves significantly better WikiText-2 perplexity on LLaMA-7B (32.57 PPL) compared to Dobi-SVD (46.18 PPL), demonstrating superior compression performance.
AI.conclusion: LACE-SVD effectively addresses the limitations of existing SVD-based compression methods by considering both layer-wise loss sensitivity and cumulative error propagation, resulting in more efficient LLM compression with better performance preservation at high compression ratios.

title: Explainable AI for Screening Abuse-Related Trauma in Bangladeshi Children: A Training-Free Multimodal Framework Evaluated on Noise-Aware Synthetic Data
summary: Bangladesh has an estimated 1.17 mental-health professionals per 100,000 population and only six child psychiatrists nationwide. No Bengali-language, culturally adapted tool exists for early screening of abuse-related psychological trauma in children. We present ShishuRaksha AI, a decision-support (not diagnostic) framework that fuses four screening modalities: validated questionnaires (SDQ, CPSS), Bengali narrative text, House-Tree-Person (HTP) drawing features, and facial affect. The fusion is training-free and clinically weighted, uses cross-modal attention, and includes a single-modality override rule. Every risk score is explained through clinically weighted, perturbation-based additive attribution and rendered as a bilingual (Bangla/English) report with referral routing to national child-protection services (OCC, DSS, NMHH) under the Children Act 2013. No clinical dataset of abused children can be collected ethically at this stage, so we introduce a noise-aware synthetic benchmark (500 cases, 116 positive [23.2%], four deliberate noise layers, literature-grounded HTP priors) and evaluate tree-ensemble surrogates of the fusion design (facial channel excluded) under 5-fold stratified cross-validation. The fused model reaches an AUC of 0.874 [0.834-0.908], against 0.756 [0.705-0.803] for an SDQ-only baseline, with ablation, operating-point, subgroup, and calibration analyses. We state all limitations openly, including synthetic-only data, no held-out set, text-feature circularity, and an urban-rural subgroup gap. This work is a feasibility study and a design contribution toward ethically deployable child-protection screening in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: ShishuRaksha AI is a decision-support framework for early screening of abuse-related psychological trauma in Bangladeshi children, fusing four screening modalities (questionnaires, narrative text, drawings, facial affect) with clinical weighting and explanation features, evaluated on synthetic data due to ethical constraints.
AI.motivation: Bangladesh faces a severe shortage of mental health professionals (1.17 per 100,000) and child psychiatrists (only 6 nationwide), with no culturally adapted Bengali-language tools for early screening of abuse-related psychological trauma in children.
AI.method: Developed ShishuRaksha AI framework that fuses four modalities: validated questionnaires (SDQ, CPSS), Bengali narrative text, House-Tree-Person drawing features, and facial affect. Uses training-free clinical weighting with cross-modal attention and single-modality override rule. Created synthetic benchmark (500 cases with 23.2% positive, four noise layers) due to ethical constraints, with evaluation using tree-ensemble surrogates under 5-fold stratified cross-validation.
AI.result: Fused model achieved AUC of 0.874 [0.834-0.908] compared to SDQ-only baseline of 0.756 [0.705-0.803]. Provides bilingual (Bangla/English) reports with referral routing to national child-protection services under the Children Act 2013.
AI.conclusion: The work is a feasibility study demonstrating potential for ethically deployable child-protection screening in low-resource settings, with acknowledged limitations including synthetic-only data, no held-out validation, and urban-rural subgroup gaps.

title: Spectral Rewiring for Exploration, Purification, and Model Merging
summary: Reinforcement learning has become a standard post-training recipe for large language models, but dense full-parameter updates create two deployment-relevant bottlenecks: suppressed reasoning performance, often reflected by premature saturation of test-time scaling, and interference when consolidating multiple capabilities through multi-domain training or model merging. We show that the reasoning-effective component of these updates is largely concentrated in the base model's spectral space, motivating Subspace-Aligned Rewiring (SAR), a post-hoc editing method that retains this spectral core while removing orthogonal components. SAR therefore preserves reasoning gains and filters residual update directions that suppress performance or amplify cross-domain interference. Across several model families and scales, SAR extracts compact reasoning cores using as little as approximately 0.58% of total parameters: it preserves over 99% of post-training performance and improves high-k exploration in mathematical reasoning, and generalizes to agentic coding by improving six of seven open benchmarks on an in-house model. SAR also purifies mixed-domain training updates by releasing suppressed coding capability while maintaining math reasoning and instruction following. It further enables model merging across experts, yielding cross-domain generalization that surpasses previous merging baselines and even the best single-domain experts. Overall, SAR shows that extracting reasoning-effective updates from parameter geometry can serve as a training-free mechanism to improve reasoning and multi-domain performance.

AI.tldr: Subspace-Aligned Rewiring (SAR) extracts compact reasoning cores from reinforcement learning updates using only 0.58% of parameters, preserving reasoning gains while reducing interference and enabling better multi-domain performance.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for large language models creates deployment bottlenecks: suppressed reasoning performance with premature test-time scaling saturation, and interference when consolidating multiple capabilities through multi-domain training or model merging.
AI.method: Subspace-Aligned Rewiring (SAR), a post-hoc editing method that retains the spectral core of reinforcement learning updates while removing orthogonal components. It identifies reasoning-effective components concentrated in the base model's spectral space and preserves only this spectral core.
AI.result: SAR extracts compact reasoning cores using only ~0.58% of total parameters, preserves over 99% of post-training performance, improves high-k exploration in mathematical reasoning, improves six of seven open benchmarks on an in-house coding model, purifies mixed-domain training updates, and enables superior model merging across experts.
AI.conclusion: Extracting reasoning-effective updates from parameter geometry serves as a training-free mechanism to improve reasoning and multi-domain performance, showing that the reasoning-effective component of reinforcement learning updates is concentrated in the spectral space.

title: What is Left for Us? Second Scholarship Against the Degradation of Research by AI
summary: We argue that generative AI can degrade research by eroding the very practices through which scholarly judgement is formed and academic trust is built. As constitutive conditions for the production and validation of knowledge, these practices cannot be reduced to the final outputs of research, which is what AI so effectively simulate. Accordingly, when researchers delegate central tasks of inquiry to systems like Large Language Models, they may stop enacting these practices and, with them, lose access to the formation they provide. An individual research output generated by AI may even appear improved but the researcher behind it fails to develop. Against this risk, merely keeping humans in the loop as prompters or quality checkers of AI outputs is insufficient to preserve research as a site of intellectual formation. What is needed instead is a renewed commitment to research as a lived practice in which judgement is formed gradually, often through frictions, and participation in a scholarly community. We defend it because it rests on four sources and warrants of research that cannot be automated: tacit knowledge, personal commitment, socialisation, and deep reading. This practice enacts what we call second scholarship, by which we understand the reappropriation of scholarly craft, chosen out of a critical experience of what generative AI can and cannot do. What cannot and should not be delegated becomes what research communities must value and answer for. This is what is left for us.

AI.tldr: Generative AI risks degrading research by replacing the scholarly practices that form judgement and build academic trust, requiring a renewed commitment to "second scholarship" that preserves irreplaceable human elements of research.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by concerns that generative AI's ability to simulate research outputs may erode the fundamental scholarly practices through which researchers develop judgement, build trust, and grow intellectually - practices that are essential to the validation and production of knowledge.
AI.method: The authors use conceptual analysis and philosophical argumentation to identify four irreplaceable sources/warrants of research (tacit knowledge, personal commitment, socialisation, deep reading) that cannot be automated, and propose the concept of "second scholarship" as a practice-based alternative to AI-driven research.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that merely keeping humans "in the loop" as prompters or quality checkers of AI outputs is insufficient; research must be preserved as a lived practice where judgement forms gradually through friction and community participation. The concept of "second scholarship" emerges as a framework for reappropriating scholarly craft.
AI.conclusion: Researchers must commit to practices that cannot and should not be delegated to AI - specifically those involving tacit knowledge, personal commitment, socialization, and deep reading. Research communities must value and defend these human elements of scholarship, making "second scholarship" a necessary response to the limitations and risks of generative AI in academic work.

title: Robustness Meets Uncertainty: Evidential Adversarial Training for Robust Selective Classification
summary: Safety-critical applications require classifiers that are both robust and reliable. Adversarial training is a widely adopted defense for improving robustness in deep neural networks; however, its effect on the reliability of predictive uncertainty remains underexplored. We investigate this gap through the lens of selective classification, which has rarely been systematically analyzed alongside adversarial robustness. We introduce a unified benchmark for the robustness-uncertainty trade-off. It standardizes architectures, augmentations, threat models, and evaluation metrics across clean, adversarial, and common-corruption settings. Across a wide range of state-of-the-art adversarial training methods, we uncover a recurring failure mode: several approaches improve robust accuracy while degrading uncertainty ranking, leading to poorer selective behavior. To address this, we propose Evidential Adversarial Training (EV-AT), which models uncertainty through a Dirichlet distribution and combines (i) an evidence-based loss promoting clean accuracy and reliable uncertainty with (ii) a robust evidence-alignment loss matching clean and adversarial predictions in log Dirichlet-parameter space. Extensive experiments show that EV-AT shifts the Pareto frontier of robustness-uncertainty trade-offs beyond prior state-of-the-art adversarial training methods. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/NicolasSournac/Robustness_Meets_Uncertainty.EV-AT.

AI.tldr: EV-AT (Evidential Adversarial Training) improves both adversarial robustness and uncertainty reliability in deep neural networks by modeling uncertainty with Dirichlet distributions and using evidence-based loss functions, outperforming existing adversarial training methods.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical applications require classifiers that are both robust to adversarial attacks and reliable in their predictive uncertainty. While adversarial training improves robustness, its impact on uncertainty reliability remains underexplored, and selective classification (which uses uncertainty for abstention) has rarely been systematically analyzed alongside adversarial robustness.
AI.method: The authors introduce Evidential Adversarial Training (EV-AT) which: 1) Models uncertainty through a Dirichlet distribution; 2) Uses an evidence-based loss promoting clean accuracy and reliable uncertainty; 3) Incorporates a robust evidence-alignment loss that matches clean and adversarial predictions in log Dirichlet-parameter space. They also create a unified benchmark standardizing architectures, augmentations, threat models, and evaluation metrics.
AI.result: Across state-of-the-art adversarial training methods, a recurring failure mode is uncovered: many approaches improve robust accuracy while degrading uncertainty ranking, leading to poorer selective behavior. EV-AT successfully addresses this by shifting the Pareto frontier of robustness-uncertainty trade-offs beyond prior state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating better balance between robustness and reliable uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: EV-AT effectively bridges the gap between adversarial robustness and uncertainty reliability, offering a solution to the common trade-off problem where adversarial training methods typically sacrifice uncertainty quality for improved robustness. The unified benchmark provides a standardized evaluation framework for future research in this area.

title: PLACEMEM: Toward a Compute-Aware Memory Plane for Lifelong Agents
summary: Lifelong agents need more than larger context windows and better retrieval. They need memories that can persist, evolve, and be corrected without forcing the serving stack to recompute the same history on every turn or silently reuse stale runtime state. We present PLACEMEM as a systems position on lifelong-agent memory, instantiated by an executable control-plane prototype. The central claim is that agent memory should be represented as versioned capsules that unify semantics, provenance, validity, and reusable runtime state under one correction-aware identity. In the current prototype, capsules drive prompt-level text retrieval, KV-aware routing, and cascading invalidation over live streamed backends; prospective layer-frontier replay is intentionally framed as a deeper integration agenda rather than a claimed engine feature. We describe a vLLM-first prototype with persistent capsule state, concurrency-safe invalidation, an OpenAI-compatible routing sidecar, a typed metadata contract, and a benchmark harness that measures live first-token latency, reuse, and post-correction behavior. The result is both an executable artifact that demonstrates correction-aware control-plane behavior today and a concrete roadmap for replay-aware serving integration in future lifelong-agent systems.

AI.tldr: PLACEMEM introduces versioned memory capsules for lifelong agents that unify semantics, provenance, validity, and runtime state under correction-aware identities, enabling persistent and evolvable agent memory without recomputation.
AI.motivation: Current lifelong agents need more than just larger context windows and better retrieval. They require memories that can persist, evolve, and be corrected without forcing recomputation of history on every turn or silently reusing stale runtime state.
AI.method: Presents PLACEMEM as a systems position with an executable control-plane prototype using versioned capsules that unify semantics, provenance, validity, and reusable runtime state. Includes vLLM-first prototype with persistent capsule state, concurrency-safe invalidation, OpenAI-compatible routing sidecar, typed metadata contract, and benchmark harness.
AI.result: An executable artifact demonstrating correction-aware control-plane behavior today and a concrete roadmap for replay-aware serving integration. The prototype enables prompt-level text retrieval, KV-aware routing, and cascading invalidation over live streamed backends.
AI.conclusion: PLACEMEM provides both a working prototype showing correction-aware memory management for lifelong agents and a roadmap for deeper integration of replay-aware serving in future systems, addressing fundamental memory persistence and evolution challenges.

title: STELLA: Efficient Sensor-to-LLM Translation for On-Device Human Activity Recognition
summary: HAR is increasingly expected to run continuously on edge devices, yet recent LLM-based methods remain hard to deploy: raw sensor prompts are long, cloud inference adds latency and privacy risk, and fine-tuned LLM pipelines turn general-purpose models into task-specific classifiers. We present STELLA, an efficient sensor-to-LLM translation framework for on-device HAR that shifts the burden from LLM adaptation to sensor tokenization. A lightweight hierarchical tokenizer compresses an entire multi-channel inertial window into a fixed set of compact latent sensor tokens, which are projected into the embedding space of a frozen pretrained LLM and combined with a natural-language prompt for label scoring. This preserves activity-relevant temporal and cross-channel structure while keeping LLM-side computation predictable across sensor configurations. STELLA also supports on-device personalization, adapting only the lightweight tokenizer on small amounts of user-specific labelled data and augmenting inference with a local retrieval context, keeping the LLM, user data, and retrieval on device. Across seven public HAR datasets and eight benchmark settings, STELLA achieves new state-of-the-art performance, improving over prior methods by up to 11.83% F1; on-device personalization yields up to a further 21.91% F1 as user data accumulates after deployment. STELLA also outperforms representative time-series tokenizers under the same LLM pipeline and achieves real-time inference under practical mobile and edge budgets, showing that efficient sensor tokenization is a practical path toward accurate, private, and personalized LLM-based HAR on edge devices.

AI.tldr: STELLA is an efficient on-device HAR framework that compresses sensor data into compact tokens for frozen LLMs, achieving SOTA performance with privacy-preserving personalization.
AI.motivation: LLM-based HAR methods face deployment challenges: long sensor prompts, cloud latency/privacy risks, and task-specific fine-tuning that loses LLM generality. Need efficient on-device HAR with preserved LLM capabilities.
AI.method: Lightweight hierarchical tokenizer compresses multi-channel inertial windows into compact latent sensor tokens, projects them into frozen pretrained LLM embedding space with natural-language prompts for label scoring. Supports on-device personalization by adapting only the tokenizer with user data and local retrieval context.
AI.result: Achieves new SOTA across 7 HAR datasets and 8 benchmark settings, improving up to 11.83% F1; on-device personalization yields up to 21.91% further F1 improvement. Outperforms other time-series tokenizers and achieves real-time inference on mobile/edge devices.
AI.conclusion: Efficient sensor tokenization enables accurate, private, personalized LLM-based HAR on edge devices, shifting burden from LLM adaptation to sensor processing while maintaining LLM generality and performance.

title: Forethought: Verifiable Reasoning from Neurosymbolic Primitive Programming
summary: Current agentic workflows usually involve decomposing user requests into sequences of tool calls with correctly resolved parameters, the results of which are processed through reasoning traces in the language model's context window. The prevailing route to improve such reasoning is test-time scaling, which trains models to search over long chains of thought; but the resulting capability is entangled in model weights, is not verifiable step-by-step, and is costly at inference. We present Forethought, a neurosymbolic reasoning system that instead treats reasoning as an explicit, verifiable program, that builds from a library of symbolic and neural primitives which are composed through a domain-specific language. The result are reasoning programs, which are concrete representations of the model's work, and as such can be inspected and modified before deployment. Instantiated as a tool-calling execution kernel and evaluated across five benchmarks, Forethought improves base-model accuracy by about 30% relative and outperforms vanilla prompting, reinforcement learning scaffolds, and prompt-evolution methods, enabling small models to match or exceed frontier models capabilities. In a direct comparison, a non-reasoning model augmented with Forethought competes with a dedicated reasoning model while requiring roughly three orders of magnitude less post-training investment, and remains model-agnostic and auditable.

AI.tldr: Forethought is a neurosymbolic reasoning system that treats reasoning as explicit, verifiable programs composed from symbolic and neural primitives, enabling small models to match frontier models with much less training investment.
AI.motivation: Current agentic workflows rely on test-time scaling which entrenches reasoning capabilities in model weights, making them non-verifiable, costly at inference, and not auditable step-by-step. There's a need for explicit, verifiable reasoning systems that are model-agnostic and more efficient.
AI.method: Forethought treats reasoning as explicit programs built from a library of symbolic and neural primitives composed through a domain-specific language. It creates reasoning programs as concrete representations that can be inspected and modified. It's instantiated as a tool-calling execution kernel.
AI.result: Forethought improves base-model accuracy by about 30% relative across five benchmarks, outperforms vanilla prompting, reinforcement learning scaffolds, and prompt-evolution methods. Small models with Forethought match or exceed frontier models. A non-reasoning model with Forethought competes with dedicated reasoning models while requiring ~1000x less post-training investment.
AI.conclusion: Forethought demonstrates that neurosymbolic reasoning systems can provide explicit, verifiable reasoning capabilities that are model-agnostic, auditable, and dramatically more efficient than weight-entangled approaches, enabling smaller models to achieve state-of-the-art performance.

title: Stacked LoRA for Subject-Adaptive EEG Foundation Models in Motor Imagery Decoding
comment: 10 pages, 1 figure, 2 tables. Accepted at the 2026 IEEE World Congress on Computational Intelligence (WCCI 2026), Maastricht, Netherlands
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) decoding for brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) faces a major challenge: substantial inter-subject variability limits effective cross-subject generalization. Consequently, practical systems still rely largely on subject-specific models trained from scratch and requiring individual recalibration. EEG foundation models have recently emerged as a promising alternative; however, even large pretrained models cannot simply be used as fixed feature extractors and still require additional adaptation before they can be reliably applied to downstream tasks. In this work, we address this challenge through targeted adaptation strategies. Building on recent EEG foundation models such as REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA, we examine the impact of different low-rank adaptation strategies on motor imagery classification. We propose a framework that structurally decouples subject-invariant knowledge from subject-specific neural signatures: the low-rank update at each adapted layer is split into a Global adapter, trained jointly across all subjects, and Subject-Specific adapters, each absorbing individual variability. To assess the contribution of each path, we compare three adaptation strategies: (i) subject-specific LoRA (ii) global LoRA and (iii) stacked LoRA, combining both Global and Subject Specific adapters. Experiments on BCI Competition IV-2a, PhysioNet Motor Imagery, and the clinical Zuo2025 benchmark show that Stacked LoRA effectively mitigates inter-subject variability, achieving the best accuracy in the large majority of backbone and dataset combinations. Our analysis further reveals that the optimal balance between the global and subject-specific paths depends on the target population: a shared adapter is sufficient for large, diverse cohorts, whereas subject-specific adaptation is decisive under the high inter-session variability of clinical recordings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Stacked LoRA, a dual-adapter framework that decouples subject-invariant and subject-specific components for EEG foundation model adaptation, outperforming single-adapter approaches in cross-subject BCI classification.
AI.motivation: EEG decoding for BCIs suffers from substantial inter-subject variability that limits cross-subject generalization. Current systems require subject-specific training and recalibration, while existing EEG foundation models still need adaptation for downstream tasks despite pre-training.
AI.method: The authors propose a structural framework that splits low-rank adaptation at each layer into two components: a Global adapter trained jointly across all subjects, and Subject-Specific adapters for individual variability. They compare three strategies: subject-specific LoRA, global LoRA, and stacked LoRA combining both. Built on EEG foundation models like REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA for motor imagery classification.
AI.result: Stacked LoRA effectively mitigates inter-subject variability, achieving the best accuracy in most backbone and dataset combinations across BCI Competition IV-2a, PhysioNet Motor Imagery, and the clinical Zuo2025 benchmark. Analysis shows optimal balance depends on target population - shared adapter works for large diverse cohorts, while subject-specific adaptation is crucial for clinical recordings with high inter-session variability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Stacked LoRA framework successfully addresses EEG decoding's cross-subject generalization challenge by structurally decoupling subject-invariant knowledge from subject-specific neural signatures, providing an effective adaptation strategy for EEG foundation models in BCI applications with varying population characteristics.

title: Language models guide symbolic equation discovery by controlling search
summary: Scientific equation discovery must combine broad domain priors with strict numerical testing. Symbolic regression supplies numerical grounding but faces a combinatorial search space, whereas many language-model systems ask the model to propose or select formulas directly. We test a different division of labour. We compare role specifications in which the language model acts as equation author, candidate decider or search controller, alongside end-to-end language-model and purely numerical baselines. In the controller setting we propose here, implemented as LLM-PySR, language models specify variables, operators, transformations and search depth; symbolic regression enumerates and fits expressions; and deterministic metrics govern retention. Across 74 AI-Feynman equations and seven complex formula-recovery tasks, search control achieved the strongest observed balance of accuracy, complexity, stability and cost. On an independent battery dataset, LLM-PySR identified a compact piecewise-linear relation between early voltage-curve displacement and cycle life. The results suggest that language models should shape hypothesis exploration rather than decide which equations survive.

AI.tldr: LLM-PySR: Using language models as search controllers rather than equation generators for symbolic regression, achieving better accuracy, complexity, stability, and cost balance across scientific equation discovery tasks.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to scientific equation discovery face limitations: symbolic regression has combinatorial search space problems, while language models that directly propose formulas lack numerical grounding. There's a need for a better division of labor between symbolic methods and language models.
AI.method: The paper proposes LLM-PySR, where language models act as search controllers specifying variables, operators, transformations, and search depth for symbolic regression. The system uses deterministic metrics for retention, with symbolic regression handling enumeration and fitting of expressions.
AI.result: The method achieved the strongest balance of accuracy, complexity, stability, and cost across 74 AI-Feynman equations and seven complex formula-recovery tasks. On a battery dataset, it identified a compact piecewise-linear relation between early voltage-curve displacement and cycle life.
AI.conclusion: Language models should be used to shape hypothesis exploration rather than deciding which equations survive. The search controller approach provides an effective division of labor between symbolic methods (numerical grounding) and language models (domain knowledge and search guidance).

title: Heterogeneous Graph Condensation via Role-Aware Clustering
summary: Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks (HGNNs) have exhibited remarkable efficacy in modeling complex systems with multiple types of nodes and relations, yet their training on large-scale heterogeneous graphs remains computationally prohibitive. Although graph condensation methods can effectively improve learning efficiency on large-scale graphs, existing condensation processes are mainly designed for homogeneous graphs and typically rely on computationally expensive gradient matching or bilevel optimization paradigms, rendering them impractical for heterogeneous settings. To address these limitations, we propose HGC-RC, a simple yet effective role-aware heterogeneous graph condensation framework. Specifically, HGC-RC first extracts semantically enhanced node embeddings via lightweight propagation. It then introduces a role-aware hybrid clustering strategy consisting of class-partitioned clustering for labeled target nodes to preserve class distributions and unsupervised type-wise clustering for non-target nodes to retain critical cross-type connectivity. Finally, a compact heterogeneous graph is efficiently reconstructed based on the resulting cluster assignments. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HGC-RC outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering a practical pathway to accelerate HGNN training on large-scale heterogeneous graphs without sacrificing task performance

AI.tldr: A role-aware heterogeneous graph condensation framework (HGC-RC) that efficiently compresses large-scale heterogeneous graphs while preserving structural and semantic information to accelerate HGNN training.
AI.motivation: HGNNs are effective for modeling complex heterogeneous systems but training on large-scale heterogeneous graphs is computationally prohibitive. Existing graph condensation methods are designed for homogeneous graphs and rely on expensive gradient matching or bilevel optimization, making them impractical for heterogeneous settings.
AI.method: HGC-RC uses lightweight propagation for semantically enhanced node embeddings, then employs role-aware hybrid clustering: class-partitioned clustering for labeled target nodes to preserve class distributions, and unsupervised type-wise clustering for non-target nodes to retain cross-type connectivity. Finally reconstructs compact heterogeneous graph from cluster assignments.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show HGC-RC outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering practical acceleration of HGNN training on large-scale heterogeneous graphs without sacrificing task performance.
AI.conclusion: HGC-RC provides an effective and efficient framework for heterogeneous graph condensation that addresses computational limitations while maintaining performance, enabling practical application of HGNNs to large-scale heterogeneous graphs.

title: A Clustering-Based Framework for Identifying Suspicious Trading Patterns in Capital Market
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence & Networking (QPAIN), 2026. This is the authors preprint version. The final version is available via IEEE Xplore
summary: Market manipulation is the dubious practice of manipulating stock prices in order to make a quick profit, which truly degrades confidence on trading platforms. We implemented an unsupervised fraud-detection toolkit that begins with K-Means++ clustering to address this issue. A dataset of roughly one million financial transactions from 2012 to 2024 is used. In order to identify fraudulent trades and categorize them using market practice heuristic thresholds, the study suggests a clustering-based pipeline. The method highlights 2.02% of trades as suspicious where 51.10% clearly indicate spoofing, 0.10% indicate pump and dump, 0.55% indicate insider trading, 1.43% indicate a fake breakout, and 46.83% are unclassified. Despite the lack of ground truth, the model's performance is confirmed by a Silhouette Score of 0.561.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed an unsupervised fraud detection system using K-Means++ clustering on 1M financial transactions (2012-2024) to identify market manipulation patterns like spoofing and pump-and-dump schemes.
AI.motivation: Market manipulation degrades confidence in trading platforms, creating a need for effective detection methods to identify dubious practices like spoofing, pump-and-dump, and insider trading.
AI.method: Used K-Means++ clustering on approximately 1 million financial transactions from 2012-2024, implementing a clustering-based pipeline that applies market practice heuristic thresholds to categorize fraudulent trades.
AI.result: The system identified 2.02% of trades as suspicious, with breakdowns: 51.10% spoofing, 0.10% pump and dump, 0.55% insider trading, 1.43% fake breakout, and 46.83% unclassified. Achieved Silhouette Score of 0.561 despite lack of ground truth.
AI.conclusion: The unsupervised clustering approach effectively detects various market manipulation patterns in financial transactions, providing a practical toolkit for fraud detection without requiring labeled data, though challenges remain with unclassified suspicious trades.

title: Observable- and Positional-Encoding-Dependent Symmetry Readout from Neural Network Weights
summary: Post-hoc analysis of trained neural network weights often seeks to recover geometric structure directly from the parameters. We show that, for positional-encoding-equipped neural fields, the symmetry visible from weights is not the true symmetry group itself, but an observable symmetry set determined by the trained parameters, the positional encoding (PE), and readout observable. We formulate this dependence through an exact observability hierarchy, $G_{\mathrm{obs}}^{\mathrm{exact}} \subseteq G_{\mathrm{lift}}^{\mathrm{exact}}(φ) \cap G_{\mathrm{true}}$, where $G_{\mathrm{lift}}^{\mathrm{exact}}(φ)$ is the set of input transformations that the PE can exactly lift to the feature space. The hierarchy implies that even when a target function has a geometric symmetry, that symmetry may be structurally invisible to weight-level observables if the PE does not represent the corresponding transformation. We test this prediction using MLPs trained on two-dimensional signed distance functions with multiple shape symmetry groups, positional encodings, and Gram-based observables. The results show a consistent PE-dependent pattern: DyadicAxisPE supports $D_4$-sensitive readout but structurally suppresses $D_3$ rotations, TriAxisPE yields lower $D_3$ / $D_6$ readout scores under the tested Gram observables by replacing coordinate axes with three 120-degree-separated axes, and random Fourier features mainly exhibit a $π$-rotation response under these readouts. These findings show that PE design affects not only approximation behavior but also which structures are accessible to post-hoc weight-level readouts. This provides a basis for a principled observable-dependent symmetry readout.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that symmetry detection from neural network weights depends not just on the target function's true symmetries, but also on the positional encoding design and readout observables, creating a structural constraint on what symmetries can be observed post-hoc.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand why post-hoc weight analysis sometimes fails to detect geometric symmetries present in target functions, and to establish a principled framework for symmetry readout from neural network parameters that accounts for architectural constraints.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework with an exact observability hierarchy (G_obs^exact ⊆ G_lift^exact(φ) ∩ G_true), relating observable symmetries to positional encoding capabilities. They test this with MLPs trained on 2D signed distance functions using various symmetry groups (D3, D4, D6), different positional encodings (DyadicAxisPE, TriAxisPE, random Fourier features), and Gram-based observables.
AI.result: Results show PE-dependent patterns: DyadicAxisPE supports D4 symmetry detection but suppresses D3, TriAxisPE reduces D3/D6 readout scores by using 120-degree separated axes, and random Fourier features mainly show π-rotation response. The positional encoding design determines which symmetries are structurally accessible to weight-level readouts.
AI.conclusion: Positional encoding design fundamentally affects which geometric symmetries can be observed from neural network weights, providing a theoretical basis for principled, observable-dependent symmetry readout methods that account for architectural constraints.

title: Integrating Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Safe Reinforcement Learning in a 1-DoF Helicopter System
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) offers powerful control for industrial cyber-physical systems (ICPSs), but its "black-box" exploration risks violating strict hardware safety limits. Typically, these constraints are managed through complex reward shaping. In this work-in-progress paper, we embed a differentiable physics model directly into the proximal policy optimization (PPO) actor loss function. By simulating short-horizon future trajectories during training, the policy is penalized for anticipated safety violations independent of the task-reward signal. Evaluated on a simulated 1-degree-of-freedom helicopter testbed with strict pitch constraints, our physics-informed soft regularizations substantially reduce constraint violations while maintaining reliable target tracking.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed PPO with embedded differentiable physics model reduces safety violations in industrial cyber-physical systems while maintaining task performance.
AI.motivation: Deep reinforcement learning in industrial cyber-physical systems risks violating strict hardware safety limits due to its "black-box" exploration. Traditional constraint management through complex reward shaping is insufficient for ensuring safety during exploration.
AI.method: Embed a differentiable physics model directly into the proximal policy optimization (PPO) actor loss function. The method simulates short-horizon future trajectories during training to penalize policies for anticipated safety violations, independent of the task-reward signal.
AI.result: Evaluated on a simulated 1-degree-of-freedom helicopter testbed with strict pitch constraints, the physics-informed soft regularizations substantially reduce constraint violations while maintaining reliable target tracking performance.
AI.conclusion: Integrating differentiable physics models into RL training offers a promising approach for ensuring safety in industrial cyber-physical systems by proactively penalizing policies for anticipated violations, reducing the need for complex reward shaping while maintaining task performance.

title: Unsupervised Features Mining via Activation Geometry
summary: Interpretability methods aim to reveal the features represented inside large language models (LLMs). Many existing methods begin with labeled examples of a human-defined concept that may reflect human biases, and then identify how that concept is represented within the model, for example in its activation space or through other decomposition methods. We introduce \emph{Mining via Activation Geometry} (MAG), a simple unsupervised framework for extracting reasoning features from model activations by prepending the same natural-language instruction $Q$ to every input $p$, where $Q$ defines the reasoning feature of interest, such as ``Can this object be found in the desert?'' or ``Is this prompt malicious?'' We measure how the instruction changes the model's internal representation using $m(Q \mid p) - m(p)$ at a single readout point. We explore eight different MAGs. The extracted reasoning features predict the models' own world understanding and judgment, can be approximated into a single activation direction, we found that some features are more linearly represented and some less, this linear representation, which is vector steering, can change the LLMs' decisions through activation steering by injecting reasoning features. Finally, we use the same method to select the best training datasets for prompt-injection classifier probes: while similarity between ordinary activations is almost unrelated to downstream performance, RFD-based similarity achieves $94.7\%$ Top-1 and $100\%$ Top-2 accuracy.

AI.tldr: MAG (Mining via Activation Geometry) is an unsupervised framework that extracts reasoning features from LLM activations by comparing activations with and without natural-language instructions, enabling analysis and steering of model reasoning processes.
AI.motivation: Existing interpretability methods often rely on labeled examples of human-defined concepts, which may reflect human biases. There's a need for unsupervised approaches that can extract model-internal reasoning features without predefined human concepts.
AI.method: MAG prepends a natural-language instruction Q to every input p, then measures how the instruction changes the model's internal representation using m(Q|p) - m(p) at a single readout point. Eight different MAG variants were explored, extracting reasoning features that can be approximated into single activation directions.
AI.result: Extracted reasoning features predict models' own world understanding and judgment. Some features are more linearly represented than others, and this linear representation enables vector steering to change LLMs' decisions. RFD-based similarity achieved 94.7% Top-1 and 100% Top-2 accuracy for selecting training datasets for prompt-injection classifier probes.
AI.conclusion: MAG provides an unsupervised framework for extracting reasoning features from LLM activations, enabling interpretability without human-biased labels. The method reveals how reasoning features are represented geometrically in activation space and enables practical applications like steering model decisions and selecting optimal training datasets.

title: ACPO: Adaptive Credit Policy Optimization via Fine-Grained Surrogate Entropy
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has substantially improved the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs), but sparse outcome rewards still make token-level credit assignment difficult. Existing scalable RL methods typically assign trajectory-level rewards uniformly across tokens, while recent entropy-aware approaches either rely on coarse detached heuristics or directly optimize true entropy, which can introduce non-local gradient components misaligned with sampled-token policy updates. We propose Adaptive Credit Policy Optimization (ACPO), a token-level credit assignment framework based on a mode-local surrogate entropy. ACPO asymmetrically modulates policy updates by emphasizing uncertain decisions in successful rollouts and overconfident tokens in failed rollouts. We show that the surrogate admits deterministic entropy bounds and, under modal alignment and proximal updates, preserves the policy-gradient direction to leading order. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and coding benchmarks, including AIME 2025 and HumanEvalPro, show that ACPO consistently improves over strong RL baselines such as DAPO, GTPO, and SAPO.

AI.tldr: ACPO is a new reinforcement learning framework that improves token-level credit assignment in LLMs using a mode-local surrogate entropy approach, achieving better performance on reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current RL methods for LLMs struggle with sparse outcome rewards, making token-level credit assignment difficult. Existing approaches either assign rewards uniformly across tokens or use coarse heuristics, while true entropy optimization can introduce non-local gradients that misalign with policy updates.
AI.method: ACPO uses a token-level credit assignment framework based on mode-local surrogate entropy. It asymmetrically modulates policy updates by emphasizing uncertain decisions in successful rollouts and overconfident tokens in failed rollouts. The approach maintains deterministic entropy bounds and preserves policy-gradient direction under modal alignment and proximal updates.
AI.result: Experiments on mathematical reasoning (AIME 2025) and coding (HumanEvalPro) benchmarks show that ACPO consistently outperforms strong RL baselines including DAPO, GTPO, and SAPO.
AI.conclusion: ACPO provides an effective solution to token-level credit assignment in RL for LLMs, addressing limitations of existing approaches through mode-local surrogate entropy and asymmetric policy modulation, leading to improved performance on reasoning tasks.

title: Biological Motifs for Agentic Control
summary:
The transition of Large Language Models (LLMs) from passive generators to autonomous agents has introduced significant challenges in reliability, security, and state management. Current agentic architectures are often constructed ad-hoc, prone to hallucination cascades, infinite loops, and prompt injection attacks. This paper argues that many of these failure modes can be analyzed using control motifs long studied in systems biology, provided the comparison is made at the level of typed interfaces and coordination structure rather than literal biological mechanism.
  We develop a typed interface correspondence between Gene Regulatory Networks and agentic software systems using polynomial functors and wiring diagrams. Five biological motifs are mapped to composable software design patterns: Coherent Feed-Forward Loops for noise suppression, Adaptive Immunity for layered security, Mitochondrial Signaling for resource governance, Endosymbiosis for neuro-symbolic integration, and Morphogen Diffusion for spatially varying coordination. An epistemic topology layer derives Kripke-style knowledge operators from the wiring diagram's observation structure and proves four predictive theorems for multi-agent scaling.
  The core contributions are: (1) the Agentic Operad, a typed syntax for agent composition with provable error suppression bounds for feed-forward topologies; (2) an epistemic topology with four theorems (error amplification, sequential penalty, parallel acceleration, and tool density scaling) whose qualitative predictions are consistent with published multi-agent benchmarks; and (3) a six-layer progression from structure through development, grounded in autonomous learning frameworks and convergence proxies from the empirical literature. A reference implementation with 1,813 tests and 116 examples illustrates practical feasibility.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel framework called the Agentic Operad that applies biological control motifs to improve LLM agent reliability, using typed interfaces and polynomial functors to map gene regulatory network patterns to composable agent design patterns with provable error bounds.
AI.motivation: The transition of LLMs from passive generators to autonomous agents has created challenges in reliability, security, and state management. Current agentic architectures suffer from hallucination cascades, infinite loops, and prompt injection attacks, suggesting a need for more systematic design principles.
AI.method: Developed a typed interface correspondence between Gene Regulatory Networks and agentic software systems using polynomial functors and wiring diagrams. Mapped five biological motifs to composable software design patterns. Created an epistemic topology layer with Kripke-style knowledge operators and four predictive theorems. Built the Agentic Operad as a typed syntax for agent composition.
AI.result: Created a six-layer framework with provable error suppression bounds for feed-forward topologies. Derived four theorems (error amplification, sequential penalty, parallel acceleration, and tool density scaling) whose predictions match published multi-agent benchmarks. Provided a reference implementation with 1,813 tests and 116 examples demonstrating practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Biological control motifs provide valuable design patterns for improving LLM agent reliability and security. The Agentic Operad framework offers a systematic, composable approach to agent design with mathematical guarantees, addressing current ad-hoc architectures' failure modes through principles inspired by systems biology.

title: Anticipatory Reinforcement Learning for Trajectory Tracking
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) in industrial control often suffers from lag and overshoot due to purely reactive control based on the current tracking error. To achieve anticipatory control without high computational overhead, we introduce a predictive formulation that augments the DRL state space with target velocities and future reference horizons. Evaluating eight configurations using proximal policy optimization (PPO) on a 1-degree-of-freedom (1-DoF) helicopter testbed, simulation results showed a 9-fold error reduction, lowering the mean absolute deviation from 2.73° to 0.31°. However, zero-shot transfer to physical hardware revealed a sim-to-real gap. Interestingly, a simpler configuration using a single, further look-ahead horizon matched the real-world top performance of the most complex model (1.11°). Overall, evaluating various combinations of prediction horizons and target velocities demonstrated that highly granular predictive data is not necessarily required for physical transfer.

AI.tldr: Predictive state augmentation in deep reinforcement learning reduces tracking error in industrial control by adding target velocities and future reference horizons, achieving 9x error reduction in simulation, though simpler configurations perform best in physical transfer.
AI.motivation: Deep reinforcement learning in industrial control suffers from lag and overshoot due to purely reactive control based on current tracking error. There's a need for anticipatory control without high computational overhead.
AI.method: Introduced a predictive formulation that augments DRL state space with target velocities and future reference horizons. Evaluated eight configurations using proximal policy optimization (PPO) on a 1-degree-of-freedom helicopter testbed, both in simulation and zero-shot transfer to physical hardware.
AI.result: Simulation showed 9-fold error reduction (mean absolute deviation from 2.73° to 0.31%). Real-world transfer revealed sim-to-real gap, but simpler configuration using single further look-ahead horizon matched top performance of most complex model (1.11° error).
AI.conclusion: Highly granular predictive data is not necessarily required for physical transfer. A simpler predictive approach with single look-ahead horizon can achieve competitive real-world performance while being more practical for deployment.

title: Progress- and Reliability-Oriented Group Policy Optimization for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Group-based reinforcement learning (RL) has become an effective paradigm for improving large language model agents on long-horizon interactive tasks. To obtain finer-grained policy updates than trajectory-level optimization, recent work has moved toward step-level group-based RL, where intermediate steps are grouped and compared within a rollout batch. However, step-level advantage estimation is sensitive to how groups are formed: grouping by broad state keys improves coverage but may compare actions taken under different histories, while enforcing historical consistency yields fairer comparisons at the cost of fragmented groups and missing peer-comparison signal. In this paper, we propose ProGPO (Progress- and Reliability-Oriented Group Policy Optimization), a learned-critic-free method for context-consistent step-level learning. ProGPO keeps exact-prefix action comparison, and complements sparse peer comparisons with transition credit derived from rollout-based state potentials. To estimate these potentials reliably, ProGPO combines semantic expansion with inverse-variance fusion across history depths. We evaluate ProGPO on two challenging agentic tasks, ALFWorld and WebShop, with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct. Results show that ProGPO improves over matched agentic RL baselines under comparable computational overhead, and additional Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct experiments further test the scalability of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: ProGPO is a learned-critic-free group-based RL method for LLM agents that maintains exact-prefix action comparison and supplements sparse peer comparisons with transition credits from rollout-based state potentials, improving policy updates for long-horizon interactive tasks.
AI.motivation: Current step-level group-based RL methods face a trade-off: grouping by broad state keys improves coverage but compares actions under different histories, while enforcing historical consistency yields fairer comparisons but creates fragmented groups with missing peer-comparison signals.
AI.method: ProGPO keeps exact-prefix action comparison for context consistency, complements sparse peer comparisons with transition credit from rollout-based state potentials, and estimates these potentials reliably through semantic expansion with inverse-variance fusion across history depths.
AI.result: ProGPO outperforms matched agentic RL baselines on challenging tasks (ALFWorld and WebShop) with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct under comparable computational overhead, and scalability is further tested with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct experiments.
AI.conclusion: ProGPO successfully addresses the grouping trade-off in step-level RL by combining exact-prefix action comparison with transition credits from reliably estimated state potentials, offering an effective learned-critic-free approach for improving LLM agents on long-horizon tasks.

title: Sample-Efficient Pareto Front Modeling for Energy-Aware Reinforcement Learning Using Bayesian Optimization
summary: Industrial automation increasingly demands control strategies that balance operational performance with strict energy efficiency requirements. A common approach to solving this multi-objective problem, particularly within the framework of reinforcement learning (RL), is to formulate a single, scalar reward function that linearly combines the competing objectives. However, the manual weighting of these different objectives is heavily reliant on domain intuition, incredibly time-consuming, prone to human bias, and frequently fails to uncover optimal trade-off solutions. This work addresses the critical challenge of automating the weight selection process to systematically and efficiently discover the Pareto front of optimal trade-off policies. We formulate the weight selection process as a multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) problem and evaluate its sample efficiency against a standard uniform grid search baseline. Using a physical Quanser Aero 2 testbed configured for 1-DoF pitch control, our results demonstrate that the MOBO approach, utilizing the expected hypervolume improvement (qEHVI) acquisition function, consistently outperforms uniform grid sampling. MOBO achieves superior hypervolume and maximum spread, successfully identifying high-quality, diverse trade-off policies with a reduced evaluation budget, thereby enabling highly efficient energy-aware control in complex mechatronic systems.

AI.tldr: MOBO automates RL weight selection for multi-objective control, outperforming manual grid search with better efficiency and diverse Pareto-optimal policies.
AI.motivation: Manual weighting of competing objectives in RL-based control systems is time-consuming, biased, and often fails to find optimal trade-offs between performance and energy efficiency in industrial automation.
AI.method: Formulates weight selection as a multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) problem using expected hypervolume improvement (qEHVI) acquisition function, evaluated against uniform grid search on a Quanser Aero 2 testbed for 1-DoF pitch control.
AI.result: MOBO consistently outperforms uniform grid sampling, achieving superior hypervolume and maximum spread, identifying high-quality diverse trade-off policies with reduced evaluation budget.
AI.conclusion: Automated weight selection via MOBO enables efficient discovery of Pareto-optimal control policies, making energy-aware control in complex mechatronic systems more practical and effective.

title: Shortcut Learning in Legal Judgment Prediction: Empirical Evidence from the UK Employment Tribunal
summary: Current Legal Judgment Prediction (LJP) is constrained by its reliance on post-hoc judicial materials, increasing the likelihood that models perform retrospective classification rather than true forecasting. This paper empirically investigates shortcut learning in this context by studying claim-level outcome prediction in UK Employment Tribunal (UKET) decisions. Using a corpus of 33,158 individual claims, we predict outcomes from claim texts and LLM-extracted case summaries, evaluating models ranging from interpretable TF-IDF-based classifiers to black-box LLMs. While headline predictive performance figures appear strong, we demonstrate that such performance in LJP systems trained on post-hoc judicial text can be driven by the retrospective nature of the source material. Stratifying the test data by human judgments of leakage reveals that performance increases where outcome-revealing cues are embedded in the narrative. Moreover, a model trained on just the 4% of features identified as leakage achieves high performance, outperforming human experts. These findings substantiate concerns that LJP performance may be exaggerated by linguistic artefacts. Yet this vulnerability is not fatal to the research agenda. Instead, post-hoc judgments might be treated as potentially contaminated texts, requiring active auditing. Retraining models after masking leakage features results in only a negligible reduction in Macro-F1. Hence, while models will opportunistically exploit shortcuts when available, they remain capable of extracting useful predictive signals when these artefacts are removed.

AI.tldr: Legal Judgment Prediction models trained on post-hoc judicial materials often exploit linguistic shortcuts rather than performing true forecasting, but models remain capable of useful prediction when leakage features are removed.
AI.motivation: Current Legal Judgment Prediction (LJP) systems are constrained by reliance on post-hoc judicial materials, increasing the risk of retrospective classification rather than true forecasting. The paper aims to empirically investigate shortcut learning in LJP using UK Employment Tribunal decisions.
AI.method: Used a corpus of 33,158 individual claims from UK Employment Tribunal decisions. Predicted outcomes from claim texts and LLM-extracted case summaries. Evaluated models ranging from TF-IDF classifiers to black-box LLMs. Stratified test data by human judgments of leakage to analyze performance variation. Also trained models on just leakage features and retrained models after masking leakage features.
AI.result: Headline predictive performance appears strong but is driven by retrospective nature of source material. Performance increases where outcome-revealing cues are embedded in narrative. A model trained on just 4% of features identified as leakage achieves high performance, outperforming human experts. Retraining models after masking leakage features results in only negligible reduction in Macro-F1 (0.008).
AI.conclusion: LJP performance may be exaggerated by linguistic artefacts, but this vulnerability is not fatal to the research agenda. Post-hoc judgments should be treated as potentially contaminated texts requiring active auditing. Models opportunistically exploit shortcuts when available but remain capable of extracting useful predictive signals when these artefacts are removed.

title: CuBAS: Information Geometric Curvature-Based Adaptive Sampling for Supervised Classification
summary: The informativeness of a training set is as consequential as its size, yet most sampling strategies remain agnostic to the intrinsic geometry of the data distribution. We introduce CuBAS (Curvature-Based Adaptive Sampling), an information-geometric framework for adaptive data selection in supervised classification, grounded in the q-state Potts Markov random field (MRF) model. The central insight is that a labeled dataset can be viewed as a statistical manifold, on which local curvature, estimated via the ratio of second to first-order observed Fisher information, faithfully encodes the geometric complexity of the data distribution. We construct a k-nearest-neighbor graph over the labeled data and derive a closed-form curvature score at each vertex from the Potts sufficient statistics. This curvature signal partitions the graph into two complementary regimes: low-curvature regions, corresponding to smooth, homogeneous clusters, and high-curvature regions, concentrated around decision boundaries that are disproportionately informative for classification. By selecting nodes from both regimes, CuBAS constructs compact yet maximally informative training subsets. Empirical evaluation across more than 60 benchmark datasets demonstrates consistent and statistically significant improvements over random sampling and uncertainty-based baselines, across a wide range of labeling budgets and classifier architectures. CuBAS is computationally efficient (linear in the number of k-NN graph edges), theoretically grounded in the differential geometry of statistical manifolds, and interpretable in terms of the local shape operator of the data manifold.

AI.tldr: CuBAS uses curvature estimation from statistical manifolds to adaptively sample training data, focusing on high-curvature decision boundaries for more informative classification subsets.
AI.motivation: Current data sampling strategies ignore the intrinsic geometry of data distributions, despite the informativeness of training sets being as important as their size. There's a need for methods that leverage the geometric complexity of data for more effective sampling.
AI.method: CuBAS treats labeled datasets as statistical manifolds and estimates local curvature using the ratio of second to first-order observed Fisher information from a q-state Potts Markov random field model. It constructs a k-nearest-neighbor graph over labeled data, computes curvature scores at each vertex, and partitions the graph into low-curvature (smooth clusters) and high-curvature (decision boundary) regions for adaptive sampling.
