We prove that the competence signal tracks domain risk, that the participation program has a unique fixed point reached by a contraction, and that its solution performs transfer-aware water-filling; analysis, ablations, and controls corroborate each claim.

AI.tldr: CoDA is a forward-only controller that adaptively sets domain participation in multi-task LoRA fine-tuning using competence signals and cross-domain affinity from unlabeled probes, improving performance over uniform mixtures while using less data.
AI.motivation: Current efficient fine-tuning pipelines ignore the multi-task learning challenge of domain co-learning, using fixed uniform mixtures that fail to account for positive transfer and negative interference between domains, leaving unanswered how much each domain should participate and which domains should be co-trained.
AI.method: CoDA uses a forward-only controller that computes per-domain competence signals (tracking headroom and learning speed) and cross-domain affinities from unlabeled probes, then solves an entropy-regularized quadratic program to set domain participation (loss weights and data sampling rates) by rewarding high-headroom, mutually synergistic domains while damping interfering ones.
AI.result: Across five heterogeneous domains and two backbones, CoDA improves average performance over uniform mixing, learned mixtures, gradient-surgery multi-task optimizers, and online data selection while using half the data, and reduces cross-domain gradient conflict.
AI.conclusion: CoDA provides a theoretically grounded, parameter-free solution for adaptive domain participation in multi-task LoRA fine-tuning, with proven properties showing that competence signals track domain risk, the controller has a unique fixed point, and it performs transfer-aware water-filling optimization.

title: STAPO: Selective Trajectory-Aware Policy Optimization for LLM Agent Training
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is the dominant paradigm for training Large Language Model (LLM) agents on long-horizon tasks. However, sparse and delayed rewards often lead to trajectory neglect, in which agents lose focus on the task goal and interaction history at intermediate steps. Prior work has explored step-level supervision using Shannon-entropy-based uncertainty signals, which conflate inherent state complexity with agent confidence and therefore provide unreliable estimates of decision reliability. To address this issue, we propose normalized entropy, which measures confidence deviations relative to an agent's average behavior under a given state, thereby strengthening the association between low-quality actions and trajectory neglect. Building on this insight, we introduce Selective Trajectory-Aware Policy Optimization (STAPO), a hierarchical group-based RL framework. STAPO leverages normalized entropy to locate outlier steps associated with trajectory neglect and optimizes them via a joint mechanism of trajectory-aware reward and trajectory-independent penalty, enhancing trajectory awareness while preserving training stability. Extensive experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-Augmented QA demonstrate that STAPO achieves state-of-the-art performance while substantially alleviating trajectory neglect, validating its effectiveness and robustness for agentic tasks.

AI.tldr: STAPO is a hierarchical RL framework that uses normalized entropy to identify trajectory neglect steps in LLM agents and optimizes them with trajectory-aware rewards and trajectory-independent penalties for better performance on long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL for LLM agents suffers from trajectory neglect due to sparse/delayed rewards, causing agents to lose focus on task goals and interaction history. Existing Shannon-entropy-based uncertainty signals conflate state complexity with agent confidence, providing unreliable estimates of decision reliability.
AI.method: Proposes normalized entropy to measure confidence deviations relative to an agent's average behavior under a given state, strengthening association between low-quality actions and trajectory neglect. Introduces Selective Trajectory-Aware Policy Optimization (STAPO), a hierarchical group-based RL framework that uses normalized entropy to locate outlier steps and optimizes them via joint mechanism of trajectory-aware reward and trajectory-independent penalty.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-Augmented QA demonstrate state-of-the-art performance while substantially alleviating trajectory neglect. Shows effectiveness and robustness for agentic tasks.
AI.conclusion: STAPO effectively addresses trajectory neglect in LLM agents through normalized entropy-based identification and hierarchical optimization, achieving superior performance on long-horizon tasks while maintaining training stability.

title: Aligning Language Models with Selective Prediction
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026 Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond Workshop, Project Page: https://sun-umn.github.io/RLSR
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as critical decision-making components in high-stakes real-world AI systems, rendering LLM reliability a foremost practical concern. In this paper, we focus on enhancing LLM reliability through selective prediction (SP), a strategy that allows an LLM to only predict for inputs where it is likely to be correct (i.e., coverage) and hence reduce the error rate (i.e., risk) on that portion of inputs -- flagging the remaining inputs for future human discretion. In other words, SP improves LLM reliability by balancing the risk-coverage trade-off and enabling seamless human-AI collaboration. To integrate SP into LLMs, we focus on the LLM post-training alignment stage and propose to align LLMs with SP performance metrics, in contrast with existing LLM alignment methods that focus primarily on correctness or calibration metrics. Specifically, we propose a novel alignment framework, Reinforcement Learning for Selection Reward (RLSR), which targets the area under the risk-coverage curve (AURC) -- a popular SP performance metric -- as its alignment objective. RLSR achieves substantially better risk-coverage trade-off compared to multiple alignment baselines on both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks.

AI.tldr: A novel reinforcement learning framework (RLSR) for selective prediction that aligns LLMs to optimize the risk-coverage trade-off using area under risk-coverage curve as the objective.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in high-stakes decision-making, making reliability crucial. Selective prediction can improve reliability by allowing LLMs to only predict when confident, flagging uncertain cases for human review.
AI.method: Proposes RLSR (Reinforcement Learning for Selection Reward), which aligns LLMs during post-training to optimize the area under the risk-coverage curve (AURC) metric rather than just correctness or calibration.
AI.result: RLSR achieves substantially better risk-coverage trade-off compared to multiple alignment baselines on both in-domain and out-of-domain tasks.
AI.conclusion: Aligning LLMs with selective prediction performance metrics during post-training can significantly enhance LLM reliability through better risk-coverage balancing, enabling more effective human-AI collaboration in high-stakes applications.

title: Quantum-Inspired Harmonic Decision Models: A Computational Framework for Music Generation
comment: 17 pages, 3 figures. Preprint. Code and evaluation data available at GitHub
summary:
This paper introduces a quantum-inspired computational framework for harmonic decision-making in music. The proposed approach formulates harmonization as an optimization problem within a structured combinatorial space, where multiple candidate chord sequences are evaluated under interacting musical constraints. The model combines an interference-based harmonization stage with a classical optimization procedure grounded in tonal harmony. The quantum-inspired component enables the parallel consideration of multiple harmonic alternatives, while the classical stage refines the resulting sequences to ensure structural coherence and stylistic plausibility.
  The framework is evaluated on selected musical examples, including Autumn Leaves and It's a Long Way to Tipperary. Quantitative analysis shows that the optimization stage significantly reduces chord density, increases harmonic stability, and improves functional organization. At the same time, expert evaluation highlights the importance of stylistic context, demonstrating that increased harmonic complexity is not always perceived as more natural. The results suggest that harmonic generation can be interpreted as a structured decision-making process in a constrained search space. The proposed approach provides a computational model that integrates domain-specific knowledge with an interference-based search mechanism.
  Although preliminary, this work indicates that quantum-inspired methods may offer a useful framework for modeling complex decision processes in creative domains such as music. The proposed framework contributes to ongoing research on quantum-inspired models of cognition and decision-making in complex biological and creative systems.

AI.tldr: A quantum-inspired computational framework for music harmonization that treats harmonic decision-making as an optimization problem, combining interference-based parallel search with classical tonal harmony optimization.
AI.motivation: To develop a computational model for harmonic generation that treats it as a structured decision-making process in a constrained search space, integrating domain-specific musical knowledge with quantum-inspired parallel search mechanisms for more natural harmonic sequences.
AI.method: A hybrid approach combining quantum-inspired interference-based harmonization (for parallel consideration of multiple harmonic alternatives) with classical optimization procedures grounded in tonal harmony rules. The framework formulates harmonization as an optimization problem within a structured combinatorial space.
AI.result: Evaluation on musical examples (Autumn Leaves and It's a Long Way to Tipperary) shows the optimization stage significantly reduces chord density, increases harmonic stability, and improves functional organization. Expert evaluation reveals that increased harmonic complexity is not always perceived as more natural, highlighting the importance of stylistic context.
AI.conclusion: Harmonic generation can be interpreted as a structured decision-making process in a constrained search space. The proposed framework integrates domain-specific knowledge with interference-based search mechanisms and suggests quantum-inspired methods may offer useful frameworks for modeling complex decision processes in creative domains like music.

title: WeightCLIP: Aligning Datasets and Models for Weight Space Learning
summary: Weight space learning aims to learn representations of neural network (NN) weights, enabling different downstream tasks. Existing approaches show promising performance, but lacking a way to shape these weight-space representations using information about the datasets the models were trained on, thus limiting downstream applications. We propose WeightCLIP, a method for learning a dataset-aligned latent space for neural networks, where datasets information is induced during training. The NNs are encoded as latent representations using an autoencoder, while dataset samples are encoded using a dataset encoder. The two representations are aligned using a contrastive objective, effectively reshaping the weight-space representations according to the datasets. We demonstrate that such representations can be used for different downstream tasks, including mapping dataset information to a weight-space representation that decode to strong models. In addition, we introduce a latent refinement process for generating models that outperforms standard fine-tuning. Overall, our results demonstrate that explicitly incorporating dataset information improves what can be achieved with weight-space representations across retrieval, generation, and refinement. Code will be available at https://github.com/HSG-AIML/WeightCLIP.

AI.tldr: WeightCLIP learns dataset-aligned latent representations of neural network weights using contrastive learning, enabling better weight-space learning for retrieval, generation, and refinement tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing weight-space learning methods lack the ability to incorporate dataset information when learning representations of neural network weights, which limits downstream applications that could benefit from dataset-aligned weight representations.
AI.method: Proposes WeightCLIP with: 1) Autoencoder to encode NNs as latent representations, 2) Dataset encoder to encode dataset samples, 3) Contrastive objective to align weight and dataset representations, reshaping weight-space representations according to datasets.
AI.result: Dataset-aligned representations enable better downstream tasks including: mapping dataset information to weight-space representations that decode to strong models, and latent refinement process for generating models that outperforms standard fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly incorporating dataset information improves weight-space representations across retrieval, generation, and refinement tasks, demonstrating the value of dataset-aligned latent spaces for neural network weight representation learning.

title: Toward Trustworthy Large Language Model Agents in Healthcare
summary: Healthcare appointment scheduling remains a persistent operational bottleneck, driven by manual coordination, fragmented legacy systems, and high administrative overhead. These inefficiencies constrain provider availability and degrade patient access to care. This paper presents CareConnect, a safety-first conversational agent for healthcare logistics automation that leverages large language model (LLM) function calling, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and layered deterministic safety guardrails. The system orchestrates eight domain-specific tools to support appointment booking, modification, cancellation, and facility information retrieval, while enforcing strict scope constraints that prohibit medical advice or diagnosis. Safety-critical situations are handled through deterministic short-circuit mechanisms for emergency detection and medical intent refusal. We evaluate CareConnect on a comprehensive benchmark of 680 task-oriented scenarios spanning end-to-end workflows, multi-turn interactions, and edge cases. Experimental results demonstrate a 91.8% task completion rate with a median per-request latency of 2.2 seconds, 96.0% safety compliance on the dedicated safety-critical evaluation subset, and an average operational cost of $0.0324 per appointment, yielding a significant cost reduction compared to manual human scheduling. These findings show that carefully scoped and rigorously safeguarded LLM-based agents can reliably automate complex healthcare operational workflows while maintaining safety guarantees and achieving substantial cost efficiency. The source code and system implementation are publicly available at https://github.com/Hadi-Hsn/CareConnect.

AI.tldr: CareConnect is a safety-focused conversational AI agent for healthcare appointment scheduling that uses LLMs with safety guardrails, achieving 91.8% task completion while reducing operational costs.
AI.motivation: Healthcare appointment scheduling faces major inefficiencies due to manual coordination, fragmented legacy systems, and high administrative overhead, which limit provider availability and degrade patient access to care.
AI.method: CareConnect combines LLM function calling, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and layered deterministic safety guardrails with eight domain-specific tools for appointment management tasks. It uses deterministic short-circuit mechanisms for emergency detection and medical intent refusal to ensure safety.
AI.result: The system achieved 91.8% task completion rate with 2.2-second median latency, 96.0% safety compliance on safety-critical tasks, and $0.0324 average cost per appointment - significantly lower than manual scheduling.
AI.conclusion: CareConnect demonstrates that carefully scoped and rigorously safeguarded LLM-based agents can reliably automate complex healthcare operational workflows while maintaining safety guarantees and achieving substantial cost efficiency.

title: Teacher Supervision over Representation Equivalence Classes
summary: Knowledge distillation is usually framed as a choice of what to match in the teacher - its logits, hidden features, or sample relations - which presupposes that the teacher's representation has absolute coordinates to match. It does not: a pretrained representation is identifiable only up to an orthogonal-and-isotropic-scaling equivalence class, so a student should learn the teacher's equivalence class, not its features. The organizing fact is that capability is the teacher's output function, a class invariant that factors through the quotient by the class action, so an objective recovers capability exactly when it is defined there. This makes absolute feature matching ill-posed, and admissible supervision a matter of targeting class invariants (Gram structure, CKA, principal subspaces) or aligning coordinates first, unifying feature matching, relational distillation, alignment, and grafting in one geometric account. We validate our framework on Qwen2.5 and Llama-3.1. A restoration study recovers a corrupted model's representation (CKA ~ 0.99) but not its capability, and an ablation isolates the cause: output-function (logit) matching drives capability, while matching hidden representations aligns geometry without restoring function. Recovery is confined to the corpus-covered region, and a graft study confirms that boundary overlap predicts transplant success but is necessary, not sufficient.

AI.tldr: Knowledge distillation should focus on matching the teacher's equivalence class (orthogonal+scaling invariants) rather than absolute feature coordinates, since pretrained representations are only identifiable up to orthogonal-and-isotropic-scaling transformations. Capability recovery depends on output-function matching rather than geometric alignment alone.
AI.motivation: Traditional knowledge distillation approaches presuppose that teacher representations have absolute coordinates to match, but this is incorrect - pretrained representations are only identifiable up to an orthogonal-and-isotropic-scaling equivalence class. This makes absolute feature matching ill-posed and calls for a more theoretically sound approach to distillation.
AI.method: The paper proposes framing distillation as learning the teacher's equivalence class rather than absolute features. Validates framework on Qwen2.5 and Llama-3.1 through restoration studies (recovering corrupted model representations), ablation studies isolating factors driving capability, and graft studies examining representation boundary overlap.
AI.result: Restoration study recovers corrupted model representation with CKA ~0.99 but not its capability. Ablation shows output-function (logit) matching drives capability while matching hidden representations only aligns geometry without restoring function. Recovery is confined to corpus-covered region, and graft study shows boundary overlap predicts transplant success but is necessary, not sufficient.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge distillation should target class invariants (Gram structure, CKA, principal subspaces) rather than absolute feature matching, unifying feature matching, relational distillation, alignment, and grafting in one geometric framework. Capability recovery depends fundamentally on output-function matching, not geometric alignment alone.

title: Diffusion-Guided Uncertainty-Aware Delayed Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning in real world environments often suffers from severe performance degradation due to delayed feedback. Existing approaches typically mitigate performance degradation caused by observation delays by constructing augmented states or predicting the true states. However, these methods often overlook the inherent discrepancy between delayed state and true states induced by stochastic MDP. We theoretically prove the existence of such a discrepancy and show that it leads to the degradation of the optimal policy. To address this challenge, we propose Diffusion Guided Uncertainty Aware Delayed Policy Optimization (DUPO). Our method explicitly models the relationship between delayed state message and the current state using a diffusion model, and leverages the resulting discrepancy estimates to weight delayed policies. Extensive experiments on continuous robotic control tasks with multiple stochastic delays demonstrate that DUPO consistently outperforms existing methods and remains effective even under long and random delay scenarios.

AI.tldr: DUPO improves RL in delayed feedback environments by using diffusion models to address state discrepancies caused by stochastic MDPs, outperforming existing methods on robotic control tasks.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning in real-world environments suffers from performance degradation due to delayed feedback. Existing methods overlook the inherent discrepancy between delayed states and true states in stochastic MDPs, which theoretically leads to optimal policy degradation.
AI.method: Proposes Diffusion Guided Uncertainty Aware Delayed Policy Optimization (DUPO) which explicitly models the relationship between delayed state messages and current states using a diffusion model, and leverages discrepancy estimates to weight delayed policies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on continuous robotic control tasks with multiple stochastic delays demonstrate that DUPO consistently outperforms existing methods and remains effective even under long and random delay scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DUPO successfully addresses the challenge of delayed feedback in RL by modeling state discrepancies with diffusion models, providing a robust solution for stochastic delay scenarios in real-world applications like robotic control.

title: Differentiate the Evaluator, Not the Program: An Efficient Runtime Representation for Neuro-Symbolic Learning
summary:
AI systems increasingly propose executable scientific models whose value depends on both their symbolic structure and their fitted continuous parameters. This makes parameter calibration the bottleneck of program-and-parameter co-search: an outer loop can generate thousands of candidate programs, but each needs an inner gradient-based optimization before it can be assessed. Staging each candidate into its own differentiable graph makes individual models fast but sacrifices the program-as-data property that keeps search fluid; interpreter-based approaches preserve programs as runtime data but pay interpreter overhead that dominates the numerical work.
  We present the Native Differentiable Virtual Machine (NDVM), a runtime representation that differentiates executable programs without compiling each candidate into a separate graph. NDVM separates symbolic structure from differentiable numeric state: tags, symbols, environments, and control remain native runtime data, while numeric payloads live in dense batched buffers with exact reverse-mode gradients recorded along the realized execution trace, so one evaluator walk is amortized across large populations of parameter vectors. A locked cost model of a real differentiable self-hosted Scheme interpreter motivates the design. We realize NDVM as a native runtime with forward and gradient equivalence to the reference backend, about 60x per-lane batch amortization, near-linear multicore scaling, and two independent front ends. In fixed-budget co-search over LLM-proposed programs, NDVM reaches high-quality solutions about 24x sooner in wall-clock time, suggesting runtime differentiation as a practical systems foundation for scientific discovery workflows.

AI.tldr: NDVM enables efficient differentiable program co-search by separating symbolic structure from numeric state, allowing batched gradient computation without per-program compilation overhead.
AI.motivation: Parameter calibration is bottleneck in AI-driven scientific model discovery where outer loops generate thousands of candidate programs, each requiring expensive gradient-based optimization before evaluation.
AI.method: Native Differentiable Virtual Machine (NDVM) separates symbolic structure (tags, symbols, environments, control) from differentiable numeric state stored in dense batched buffers, with reverse-mode gradients recorded along execution trace; uses cost model from real differentiable self-hosted Scheme interpreter.
AI.result: NDVM achieves ~60x per-lane batch amortization, near-linear multicore scaling, forward/gradient equivalence to reference backend, and in co-search experiments reaches high-quality solutions ~24x faster in wall-clock time.
AI.conclusion: Runtime differentiation provides practical systems foundation for scientific discovery workflows by enabling efficient program-and-parameter co-search without sacrificing program-as-data flexibility.

title: ASSEMCAD: Production-Ready CAD Assembly Generation from Natural Language
summary: Recent advances in large language models and programmatic CAD have significantly improved Text-to-CAD generation for individual parts. However, production-ready mechanical assembly generation remains largely unsolved. Unlike single-part modeling, assemblies require coordinated reasoning over multiple components, functional interfaces, assembly relations, engineering principles, and physical consistency. Consequently, directly generating executable CAD code is insufficient for constructing mechanically valid and reusable assemblies. We present AssemCAD, an axiom-grounded framework for production-ready CAD assembly generation from natural language. Instead of representing an assembly as monolithic CAD code, AssemCAD first constructs an axiomatic Assembly Specification consisting of typed parts, geometry-backed ports, executable mates, and engineering axioms. Each assembly relation is explicitly grounded in one or more engineering principles, making the resulting specification interpretable, reusable, and verifiable. To realize this specification, AssemCAD introduces a port- and mate-based CAD assembly library that executes symbolic assembly relations through deterministic mate transformations and validates declared interfaces using concrete B-Rep geometric evidence. Built on this representation and library, AssemCAD further supports on-demand synthesis of reusable parametric component factories for both standard and open-world geometries. Experiments on AssemBench show that AssemCAD substantially improves assembly preservation and physical validity over code-centric CAD generation baselines, while generalizing across different foundation-model backbones. By combining axiom-grounded assembly reasoning with deterministic geometric execution, AssemCAD extends Text-to-CAD from isolated part generation toward production-ready mechanical assembly design.

AI.tldr: AssemCAD is a framework for generating production-ready CAD assemblies from natural language by first creating axiomatic assembly specifications with explicit engineering principles, then executing them through deterministic mate transformations, improving over direct CAD code generation approaches.
AI.motivation: Text-to-CAD generation for individual parts has advanced, but mechanical assembly generation remains unsolved. Direct CAD code generation fails for assemblies because they require coordinated reasoning over multiple components, functional interfaces, assembly relations, engineering principles, and physical consistency.
AI.method: Instead of monolithic CAD code, AssemCAD first constructs an axiomatic Assembly Specification with typed parts, geometry-backed ports, executable mates, and engineering axioms. It then uses a port- and mate-based CAD assembly library that executes symbolic assembly relations through deterministic mate transformations and validates interfaces with B-Rep geometric evidence. The framework also supports on-demand synthesis of reusable parametric component factories.
AI.result: Experiments on AssemBench show AssemCAD substantially improves assembly preservation and physical validity over code-centric CAD generation baselines while generalizing across different foundation-model backbones.
AI.conclusion: By combining axiom-grounded assembly reasoning with deterministic geometric execution, AssemCAD extends Text-to-CAD from isolated part generation toward production-ready mechanical assembly design, making assemblies interpretable, reusable, and verifiable.

title: When Geometry Aligns: Dihedral Hidden-State Transformations in UNet, ViT, and DiT Architectures
comment: Accepted at the Conference on Lifelong Learning Agents (CoLLAs), 2026
summary: Diffusion architectures now encompass convolutional UNets as well as transformer-based designs such as Diffusion Transformers (DiTs), inspired by Vision Transformers (ViTs), yet the effects of structured geometric perturbations within these architectures remain poorly understood. We study this question through a unified framework that applies reflection-based elements of the dihedral group to intermediate hidden states as controlled internal interventions, contrasting geometrically consistent and inconsistent variants. Using activation-level diagnostics, including Self-Consistency Shift (SCS), Activation Mass Scatter (AMS), and Drift, we analyze feature stability and geometric drift. We find that consistent transformations improve stability, while inconsistent ones induce predictable, architecture-specific failures. In the main Stable Diffusion 2.1 U-Net study, we evaluate seven intervention modes over three seeds and complement the internal diagnostics with image-level FID, KID, CLIP score, and LPIPS diversity. Taken together with supporting ViT and controlled DiT analyses, these results establish geometric consistency as a key principle for stable hidden-state interventions in spatially structured vision and diffusion models.

AI.tldr: A study shows that geometric consistency of transformations (like reflections) applied to hidden states is crucial for stable interventions in diffusion models, with consistent transformations improving stability while inconsistent ones cause architecture-specific failures.
AI.motivation: The effects of structured geometric perturbations (like reflections) within diffusion architectures (UNets and transformer-based DiTs) remain poorly understood, despite their importance for spatial vision models.
AI.method: Use a unified framework applying reflection-based dihedral group transformations to intermediate hidden states as controlled interventions, contrasting geometrically consistent vs inconsistent variants. Analyze feature stability using activation-level diagnostics: Self-Consistency Shift (SCS), Activation Mass Scatter (AMS), and Drift.
AI.result: Consistent transformations improve stability, while inconsistent ones induce predictable, architecture-specific failures. In the main Stable Diffusion 2.1 U-Net study with seven intervention modes over three seeds, results were complemented with image-level metrics (FID, KID, CLIP score, LPIPS diversity).
AI.conclusion: Geometric consistency is a key principle for stable hidden-state interventions in spatially structured vision and diffusion models, establishing it as crucial for understanding and designing robust interventions in such architectures.

title: TacReasoner: A Dynamic Tactile-Language Framework for Interactive Reasoning in Real-World Scenarios
summary: Among the five primary human senses, tactile is arguably the most fundamental to survival, as it enables the perception of physical contact and interaction in real-world environments. In this paper, we explore two key challenges of integrating tactile sensing into intelligent systems for multimodal reasoning: (i) insufficient modeling of dynamic tactile signals, which restricts reasoning over temporally evolving properties, and (ii) hallucination in tactile foundation models caused by the absence of explicit reasoning mechanisms, leading to unstable real-world inference. To address these challenges, we propose TacReasoner, a dynamic tactile-language framework for interactive reasoning in real-world scenarios. First, TacReasoner incorporates a Dynamic-aware Tactile Encoder to enhance the perception and representation of dynamic tactile signals. More importantly, we introduce TouchCoT-10k, the first tactile chain-of-thought dataset for structured reasoning over tactile inputs. Upon it, we establish DynTac-Bench to systematically evaluate dynamic tactile perception and real-world commonsense reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that TacReasoner achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art models across multiple datasets. Notably, despite using only 7B parameters, TacReasoner outperforms the 14B VTV-LLM model on most subtasks, highlighting its effectiveness and efficiency in tactile commonsense reasoning.

AI.tldr: TacReasoner is a dynamic tactile-language framework that addresses challenges in tactile sensing for multimodal reasoning, using a dynamic-aware encoder and the first tactile chain-of-thought dataset to enable structured reasoning about evolving tactile properties.
AI.motivation: Tactile sensing is fundamental for intelligent systems but faces two key challenges: (1) insufficient modeling of dynamic tactile signals limits reasoning over temporally evolving properties, and (2) tactile foundation models suffer from hallucination due to lack of explicit reasoning mechanisms, causing unstable real-world inference.
AI.method: Proposes TacReasoner with: 1) Dynamic-aware Tactile Encoder for enhanced perception of dynamic tactile signals, 2) TouchCoT-10k - the first tactile chain-of-thought dataset for structured reasoning over tactile inputs, and 3) DynTac-Bench for systematic evaluation of dynamic tactile perception and real-world commonsense reasoning.
AI.result: TacReasoner achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art models across multiple datasets. Notably, with only 7B parameters, it outperforms the 14B VTV-LLM model on most subtasks, demonstrating effectiveness and efficiency in tactile commonsense reasoning.
AI.conclusion: TacReasoner successfully addresses the limitations of current tactile sensing systems by introducing explicit reasoning mechanisms and dynamic signal modeling, enabling more stable and accurate real-world inference for tactile commonsense reasoning.

title: Modular Foundation Models for Time-Series Perception in Digital Twins
summary: Engineering Digital Twins and Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) systems rely on robust perception modules to extract actionable information from heterogeneous and non-stationary time-series data. However, most existing approaches remain task-specific, data-hungry, and difficult to integrate into scalable monitoring and decision-making pipelines. Moreover, purely data-driven models often lack robustness and transferability across varying operating conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a modular foundation model for time-series perception based on a collection of pretrained representation encoders. The framework leverages self-supervised learning on heterogeneous datasets to learn transferable and task-agnostic representations, which can be reused across multiple PHM tasks. A gating mechanism is introduced to dynamically select relevant encoders for a given target dataset, enabling conditional computation and adaptive model composition. The selected representations are projected into a shared latent space and aggregated using a Transformer-based self-attention module that explicitly models cross-encoder interactions. The resulting architecture supports multiple downstream tasks, including imputation, long-term forecasting, and few-shot learning, through lightweight task-specific heads, while keeping pretrained encoders frozen during adaptation. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate the complementary roles of self-supervised pretraining, encoder selection, representation alignment, and adaptive aggregation. Experimental results on the ETT benchmark show competitive performance across tasks, while a real-world industrial case study on virtual sensing for hydro-generator rotor temperature highlights the practical relevance of the approach.

AI.tldr: A modular foundation model for time-series perception that combines pretrained representation encoders with a gating mechanism for adaptive model composition, enabling transferable, task-agnostic representations across multiple PHM tasks.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in current PHM systems: task-specific approaches, data-hungry models, lack of robustness/transferability across varying operating conditions, and difficulty integrating into scalable monitoring pipelines.
AI.method: Proposes a framework using self-supervised learning on heterogeneous datasets to create pretrained representation encoders. Introduces a gating mechanism for dynamic encoder selection, projects representations into shared latent space, and uses Transformer-based self-attention for cross-encoder aggregation. Supports downstream tasks via lightweight task-specific heads while keeping pretrained encoders frozen.
AI.result: Demonstrates competitive performance on ETT benchmark across multiple tasks and shows practical relevance in industrial case study for hydro-generator rotor temperature virtual sensing. Ablation studies confirm complementary roles of pretraining, encoder selection, representation alignment, and adaptive aggregation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modular foundation model provides a scalable, transferable solution for time-series perception in PHM systems, addressing key challenges of task specificity, data requirements, and robustness while enabling adaptive composition for diverse monitoring applications.

title: DSpark: Confidence-Scheduled Speculative Decoding with Semi-Autoregressive Generation
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates Large Language Model (LLM) inference by decoupling draft generation from target verification. While recent parallel drafters efficiently propose long token sequences in a single forward pass, they suffer from rapid acceptance decay due to a lack of inter-token dependencies. Furthermore, indiscriminately verifying these extended blocks wastes critical batch capacity on tokens with high rejection risks, severely degrading throughput in high-concurrency serving systems. We introduce DSpark, a speculative decoding framework that unifies high-throughput parallel generation with adaptive, load-aware verification. To maintain draft quality, DSpark utilizes a semi-autoregressive architecture, coupling a parallel backbone with a lightweight sequential module, to introduce intra-block dependency modeling and mitigate suffix decay. To optimize system efficiency, DSpark employs confidence-scheduled verification, dynamically tailoring the verification length for each request based on estimated prefix survival probabilities and engine-specific throughput profiles. On offline benchmarks across diverse domains, DSpark substantially improves the accepted length over state-of-the-art autoregressive and parallel drafters. When deployed within the DeepSeek-V4 serving system under live user traffic, DSpark successfully mitigates verification waste. Compared to the established production baseline (MTP-1), DSpark accelerates per-user generation speeds by 60 to 85 percent at matched throughput levels. More importantly, by preventing severe throughput degradation under strict interactivity constraints, it enables performance tiers that were previously unattainable, shifting the Pareto frontier of our serving system.

AI.tldr: DSpark is a speculative decoding framework that combines parallel draft generation with adaptive verification to significantly accelerate LLM inference while maintaining high draft quality and optimizing system throughput.
AI.motivation: Current parallel drafters for speculative decoding suffer from rapid acceptance decay due to lack of inter-token dependencies, and indiscriminate verification of extended token blocks wastes batch capacity on tokens with high rejection risks, degrading throughput in high-concurrency serving systems.
AI.method: DSpark uses a semi-autoregressive architecture combining a parallel backbone with a lightweight sequential module to introduce intra-block dependency modeling and mitigate suffix decay. It also employs confidence-scheduled verification that dynamically tailors verification length per request based on estimated prefix survival probabilities and engine-specific throughput profiles.
AI.result: On offline benchmarks, DSpark substantially improves accepted length over state-of-the-art autoregressive and parallel drafters. In DeepSeek-V4 serving system under live traffic, DSpark accelerates per-user generation speeds by 60-85% compared to baseline (MTP-1), enables previously unattainable performance tiers, and shifts the Pareto frontier of the serving system.
AI.conclusion: DSpark effectively unifies high-throughput parallel generation with adaptive, load-aware verification to overcome the limitations of existing speculative decoding approaches, making it a practical and efficient solution for production LLM serving systems with high concurrency requirements.

title: Graph Classification via Network Usable Information: From Representation Evaluation to Structure Selection
summary:
We propose NetinfoGC, a framework for graph classification that extends the Network Usable Information (NUI) paradigm to graph-level learning. Unlike conventional graph neural network approaches that rely on end-to-end training of black-box embeddings, NetinfoGC constructs a family of permutation-invariant graph representations derived from propagation-based mechanisms and classical structural descriptors, including graph centrality measures.
  To evaluate representation quality, we introduce a training-free NUI estimation procedure based on clustering consistency with ground-truth labels, providing a proxy for task-relevant information without supervised learning. We further exploit the same representations using sparse-group LASSO regularization, enabling automatic selection of informative structural descriptors while suppressing redundant ones.
  Experiments on benchmark datasets show that classical centrality measures are highly competitive with learned propagation-based representations, and in several cases yield superior performance. Moreover, we observe a strong correlation between estimated NUI and downstream classification accuracy, validating NUI as an effective measure of representation utility.
  Overall, NetinfoGC provides a unified and interpretable framework for evaluating and exploiting graph representations without requiring end-to-end neural training.

AI.tldr: NetinfoGC is a graph classification framework that extends Network Usable Information to graph-level learning, using permutation-invariant representations from propagation mechanisms and classical structural descriptors (like centrality measures), with training-free NUI estimation and sparse-group LASSO for feature selection.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to move beyond conventional graph neural networks that use black-box embeddings and end-to-end training. They want to create a more interpretable framework for graph classification that doesn't require neural network training and can evaluate representation quality directly.
AI.method:
1) Constructs permutation-invariant graph representations from propagation-based mechanisms and classical structural descriptors (graph centrality measures)
2) Introduces training-free NUI estimation procedure based on clustering consistency with ground-truth labels
3) Uses sparse-group LASSO regularization for automatic selection of informative structural descriptors while suppressing redundant ones
AI.result: Experiments show classical centrality measures are highly competitive with learned propagation-based representations, sometimes superior. Strong correlation found between estimated NUI and downstream classification accuracy, validating NUI as an effective measure of representation utility.
AI.conclusion: NetinfoGC provides a unified, interpretable framework for evaluating and exploiting graph representations without requiring end-to-end neural training, offering a viable alternative to conventional GNN approaches with better interpretability.

title: The Changing Role of Symbolic Methods in Artificial Intelligence
summary:
Why do intelligent systems need to perform explicit symbolic reasoning? Computer science has traditionally regarded symbolic reasoning as a defining component of intelligence. Yet the remarkable success of modern foundation models raises a fundamental question: if increasingly capable AI systems can operate with little explicit symbolic reasoning, what role do symbolic methods actually play?
  This article argues that explicit symbolic reasoning is not a fundamental property of intelligence, but a computational consequence of operating on simplified models of reality. We propose the Compression Principle: every computational model is a simplified representation of reality, and explicit symbolic reasoning compensates for information omitted during model construction. From this principle, we derive the Modeling--Reasoning Trade-off: as computational models preserve richer representations of the world, the need for explicit symbolic reasoning correspondingly decreases. This perspective provides a unified explanation for both the historical success of symbolic methods and the remarkable effectiveness of modern foundation models.
  Paradoxically, the same development makes symbolic methods increasingly important for humans. As intelligent systems become more capable and more opaque, symbolic representations increasingly serve as interfaces through which humans specify requirements, verify behavior, regulate autonomous systems, and establish trust. We therefore argue that the future of symbolic methods lies not primarily as the computational engine of intelligent systems, but as the symbolic interface between increasingly capable AI systems and the humans who build, govern, and depend upon them.

AI.tldr: Symbolic reasoning is not fundamental to intelligence but compensates for simplified models; as models become richer (like foundation models), symbolic reasoning becomes less needed for computation but more important for human-AI interfaces.
AI.motivation: To resolve the paradox of why symbolic reasoning has been traditionally seen as essential for intelligence, yet modern foundation models achieve remarkable success with little explicit symbolic reasoning. The paper aims to explain both the historical importance of symbolic methods and their changing role in the age of foundation models.
AI.method: Proposes the Compression Principle (computational models are simplified representations of reality) and derives the Modeling-Reasoning Trade-off (richer representations reduce need for explicit reasoning). Uses this theoretical framework to analyze the evolution from symbolic AI to foundation models.
AI.result: Provides a unified explanation: symbolic reasoning compensates for information loss in simplified models. As foundation models preserve richer representations, they need less explicit symbolic reasoning for computation. Paradoxically, symbolic methods become increasingly important as human-readable interfaces for specifying requirements, verifying behavior, regulating systems, and establishing trust.
AI.conclusion: The future of symbolic methods lies not as computational engines of intelligent systems, but as interfaces between increasingly capable AI systems and the humans who build, govern, and depend on them.

title: Implicit Bias of SGD in Multivariate ReLU Networks: Effective Width Collapse
summary: We study the implicit bias of noisy stochastic gradient descent in training wide two-layer ReLU networks for multivariate regression. In a mean-field regime, the training dynamics are approximated by a Wasserstein gradient flow that converges to a unique stationary measure. We characterize the structure of this stationary measure and the predictor it represents. We show that, despite the network being infinitely overparameterized, the learned predictor admits an effectively finite representation: the input weights and biases align along finitely many directions, leading to an effective width collapse. In particular, the solution function is continuous piecewise affine, with affine regions determined by the cells of a finite hyperplane arrangement. The number of learned directions, and hence hyperplanes, is bounded above by $2\mathcal{P}-1$, where $\mathcal{P}$ denotes the number of linear dichotomies realizable on the training inputs. We further establish a non-redundancy property of the learned representation by proving that each learned direction induces a unique ternary activation pattern on the training data. Consequently, the complexity of the learned predictor is governed by the combinatorial geometry of the training data.

AI.tldr: Noisy stochastic gradient descent on wide two-layer ReLU networks leads to effective width collapse: despite infinite overparameterization, learned predictors have finite representation with input weights aligning along finitely many directions, producing piecewise affine functions bounded by linear dichotomies of training data.
AI.motivation: To understand the implicit bias (or algorithmic regularization) of noisy stochastic gradient descent when training wide two-layer ReLU neural networks for multivariate regression, particularly how infinite overparameterization doesn't lead to arbitrary solutions but results in structured predictors.
AI.method: Analyze training dynamics in mean-field regime using Wasserstein gradient flow approximation, characterize stationary measure and its structure, study effective width collapse phenomenon, and relate learned directions to combinatorial geometry of training data.
AI.result: Despite infinite overparameterization, learned predictors have effectively finite representation with input weights aligning along finitely many directions. Solution functions are continuous piecewise affine with affine regions determined by finite hyperplane arrangements. Number of learned directions bounded above by 2𝒫-1 (𝒫 = number of linear dichotomies realizable on training inputs). Each learned direction induces unique ternary activation pattern.
AI.conclusion: The implicit bias of noisy SGD leads to structured solutions governed by combinatorial geometry of training data rather than arbitrary functions, demonstrating that infinite width doesn't imply unconstrained representational capacity during training with noisy gradient descent.

title: AgentGym2: Benchmarking Large Language Model Agents in De-Idealized Real-World Environments
comment: Accepted as a main conference paper at ACL 2026
summary: Language agents, i.e., LLM agents, progress rapidly and are increasingly deployed in production environments. This trend underscores the urgent need for rigorous and realistic evaluations. However, most existing benchmarks evaluate agents in simplified, idealized settings. They typically rely on pre-packaged tool interfaces, overlook critical steps, and assume inputs are clean and fully specified. Consequently, they understate the difficulty of real deployments, where uncertainty and noise are ubiquitous and agents must proactively explore the environment to uncover new tools. To bridge this gap, we present AgentGym2, a new evaluation framework with task instances grounded in real-world end-to-end working demands. Beyond reasoning and planning, it measures agents' ability to execute end-to-end procedures, discover tools via exploration, compose tools for unseen tasks, and remain robust to noisy and underspecified information. Experiments on 15 proprietary and open-source models show that even SOTA systems like Gemini and GPT-5 struggle on AgentGym2, revealing a substantial gap between the capability of current agents and the demands of real-world applications.

AI.tldr: AgentGym2 is a new evaluation framework that tests LLM agents in realistic, noisy environments where they must discover tools, handle underspecified inputs, and execute end-to-end procedures, revealing current SOTA models still struggle with real-world demands.
AI.motivation: Existing agent benchmarks are too simplistic - they use pre-packaged tool interfaces, assume clean inputs, and overlook critical real-world steps, thus underestimating the difficulty of actual deployments where uncertainty and noise are ubiquitous.
AI.method: They developed AgentGym2, an evaluation framework with task instances grounded in real-world end-to-end working demands that measures agents' abilities to execute procedures, discover tools via exploration, compose tools for unseen tasks, and remain robust to noisy/underspecified information.
AI.result: Experiments on 15 proprietary and open-source models show that even state-of-the-art systems like Gemini and GPT-5 struggle on AgentGym2, revealing a substantial capability gap between current agents and real-world application demands.
AI.conclusion: There's an urgent need for more rigorous, realistic evaluations of language agents as they progress toward production deployment, and AgentGym2 helps bridge the gap between simplified benchmarks and real-world requirements.

title: Reflected Schrödinger Bridge Matching
summary: Recent advances in generative modeling have enabled the efficient computation of Schrödinger bridges (SB) in high-dimensional settings by leveraging partially simulation-free training methods inspired by flow matching. However, these have not covered SBs with reflecting dynamics, a useful model choice with built-in guarantees that generated samples stay in the data domain. Existing alternatives for reflected SBs instead rely on more complex training based on forward--backward SDE theory, requiring expensive higher-order derivatives and sampling entire paths during training. In this article, we introduce a partially simulation-free framework that allows reflected SBs to be trained similarly to flow matching, using a new sampling method and regression target. We demonstrate our results by coupling pairs of well-known high-dimensional image datasets. Using reflected dynamics incurs negligible additional wall-clock time during both training and inference while maintaining or slightly improving generative performance.

AI.tldr: A new simulation-free framework for training reflected Schrödinger bridges that matches flow matching efficiency while maintaining data domain constraints
AI.motivation: Existing Schrödinger bridge methods haven't covered reflecting dynamics, which guarantee samples stay in the data domain. Current alternatives for reflected SBs require complex training based on forward-backward SDE theory with expensive higher-order derivatives and full path sampling.
AI.method: Introduces a partially simulation-free framework using a new sampling method and regression target that allows reflected SBs to be trained similarly to flow matching
AI.result: Reflected dynamics incur negligible additional wall-clock time during training and inference while maintaining or slightly improving generative performance, demonstrated by coupling pairs of high-dimensional image datasets
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enables efficient training of reflected Schrödinger bridges with domain-preserving properties while matching the computational efficiency of flow matching methods

title: CP-WSP: A Declarative CP-SAT Framework for Configurable Multi-Constraint Workforce Scheduling
summary: Workforce scheduling is an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem requiring simultaneous satisfaction of labor regulations, coverage requirements, employee preferences and operational objectives. Existing CP formulations typically model simplified instances with 6-12 constraints at shift-level granularity and critically lack explicit support for: mandatory break scheduling with midpoint placement control; acuity weighted workload equity; sub-shift temporal granularity enabling demand-driven staffing; inter-week schedule stability; and cross-midnight shift patterns common in 24-hour operations. This paper presents CP-WSP: a declarative CP-SAT framework enforcing 14 hard constraints as mathematically inviolable requirements (zero regulatory violations by construction) while optimizing 15 soft objectives through a unified weighted penalty function -- all configurable via a JSON specification with no code changes required. Key contributions include: a shift-window variable decomposition enabling mandatory break scheduling with centrality control; acuity-weighted workload equity; multi-granularity temporal resolution from 30 minutes to 2 hours; inter-week schedule stability; a grid-offset preprocessing technique for cross-midnight shifts; and a reproducible 36-configuration benchmark suite for community comparison. Evaluated on INRC-II benchmarks at both hourly and shift-level granularity and on 36 synthetic configurations.

AI.tldr: CP-WSP is a declarative CP-SAT framework for complex workforce scheduling that enforces 14 hard constraints as inviolable requirements while optimizing 15 soft objectives via a unified weighted penalty function, all configurable through JSON without code changes.
AI.motivation: Existing constraint programming formulations for workforce scheduling are too simplified, typically handling only 6-12 constraints at shift-level granularity. They lack critical features needed for real-world operations: mandatory break scheduling with midpoint control, acuity-weighted workload equity, sub-shift temporal granularity for demand-driven staffing, inter-week schedule stability, and proper support for cross-midnight shift patterns common in 24-hour operations.
AI.method: The paper presents CP-WSP, a declarative CP-SAT framework that uses: 1) shift-window variable decomposition enabling mandatory break scheduling with centrality control, 2) acuity-weighted workload equity mechanisms, 3) multi-granularity temporal resolution (30 minutes to 2 hours), 4) inter-week schedule stability constraints, 5) grid-offset preprocessing for cross-midnight shifts, and 6) a reproducible 36-configuration benchmark suite. The system enforces 14 hard constraints as mathematically inviolable requirements while optimizing 15 soft objectives through a unified weighted penalty function.
AI.result: The framework was evaluated on INRC-II benchmarks at both hourly and shift-level granularity and on 36 synthetic configurations. The system achieves zero regulatory violations by construction (guaranteed satisfaction of all hard constraints) while optimizing soft objectives according to the weighted penalty function.
AI.conclusion: CP-WSP provides a comprehensive solution to complex workforce scheduling problems, addressing critical limitations of existing approaches. The framework's declarative nature and JSON configuration enable flexible deployment without code changes, making it suitable for real-world applications with diverse requirements including 24-hour operations, multi-granularity staffing, and regulatory compliance.

title: LLM-Guided Transportation Hub Capacity Planning with Textual Business Inputs
summary: While traditional hub capacity planning models optimize effectively for quantitative inputs, they often fail to digest qualitative business context. We propose a novel framework where a large language model (LLM) agent iteratively proposes hub capacity decisions guided by natural-language business context descriptions. The key mechanism is a chain-of-thought reasoning protocol: the LLM constructs a structured decision table that maps each contextual item to specific capacity adjustments based on the implied direction and magnitude of changes. The new capacity decision is then validated through a feedback loop with an optimization model, which provides routing-based performance metrics to guide the agent's selection. On a real-world 13-hub freight network in the southeastern US, our framework achieves a 2.8% optimality gap relative to the hidden ground-truth, a significant improvement over the 11.0% gap produced by the traditional optimization model without textual business inputs. This demonstrates that LLMs can serve as a contextual bridge, integrating qualitative business insights into Operations Research workflows.

AI.tldr: LLM agents can bridge qualitative business context with quantitative optimization models for hub capacity planning, achieving significantly better results than traditional optimization alone.
AI.motivation: Traditional hub capacity planning models effectively optimize quantitative inputs but fail to incorporate qualitative business context, creating a gap between technical optimization and practical business insights.
AI.method: A framework where an LLM agent iteratively proposes hub capacity decisions guided by natural-language business context using chain-of-thought reasoning. The LLM constructs structured decision tables mapping contextual items to capacity adjustments, which are validated through feedback loops with an optimization model providing routing-based performance metrics.
AI.result: On a real-world 13-hub freight network in the southeastern US, the framework achieves a 2.8% optimality gap relative to hidden ground-truth, significantly outperforming the 11.0% gap from traditional optimization without textual business inputs.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively serve as a contextual bridge to integrate qualitative business insights into Operations Research workflows, demonstrating their potential to enhance decision-making in capacity planning and similar optimization problems.

title: Rethinking On-Policy Self-Distillation for Thinking Models
summary: Self-distillation is a promising recipe for self-improvement in language models. In this setting, a model can serve as its own teacher when given privileged information, such as a solution to a math problem. This seems especially appealing for thinking models, which can use test-time reasoning to absorb the privileged information. Surprisingly, we show that privileged self-distillation degrades thinking models on long reasoning traces: across five Qwen3 and OLMo thinking models evaluated on AIME24, AIME25, and HMMT25, privileged-context distillation causes a relative drop of up to 17% in avg@16 accuracy. The degradation scales with the amount of privileged context withheld from the student and is most pronounced at long rollout budgets, where thinking models otherwise obtain their largest gains. This failure mode is not specific to self-distillation: on-policy distillation (OPD) improves thinking models, but privileged OPD reverses these gains. Our diagnostics link this failure mode to how privileged teacher context reshapes learning at high-entropy forking positions, where multiple continuations remain plausible and may lead to different reasoning paths. Privileged context lowers fork rates in thinking-model rollouts but not in instruction-model rollouts. This leads to an interesting dichotomy, where privileged context can help instruction-tuned models but hurts stronger thinking models. The effect is visible when the student begins a self-correction branch, where privileged OPD penalizes sampled reconsideration tokens that vanilla OPD supports. Thinking models trained with a privileged teacher produce fewer verification, backtracking, and hedging markers, even after length normalization. These findings indicate that self-distillation for strong thinking models requires attention to token-level signal, especially around correction and reasoning steps.

AI.tldr: Privileged self-distillation harms thinking language models' performance on long reasoning tasks, causing accuracy drops up to 17%, while instruction models benefit from the same approach.
AI.motivation: Self-distillation seems promising for language model self-improvement, especially for thinking models that can use test-time reasoning to absorb privileged information (like math solutions). The paper investigates whether this actually works as expected.
AI.method: Evaluated five Qwen3 and OLMo thinking models on AIME24, AIME25, and HMMT25 datasets. Compared privileged-context distillation (withholding privileged info from student) against on-policy distillation (OPD). Analyzed fork rates in thinking-model rollouts, token-level behaviors at high-entropy positions, and markers of verification/backtracking.
AI.result: Privileged self-distillation degrades thinking models by 17% relative accuracy drop on average, scaling with withheld privileged context. Degradation is most severe at long rollout budgets where thinking models normally excel. On-policy distillation helps, but privileged OPD reverses gains. Privileged context reduces fork rates in thinking rollouts, penalizes self-correction branches, and decreases verification/backtracking markers.
AI.conclusion: Privileged self-distillation fails for strong thinking models but helps weaker instruction models, revealing a dichotomy. Self-distillation for thinking models requires careful attention to token-level signals around correction and reasoning steps, especially at high-entropy forking positions.

title: Phase-Preserving Trimodal Transformer for Tropical Forest Biomass Estimation Using Optical and PolInSAR Data
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, one of which is a TikZ
summary: The accurate estimation of Above-Ground Biomass (AGB) in mature tropical forests remains a critical challenge in remote sensing, primarily due to the saturation of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) signals in high-density areas and persistent cloud cover affecting optical imagery. To overcome these physical limitations, we propose the Trimodal Coherent Co-attention Transformer (TCCT), a physics-informed deep learning architecture. The TCCT natively fuses optical surface reflectance (Landsat-5) with complex-valued Polarimetric SAR Interferometry (PolInSAR) data from both P and L bands. Unlike traditional fusion methods, our architecture employs complex-valued encoders to preserve spatial phase coherence, coupled with a dynamic co-attention mechanism that acts as an adaptive gating module, reducing the weight of cloud-corrupted optical pixels and shifting reliance to microwave phase data. We also derived a localized spatial allometric calibration model via Levenberg-Marquardt optimization, tailored to the specific wood density of the Paracou region in the Amazon basin. Evaluated using a two-stage protocol, the TCCT first underwent a rigorous 5-fold cross-validation to establish robust global weights (achieving a global RMSE of 4.19 m). Subsequently, following a localized spatial fine-tuning phase over 200 epochs, the model attained an absolute RMSE of 3.78 m and an $R^2$ of 0.33 for Canopy Height Models (CHM), outperforming standard Random Forest, CNN, and Vision Transformer baselines. Our ablation study confirms that preserving phase coherence mitigates deep-canopy signal saturation. When converted to AGB, the fine-tuned TCCT map yielded a Relative RMSE (rRMSE) of 4.51% in dense forest areas above 50 Mg/ha. By meeting the European Space Agency (ESA) BIOMASS mission requirement of less than 20% error, the TCCT provides a robust framework for continuous carbon stock mapping in tropical biomes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Trimodal Coherent Co-attention Transformer (TCCT) model that fuses optical and polarimetric SAR data with physics-informed constraints to accurately estimate above-ground biomass in tropical forests, overcoming signal saturation and cloud cover limitations.
AI.motivation: Accurate AGB estimation in mature tropical forests is challenging due to SAR signal saturation in dense areas and persistent cloud cover affecting optical imagery, creating a need for methods that overcome these physical limitations.
AI.method: The Trimodal Coherent Co-attention Transformer (TCCT) architecture fuses optical Landsat-5 reflectance with complex-valued P and L band PolInSAR data using complex-valued encoders to preserve phase coherence. It employs a dynamic co-attention mechanism as adaptive gating to handle cloud-corrupted optical pixels. The method includes localized spatial allometric calibration via Levenberg-Marquardt optimization and uses a two-stage evaluation protocol with cross-validation and fine-tuning.
AI.result: The model achieved 3.78 m RMSE and R² of 0.33 for CHM after fine-tuning, outperforming Random Forest, CNN, and ViT baselines. When converted to AGB, it achieved 4.51% rRMSE in dense forests (>50 Mg/ha), meeting ESA BIOMASS mission requirements of <20% error. Ablation study confirmed phase coherence preservation mitigates deep-canopy signal saturation.
AI.conclusion: The TCCT provides a robust physics-informed framework for continuous carbon stock mapping in tropical biomes by effectively overcoming SAR saturation and cloud limitations through multimodal fusion with phase coherence preservation and adaptive attention mechanisms.

title: ClassicLogic: A Knowledge-Driven Benchmark of Classic Puzzle Games for Evaluating Compositional Generalization
summary: Compositional generalization, the ability to understand and produce novel combinations of known components, remains a fundamental challenge for modern artificial intelligence. While few benchmarks exist, many focus on linguistic tasks and lack complex, explicit compositional structures. We introduce ClassicLogic, a new benchmark suite designed to evaluate an agent's ability to learn and compose problem-solving strategies. The benchmark consists of four classic logic puzzles: Sudoku, KenKen, Kakuro, and Futoshiki. Its core innovation is a hierarchical, explicit knowledge base for each game, where complex solving strategies are formally defined as compositions of simpler, foundational strategies. This structure allows for fine-grained evaluation of an agent's reasoning capabilities, from learning basic rules to applying multi-step compositional strategies to solve puzzles of increasing, mathematically validated difficulty. The open-source benchmark provides a challenging new testbed for advancing neuro-symbolic and other advanced AI reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ClassicLogic, a new benchmark suite of four classic logic puzzles (Sudoku, KenKen, Kakuro, Futoshiki) with hierarchical knowledge bases to evaluate agents' compositional reasoning abilities for learning and composing problem-solving strategies.
AI.motivation: Compositional generalization remains a fundamental challenge in AI, with few existing benchmarks. Most current benchmarks focus on linguistic tasks and lack complex, explicit compositional structures needed to evaluate sophisticated reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: The authors create ClassicLogic, a benchmark suite with four classic logic puzzles. They develop hierarchical, explicit knowledge bases for each game where complex solving strategies are formally defined as compositions of simpler foundational strategies. This structure allows fine-grained evaluation of reasoning capabilities across different levels of compositionality.
AI.result: The benchmark provides mathematically validated difficulty levels and a structured testbed for evaluating how well AI systems can learn basic rules and apply multi-step compositional strategies to solve increasingly challenging puzzles.
AI.conclusion: ClassicLogic offers a challenging new testbed for advancing neuro-symbolic and other advanced AI reasoning systems by providing a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate compositional generalization in problem-solving contexts beyond linguistic tasks.

title: A Structural Interpretation of GELU and Threshold-Transmission Activations via the First-Order Loss Function
summary: The Gaussian Error Linear Unit is usually motivated as the expected output of an input-dependent stochastic Bernoulli gate. This work gives a complementary interpretation based on the Gaussian complementary first-order loss function: GELU is the signal-transmission term of the expected surplus of a hard linear gate with a Gaussian random threshold. This view separates loss accounting from forward signal transmission and generalises to a threshold-transmission family that includes ReLU, GELU, SiLU/Swish, and hard swish as special cases. The uniform-threshold case recovers a hard-swish-like compact piecewise-polynomial gate with an explicit threshold-width parameter, yielding fixed- and learned-width variants. Controlled experiments on compact vision and language models show that calibrated or learned uniform-threshold gates are consistently competitive with GELU, ReLU, and SiLU/Swish, improve over them in most tested settings, and use the finite transition region nontrivially.

AI.tldr: GELU is reinterpreted as signal transmission in a hard linear gate with Gaussian threshold, leading to a unified threshold-transmission family that includes ReLU, GELU, SiLU/Swish variants.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to provide a complementary interpretation of GELU activation functions beyond the conventional stochastic Bernoulli gate view, seeking to separate loss accounting from forward signal transmission and generalize activation functions into a unified family.
AI.method: The authors interpret GELU through the Gaussian complementary first-order loss function framework, viewing it as signal-transmission term of expected surplus in a hard linear gate with Gaussian random threshold. They extend this to a threshold-transmission family and develop uniform-threshold variants yielding hard-swish-like piecewise-polynomial gates with explicit threshold-width parameters.
AI.result: Experiments on compact vision and language models show that calibrated or learned uniform-threshold gates are consistently competitive with GELU, ReLU, and SiLU/Swish, improve over them in most tested settings, and make nontrivial use of the finite transition region.
AI.conclusion: The threshold-transmission family provides a unified framework for understanding and designing activation functions, with uniform-threshold variants offering practical alternatives that can outperform established activation functions like GELU, ReLU, and SiLU/Swish across various tasks.

title: Reason, Reward, Refine: Step-Level Errors Corrections with Structured Feedback for Physics Reasoning in Small Language Models
summary: Physics reasoning fails structurally in small language models: an error at any step propagates forward, corrupting every inference that follows. Limited domain knowledge, hallucination under multi-step derivation, and distributional sensitivity compound this failure. We propose a step-level reward framework that identifies the first reasoning error, generates targeted structured feedback, and trains the model to revise its solution via policy gradient with KL regularization, without exposing it to ground truth solutions as generation targets. Unlike annotation-dependent step-level methods, no preference data construction is required and the external verifier operates exclusively at training time. Across five physics benchmarks, our framework delivers accuracy gains of 17-20% over CoT prompting and 10-16% over the strongest baseline, reduces calculation errors from 56.9% to 23.5%, and reduces miscomprehension errors from 22.3% to 12.0% in the best observed cases. Conceptual errors reduce from 89.7% to 68.7%, yet persist as the hardest failure mode across all conditions.

AI.tldr: A step-level reward framework that identifies first reasoning errors in physics problems, provides targeted feedback, and trains models via policy gradient with KL regularization, achieving significant accuracy improvements without ground truth supervision.
AI.motivation: Small language models struggle with physics reasoning due to error propagation where any mistake corrupts subsequent inferences, compounded by limited domain knowledge, hallucination in multi-step derivations, and distributional sensitivity.
AI.method: Step-level reward framework that identifies the first reasoning error, generates structured feedback, and trains models via policy gradient with KL regularization. Unlike annotation-dependent methods, it requires no preference data construction and the external verifier operates only during training without exposing models to ground truth solutions as generation targets.
AI.result: Across five physics benchmarks: 17-20% accuracy gains over CoT prompting, 10-16% over strongest baseline. Reduced calculation errors from 56.9% to 23.5%, miscomprehension errors from 22.3% to 12.0%. Conceptual errors reduced from 89.7% to 68.7%, though these remain the hardest failure mode across all conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed step-level reward framework effectively addresses reasoning error propagation in small language models for physics problems, achieving significant improvements in accuracy and error reduction without requiring ground truth supervision or preference data construction. Conceptual errors remain the most persistent challenge.

title: Validation-Induced Shapley Shifts: How Validation Structure Distorts Data Valuation
summary: Shapley values are widely used to attribute value to training data based on their marginal contribution to performance on a validation set. Existing practice often assumes these values are stable once the training data and model are fixed. In this work, we uncover a systematic vulnerability: even modest changes to the validation set, such as introducing noises, cause directional shifts in Shapley distributions. As noises are added, Shapley values of training samples compress toward zero. We trace this to a noise-induced neighborhood reshuffling effect: perturbations alter the local rank order between validation and training samples, flattening the valuation landscape. Using the KNN-Shapley framework, we show through synthetic and real data that these shifts are consistent and reproducible. Our findings challenge the assumption of Shapley stability and reveal a new axis of fragility in data valuation. We propose normalization and boundary-aware validation strategies to mitigate these distortions and enable more robust, interpretable valuation in machine learning marketplaces.

AI.tldr: Shapley values used for data valuation are vulnerable to validation set noise, causing systematic directional shifts that compress values toward zero due to neighborhood reshuffling effects.
AI.motivation: Current practice assumes Shapley values are stable when training data and model are fixed, but this paper investigates how even modest validation set changes (like noise addition) can systematically distort these valuations.
AI.method: Using the KNN-Shapley framework, the authors analyze how noise-induced perturbations alter local rank orders between validation and training samples, causing neighborhood reshuffling that flattens the valuation landscape. They validate through synthetic and real data experiments.
