AI.motivation: Existing causal discovery methods produce counterintuitive results for multistage processes (common in many fields) and lack computational efficiency for handling large datasets from such processes, creating a gap in effective causal analysis of hierarchical and sequential data structures.
AI.method: OCDM incorporates process knowledge into causal discovery through: 1) a structural knowledge-informed order-inferring algorithm that infers causal order using stage information of variables, and 2) a novel pruning technique using stochastic gated neural networks to eliminate spurious edges from initial causal graphs efficiently.
AI.result: Experiments on various datasets demonstrate that OCDM effectively infers causal structure of multistage processes and outperforms existing methods in both accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: OCDM successfully addresses limitations of traditional causal discovery for multistage processes by explicitly incorporating process knowledge, preserving hierarchical/sequential structure, and providing computational efficiency through innovative pruning techniques.

title: MANCE: Manifold Aware Concept Erasure
summary: Concept erasure aims to remove a target concept from a representation while preserving the other information encoded in it. This is difficult because representations encode many concepts that are often correlated with the erasure target, so removing the target risks damaging them. We propose the Manifold Constraint Hypothesis (MCH): if natural representations concentrate on a structured, lower-dimensional manifold, then interventions should be constrained to that manifold and better preserve other information encoded in the representation during interventions. We instantiate MCH in a new concept erasure method: MANifold aware Concept Erasure (MANCE). MANCE performs iterative updates to the representations using signals from a classifier that predicts a target concept. We estimate the manifold using representations obtained from natural inputs, and then we project the concept removal update to the estimated manifold. We perform extensive evaluation on 119 settings spanning text and vision, including 13 language models, three NLP concepts, and 40 CelebA-CLIP attributes. Employing MANCE on top of previous methods shows consistent improved leakage results. We also introduce MANCE+ and MANCE++, which prepend a closed-form erasure algorithm before employing MANCE, achieving better leakage--surgicality tradeoffs relative to matched full-space updates. MANCE++, our best method, achieves state-of-the-art results on nonlinear concept erasure. These results support MCH in the erasure setting: interventions should be constrained to the natural representation manifold.

AI.tldr: MANCE: A concept erasure method that improves preservation of non-target information by constraining interventions to the natural representation manifold
AI.motivation: Concept erasure methods often damage correlated non-target information when removing target concepts. The paper hypothesizes that constraining interventions to the natural representation manifold can better preserve other information.
AI.method: Proposes MANCE (MANifold aware Concept Erasure) that estimates the representation manifold from natural inputs and projects concept removal updates onto it. Also introduces MANCE+ and MANCE++ with closed-form erasure prepended for better tradeoffs.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation across 119 settings (13 language models, 3 NLP concepts, 40 CelebA-CLIP attributes) shows consistent improved leakage results. MANCE++ achieves SOTA results on nonlinear concept erasure with better leakage-surgicality tradeoffs.
AI.conclusion: The results support the Manifold Constraint Hypothesis: interventions should be constrained to the natural representation manifold to better preserve non-target information during concept erasure.

title: Knowing When to Stop: Predicting Execution-Consistency Convergence in Text-to-SQL
summary: Repeated LLM calls are the standard way to estimate how trustworthy a Text-to-SQL result is: run the pipeline multiple times, judge each SQL execution, and use the consistency of the verdicts as a confidence signal. The open question is when to stop, when the consistency has converged. We formulate this as a convergence-prediction problem and train a family of lightweight 1-D models that observe the running consistency trajectory and decide, at each step, whether further runs are unlikely to shift it materially, and we benchmark them against a principled Beta-Bernoulli stopping rule and a learned run-count baseline. On the BIRD benchmark and two production customer datasets, our method adapts its stopping point to each user question, halting sooner when consistency converges early and continuing longer when it converges late. We further show that the weak serial correlation between runs lets us permute their order as a training augmentation, controlled by a tunable shuffling weight. Performance stays consistent across the three datasets, and to mimic an imperfect production judge we inject noise into the correct/incorrect verdicts obtained by comparing the generated and ground-truth SQL results, showing that the method still predicts convergence reliably.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a lightweight method to predict when to stop repeated LLM calls for Text-to-SQL confidence estimation by observing consistency trajectories, adapting stopping points per query and maintaining reliability even with noisy judgments.
AI.motivation: Current Text-to-SQL systems use repeated LLM calls to estimate result trustworthiness, but there's no principled way to determine when to stop running additional trials. The open question is when consistency has converged and further runs won't materially shift confidence estimates.
AI.method: The authors formulate convergence prediction as a learning problem and train lightweight 1-D models that observe running consistency trajectories to decide whether further runs are needed. They use a Beta-Bernoulli stopping rule baseline and learned run-count baseline for comparison, and employ permutation augmentation with tunable shuffling weight to handle weak serial correlation between runs.
AI.result: The method adapts stopping points per user question on BIRD benchmark and two production datasets, halting sooner when consistency converges early and continuing longer when late convergence. Performance remains consistent across datasets, and the method reliably predicts convergence even with noisy verdicts mimicking imperfect production judges.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight convergence prediction approach provides an adaptive, efficient solution for determining when to stop repeated LLM calls in Text-to-SQL systems, maintaining reliability in realistic production conditions with noisy judgments.

title: Directional Curvature from Armijo Backtracking: A Low-Cost Sharpness Probe and a Calibration-Free Learning-Rate Safeguard for Adam
summary: The local sharpness of the loss, the top Hessian eigenvalue $λ_1$, determines the largest stable gradient step, but measuring it normally requires Lanczos or Hessian-vector iterations. We observe that a single Armijo backtracking line search already carries this information at the cost of a few forward passes: the accepted step $α$ brackets the \emph{directional} curvature $q = g^\top H g/\|g\|^2$ within the multiplicative band set by the backtracking factor. Across CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST and Imagenette, $\logα$ tracks $\logλ_1$ at Pearson $-0.91$ to $-0.95$, giving a low-cost online Edge-of-Stability reading. Used once at initialisation, this measurement yields a learning-rate cap (a safeguard, not a faster optimiser) that makes Adam robust to a too-large initial learning rate across more than three orders of magnitude ($10^{-3}$ to $3.0$), at about one percent overhead, and it is a no-op when the chosen rate is already safe. One probe is enough: periodic in-training probing adds no robust benefit. The raw-gradient probe exposes the mechanism but needs a safety factor calibrated to the architecture by a one-minute divergence sweep. Probing along Adam's own update direction removes this calibration: a single fixed safety factor $κ= 2$ avoids divergence on all nine architectures we test and across the full learning-rate grids of all four benchmarks, and the recipe transfers to AdamW unchanged.

AI.tldr: A simple backtracking line search step size reveals the local loss curvature and top Hessian eigenvalue, enabling cheap Edge-of-Stability monitoring and learning rate caps that make Adam robust to excessively large initial learning rates.
AI.motivation: Measuring the top Hessian eigenvalue (λ₁) to determine stable gradient steps typically requires expensive iterative methods like Lanczos or Hessian-vector products. There's a need for cheaper, online methods to monitor the Edge of Stability during training.
AI.method: Observes that a single Armijo backtracking line search step size (α) brackets the directional curvature q = gᵀHg/‖g‖² within a multiplicative band. Uses this insight to track λ₁ at low cost. Proposes two probes: raw-gradient probe (needs architecture-specific calibration) and Adam's update direction probe (uses fixed safety factor κ=2).
AI.result: logα tracks logλ₁ at Pearson correlation -0.91 to -0.95 across CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST and Imagenette. The method enables learning rate caps that make Adam robust to excessively large initial learning rates (10⁻³ to 3.0) with ~1% overhead. Adam's update direction probe with κ=2 avoids divergence on all 9 tested architectures and transfers to AdamW unchanged.
AI.conclusion: A single backtracking line search provides cheap Edge-of-Stability monitoring. The proposed learning rate cap (not an optimizer) acts as a safeguard against divergence from too-large learning rates, requiring minimal overhead and working robustly across architectures and optimizers.

title: When Does Small Data Work? Accuracy and Efficiency Trade-offs Between Tabular Foundation Models and Conventional Methods for Crowd-State Classification at Hajj and Umrah
summary: Learning from few labeled examples is a central challenge in tabular machine learning, and it becomes the binding constraint in domains where labeling is costly, such as crowd monitoring during Hajj and Umrah. Tabular foundation models, which predict from only a handful of examples without task-specific training, were recently introduced to address this very-few-label regime. In this study we test them on crowd-state classification to assess how much they help when labels are scarce, and we compare them against standard machine learning methods to characterize the accuracy and efficiency trade-offs between the two approaches. Using three real datasets we evaluate different machine learning models, in untuned and tuned forms, against three foundation models. Results show that no single family is best everywhere. The right choice depends on the label budget. When labels are very few, foundation models lead. As labels grow, tuned conventional models catch up and significantly surpass the foundation models on the more structural geometry target. Efficiency separates them further where tuned machine learning models incur a large tuning cost that foundation models avoid, although foundation models reprocess their context at every prediction. We summarize these results as a practical map of which approach to prefer under a given label budget and computational budget.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models outperform traditional ML when labels are extremely scarce for crowd-state classification, but tuned conventional models catch up as labels increase, with trade-offs in computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Learning from few labeled examples is a major challenge in tabular ML, especially in domains like crowd monitoring during Hajj and Umrah where labeling is costly. Tabular foundation models were recently introduced to address the very-few-label regime, but their practical utility needs assessment against standard methods.
AI.method: Evaluated three real datasets for crowd-state classification, comparing different ML models (both untuned and tuned) against three foundation models. Assessed performance across varying label budgets and examined efficiency trade-offs between approaches.
AI.result: No single model family is universally best. Foundation models lead when labels are very few. As labels increase, tuned conventional models catch up and significantly surpass foundation models on structural geometry tasks. Foundation models avoid tuning costs but reprocess context at every prediction.
AI.conclusion: The choice between tabular foundation models and traditional ML depends on label budget and computational constraints. Provides a practical map for selecting the right approach based on available labels and computational resources, highlighting that foundation models excel in extreme label scarcity while tuned models dominate with sufficient labels.

title: A Unified Algebraic Framework for Classification Performance Evaluation
summary: We propose a unified algebraic framework for classification performance evaluation that encompasses binary, multiclass, multilabel, ordinal, hierarchical, cost-sensitive, and soft-label settings within a single formalism. The foundation is a representation of actual and predicted labels as binary indicator matrices, combined with three aggregation operators -- global, column-wise, and row-wise -- that correspond exactly to micro, macro/weighted, and exemplar averaging. Any binary performance measure expressed in terms of true/positive/negative counts extends automatically to all settings by substituting these operators, generating multiclass and multilabel versions without measure-specific derivations. The framework further accommodates soft classifier outputs via argmax or thresholding, soft ground truth via triangular norms, ordinal classification via membership functions or cumulative encodings, and cost-sensitive evaluation via a cost matrix that subsumes MAE and MSE as special cases. We establish several theoretical results: micro-averaging equals denominator-weighted macro-averaging; the product $t$-norm is the unique one preserving the confusion-matrix partition; skew-invariant measures are characterised as functions of recall and specificity; and micro-precision, micro-recall, and micro-$F_1$ are all equal to accuracy in multiclass settings. Empirical illustrations on synthetic and real data confirm the theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: A unified algebraic framework for classification performance evaluation that extends binary metrics to multiclass, multilabel, ordinal, hierarchical, cost-sensitive, and soft-label settings through matrix representations and aggregation operators.
AI.motivation: To create a single formalism that unifies performance evaluation across diverse classification settings (binary, multiclass, multilabel, ordinal, hierarchical, cost-sensitive, soft-label), eliminating the need for measure-specific derivations for each setting.
AI.method: Uses binary indicator matrices for actual and predicted labels with three aggregation operators (global, column-wise, row-wise) corresponding to micro, macro/weighted, and exemplar averaging. Framework extends any binary performance measure by substituting these operators, accommodating soft outputs via argmax/thresholding, soft ground truth via triangular norms, ordinal via membership functions, and cost-sensitive via cost matrices.
AI.result: Established theoretical results: micro-averaging equals denominator-weighted macro-averaging; product t-norm uniquely preserves confusion-matrix partition; skew-invariant measures characterized as functions of recall/specificity; micro-precision, micro-recall, micro-F1 equal accuracy in multiclass. Empirical validation on synthetic/real data confirms findings.
AI.conclusion: Provides a comprehensive, unified algebraic framework that simplifies performance evaluation across all classification settings, eliminating redundant derivations and offering theoretical insights into relationships between different averaging methods and metric properties.

title: OmniOpt: Taxonomy, Geometry, and Benchmarking of Modern Optimizers
summary: Optimizer selection for large-scale model training has become a system-level design decision constrained jointly by compute, memory, tuning budget, and task diversity, yet the landscape of over one hundred methods remains fragmented. We therefore present OmniOpt, a unified survey and benchmark cookbook of optimizers for the research community. OmniOpt rests on four coupled components. First, we treat every optimizer update as a structured transformation through a five-stage meta-pipeline, and show that most methods engage only one or two of these stages. Second, we use norm-constrained linear minimization oracles (LMOs) to unify different optimizers. Third, these two views ground a dual-dimension taxonomy, one dimension assigning each method to a mechanism family and the other recording the measurable training objectives it aims to improve. Fourth, and at the core of this paper, we instantiate the full taxonomy in a unified cross-domain benchmark spanning representative optimizers, model scales, and training regimes from language model pretraining to image classification, systematically analyzing each method family across multiple effect objectives and laying out their trade-offs. OmniOpt thus supplies the research community with an operational coordinate system for selecting optimizers under explicit mechanism and objective assumptions, and charts a direction for the future development of the optimizer community.

AI.tldr: OmniOpt provides a unified framework for understanding optimizer selection in large-scale training by creating a dual-dimension taxonomy and comprehensive benchmark to analyze trade-offs across different optimizer families.
AI.motivation: The optimizer landscape for large-scale model training is fragmented with over 100 methods, making selection a complex system-level decision constrained by compute, memory, tuning budget, and task diversity. Researchers need a unified framework to navigate this complexity.
AI.method: Four key components: (1) modeling optimizer updates as a five-stage meta-pipeline, (2) unifying different optimizers using norm-constrained linear minimization oracles (LMOs), (3) creating a dual-dimension taxonomy classifying methods by mechanism family and measurable objectives, (4) instantiating a comprehensive cross-domain benchmark spanning various optimizers, model scales, and training regimes.
AI.result: OmniOpt creates an operational coordinate system for selecting optimizers based on explicit mechanism and objective assumptions, systematically analyzing each method family across multiple effect objectives and laying out their trade-offs through extensive benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: OmniOpt provides the research community with a unified framework and benchmark cookbook for navigating the fragmented optimizer landscape, enabling better optimizer selection decisions and charting directions for future optimizer development.

title: Reward-Gated On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation is a powerful way to transfer reasoning ability from a strong teacher to a smaller student: the student samples trajectories from its own policy, and the teacher provides dense token-level supervision on the states the student actually visits. However, this supervision is not always reliable: a teacher can assign high likelihood to plausible but incorrect solutions, or low likelihood to correct student solutions that follow different reasoning paths. Unconditionally distilling the teacher can therefore reinforce bad modes or erase useful student behavior. To address these limitations, we introduce RG-OPD: Reward-Gated On-Policy Distillation that uses verifier feedback to decide when teacher logits should be trusted. RG-OPD bridges sparse verifier rewards and dense teacher logits, preserving token-level supervision while filtering misleading teacher signals. Across reasoning and coding benchmarks, RG-OPD produces stronger distilled students, outperforming both vanilla reverse-KL distillation and the recent TSD-KD baseline. At 1K generation length, RG-OPD improves over reverse-KL by 2.9 points and over TSD-KD by 4.9 points; in the long-generation setting, it improves over the untuned student by 8.2 points. Our code is available at https://github.com/UoC-tail/RG-OPD.

AI.tldr: RG-OPD improves on-policy distillation by using verifier feedback to gate teacher supervision, filtering unreliable teacher signals while preserving useful token-level guidance.
AI.motivation: Standard on-policy distillation has limitations because teacher supervision is not always reliable - teachers can assign high likelihood to plausible but incorrect solutions, or low likelihood to correct solutions that follow different reasoning paths. This leads to reinforcing bad modes or erasing useful student behavior.
AI.method: RG-OPD (Reward-Gated On-Policy Distillation) uses verifier feedback to decide when teacher logits should be trusted. It bridges sparse verifier rewards with dense teacher logits, preserving token-level supervision while filtering misleading teacher signals.
AI.result: Across reasoning and coding benchmarks, RG-OPD produces stronger distilled students than both vanilla reverse-KL distillation and the TSD-KD baseline. At 1K generation length, it improves over reverse-KL by 2.9 points and over TSD-KD by 4.9 points; in long-generation settings, it improves over the untuned student by 8.2 points.
AI.conclusion: Reward-gating teacher supervision in on-policy distillation effectively filters unreliable teacher signals while preserving useful guidance, leading to significant improvements in student model performance across various reasoning tasks.

title: A Unified Framework for In-Context Learning with Causal and Masked Language Models
summary: In-context learning (ICL) has emerged as a central capability of pretrained language models, yet its theoretical analysis has focused primarily on causal language models trained by left-to-right autoregressive prediction, such as GPT-style models. Masked language models instead recover masked tokens from bidirectional context, and their role in ICL remains less understood. We develop a statistical learning framework that represents the context examples by their empirical measure and models prediction as a function of the context and the query. This formulation places autoregressive and masked pretraining objectives within a common excess-risk analysis. Under Wasserstein-type regularity conditions, we relate pretraining with T tasks and N samples per task to k-shot excess risk at inference, obtaining same-order upper bounds for masked and autoregressive objectives. We also study task-distribution shift, where pretraining tasks are sampled from P and inference tasks from Q; the resulting bound contains an additional term controlled by the lifted Wasserstein distance between P and Q. The bounds further imply an order-optimal allocation under a fixed pretraining data budget and refined rates under intrinsic low-dimensional structure. Experiments on controlled function-learning tasks show that the Masked Pair Encoder (MPE) can achieve performance comparable to GPT-2-style causal Transformers, suggesting that ICL behavior is not specific to causal language models.

AI.tldr: Masked language models can achieve similar in-context learning performance to causal models like GPT-2, with theoretical bounds showing both approaches have comparable excess risk under statistical learning framework.
AI.motivation: While ICL has become central to language models, theoretical analysis has focused mostly on causal (GPT-style) models. The role of masked language models in ICL remains poorly understood, despite their bidirectional context utilization differing from autoregressive approaches.
AI.method: Developed a statistical learning framework representing context examples by empirical measure, modeling prediction as function of context and query. Formulated both autoregressive and masked pretraining objectives within common excess-risk analysis. Provided theoretical bounds under Wasserstein-type regularity conditions for k-shot learning. Also studied task-distribution shift scenarios. Conducted experiments with Masked Pair Encoder (MPE) on controlled function-learning tasks.
AI.result: Obtained same-order upper bounds for both masked and autoregressive objectives under fixed pretraining data budgets. Distribution shift analysis showed additional term controlled by lifted Wasserstein distance between training and inference task distributions. Experiments showed MPE achieves comparable performance to GPT-2-style Transformers on ICL tasks.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning behavior is not specific to causal language models - masked language models can achieve similar ICL performance. The developed statistical framework provides unified analysis showing both approaches share fundamental learning properties and theoretical guarantees.

title: FedSPM: Routing-Enabled Federated Learning under Dual Heterogeneity via Semiparametric Mixture
summary: Routing-prediction federated learning has emerged as a new paradigm that reframes inter-client heterogeneity as a resource for system-level intelligence: at inference time, the server routes each external query to the best-matched client for prediction. Existing approaches, however, typically treat each client as internally homogeneous, overlooking latent subpopulations within local data. For example, patients with the same diagnosis at one hospital may exhibit morphologically distinct disease subtypes. The coexistence of inter-client and intra-client heterogeneity, which we call dual heterogeneity, can impair both routing and prediction. To address this challenge, we propose FedSPM, a routing-enabled semiparametric mixture framework that represents each client using client-specific latent components. Each component combines a predictive distribution for classification with a feature distribution for routing. To flexibly model feature distributions while effectively sharing information across clients, FedSPM models their density ratios relative to a common nonparametric measure estimated via empirical likelihood. We develop a federated expectation-maximization algorithm that optimizes a tractable surrogate and prove convergence of the exact profiled objective at the standard $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ rate when the surrogate errors are properly controlled. Experiments on controlled benchmarks and real-world medical data demonstrate consistent improvements in routing and prediction under dual heterogeneity. Code is available at https://github.com/zijianwang0510/FedSPM.

AI.tldr: FedSPM is a routing-prediction federated learning framework that addresses dual heterogeneity (both inter-client and intra-client heterogeneity) by modeling clients as mixtures of latent components with federated expectation-maximization.
AI.motivation: Existing routing-prediction federated learning approaches treat each client as internally homogeneous, overlooking latent subpopulations within local data. This dual heterogeneity (inter-client and intra-client) can impair both routing and prediction performance, especially in applications like medical data where patients with the same diagnosis may have distinct disease subtypes.
AI.method: Proposes FedSPM, a semiparametric mixture framework representing each client using client-specific latent components. Each component combines a predictive distribution for classification with a feature distribution for routing. Feature distributions are modeled via density ratios relative to a common nonparametric measure estimated via empirical likelihood. A federated expectation-maximization algorithm optimizes a tractable surrogate objective.
AI.result: Experiments on controlled benchmarks and real-world medical data demonstrate consistent improvements in routing and prediction under dual heterogeneity. The algorithm provably converges at standard O(1/√T) rate when surrogate errors are properly controlled.
AI.conclusion: FedSPM effectively addresses the challenge of dual heterogeneity in routing-prediction federated learning by modeling client-specific latent components, achieving better performance than approaches that treat clients as homogeneous entities.

title: Target-Aware Interaction-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Black-Box Node Injection Attacks on Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable performance in graph representation learning, yet their inherent vulnerability to adversarial attacks poses severe security risks. Especially, black-box node injection attacks have become a major threat to GNNs since they inject malicious nodes without altering the original graph topology. However, they typically decouple the generation of malicious node features and edge connections, thereby resulting in suboptimal attack efficacy under stringent budgets. To address this critical issue, this study proposes a novel Target-aware Interaction-guided Reinforcement learning for Black-box node injection Attacks on GNNs (TIRBA), which formulates the attack as a Markov Decision Process and jointly optimizes node feature generation and edge construction in a heterogeneous action space. Firstly, TIRBA designs a target-aware interaction encoder to fuse information of node features and edges. Further, it introduces a class-center guidance mechanism to utilize prior class distribution information, thereby guiding efficient exploration of the high-dimensional feature space. Finally, a topology difference-aware state value evaluation is adopted to explicitly capture local structural anomalies caused by injected nodes, thereby stabilizing the reinforcement learning training process. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed TIRBA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art black-box node injection attack methods.

AI.tldr: TIRBA is a reinforcement learning-based black-box node injection attack on GNNs that jointly optimizes node features and edges using target-aware interaction encoding and class-center guidance.
AI.motivation: Current black-box node injection attacks on GNNs typically decouple node feature generation and edge construction, leading to suboptimal attack performance under limited budgets. There's a need for a method that can jointly optimize both aspects to improve attack efficacy while working within black-box constraints.
AI.method: The paper proposes TIRBA (Target-aware Interaction-guided Reinforcement learning for Black-box node injection Attacks), which formulates the attack as a Markov Decision Process with three key components: 1) target-aware interaction encoder to fuse node feature and edge information, 2) class-center guidance mechanism to leverage prior class distribution for efficient feature space exploration, and 3) topology difference-aware state value evaluation to capture local structural anomalies and stabilize RL training.
AI.result: Experimental results show that TIRBA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art black-box node injection attack methods, demonstrating improved attack efficacy under stringent attack budgets.
AI.conclusion: TIRBA represents an effective approach for black-box node injection attacks on GNNs by jointly optimizing node features and edges through reinforcement learning, addressing the limitations of decoupled attack strategies while maintaining the constraints of black-box settings.

title: Dictionaries, Not Darwin: Set-Level Selection Beats LLM Evolution in Scientific Equation Discovery
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as evolutionary engines for scientific discovery: generate candidates, select winners, feed them back as parents, and repeat. We audit whether this loop actually compounds discovery in scientific equation discovery, a setting where finite samples make structure underdetermined and interpolation easy. Under matched LLM-call budgets, parent-conditioned evolution is indistinguishable from fresh independent sampling: median OOD NMSE is 0.045 vs. 0.049, instructed multi-parent crossover is worse, final success is predicted by initial proposal quality, and multiple iteration schemes fail to add solved problems. Operationally, the loop reduces to what it produces: a dictionary of candidate terms. We turn that diagnosis into PTB-Search, a one-generation method for componentized scientific discovery. PTB-Search samples independent LLM proposals once, extracts reusable terms into a per-problem dictionary, and performs train-only set-level sparse selection with least-squares coefficients. Its central principle is that underdetermined data identifies the joint behavior of term sets, not reliable per-term credit. On identical dictionaries and zero additional LLM calls, set-level selectors solve 165--169 of 717 cells, while single-term reductions solve only 74--78. On the official 239-problem LLM-SRBench split, PTB-Search reaches 73.2% Acc0.1 with Llama-3.1-8B and 77.0% with a single-seed DeepSeek-V4 anchor, versus 49.2% for the best reported baseline, using one tenth of the standardized call budget. A program-domain stress test gives a scoped boundary: generation count remains unreliable, while retained external state can help in harder non-linear spaces. Across these results, LLMs are best understood as material suppliers; discovery is carried by external set-level selection over reusable components.

AI.tldr: LLM-based evolutionary loops for scientific equation discovery don't compound knowledge but reduce to producing candidate dictionaries; PTB-Search uses one-generation sampling + set-level sparse selection to outperform evolutionary approaches with far fewer LLM calls.
AI.motivation: To audit whether LLM-based evolutionary loops actually compound scientific discovery in equation discovery settings, where structure is underdetermined by finite samples and interpolation is easy. The researchers wanted to test if parent-conditioned evolution genuinely improves discovery over independent sampling.
AI.method: The paper introduces PTB-Search: a one-generation method that samples independent LLM proposals once, extracts reusable terms into a per-problem dictionary, and performs train-only set-level sparse selection with least-squares coefficients. The key insight is that underdetermined data identifies joint behavior of term sets, not reliable per-term credit.
AI.result: Parent-conditioned evolution is indistinguishable from fresh independent sampling; set-level selectors solve 165-169/717 cells vs. 74-78 for single-term reductions; PTB-Search achieves 73.2-77.0% Acc0.1 vs 49.2% baseline, using 1/10 the call budget; LLMs function best as material suppliers rather than evolutionary engines.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based evolutionary loops don't compound discovery in scientific equation discovery - they essentially just produce dictionaries of candidate terms. The PTB-Search method demonstrates that discovery is better achieved through external set-level selection over reusable components, with LLMs serving primarily as material suppliers.

title: DynaVieW: Schema-Guided World Modeling for Understanding Hierarchical Visual Dynamics
summary: Multimodal LLMs struggle to systematically model the temporal evolution of visual scenes in videos or multi-image sequences. Such inputs require models to predict or simulate multiple levels of dynamic constituents, such as actions taken in the visual sequence, and the associated changes to the visual environment that result. To address this challenge, we propose a dynamic schema-guided world model, DynaVieW, optimized for visual dynamic prediction and simulation. DynaVieW achieves an in-depth understanding of visual dynamics by learning interleaved state-transition sequences, where states cover broad visual scenes from video keyframes, and transitions capture comprehensive dynamic constituents within a hierarchical schema. DynaVieW jointly models transition prediction and state simulation under a mixture-of-experts architecture, with a cross-expert selective attention and a schema token re-weighted loss, to ensure effective and robust learning. DynaVieW's understanding of visual dynamics boosts its downstream performance in visual narrative creation and world simulation, showing improved consistency, controllability, and instruction-following.

AI.tldr: DynaVieW: A dynamic schema-guided world model for visual dynamic prediction and simulation that learns interleaved state-transition sequences to better understand visual dynamics in videos and multi-image sequences.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs struggle to systematically model the temporal evolution of visual scenes in videos or multi-image sequences, particularly in predicting/simulating multiple levels of dynamic constituents like actions and their resulting visual changes.
AI.method: Proposes DynaVieW with hierarchical schema that learns interleaved state-transition sequences (states from video keyframes, transitions capture dynamic constituents). Uses mixture-of-experts architecture with cross-expert selective attention and schema token re-weighted loss for joint transition prediction and state simulation.
AI.result: DynaVieW achieves improved understanding of visual dynamics, boosting downstream performance in visual narrative creation and world simulation with better consistency, controllability, and instruction-following.
AI.conclusion: DynaVieW addresses key limitations in multimodal LLMs for temporal visual understanding by learning structured dynamic representations through hierarchical schema, enabling more effective visual dynamic prediction and simulation.

title: Asymptotic-Preserving A Posteriori Analysis of Diffusion and Flow-Matching Samplers
summary: Diffusion and flow-matching samplers integrate a learned probability-flow ODE from a large noise scale down to a small terminal floor $σ_{\min}$, at which the score is stiff and the flow develops a boundary layer. We treat $σ_{\min}$ as a singular-perturbation parameter and determine which fixed-step samplers are asymptotic-preserving (AP), that is, stable and uniformly accurate as $σ_{\min}\to0$, casting the criteria as an a posteriori audit: residual functionals with $σ_{\min}$-uniform coefficients, computable on a pretrained checkpoint without ground-truth scores or exact trajectories. On the terminal layer, Euler in the $σ$-clock, the deterministic DDIM update, is the unique layer-exact discretization up to affine reparameterization, with rectified flow its flow-matching counterpart; the $λ$-clock is stable only for steps $h\le h_\star=1+W(1/e)$, and the uniform-$σ^2$ heat clock stalls a $σ_{\min}$-independent distance from the data. On two solvable models (rank-deficient Gaussian, symmetric two-point mixture), deterministic samplers remain first-order uniformly accurate with no $\log(1/σ_{\min})$ factor, even across a symmetric posterior-switching interface whose distributional budget is a universal constant; the logarithm is charged entirely to the Itô term of stochastic samplers, whose path-KL scales as $Λ^2/N$ against the ODE's $O(Λ^2/N^2)$ budget, with $Λ=\log(σ_{\max}/σ_{\min})$. On the EDM CIFAR-10 checkpoint, spectra measured once predict held-out residual budgets across step count, schedule, and noise level against pre-specified gates with no per-configuration refitting, and calibrate the Itô coefficient at $M_1=1.00\pm0.01$. The clock decides stability; the noise, not the geometry, charges the logarithm.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes asymptotic-preserving samplers for diffusion models, showing that deterministic Euler/DDIM updates are optimal for terminal layers, while stochastic samplers incur logarithmic costs due to Itô terms.
AI.motivation: Diffusion samplers encounter numerical stiffness when approaching small terminal noise scales (σ_min), creating boundary layers that challenge stability and accuracy of fixed-step methods as σ_min → 0.
AI.method: Treats σ_min as singular-perturbation parameter; develops a posteriori audit criteria using residual functionals with σ_min-uniform coefficients; analyzes deterministic and stochastic samplers across different time parametrizations (σ-clock, λ-clock, heat clock).
AI.result: Euler in σ-clock (DDIM) is unique layer-exact discretization; deterministic samplers achieve first-order uniform accuracy without log(1/σ_min) factor; stochastic samplers incur logarithmic costs; validation on EDM CIFAR-10 confirms predictions and calibrates Itô coefficient.
AI.conclusion: Clock choice determines stability; logarithmic costs originate from noise (Itô terms) not geometry; deterministic Euler/DDIM updates are asymptotic-preserving for terminal boundary layers in diffusion sampling.

title: GlacierCastAI: Predicting Glacier Retreat from Multi-Modal Satellite Imagery and Climate Signals
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Code available at GitHub
summary: ERA5 seasonal climate variables contain predictive information about future glacier retreat beyond what satellite imagery alone provides, yet existing deep learning methods focus on mapping current boundaries rather than forecasting future ones. This paper presents GlacierCastAI, which reframes glacier boundary prediction as a multi-modal spatiotemporal forecasting problem, fusing multi-temporal Landsat imagery with ERA5 reanalysis climate variables and Copernicus DEM terrain features to forecast glacier boundaries across five glaciers spanning four climate regimes. The architecture couples a ResNet50 spatial encoder with a ConvLSTM temporal model and a cross-attention climate fusion module. Because forecasting is inherently more uncertain than mapping current boundaries, the reported IoU values (0.320-0.337) are not directly comparable to state-of-the-art mapping models. Comparisons are against traditional baselines and experimental conditions. Through a pre-registered ablation study, adding ERA5 climate signals improves image-only IoU from 0.326 to 0.337 (+3.4%), suggesting that atmospheric forcing carries predictive information beyond imagery alone. All deep learning models substantially outperform persistence and linear trend baselines (IoU 0.160 and 0.169 respectively), with improvements of 89-99% relative IoU. A lightweight climate-only MLP baseline (661K parameters) achieves an IoU of 0.320 (98% of image-only performance) using 85x fewer parameters, suggesting that ERA5 variables encode substantial predictive signal independently of satellite imagery. SHAP attribution analysis suggests that spring solar radiation (MAM) is the dominant climate driver, consistent with the known role of spring insolation in setting melt season trajectories.

AI.tldr: GlacierCastAI is a multi-modal forecasting model that combines satellite imagery with climate data (ERA5) and terrain features to predict future glacier boundaries, showing that climate variables provide significant predictive information beyond imagery alone.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods focus on mapping current glacier boundaries rather than forecasting future ones, even though ERA5 seasonal climate variables contain predictive information about future glacier retreat beyond what satellite imagery alone provides.
AI.method: The authors reframe glacier boundary prediction as a multi-modal spatiotemporal forecasting problem, fusing multi-temporal Landsat imagery with ERA5 reanalysis climate variables and Copernicus DEM terrain features. The architecture couples a ResNet50 spatial encoder with a ConvLSTM temporal model and a cross-attention climate fusion module.
AI.result: Adding ERA5 climate signals improves image-only IoU from 0.326 to 0.337 (+3.4%), showing atmospheric forcing carries predictive information beyond imagery. All deep learning models substantially outperform persistence and linear trend baselines (IoU 0.160 and 0.169). A lightweight climate-only MLP baseline achieves 0.320 IoU using 85x fewer parameters. SHAP analysis shows spring solar radiation is the dominant climate driver.
AI.conclusion: GlacierCastAI successfully demonstrates that climate variables provide substantial predictive information for glacier boundary forecasting, with ERA5 variables encoding significant predictive signal independently of satellite imagery. The framework enables forecasting future glacier boundaries rather than just mapping current ones.

title: Parametric Memory Decoding for Zero-Shot Routing in LoRA-Based External Parametric Memory
summary: With the rise of parametric memory, LoRA-based External Parametric Memory (EPM) has emerged as a modular solution, but existing routing methods often introduce additional training, deployment, and maintenance overhead. This raises a natural question: can a LoRA-based EPM bank be routed without maintaining an additional routing component? However, existing zero-shot LoRA routing methods still face two problems under the EPM setting: (1) their evaluations are scattered across different task settings rather than organized around EPM access, and (2) their routing signals lack a unified perspective to guide systematic improvement. To address these problems, we organize PMD-Bench, covering document-level, domain-level knowledge, and task-skill, and propose Parametric Memory Decoding (PMD), the first framework designed to systematically improve zero-shot LoRA routing by reframing it as decoding activations over external parametric memory. Based on PMD, we further instantiate PMDRouter, which scores each LoRA by its response magnitude from a single base-model prefill. Experiments on PMD-Bench show that PMDRouter achieves the strongest internal-signal performance across multiple zero-shot routing settings. These results demonstrate the feasibility of zero-shot LoRA routing and suggest that PMD can serve as a general framework for improving zero-shot routing methods. Sources: Github (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Parametric-Memory-Decoding-872A/)

AI.tldr: PMD-Bench benchmark and PMD framework for zero-shot LoRA routing without additional routing components, enabling efficient external parametric memory access.
AI.motivation: Existing LoRA-based External Parametric Memory (EPM) routing methods introduce training/deployment overhead. Need zero-shot routing without maintaining additional components, but current methods lack unified evaluation and systematic improvement framework.
AI.method: Propose Parametric Memory Decoding (PMD) framework reframing routing as decoding activations over external memory. Instantiate PMDRouter that scores LoRAs by response magnitude from single base-model prefill. Create PMD-Bench benchmark covering document, domain, and task-skill settings.
AI.result: PMDRouter achieves strongest internal-signal performance across multiple zero-shot routing settings on PMD-Bench, demonstrating feasibility of zero-shot LoRA routing.
AI.conclusion: PMD provides general framework for improving zero-shot routing methods, enabling efficient EPM access without additional routing components, with PMDRouter showing practical viability.

title: CertMix: Certified, Data-Efficient Metamaterial Design by Affine Mixing of Aligned Neural-Implicit Weight Spaces
summary: Inverse design of mechanical metamaterials seeks a periodic unit cell whose homogenized elastic properties meet a prescribed target, but current learning-based methods are data-hungry, mostly interpolative, and provide no guarantee that the generated design satisfies the specification. We introduce CertMix, a data-efficient framework that represents each exemplar unit cell as a small periodic neural implicit field, specifically a SIREN signed-distance decoder overfit from a shared anchor, so that exemplar weight vectors become aligned and directly comparable. The key observation is that, in this aligned weight space, the homogenized elasticity tensor is approximately linear in the mixing coefficients. Targeted design therefore reduces to a small constrained affine-mixing problem solved with a differentiable periodic homogenizer in the loop. Negative coefficients enable extrapolation beyond the exemplar range, a linearity-mismatch trust region keeps blends valid, and split-conformal calibration converts the mismatch signal into a distribution-free certificate on achieved-property error. From as few as 50 exemplars, CertMix attains a scaled property error of $10^{-4}$, roughly two to three orders of magnitude below conditional generative baselines trained on 1000 cells. It remains accurate far outside the exemplar range, is $57\times$ faster than per-target topology optimization while avoiding checkerboards and enclosed voids, and extends to spatially graded fields, 3D triply periodic surfaces, and a certified running-shoe midsole application.

AI.tldr: CertMix is a data-efficient framework for inverse design of mechanical metamaterials that represents unit cells as aligned periodic neural implicit fields, enabling targeted design through constrained affine mixing with certification guarantees.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based methods for inverse design of mechanical metamaterials are data-hungry, mostly interpolative, and provide no guarantee that generated designs satisfy target specifications.
AI.method: 1) Represent exemplar unit cells as small periodic neural implicit fields (SIREN signed-distance decoders overfit from shared anchor), aligning weight vectors for comparability. 2) Key observation: homogenized elasticity tensor is approximately linear in mixing coefficients in aligned weight space. 3) Targeted design reduces to constrained affine-mixing problem solved with differentiable periodic homogenizer. 4) Incorporate negative coefficients for extrapolation, linearity-mismatch trust region for validity, and split-conformal calibration for error certification.
AI.result: From only 50 exemplars, CertMix achieves scaled property error of 10^-4 (2-3 orders better than conditional generative baselines trained on 1000 cells). It's 57× faster than per-target topology optimization, avoids checkerboards/enclosed voids, works far outside exemplar range, and extends to graded fields, 3D surfaces, and certified running-shoe midsoles.
AI.conclusion: CertMix provides a highly data-efficient, accurate, and certifiable framework for mechanical metamaterial design that outperforms existing methods in accuracy, speed, and reliability while enabling guaranteed specification satisfaction.

title: MDL Meets Latent Confounders: LNML-based Causal Discovery
summary: Causal discovery with nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders remains challenging. Existing methods often rely on either linear assumptions or causal sufficiency, limiting their applicability. We propose an MDL-based causal discovery framework that explicitly accounts for latent confounders while allowing flexible nonlinear mechanisms by minimizing the luckiness normalized maximum likelihood (LNML) code-length. The causal relationship between each variable pair is determined by selecting the shortest code-length of the causal model, and we introduce the notion of $Δ$-pseudo-collinearity to identify dependencies induced by latent confounders. Based on these ideas, we develop a greedy algorithm, termed Pseudo-Collinearity Guided Causal Discovery (PCG-CD). Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method accurately recovers directed causal relationships and effectively detects latent confounders.

AI.tldr: A causal discovery method using MDL/LNML coding to handle nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders via Δ-pseudo-collinearity detection and greedy search.
AI.motivation: Existing causal discovery methods struggle with nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders - most rely on linear assumptions or causal sufficiency, limiting real-world applicability.
AI.method: Proposes MDL-based framework minimizing luckiness normalized maximum likelihood (LNML) code-length to select causal models. Introduces Δ-pseudo-collinearity to detect latent confounder dependencies, and develops greedy PCG-CD algorithm.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real datasets show accurate recovery of directed causal relationships and effective detection of latent confounders.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses challenges in causal discovery with nonlinear mechanisms and latent confounders using MDL principles and pseudo-collinearity detection.

title: Masked Generative-Contrastive Representation Learning for Cross-Dataset EEG-Based Emotion Recognition
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) shows strong potential for cross-dataset transfer by improving feature representation and generalization. However, its application to EEG-based emotion recognition remains largely unexplored. Existing SSL methods struggle to capture the intricate spatiotemporal dependencies of EEG signals under varying channel configurations, extract fine-grained representations resilient to noise, and derive global features that generalize well across subjects. To address these challenges, we propose Masked Generative-Contrastive Representation Learning (MGCRL), a novel SSL framework specifically designed for EEG-based emotion recognition. Built upon a region-aware spatiotemporal encoder, MGCRL integrates generative and contrastive learning to achieve both fine-grained and global discriminative representations for cross-dataset generalization. MGCRL introduces three key designs: 1) a spatiotemporal encoder that incorporates region-based graph convolution to capture localized spatial and functional relationships, enhancing region-specific feature learning and mitigating the impact of varying EEG channel configurations across datasets; 2) a generative learning mechanism based on the joint embedding predictive architecture (JEPA) that utilizes masked features to capture noise robustness fine-grained representations, improving the model's capability to characterize subtle emotional states; and 3) a contrastive learning strategy that leverages masked and original features to learn temporally stable and cross-subject-invariant representations across the same stimuli, boosting emotion discrimination and cross-subject generalization. Under these designs, MGCRL exhibits remarkable ability to learn universal representation. Extensive experiments involving pretraining on the large FACED dataset and fine-tuning on multiple SEED-series datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of MGCRL.

AI.tldr: MGCRL is a self-supervised learning framework for EEG-based emotion recognition that combines generative and contrastive learning to learn universal representations that generalize across datasets and subjects.
AI.motivation: Current SSL methods fail to adequately capture EEG's spatiotemporal dependencies across varying channel configurations, extract noise-resilient fine-grained features, and learn cross-subject generalizable global features for emotion recognition.
AI.method: Masked Generative-Contrastive Representation Learning (MGCRL) with three key designs: 1) region-aware spatiotemporal encoder with graph convolution for localized spatial/functional relationships, 2) generative learning using JEPA architecture with masked features for noise robustness, and 3) contrastive learning with masked/original features for temporal stability and cross-subject invariance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show MGCRL's effectiveness with pretraining on FACED dataset and fine-tuning on multiple SEED-series datasets, demonstrating remarkable ability to learn universal EEG representations for emotion recognition.
AI.conclusion: MGCRL successfully addresses key challenges in EEG-based emotion recognition by integrating generative and contrastive SSL approaches, enabling robust cross-dataset and cross-subject generalization through its specialized spatiotemporal architecture.

title: Binary Iterative Method for Non-targeted Adversarial Attack
summary: Adversarial attacks guide and provide additional training and test data for both adversarial training and adversarial robustness validation, and expose the 'piecewise linearity' of deep learning based models. Since adversarial attacks and adversarial robustness are mathematically defined problems that can be optimised directly with end-to-end differentiable search, adversarial robustness is more widely applicable than other robustness metrics such as corruption and perturbation robustness, and new kinds of adversarial attacks are beneficial for robustness testing. Attacks are targeted or non-targeted depending on whether the image is modified to misclassify to a particular class or to any incorrect class; we focus on the non-targeted setting. Finding the optimal input data points and hyper-parameters for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks remains a challenge for current methods like the Fast Gradient Method, Basic Iterative Method and Virtual Adversarial Method. We propose a new method, the "Binary Iterative Method" (BinIM), which uses a divide-and-conquer paradigm to optimise parameters and hyper-parameters for the generation of non-targeted attacks. We compare our method to other gradient-based adversarial attacks evaluated over pre-trained networks (InceptionV3, InceptionV2, ResNet V2 152) on classification tasks. On 1000 randomly-sampled images from the standard ImageNet dataset, the Binary Iterative Method outperforms all other gradient-based methods, qualitatively making the classifier misclassify with confidence up to 0.995 while reducing the probability of the true label to 2.21e-09 (approximately 0).

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new Binary Iterative Method (BinIM) for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks, which outperforms existing gradient-based methods by using a divide-and-conquer approach to optimize parameters and hyperparameters.
AI.motivation: Current methods for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks (like Fast Gradient Method, Basic Iterative Method, Virtual Adversarial Method) struggle to find optimal input data points and hyperparameters. Adversarial attacks are important for both adversarial training and robustness validation, exposing the 'piecewise linearity' of deep learning models.
AI.method: Proposes Binary Iterative Method (BinIM) which uses a divide-and-conquer paradigm to optimize parameters and hyper-parameters for generating non-targeted adversarial attacks. The method focuses on the non-targeted setting where images are modified to misclassify to any incorrect class.
AI.result: BinIM outperforms other gradient-based methods (FGM, BIM, VAM) when evaluated on pre-trained networks (InceptionV3, InceptionV2, ResNet V2 152) using 1000 randomly-sampled ImageNet images. It achieves classifier misclassification confidence up to 0.995 while reducing true label probability to approximately 0 (2.21e-09).
AI.conclusion: The Binary Iterative Method represents an effective new approach for generating adversarial attacks, offering superior performance over existing gradient-based methods and demonstrating the value of divide-and-conquer optimization for parameter and hyperparameter search in adversarial attack generation.

title: Mask-based Predictive Representations for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Vision-based deep reinforcement learning involves dealing with high-dimensional inputs of image information. It is crucial to abstract effective states from high-dimensional image inputs and limited samples for sample-efficient reinforcement learning. To address this challenge, inspired by fields such as natural language processing and computer vision, we propose a self-supervised task based on mask prediction as an auxiliary task for reinforcement learning. This non-reconstruction method uses the sequence information collected by the agent from the environment and the context information in the sequence to predict the masked information, thereby strengthening the agent's understanding of the task and learning effective representations. Combined with transformers, we find that the model reconstructs the masked input sequence in the latent space. By feeding the compressed representations learned by this method into reinforcement learning models, we observe an improvement in the sample efficiency of reinforcement learning. Moreover, the model outperforms state-of-the-art sample-efficient reinforcement learning methods on multiple continuous and discrete control benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised mask prediction task with transformers improves sample efficiency in vision-based deep reinforcement learning by learning effective latent representations from image sequences.
AI.motivation: Vision-based deep RL faces challenges with high-dimensional image inputs and limited samples. There's a need to abstract effective states from images efficiently for sample-efficient learning.
AI.method: Proposes a self-supervised mask prediction task as auxiliary RL task. Uses transformers to predict masked information in agent-collected environment sequences, learning compressed latent representations without reconstruction. Combines these learned representations with RL models.
AI.result: The model reconstructs masked input sequences in latent space. When fed into RL models, improves sample efficiency. Outperforms state-of-the-art sample-efficient RL methods on multiple continuous and discrete control benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Mask prediction-based self-supervised learning combined with transformers effectively extracts meaningful representations from visual sequences, significantly improving sample efficiency in vision-based reinforcement learning across various benchmarks.

title: Geometry of Ordinal Representations in Language Models
summary: Recent work showed that language models represent character counts on curved 1D manifolds, with attention heads performing geometric transformations to enable computation. We test whether this generalizes across four ordinal tasks (bracket depth, indentation, table position, numeric magnitude) in Gemma-2-2B, Gemma-2-9B, and Qwen3-4B. We find that 1D manifolds with place-cell feature tiling emerge for tasks where the ordinal variable is locally computable from token identity, while tasks requiring cross-position integration or semantic extraction produce higher-dimensional or incoherent representations. Geometric computation is architecture-dependent: Qwen3-4B shows substantially stronger twisting than Gemma models for indentation, and its twisters preserve ordinal order, unlike its numeric twisters. Activation patching confirms that the identified manifold subspaces concentrate task-relevant information, with manifold-direction ablation causing dramatically larger probe accuracy drops than random-direction controls.

AI.tldr: Language models represent ordinal variables on curved 1D manifolds for locally computable tasks, but higher-dimensional representations for tasks requiring cross-position integration, with geometric computation being architecture-dependent.
AI.motivation: To test whether language models' geometric computation of ordinal variables generalizes across different ordinal tasks and model architectures, building on previous findings that character counts are represented on curved 1D manifolds with attention heads performing geometric transformations.
AI.method: Experimental analysis of three language models (Gemma-2-2B, Gemma-2-9B, Qwen3-4B) on four ordinal tasks: bracket depth, indentation, table position, and numeric magnitude. Used activation patching to confirm task-relevant information concentration in identified manifold subspaces and manifold-direction ablation to measure importance.
AI.result: 1D manifolds with place-cell feature tiling emerge for tasks where ordinal variables are locally computable from token identity. Tasks requiring cross-position integration or semantic extraction produce higher-dimensional or incoherent representations. Qwen3-4B shows stronger geometric twisting than Gemma models, particularly for indentation tasks. Activation patching confirms manifold subspaces concentrate task-relevant information.
AI.conclusion: Geometric computation in language models is task-dependent and architecture-dependent. The emergence of 1D manifolds occurs for locally computable ordinal tasks, while more complex tasks yield higher-dimensional representations. Manifold structures concentrate task-relevant information, as demonstrated by ablation experiments showing significantly larger accuracy drops for manifold directions compared to random directions.

title: FedFFT: Taming Client Drift in Federated SAM via Spectral Perturbation Filtering
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables decentralized training without data sharing, but suffers from statistical heterogeneity across clients, leading to client drift, poor generalization, and sharp minima compared to centralized training. Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) has emerged as a promising approach to improve generalization, yet its application in federated learning still suffers from divergence problems, since perturbations are computed locally and reflect client-specific loss geometries. To better understand this issue, we provide experimental evidence from a new perspective, the frequency domain, for SAM perturbations in federated settings, revealing that inter-client perturbation inconsistencies are predominantly concentrated in the low-frequency spectrum. Motivated by this insight, we propose Federated learning with Frequency-domain Filtering of SAM perturbations (FedFFT). It is a lightweight and plug-and-play method that filters out low-frequency components of SAM perturbations without requiring additional communication, thereby suppressing inconsistent components in client updates while preserving consistent learning signals. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and diverse backbones demonstrate that FedFFT consistently outperforms SAM-based FL methods, particularly under severe non-IID distributions. These results highlight the effectiveness, scalability, and general applicability of our frequency-domain perspective for sharpness-aware federated optimization.

AI.tldr: FedFFT improves federated learning by filtering low-frequency perturbation inconsistencies in Sharpness-Aware Minimization using FFT-based filtering, achieving better generalization under non-IID data distributions.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning suffers from statistical heterogeneity across clients, causing client drift, poor generalization, and sharp minima. While Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) improves generalization, it diverges in FL due to local perturbations reflecting client-specific loss geometries. The authors identify that inter-client perturbation inconsistencies are concentrated in the low-frequency spectrum.
AI.method: Proposes FedFFT (Federated learning with Frequency-domain Filtering of SAM perturbations) - a lightweight plug-and-play method that applies FFT filtering to SAM perturbations, removing low-frequency components that cause inconsistencies while preserving consistent learning signals, without requiring additional communication.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and diverse backbones show FedFFT consistently outperforms SAM-based FL methods, especially under severe non-IID distributions, demonstrating effectiveness, scalability, and general applicability.
AI.conclusion: The frequency-domain perspective effectively addresses perturbation inconsistency in sharpness-aware federated optimization, and FedFFT provides a practical solution for improving FL generalization without extra communication overhead.

title: Physics-Informed Graph Learning with Uncertainty Awareness for Open-Set Domain Generalization in Fault Diagnosis
summary: Intelligent industrial maintenance critically relies on reliable fault diagnosis of rotating machinery. However, it faces formidable challenges from unknown fault types and domain shifts induced by varying operating conditions, which is formally formulated as the open-set domain generalization (OSDG) problem. Existing methods are mainly data-driven, thereby overlooking the cascaded propagation of uncertainty across feature extraction, topological learning, and decision-making stages.To tackle this challenge, we propose PGU-OD, a novel Physics-Informed Graph Learning framework with Uncertainty Awareness for Open-set Domain generalization. First, it designs a physics-informed spectral attention module to extract condition-robust fault features, thereby suppressing perceptual uncertainty caused by frequency shifts. Further, it constructs an uncertainty aware adaptive graph learning mechanism to dynamically adjust the edge weights of the sample graph guided by class-scale Gaussian distribution parameters, which mitigates the structural propagation of uncertainty. Finally, a Gaussian-distribution-based adaptive boundary loss function and a dual-criteria open-set inference strategy are developed to optimize decision boundaries and reliably reject unknown faults. Extensive experimental evaluations on two public and widely used rotating machinery fault datasets demonstrate that the proposed PGU-OD outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both known fault classification and unknown fault rejection under domain shifts.

AI.tldr: PGU-OD is a physics-informed graph learning framework with uncertainty awareness for open-set domain generalization in rotating machinery fault diagnosis, addressing unknown fault types and domain shifts under varying operating conditions.
AI.motivation: Intelligent industrial maintenance requires reliable fault diagnosis for rotating machinery, but faces challenges from unknown fault types and domain shifts due to varying operating conditions (open-set domain generalization problem). Existing data-driven methods overlook the cascaded propagation of uncertainty across feature extraction, topological learning, and decision-making stages.
AI.method: Proposes PGU-OD framework with three key components: 1) physics-informed spectral attention module to extract condition-robust fault features and suppress perceptual uncertainty from frequency shifts; 2) uncertainty-aware adaptive graph learning mechanism that dynamically adjusts sample graph edge weights guided by class-scale Gaussian distribution parameters to mitigate structural uncertainty propagation; 3) Gaussian-distribution-based adaptive boundary loss function and dual-criteria open-set inference strategy to optimize decision boundaries and reliably reject unknown faults.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two public rotating machinery fault datasets demonstrate that PGU-OD outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both known fault classification and unknown fault rejection under domain shifts.
AI.conclusion: PGU-OD effectively addresses the open-set domain generalization problem in rotating machinery fault diagnosis by integrating physics-informed feature extraction with uncertainty-aware graph learning and decision optimization, achieving superior performance in handling both known faults and unknown fault types across varying operating conditions.

title: SpecGradFilter: A Spectral Gradient Filtering Framework for Taming Federated Heterogeneity
summary: Federated Learning (FL) is fundamentally challenged by statistical heterogeneity, where non-identically distributed (non-IID) data induces client drift that severely hampers global convergence. While existing approaches attempt to mitigate this drift through spatial-domain gradient correction or regularization, they overlook the intrinsic spectral structure of optimization signals. In this work, we revisit client drift from a novel frequency-domain perspective and uncover a critical Spectral Bias of Drift: inter-client gradient divergence is predominantly concentrated in low-frequency components which encode client-specific distributional shifts, while high-frequency components representing fine-grained features remain relatively consistent. Motivated by this, we propose SpecGradFilter, a unified Spectral Gradient Filtering Framework that tames heterogeneity by suppressing discordant low-frequency signals. Crucially, we demonstrate that SpecGradFilter is a generalizable principle, effective not only via precise FFT-based truncation but also through spatial approximations like Gaussian detrending. Extensive experiments on benchmarks such as CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet demonstrate that SpecGradFilter significantly performs better performance in highly Non-IID settings with negligible communication overhead, establishing a new paradigm for robust federated optimization.

AI.tldr: SpecGradFilter introduces a frequency-domain approach to mitigate client drift in federated learning by filtering out discordant low-frequency gradient components that encode client-specific distributional shifts.
AI.motivation: Traditional federated learning approaches struggle with statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data) that causes client drift and hampers global convergence. Existing spatial-domain methods overlook the intrinsic spectral structure of optimization signals, leaving room for improvement.
AI.method: The paper proposes SpecGradFilter, a spectral gradient filtering framework that analyzes client drift from a frequency-domain perspective. The key insight is that inter-client gradient divergence is concentrated in low-frequency components, so the method suppresses these discordant low-frequency signals using either precise FFT-based truncation or spatial approximations like Gaussian detrending.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet benchmarks demonstrate that SpecGradFilter significantly outperforms existing methods in highly non-IID settings while adding negligible communication overhead.
AI.conclusion: SpecGradFilter establishes a new paradigm for robust federated optimization by leveraging spectral analysis to tame statistical heterogeneity, showing that the spectral bias of drift principle is generalizable and effective through various implementation approaches.

title: Exploring Convolutional Neural Processes for Weather Downscaling
summary: Global reanalysis products such as ERA5-Land provide spatially complete weather fields but at resolutions too coarse for local applications, particularly in mountainous regions where temperature can vary by several degrees over short distances. This project investigates Convolutional Conditional Neural Processes (ConvCNPs) for statistical downscaling of daily maximum temperature from the ~11km resolution ERA5-Land grid to ~1km resolution over Switzerland, building upon the architecture of Vaughan et al. (2022) and adapting it to the topographically complex Swiss domain with high-resolution elevation features from the swisstopo DHM25. The best model, trained on ten years of data (2014-2023) with five-fold temporal cross-validation, achieves a mean absolute error of 1.31 Celsius and a CRPS-based skill score of 0.524 relative to bilinear interpolation, reducing the expected prediction error by more than half. An ablation study reveals that the elevation MLP is the indispensable component - without it, the model diverges entirely - while explicit seasonal features and Topographic Position Index provide secondary benefits. Under sparse on-grid input the model degrades gracefully, maintaining positive skill down to approximately 10% of the input grid; however, zero-shot deployment on off-grid station observations does not achieve positive skill at any density tested. All configurations exhibit severely overconfident uncertainty estimates, a structural limitation of the Gaussian likelihood training objective. These results demonstrate that ConvCNPs are a viable and effective approach to climate downscaling in complex terrain, and identify uncertainty calibration and native support for non-gridded input as the key challenges for operational deployment.

AI.tldr: ConvCNPs effectively downscale 11km ERA5-Land temperature data to 1km resolution over Switzerland, cutting prediction error by half compared to bilinear interpolation.
AI.motivation: Global reanalysis products like ERA5-Land have coarse resolution unsuitable for local applications in mountainous regions where temperature varies dramatically over short distances.
AI.method: Adapted Convolutional Conditional Neural Processes architecture with high-resolution elevation features from swisstopo DHM25. Trained on 2014-2023 data with five-fold temporal cross-validation, using ablation studies to assess component importance.
AI.result: Best model achieved MAE of 1.31°C and CRPS skill score of 0.524 (52.4% error reduction vs bilinear). Elevation MLP is essential; model degrades gracefully with sparse grid inputs but fails on off-grid station observations. All models produce overconfident uncertainty estimates.
AI.conclusion: ConvCNPs are viable for climate downscaling in complex terrain but need improvements in uncertainty calibration and support for non-gridded input for operational deployment.

title: Channel-Adaptive Robust Aggregation for Over-the-Air Federated Learning in Heterogeneous Networks
summary: The growing demand for privacy-preserving, data-intensive applications such as IoT, augmented reality, and autonomous systems positions Federated Learning (FL) as a key enabler in 6G networks. Over-the-Air FL (OTA-FL) leverages the superposition property of the wireless multiple access channel for efficient aggregation via simultaneous transmissions. Existing methods rely on fixed aggregation schedules and do not jointly address noise, fading, and client heterogeneity. We propose CHARGE-FL (CHannel-Adaptive Robust agGrEgation), a framework that adaptively schedules aggregation based on channel dynamics and application readiness. By combining a tailored optimization strategy with a dual-purpose precoding mechanism, CHARGE-FL mitigates channel distortion and bias from partial updates, achieving superior accuracy, stability, and convergence under realistic wireless conditions. Empirical results under realistic wireless conditions show that CHARGE-FL significantly improves accuracy, stability, and convergence over state-of-the-art OTA-FL methods, particularly in straggler-prone and noisy scenarios.

AI.tldr: CHARGE-FL is a channel-adaptive federated learning framework that dynamically schedules model aggregation in wireless 6G networks, addressing noise, fading, and client heterogeneity to improve accuracy and convergence.
AI.motivation: The growing demand for privacy-preserving applications (IoT, AR, autonomous systems) in 6G networks requires efficient federated learning. Existing Over-the-Air FL methods use fixed aggregation schedules and fail to jointly address wireless challenges like noise, fading, and client heterogeneity.
AI.method: CHARGE-FL adaptively schedules aggregation based on channel dynamics and application readiness. It combines a tailored optimization strategy with a dual-purpose precoding mechanism to mitigate channel distortion and bias from partial updates.
AI.result: Empirical results under realistic wireless conditions show CHARGE-FL significantly improves accuracy, stability, and convergence over state-of-the-art OTA-FL methods, especially in straggler-prone and noisy scenarios.
AI.conclusion: CHARGE-FL provides an effective solution for federated learning in realistic 6G wireless environments by adaptively handling channel dynamics and client heterogeneity, addressing limitations of existing OTA-FL approaches.
