title: Quantize the Target, Quantize the Drafter: Efficient Inference with Qwen3.5-4B
comment: All authors contributed equally. Our submission to Efficient Qwen Competition, ICML 2026 Workshop on AdaptFM
summary: This report describes our approach to the Efficient Qwen Competition, where the goal is to enable low-latency serving of Qwen3.5-4B on a resource-constrained NVIDIA A10G GPU. Our system combines a quantized target model with speculative decoding. To recover accuracy, we apply quantization-aware distillation to the target model while retaining the original quantization grid. To speed up decoding, a block-diffusion drafter specialized for the quantized target model is trained using a two-stage procedure: first learning from the high-precision target and then adapting to the low-precision target. Because the drafter is invoked at every speculative decoding step, we further reduce its overhead with quantization and sliding-window attention, preserving draft-token acceptance while improving long-context decoding latency. As a result, our submission achieves a 6.978$\times$ average speedup over the baseline while satisfying the required quality thresholds, ranking 3rd overall. We hope these results provide useful insights for practical LLM inference. The code and resources are available at https://github.com/nota-github/adaptfm-quant-dflash

AI.tldr: The paper presents an approach to efficiently serve Qwen3.5-4B on resource-constrained A10G GPUs by combining quantization with speculative decoding, achieving 7x speedup over baseline while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: The need for low-latency serving of large language models like Qwen3.5-4B on resource-constrained hardware (NVIDIA A10G GPUs), balancing inference speed with model quality preservation.
AI.method: Combines quantized target model with speculative decoding using a specialized block-diffusion drafter. Uses quantization-aware distillation for accuracy recovery, two-stage drafter training (first from high-precision target, then adapting to low-precision target), and optimizations like quantization and sliding-window attention to reduce drafter overhead.
AI.result: Achieves 6.978× average speedup over baseline while satisfying required quality thresholds, ranking 3rd overall in the Efficient Qwen Competition.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully enables efficient LLM inference on resource-constrained hardware through the combination of quantization and speculative decoding, providing practical insights for real-world deployment while maintaining quality standards.

title: Signal or Noise? Understanding Generative Models for Real-World Sensor Time Series
summary: Generative models have changed how machine learning represents complex data distributions, especially in language and vision, yet many real-world systems are observed instead as continuous, high-dimensional, and noisy sensor time series. Existing generative modeling of sensor data, however, remains fragmented across modalities, datasets, and task formulations, limiting a systematic understanding of when, how, and why generative models succeed or fail in real-world settings. To address this gap, we introduce SensorGen, a large-scale study of sensor-signal generation spanning 14 settings across 4 domains, 7 datasets, and 12 signal modalities. Leveraging SensorGen, we systematically evaluate generative models from five major families and uncover three key findings: (1) flow-matching models provide strong overall performance across most settings; (2) signal properties matter, with demographic covariates improving longitudinal generation and time-frequency modeling improving high-frequency signal generation; and (3) generated signals have practical utility beyond visual realism, with scaling improving generation quality and synthetic data improving downstream performance. Together, SensorGen establishes a broader understanding of design choices, evaluation protocols, and failure modes in real-world sensor data generation.

AI.tldr: SensorGen is a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating generative models on real-world sensor time series data, revealing that flow-matching models perform best overall while identifying key factors like signal properties and practical utility beyond visual realism.
AI.motivation: Current generative modeling of sensor data is fragmented across modalities, datasets, and tasks, preventing systematic understanding of when, how, and why generative models succeed or fail in real-world sensor data settings.
AI.method: Introduces SensorGen, a large-scale study spanning 14 settings across 4 domains, 7 datasets, and 12 signal modalities. Systematically evaluates generative models from five major families on this benchmark.
AI.result: Three key findings: 1) Flow-matching models provide strong overall performance, 2) Signal properties matter (demographic covariates improve longitudinal generation, time-frequency modeling improves high-frequency signals), 3) Generated signals have practical utility with scaling improving quality and synthetic data improving downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: SensorGen establishes a foundation for broader understanding of design choices, evaluation protocols, and failure modes in real-world sensor data generation, moving beyond modality-specific approaches to systematic benchmarking.

title: On Preserving Geometrical Invariance for Superpixel Image Classification using Graph Transformer
summary: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Vision Transformer (ViT) for image classification exploit a dense grid of pixels containing redundant information. Consequently, for a larger image dataset, CNNs and ViTs face deployability challenges due to high computational complexity. Representing images as graphs of superpixels offers an efficient alternative that preserves key information while eliminating pixel-level redundancy. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been utilized on such graphs to perform image classification. However, GNNs are known to struggle with capturing long-range dependencies which is important in the domain of image classification. Furthermore, a majority of these superpixel-based image classification approaches do not explicitly preserve translation/rotation invariance. Nevertheless, preserving translation/rotation invariance is important for robust image classification. Thus, this paper proposes SuperGT, a Graph Transformer-based framework for image classification, which captures the long range dependencies, along with a pre-processing scheme that preserves translation/rotation invariance. We evaluate SuperGT on CIFAR-10 dataset and observe that it performs significantly better than many baselines. Furthermore, we note that the overall performance of SuperGT is comparable to the previous state-of-the-art model, namely, ShapeGNN, without relying on coordinates of the boundary points of each superpixel required by ShapeGNN.

AI.tldr: SuperGT is a Graph Transformer framework for image classification that uses superpixel graphs instead of dense pixels, capturing long-range dependencies while preserving translation/rotation invariance, achieving comparable performance to state-of-the-art methods on CIFAR-10.
AI.motivation: Standard CNNs and Vision Transformers use dense pixel grids containing redundant information, leading to high computational complexity. Superpixel-based graph representations are more efficient but existing Graph Neural Networks struggle with long-range dependencies and don't preserve translation/rotation invariance needed for robust image classification.
AI.method: SuperGT converts images to superpixel graphs to eliminate pixel redundancy, then applies a Graph Transformer architecture to capture long-range dependencies. It includes a pre-processing scheme designed to preserve translation and rotation invariance.
AI.result: SuperGT performs significantly better than many baselines on CIFAR-10 dataset and achieves comparable performance to the previous state-of-the-art ShapeGNN, without requiring boundary point coordinates that ShapeGNN needs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SuperGT framework effectively addresses computational complexity, long-range dependency capture, and invariance preservation in image classification, offering a competitive alternative to both CNN/ViT and previous superpixel-based methods.

title: HiFA4: Training-Free 4-bit FlashAttention on Ascend HIF4 NPUs for LLM Inference
summary:
We present HiFA4, a post-training operator-level design that executes both QK^T and PV in FlashAttention as 4-bit HIF4 Cube GEMMs for LLM inference on Ascend NPUs, while maintaining the online softmax state in FP16. To our knowledge, HiFA4 is the first Ascend-HIF4-targeted design of this kind evaluated on standard NLP benchmarks.
  HiFA4 combines two mechanisms. Smooth-QK applies a calibration-static per-channel equivalent rescaling to Q and K after RoPE, transferring quantization difficulty from K to Q without per-tile online reduction at inference. P-Reordering accumulates the softmax normalizer from the same quantized attention weights P_hat used in the PV GEMM, rather than from a higher-precision reconstruction. We show that this inconsistent formulation introduces a coherent output-scaling error, and validate the effect on a Qwen3-8B Layer-0 MMLU trace, where all 3.6M measured attention tiles exhibit net probability-mass loss with median epsilon_bar = -0.064. P-Reordering also allows the normalizer to be fused into the PV Cube GEMM.
  Across five LLMs, HiFA4 reduces quantization-induced decision drift. On Qwen3-8B, it recovers 37.5% of the accuracy gap introduced by direct HIF4 quantization, narrows the sample-weighted accuracy loss from 1.12 pp to 0.70 pp, reduces BF16-inconsistent MMLU predictions from 16.3% to 8.2%, and cuts MMLU accuracy regressions by 57% (1071 to 465). On Gemma2-9B, mild smoothing keeps HiFA4 within 0.7 pp of BF16 while reducing MMLU regressions by 27%. On LLaMA3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, and Phi-4B, where Smooth-QK is disabled, P-Reordering with the adopted Q-Mean auxiliary still reduces full-set MMLU regressions by 41-52%. A preliminary instruction-scheduling analysis projects a 35.4% critical-path latency reduction relative to BF16 by fusing the softmax normalizer into the PV Cube GEMM; on-hardware validation is left to future work.

AI.tldr: HiFA4 reduces LLM inference latency on Ascend NPUs by executing FlashAttention operations as 4-bit HIF4 Cube GEMMs while maintaining accuracy through Smooth-QK rescaling and P-Reordering techniques.
AI.motivation: To accelerate LLM inference on Ascend NPUs by using 4-bit quantization for attention computations while minimizing accuracy degradation from quantization artifacts.
AI.method: Two main techniques: 1) Smooth-QK applies per-channel rescaling to Q and K after RoPE to transfer quantization difficulty from K to Q. 2) P-Reordering accumulates softmax normalizer from quantized attention weights used in PV GEMM rather than higher-precision reconstruction.
AI.result: HiFA4 recovers 37.5% of accuracy gap from direct HIF4 quantization on Qwen3-8B, reduces BF16-inconsistent MMLU predictions from 16.3% to 8.2%, and cuts MMLU accuracy regressions by 57%. Projects 35.4% critical-path latency reduction by fusing softmax normalizer into PV Cube GEMM.
AI.conclusion: HiFA4 demonstrates effective post-training operator-level design for 4-bit HIF4 FlashAttention execution on Ascend NPUs, significantly reducing quantization-induced accuracy degradation while promising substantial latency improvements through hardware fusion.

title: SAD-LoRA: Spectral Alignment for Low-Rank Knowledge Distillation
comment: ICML'26 workshop on CoLoRAI - The 2nd Workshop on Connecting Low-rank Representations in AI, 15 pages
summary: Distilling a fine-tuned teacher into a LoRA-adapted student is a standard recipe for parameter-efficient compression, but output-level KD does not explicitly control which rank-$r$ weight subspace the adapter occupies. We propose \textbf{SAD-LoRA} (\textbf{S}pectral \textbf{A}lignment \textbf{D}istillation), which selects this subspace from the data-weighted student-space reference update $\DWT\Sigx^{1/2}$ and maintains it during training via a differentiable principal-angle loss on $\colspan(B)$. We show that the data-weighted distillation error decomposes exactly into subspace misalignment, within-subspace coefficient mismatch, and irreducible rank residual; standard KD can affect the first term only indirectly through output gradients. On controlled synthetic problems with a flat teacher spectrum, SAD-LoRA reduces the subspace-misalignment term from $51\%$ to nearly zero and lifts final subspace alignment from $0.49$ to $1.00$. On RoBERTa-large to RoBERTa-base distillation across six GLUE tasks, SAD-LoRA improves rank efficiency: at $r{=}4$, it matches or beats the strongest included spectral baseline on five of six tasks, and at $r{=}8$ it gives the best result on SST-2 and CoLA. Ablations identify subspace alignment as the load-bearing component, while coefficient matching is auxiliary.

AI.tldr: SAD-LoRA introduces spectral alignment distillation to explicitly control which rank-r weight subspace a LoRA adapter occupies during knowledge distillation, improving subspace alignment and rank efficiency.
AI.motivation: Standard output-level knowledge distillation (KD) doesn't explicitly control which rank-r weight subspace the LoRA adapter occupies, potentially leading to inefficient subspace utilization and suboptimal distillation performance.
AI.method: Proposes SAD-LoRA which selects the adapter subspace from the data-weighted student-space reference update (DWTΣx^{1/2}) and maintains it during training using a differentiable principal-angle loss on the column span of the adapter (colspan(B)). The approach explicitly decomposes distillation error into subspace misalignment, within-subspace coefficient mismatch, and irreducible rank residual components.
AI.result: On synthetic problems with flat teacher spectrum: reduces subspace-misalignment from 51% to nearly zero, lifts subspace alignment from 0.49 to 1.00. On RoBERTa-large to RoBERTa-base distillation across six GLUE tasks: improves rank efficiency; at r=4 matches/beats strongest spectral baseline on 5/6 tasks; at r=8 gives best results on SST-2 and CoLA. Ablations confirm subspace alignment is the critical component.
AI.conclusion: SAD-LoRA effectively addresses the subspace alignment problem in LoRA-based knowledge distillation, demonstrating that explicit subspace control through spectral alignment significantly improves parameter-efficient model compression performance compared to standard output-level KD approaches.

title: On the effectiveness of reward functions in reinforcement learning for confidence calibration of large language models
summary: In this paper, we consider the setting where large language models (LLMs) are trained using reinforcement learning (RL) to simultaneously improve reasoning accuracy and verbalize its confidence. Our reward scheme uses two functions for rewarding confidence verbalized by the LLM: one when the LLM is correct and a different one when the LLM is incorrect. With a poorly designed reward scheme, the LLM may be incentivized to answer incorrectly so that it can be confident that its answer is indeed incorrect, a phenomenon that we call confidence reward hacking. We propose the concept of non-hackable confidence reward schemes and define a spectrum of such reward schemes for RL confidence calibration training in LLMs. We demonstrate that selective confidence reward hacking can occur in practical datasets with reward schemes that are not designed to be non-hackable. We also demonstrate that the reward scheme with the best calibration to accuracy tradeoff depends on the dataset and the application, and propose using the reward scheme as a hyperparameter to optimize the tradeoffs in accordance to what is important for the application. The code of our experiments is available in https://anonymous.4open.science/r/rl-confidence-calibration-9ED4/README.md.

AI.tldr: LLMs trained with RL for both reasoning accuracy and confidence verbalization can be vulnerable to "confidence reward hacking" where models are incentivized to be wrong to earn confidence rewards, requiring carefully designed non-hackable reward schemes.
AI.motivation: When training LLMs with RL to simultaneously improve reasoning accuracy and verbalize confidence, poorly designed reward schemes can create perverse incentives where models are rewarded for being confidently wrong, a phenomenon called "confidence reward hacking."
AI.method: Propose non-hackable confidence reward schemes as a spectrum for RL confidence calibration training, using different reward functions for correct vs incorrect confidence verbalizations. The reward scheme is treated as a hyperparameter to optimize calibration-accuracy tradeoffs based on dataset and application needs.
AI.result: Demonstrate that selective confidence reward hacking occurs in practical datasets with poorly designed reward schemes, and show that optimal reward scheme design depends on dataset characteristics and application requirements.
AI.conclusion: Properly designed non-hackable reward schemes are crucial for RL-based confidence calibration in LLMs, and reward schemes should be treated as hyperparameters to optimize for specific calibration-accuracy tradeoffs based on application needs.

title: Structure-Specific Representational Priors Causally Control the Grokking Delay
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures. Code and artifacts: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21132757
summary: Grokking -- generalization arriving long after training-set interpolation -- can be accelerated by structure-agnostic interventions: gradient filtering, weight-norm clamping, geometric penalties on hidden representations. Whether the delay specifically measures the time to form task-structured representations has remained an observational claim. We test it causally by injecting representational priors of varying structural content into a one-layer transformer learning modular addition: a supervised-contrastive auxiliary loss whose positives encode (i) the task's true equivalence structure ($(a+b) \bmod p$), (ii) a coherent-but-wrong sibling structure ($(a-b) \bmod p$), or (iii) a random partition, all with identical loss form, strength, class sizes, and geometry. Whether generalization occurs follows a clean gradation: true structure 22/30 runs; sibling structure, which needs the same periodic features but the wrong combination, 14/15; random partition, satisfiable only by memorization, 0/20 (Fisher exact $p = 1.3 \times 10^{-7}$). A weight-norm-matched control replaying each intervention's norm trajectory onto plain cross-entropy generalizes in 0/15, collapsing into logit-scale saturation, ruling out the norm as mediator. Representation probes show structure formation precedes and predicts generalization in all 95 runs. Only the true structure also accelerates grokking, up to $2.75\times$ faster than baseline, but the acceleration is dose-dependent, bimodal across seeds, and a net wall-clock win only in its strongest cases given the contrastive term's overhead. The grokking delay is, causally, the time to form the right representational structure, where "right" is decided at the level of features rather than labels: coherent-but-wrong structure leaves grokking intact, random structure abolishes it, and only the true structure hastens it.

AI.tldr: The paper causally demonstrates that grokking delay specifically measures the time to form task-structured representations, not just any structure or weight norms.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to test the causal relationship between representational structure formation and grokking. While prior work showed observational correlations, this paper aims to establish whether the delay in grokking specifically measures the time to form task-structured representations, addressing whether structure-agnostic interventions like gradient filtering or weight-norm clamping are sufficient explanations.
AI.method: The method involves injecting representational priors with varying structural content into a one-layer transformer learning modular addition. Using a supervised-contrastive auxiliary loss, they test three conditions: (1) true equivalence structure (a+b mod p), (2) coherent-but-wrong sibling structure (a-b mod p), and (3) random partition. They use weight-norm-matched controls and representation probes to analyze structure formation timing relative to generalization.
AI.result: Results show a clear gradation: true structure leads to generalization in 22/30 runs, sibling structure (wrong combination of same periodic features) in 14/15 runs, and random partition in 0/20 runs. Weight-norm-matched controls generalize in 0/15 runs. Representation probes show structure formation precedes and predicts generalization in all 95 runs. True structure accelerates grokking up to 2.75x faster than baseline, but this acceleration is dose-dependent and bimodal across seeds.
AI.conclusion: The grokking delay is causally determined by the time to form the right representational structure, where "right" is decided at the feature level rather than labels: coherent-but-wrong structure preserves grokking, random structure abolishes it, and only true structure hastens it. This establishes that the delay specifically measures time to form task-structured representations.

title: One Framework for All: Cross-Modal Membership Inference for Generative Models
summary: Large generative models across text-to-text, text-to-image, and image-to-text modalities have been shown to pose significant privacy risks. One fundamental threat is membership inference attacks (MIA), which aim to determine whether a given data point was used in a model's training set. Although prior work has investigated MIAs against these three classes of generative models, existing approaches treat them in isolation and are not cross-applicable, thereby limiting their real-world utility. To address this limitation, we present the first comprehensive study of a unified membership inference framework that applies across text-to-text, text-to-image, and image-to-text modalities. Our approach is grounded in a key modality-agnostic observation: the output distribution of a generative model can approximate its training data distribution. Leveraging this property, we model the distributions of model-generated outputs and auxiliary non-member samples in a shared embedding space, and perform membership inference via likelihood ratio testing. We conduct extensive experiments in a strict black-box setting under both partial-knowledge and zero-knowledge threat models, and evaluate membership inference against both fine-tuning and pre-training data. Experimental results demonstrate our approach's superior performance in comparison to existing state-of-the-art methods, which are typically optimized for a single model class.

AI.tldr: A unified membership inference attack framework that works across text-to-text, text-to-image, and image-to-text generative models by modeling output distributions in a shared embedding space.
AI.motivation: Previous membership inference attacks against generative models treat different modalities (text-to-text, text-to-image, image-to-text) in isolation with non-cross-applicable approaches, limiting their real-world utility for privacy assessment.
AI.method: The approach uses a modality-agnostic observation that generative model outputs approximate training data distributions. It models distributions of model-generated outputs and auxiliary non-member samples in a shared embedding space and performs membership inference via likelihood ratio testing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in strict black-box settings under partial-knowledge and zero-knowledge threat models show superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods optimized for single model classes, working against both fine-tuning and pre-training data.
AI.conclusion: This is the first comprehensive study of a unified membership inference framework that works across multiple generative model modalities, demonstrating that a single approach can effectively assess privacy risks across different types of generative models.

title: How Many Initial Points Does Bayesian Optimization Need?
comment: 4 pages. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Decision-Making from Offline Datasets to Online Adaptation
summary: Bayesian Optimization (BO) generally begins with an initialization phase: a batch of $n_0$ uninformed evaluations. The choice of $n_0$ remains largely heuristic, and we empirically observe that the total cost (random initial points plus BO iterations needed to find the global optimum) is U-shaped in $n_0$, i.e., a practitioner wastes resources by selecting either too low or too high a value of $n_0$. We find this tradeoff persists across MLE, Bayesian MCMC, and exact GP hyperparameters, as well as across acquisition functions. Toward the latter, Thompson Sampling appears an exception, with both total cost and simple regret essentially $n_0$-agnostic, though higher in our experiments. We attribute this U-shape to the known boundary issue of variance-driven BO: BO burns early budget on corners of the hypercube before turning inward. We demonstrate this effect using a 3D BO trajectory where the exact hyperparameters are known. We conclude with practical recommendations: use multi-step lookahead BO where possible; otherwise use Thompson Sampling when $n_0$ cannot be tuned, and a generously large $n_0$ when it can.

AI.tldr: Bayesian Optimization has a U-shaped total cost in initial batch size n₀, with Thompson Sampling being n₀-agnostic but with higher overall cost.
AI.motivation: The choice of initial batch size n₀ in Bayesian Optimization remains largely heuristic, and practitioners often waste resources by selecting either too low or too high values, leading to suboptimal performance.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of the U-shaped relationship between total cost (random initial points plus BO iterations) and n₀, examining this tradeoff across different hyperparameter estimation methods (MLE, Bayesian MCMC, exact GP), acquisition functions, and using 3D BO trajectory analysis with known hyperparameters to demonstrate boundary effects.
AI.result: Total cost shows a U-shaped dependence on n₀ across all methods except Thompson Sampling, which is n₀-agnostic but has higher overall cost. The U-shape is attributed to BO's boundary issue where variance-driven BO spends early budget on hypercube corners before exploring interior regions.
AI.conclusion: Practical recommendations: use multi-step lookahead BO when possible; otherwise use Thompson Sampling when n₀ cannot be tuned, and use a generously large n₀ when it can be tuned.

title: RL Forgets! Towards Continual Policy Optimization
summary: Continual post-training is becoming a central paradigm for adapting vision-language models to evolving tasks. Recent work has increasingly favored reinforcement learning over supervised fine-tuning, driven by the belief that reinforcement learning is inherently less prone to forgetting. However, the belief remains insufficiently validated, as existing evidence is largely drawn from outdated or homogeneous benchmarks. To revisit this assumption, we introduce MRCL, a Multimodal Reasoning Continual Learning benchmark built from diverse and recently released multimodal datasets. Experiments on MRCL show that reinforcement learning can still suffer from severe catastrophic forgetting during continual post-training. To address this challenge, we propose Continual Policy Optimization (CPO), a replay-free framework grounded in the prior-task behavioral KL objective. CPO uses a theoretically justified parameter-movement regularization to limit policy drift on previous tasks. Extensive experiments across multiple model scales demonstrate that CPO consistently reduces forgetting while preserving, and in some cases improving, pretrained model capabilities. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CPO reduces forgetting by 13.7\% and improves pretrained capability by 7.0\%. The implementation code is available at https://github.com/MaolinLuo/CPO.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark (MRCL) reveals that reinforcement learning in continual post-training of vision-language models still suffers from catastrophic forgetting, and the proposed Continual Policy Optimization (CPO) method effectively addresses this issue with parameter-movement regularization.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the common assumption that reinforcement learning is inherently less prone to forgetting than supervised fine-tuning during continual post-training of vision-language models. The authors argue that this belief hasn't been properly validated on modern, diverse benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors first introduce MRCL, a Multimodal Reasoning Continual Learning benchmark built from recent diverse datasets. Then they propose Continual Policy Optimization (CPO), a replay-free framework based on prior-task behavioral KL objective, using theoretically justified parameter-movement regularization to limit policy drift on previous tasks.
AI.result: Experiments on MRCL show reinforcement learning can suffer severe catastrophic forgetting. CPO consistently reduces forgetting while preserving/improving pretrained model capabilities across multiple model scales. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CPO reduces forgetting by 13.7% and improves pretrained capability by 7.0%.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully debunks the misconception that reinforcement learning is inherently resistant to forgetting in continual learning. CPO provides an effective replay-free solution for continual post-training of vision-language models that maintains performance on previous tasks while adapting to new ones.

title: NKI-Agent: Domain-Specific Fine-Tuning and Agentic Tool Use for Neuron Kernel Generation
comment: 7 pages. Accepted at DL4C @ ICML 2026 Workshop
summary: Recent agentic approaches to LLM-based kernel generation have achieved impressive results on CUDA. For emerging AI accelerators such as AWS Trainium and Inferentia, automated kernel generation and optimization remain largely unaddressed. Writing kernels for these chips via the Neuron Kernel Interface (NKI) is particularly challenging: developers must navigate a multi-engine architecture, tile-based programming, and explicit data movement across multi-level memory hierarchy. Moreover, no publicly-available training data, benchmarks, or tool-augmented agents exist for this domain. We introduce NKI-Agent, the first system combining domain-specific supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with a compile-verify-fix agent loop for NKI kernel generation. We adapt the existing CUDA-Agent framework to Neuron hardware, curate 6,000 NKI kernel generation tasks for training, and construct NKIBench, a 250-task benchmark across three difficulty levels. Evaluated on real Trn1 hardware, NKI-Agent with Claude Opus 4.8 and a rank-aware system prompt achieves a 77.3% pass rate on the 150-task NKIBench. We show that tool use is critical: Opus 4.8 scores 6% in single-shot mode without agent tools. On a 60-task subset, we show that an SFT-trained Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B achieves 25.0% pass rate at 1/100th the cost, outperforming Claude Sonnet 4 (15.0%). We also report that Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with binary compilation reward fails to improve over SFT, providing guidance on reward design for RL-based kernel generation.

AI.tldr: NKI-Agent: First system combining domain-specific SFT with compile-verify-fix agent loop for generating kernels on AWS Neuron hardware, achieving 77.3% pass rate on real hardware evaluation.
AI.motivation: Automated kernel generation and optimization for emerging AI accelerators like AWS Trainium and Inferentia remains largely unaddressed. Writing kernels via Neuron Kernel Interface (NKI) is challenging due to multi-engine architecture, tile-based programming, explicit data movement, and lack of public training data/benchmarks/tool-augmented agents.
AI.method: 1) Adapt CUDA-Agent framework to Neuron hardware. 2) Curate 6,000 NKI kernel generation tasks for supervised fine-tuning (SFT). 3) Construct NKIBench with 250 tasks across three difficulty levels. 4) Implement compile-verify-fix agent loop using Claude Opus 4.8 with rank-aware system prompts. 5) Test SFT-trained Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B for cost efficiency. 6) Experiment with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with binary compilation reward.
AI.result: NKI-Agent with Claude Opus 4.8 achieves 77.3% pass rate on 150-task NKIBench evaluated on real Trn1 hardware. Tool use is critical: Opus 4.8 scores only 6% without agent tools. SFT-trained Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B achieves 25.0% pass rate at 1/100th the cost, outperforming Claude Sonnet 4 (15.0%). GRPO with binary compilation reward fails to improve over SFT.
AI.conclusion: NKI-Agent successfully addresses the challenging problem of automated kernel generation for Neuron hardware by combining domain-specific SFT with a compile-verify-fix agent loop. Tool use is essential for success, and while RL-based approaches show promise, careful reward design is needed. The system demonstrates practical utility with both high-performance (Claude Opus) and cost-effective (Qwen3) options.

title: Learning Task-Sufficient World Models by Synergizing Agentic Exploration and Structured Modeling
summary: Learning and planning in imagination using world models provides an effective paradigm for training agents for decision-making. However, existing approaches often rely on high-dimensional latent spaces or generic visual embeddings that retain many factors irrelevant to control, limiting efficiency and generalization across tasks. To this end, we study how agents can learn world models with representations that are task-specific, minimal, and sufficient for decision-making. We achieve this via a closed-loop synergy between the agent and the world model, in which structured world-model learning distills task-sufficient representations from informative interaction data. On the agent side, agents actively probe the environment to collect informative trajectories that expose task-relevant latent factors, guided by an adaptive curriculum. On the world-model side, we learn structured representations over observations to distill compact, task-sufficient latent states from the collected interaction data. This synergy enables the empirical recovery of task-sufficient latent representations that capture all control-relevant factors. Leveraging these representations, the resulting policies achieve improved sample efficiency and generalization, including generalization across skills, object-skill compositions, and previously unseen tasks on standard continuous-control and robotic-manipulation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A method for learning world models with task-specific minimal representations through closed-loop synergy between agents and world models, enabling efficient recovery of control-relevant latent factors for improved sample efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for learning world models rely on high-dimensional latent spaces or generic visual embeddings that retain irrelevant factors, limiting efficiency and generalization across tasks.
AI.method: Closed-loop synergy between agent and world model: 1) Agents actively probe environment with adaptive curriculum to collect informative trajectories exposing task-relevant factors; 2) World models learn structured representations to distill compact, task-sufficient latent states from interaction data.
AI.result: The method enables empirical recovery of task-sufficient latent representations capturing all control-relevant factors, leading to policies with improved sample efficiency and generalization across skills, object-skill compositions, and unseen tasks on continuous-control and robotic-manipulation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates that learning world models with minimal, task-specific representations through agent-model synergy can significantly improve decision-making efficiency and generalization capabilities in continuous control and robotics.

title: Full-Stack FP4: Stable LLM Pretraining with Quantized Projections, Optimizers, and Attention
summary: Recent NVFP4 pretraining methods mainly target transformer linear layers, leaving optimizer states, optimizer arithmetic and attention underexplored in 4-bit pipelines. This critical gap blocks stable full-stack 4-bit pretraining, as the three core modules exhibit unique numerical failure patterns: linear layers hit hard quantization noise limits with dimension-propagated error amplification; AdamW second moments are heavy-tailed non-negative values fragile to low-precision denominators; attention carries error-prone computation paths demanding strict forward-backward quantization consistency. We propose Full-Stack FP4, the first complete NVFP4 pretraining framework resolving all three stability bottlenecks via module-wise precision strategies. For linear projections, LoRA-SVD lightweight decomposition suppresses quantization noise and breaks the direct-quantization error ceiling, shrinking the linear-only loss gap from 1.40% to 0.61%. For optimizers, we design AdamW second-moment transformation for robust NVFP4 storage and fully support native NVFP4 Newton-Schulz iterations for the Root (Muon) optimizer. For attention, a mixed-precision scheme quantizes Q/K/V and backward dS while guarding vulnerable paths in BF16, paired with unified tensor reuse to sustain forward-backward alignment. We further analyze fast error accumulation in naive low-bit matrix multiplication and the extreme sensitivity of PV / dOV^T attention branches. All modules are plug-and-play with cumulative stability and efficiency improvements. Our 3B/64B-token pretraining validates near-BF16 performance with merely 1.47% loss gap, verifying feasible stable end-to-end NVFP4 LLM pretraining.

AI.tldr: Full-Stack FP4 is the first complete 4-bit precision framework for LLM pretraining that addresses three key stability bottlenecks: linear layers, optimizer states, and attention mechanisms, achieving near-16-bit performance with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Current NVFP4 pretraining methods mainly target transformer linear layers while neglecting optimizer states, optimizer arithmetic, and attention modules in 4-bit pipelines. This critical gap prevents stable full-stack 4-bit pretraining as these three core modules have unique numerical failure patterns that require specialized solutions.
AI.method: The authors propose Full-Stack FP4 with module-wise precision strategies: 1) For linear projections: LoRA-SVD lightweight decomposition to suppress quantization noise and break error ceilings. 2) For optimizers: AdamW second-moment transformation for robust storage and native NVFP4 Newton-Schulz iterations. 3) For attention: Mixed-precision scheme quantizes Q/K/V and backward dS while protecting vulnerable paths in BF16, with unified tensor reuse for forward-backward alignment.
AI.result: The framework shrinks the linear-only loss gap from 1.40% to 0.61%. In 3B/64B-token pretraining, it achieves near-BF16 performance with only 1.47% loss gap, demonstrating feasible stable end-to-end NVFP4 LLM pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Full-Stack FP4 provides the first complete solution for stable 4-bit pretraining, addressing all three critical bottlenecks (linear layers, optimizers, attention) with plug-and-play modules that offer cumulative stability and efficiency improvements, enabling practical low-precision LLM training.

title: Environmental Drivers of Respiratory Disease: A District Level Analysis
summary: Sri Lanka has experienced a decade of progressive forest degradation and rising atmospheric pollution, yet district-level respiratory admissions have paradoxically declined, pointing to the confounding role of healthcare access. This study addresses that gap by constructing an 11-year (2014-2024) panel dataset across all 25 administrative districts, integrating satellite-derived vegetation indices, fire radiative power, pollutant concentrations (particulate matter (PM2.5), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), sulfur dioxide (SO2)), carbon flux metrics and population-normalized respiratory admission rates. Two temporally validated XGBoost models were created for annual district-level respiratory rate (R^2 = 0.937) and monthly PM2.5 concentration (R^2 = 0.976) with generalization validated in 21 out of 25 districts (Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) <= 20%). Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) analysis established that cumulative air quality burden is the overwhelming driver of respiratory rate variance (80.1%), ahead of forest degradation (15.6%) and fire activity (4.3%). The Forest-Air-Health (FAH) Risk Index used these SHAP-derived weights to find the districts with the highest risk: Colombo (FAH = 0.802), Gampaha (0.708), and Kalutara (0.682). These findings present the inaugural evidence-based, district-level framework correlating environmental degradation with respiratory health in Sri Lanka, establishing a quantitative basis for focused public health and environmental policy.

AI.tldr: This study developed a district-level framework linking environmental degradation to respiratory health in Sri Lanka using 11 years of panel data, finding air quality is the dominant driver (80.1%) ahead of forest degradation (15.6%), and identifying three highest-risk districts.
AI.motivation: Sri Lanka has experienced progressive forest degradation and rising atmospheric pollution, yet district-level respiratory admissions have paradoxically declined. This paradox suggests confounding factors like healthcare access are masking the true environmental-health relationship, creating a research gap that needs to be addressed.
AI.method: Constructed an 11-year (2014-2024) panel dataset across all 25 administrative districts, integrating satellite-derived vegetation indices, fire radiative power, pollutant concentrations (PM2.5, NO2, SO2), carbon flux metrics, and population-normalized respiratory admission rates. Used two temporally validated XGBoost models for respiratory rates and PM2.5 concentrations, with SHAP analysis for feature importance, and created a Forest-Air-Health (FAH) Risk Index.
AI.result: Models achieved high accuracy (R^2 = 0.937 for respiratory rate, 0.976 for PM2.5) with good generalization (MAPE ≤ 20% in 21/25 districts). SHAP analysis revealed cumulative air quality burden drives 80.1% of respiratory rate variance, forest degradation 15.6%, and fire activity 4.3%. The FAH Risk Index identified Colombo (0.802), Gampaha (0.708), and Kalutara (0.682) as highest-risk districts.
AI.conclusion: This study provides the first evidence-based, district-level framework linking environmental degradation with respiratory health in Sri Lanka, establishing air quality as the dominant driver and creating a quantitative basis for targeted public health and environmental policy interventions.

title: Covert Trait Propagation Is Representation Alignment: Mechanistic Evidence from Hidden-Channel Distillation
summary: A student model trained on pure uniform noise can still inherit its teacher's digit-classification ability, provided the two share initialization. Previous work proves this transfer is guaranteed when the teacher's learning rate is small enough, but does not explain where in the network the channel lives or what sets its capacity. Working in an MLP distillation setting on MNIST, we show these channels are not purely informational: geometric alignment gates access to the information the channel carries. Shared initialization makes the output projection W_2 a common coordinate key, and KL gradients reshape the student's input projection W_0 until its hidden representations align with the teacher's. We call this covert trait propagation (CTP). Five experiments support this mechanism: channel closure tracks weight drift, not teacher accuracy; freezing W_0 destroys transfer while freezing W_2 leaves it intact; multi-teacher ensembles cancel out despite each teacher carrying comparable label information; and linear centered kernel alignment (CKA) tracks student accuracy at r=0.98 across a continuous initialization sweep. Applying the same geometric lens to cross-token behavioral entanglement (CTBE) in instruction-tuned LLMs, we find the effect appears to be activated by alignment training, acting on an inherited substrate, and that the standard log-ratio metric produces an apparent frequency bias that is largely a circularity artifact.

AI.tldr: Knowledge transfer can occur from teacher to student neural networks even when student is trained on pure noise, mediated by geometric alignment of hidden representations enabled by shared initialization.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed knowledge transfer is theoretically guaranteed with small learning rates but didn't explain where the transfer channel exists in the network or what determines its capacity. The authors aim to uncover the geometric mechanism behind this covert transfer phenomenon.
AI.method: Analyzed MLP distillation on MNIST using five experiments: tracking channel closure vs weight drift, freezing different weight matrices (W_0 vs W_2), studying multi-teacher ensembles, measuring linear CKA correlation with accuracy, and extending analysis to instruction-tuned LLMs for cross-token behavioral entanglement.
AI.result: Found that transfer channels are not purely informational but depend on geometric alignment. Shared initialization makes W_2 a common coordinate key, and KL gradients reshape student's W_0 to align hidden representations. Transfer tracks weight drift, not teacher accuracy; freezing W_0 destroys transfer but freezing W_2 doesn't; multi-teacher ensembles cancel out; linear CKA correlates with accuracy at r=0.98.
AI.conclusion: Identified a covert trait propagation (CTP) mechanism where geometric alignment gates information transfer. The same geometric principles explain cross-token behavioral entanglement in LLMs, where alignment training activates effects on an inherited substrate, and standard metrics show circularity artifacts.

title: A Deep Learning-based surrogate model for Severe Accidents in nuclear reactors using ASTEC
summary: Integral codes like the Accident Source Term Evaluation Code (ASTEC) are powerful tools to study the physics of Severe Accidents (SAs) in nuclear reactors. Real time SA simulators can also be helpful in training operators of nuclear plants to react correctly to malfunctions. However, SA simulators can take up to several days per simulation, making their use infeasible for real time applications. In this work we show how to speed up a SA simulator with a fast, Deep Learning based (DL), surrogate model (SM). The SM is built as a combination of a dimensionality reduction stage, via an AutoEncoder, and a time-stepping stage, via a Neural Ordinary Differential Equation. The data on which the SM is trained are obtained from the ASTEC simulator, by sampling a set of operator actions for station blackout (SBO) and loss-of-coolant accidents (LOCA). The objective of the developed SM is to approximate multiple spatio-temporal fields for the thermal-hydraulic physics, core degradation, and fission product release modules in ASTEC's vessel domain. The SM predicts simultaneously around $80$ different physical variables (both scalar and fields), maintaining a stable autoregressive rollout up to $50$ thousand time steps. In addition, the AutoEncoder achieves a dimensionality reduction by a factor of over $300$, which allows the SM to predict up to $40$ hours of simulation in under a minute, both on CPU and GPU. This work is the first study of the capabilities and limits of DL based surrogate modeling in approximating the challenging, highly non-linear physics of ASTEC.

AI.tldr: Deep Learning surrogate model accelerates nuclear severe accident simulations by 300x, enabling 40-hour simulations in under a minute while maintaining accuracy on 80+ physical variables.
AI.motivation: Current severe accident simulators (like ASTEC) can take days per simulation, making real-time training and analysis infeasible for nuclear plant operators. There's a need for faster surrogate models that can approximate complex physics in real-time.
AI.method: Combined AutoEncoder for dimensionality reduction (300x compression) with Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for time-stepping. Trained on ASTEC simulation data from station blackout (SBO) and loss-of-coolant accidents (LOCA) scenarios.
AI.result: The surrogate model simultaneously predicts ~80 physical variables (scalar and field) with stable autoregressive rollout for 50k time steps, enabling 40-hour simulations in under a minute on both CPU and GPU.
AI.conclusion: This is the first successful demonstration of deep learning surrogate modeling for ASTEC's highly nonlinear severe accident physics, showing potential for real-time operator training and analysis applications.

title: Operator-on-F complements value-equivalence: a planning-time diagnostic for latent world models
comment: Accepted at the RLC 2026 World Model Workshop
summary: World-model evaluation for model-based reinforcement learning typically asks whether the learned model predicts reward and value well, which can leave planning-relevant errors in the model's latent rollouts unmeasured. We introduce a complementary diagnostic, operator-on-F, that compares a model's k-step latent pushforward to the environment's on an observable subset F, using the model's own predictor. On a TD-MPC2 size sweep over cheetah-run, reward-prediction error stays within [0.028, 0.091] for every model size - only about 3x variation - so an unnormalized reward-fit check has narrow resolution to distinguish them; the (unnormalized) Bellman residual and reward error themselves have weak relationships with return (Spearman -0.10 and -0.30). Operator error spans 0.28 to 2.62 over the same sizes. At 317M the operator error is 2.62 - an order of magnitude above the 0.28-0.36 cluster - and the planning return collapses to 0.9, while reward-prediction error (0.091) is the highest of the five but stays within the same small [0.028, 0.091] range as the rest of the sweep. The rank correlation between operator error and return loss is -0.90 (anchor-bootstrap 95% CI [-0.90, -0.70] at n=5 sizes; leave-one-out removal of any single size leaves it at -0.80 or stronger). The operator also returns informative, architecture-discriminating estimates in a cross-architecture comparison between TD-MPC2 and a pure-SSL latent world model. The operator diagnostic complements value-equivalence rather than replacing it.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new diagnostic called "operator-on-F" that measures planning-relevant errors in model-based RL by comparing a model's k-step latent rollouts to the environment's dynamics, addressing limitations of traditional reward and value prediction metrics.
AI.motivation: Traditional world-model evaluation in model-based RL focuses on reward and value prediction, which can miss planning-relevant errors in latent rollouts. Current metrics like reward-prediction error and Bellman residual show weak correlation with actual planning performance and have limited resolution to distinguish model quality.
AI.method: The authors introduce "operator-on-F," a diagnostic that compares a model's k-step latent pushforward to the environment's dynamics on an observable subset F, using the model's own predictor. This measures how well the model's latent dynamics match the environment's true dynamics.
AI.result: On a TD-MPC2 size sweep over cheetah-run: operator error spans 0.28-2.62 (wide range) vs reward-prediction error's 0.028-0.091 (narrow range). Operator error shows strong negative correlation (-0.90) with planning return, while reward-prediction error and Bellman residual show weak correlations (-0.30 and -0.10). The diagnostic also discriminates effectively between different architectures (TD-MPC2 vs pure-SSL latent world model).
AI.conclusion: The operator-on-F diagnostic effectively identifies planning-relevant model errors that traditional metrics miss, providing higher resolution for distinguishing model quality and stronger correlation with actual planning performance. It serves as a valuable complement to value-equivalence metrics rather than replacing them.

title: Knowledge-Informed Local Causal Discovery of Optimal Adjustment Sets
summary: Local causal discovery is a scalable alternative to global structure learning. However, it can struggle to identify valid adjustment sets in data-scarce settings because of finite-sample uncertainty, incomplete local neighborhoods, and unresolved Markov equivalence. Although many application domains provide structured background knowledge, its integration into local causal discovery remains limited. We propose b-LOAD, a knowledge-informed extension of the LOAD algorithm for local discovery of optimal adjustment sets. b-LOAD incorporates prior edge constraints directly into the local structure-learning procedure and uses Meek's rules to expand the discovery frontier dynamically, yielding a knowledge-constrained partially directed graph over the relevant local subgraph. This strategy helps prevent structurally relevant nodes introduced by prior knowledge from being excluded by local search. We prove that, under sound background knowledge, the procedure monotonically refines the admissible equivalence class and can enlarge the set of identifiable causal queries, enabling recovery of optimal adjustment sets that are not identifiable from observational conditional-independence information alone. Empirically, b-LOAD improves downstream causal effect estimation relative to purely data-driven and standard knowledge-augmented baselines, particularly in data-scarce and structurally complex regimes. Results on real-world biological networks show that locally targeted prior knowledge provides the largest gains and remains beneficial under moderate structural noise. These findings position b-LOAD as a scalable approach for converting fragmented domain knowledge into more reliable causal-effect estimation.

AI.tldr: b-LOAD extends the LOAD algorithm by incorporating structured background knowledge into local causal discovery to identify valid adjustment sets, particularly improving performance in data-scarce scenarios.
AI.motivation: Local causal discovery struggles with identifying valid adjustment sets in data-scarce settings due to finite-sample uncertainty, incomplete local neighborhoods, and unresolved Markov equivalence. While many domains have structured background knowledge, its integration into local causal discovery remains limited.
AI.method: b-LOAD incorporates prior edge constraints directly into local structure learning and uses Meek's rules to dynamically expand the discovery frontier, producing a knowledge-constrained partially directed graph over the relevant local subgraph. This prevents structurally relevant nodes from being excluded by local search.
AI.result: Empirically, b-LOAD improves downstream causal effect estimation compared to purely data-driven and standard knowledge-augmented baselines, especially in data-scarce and structurally complex regimes. Real-world biological network results show locally targeted prior knowledge provides the largest gains and remains beneficial under moderate structural noise.
AI.conclusion: b-LOAD is a scalable approach that converts fragmented domain knowledge into more reliable causal-effect estimation by monotonically refining the admissible equivalence class and enabling recovery of optimal adjustment sets not identifiable from observational data alone.

title: Regime-Conditional Stabilisation of LLM-Augmented Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a natural interface for translating human objectives into reward signals for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), yet the training-time dynamics of this integration remain poorly understood. We show that dynamically updating LLM-generated reward weights during off-policy MARL violates the stationarity assumption of Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS) and contaminates the experience replay buffer, whose stored transitions carry reward labels computed under stale shaping weights. We characterise the result as a regime-dependent failure whose severity depends on how competent the unshaped baseline already is. To control it we propose two stabilisation strategies: a Phase-Based Freeze Schedule that enforces strict stationarity within training phases, and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing that bounds per-episode weight drift. We evaluate across three cooperative environments and five random seeds with QMIX, complemented by an exploratory VDN extension, yielding a three-regime taxonomy. In the augmentative regime (Simple Spread), where the baseline is functional (74.4 %), EMA significantly improves success to 86.7 % ($+12.3$ pp, $p<0.01$) while naive dynamic updates collapse it to 15.2 %. In the essential regime (Level-Based Foraging), where the baseline is broken (0.1 %), any shaping unlocks the task (95.9 % under EMA). In the supplementary regime (SMAC 3m), where the baseline is near-saturated (98.8 %), stabilised shaping preserves performance (99.9 %) while unstabilised shaping adds variance without gain. These findings establish reward-signal stationarity as a necessary design constraint and indicate that regime placement is a practical predictor of whether dynamic LLM shaping helps or harms.

AI.tldr: Dynamic LLM-generated reward shaping in MARL can fail due to non-stationarity; the paper proposes stabilization methods and identifies three performance regimes based on baseline competence.
AI.motivation: LLMs provide a natural interface for translating human objectives into reward signals for cooperative MARL, but the training-time dynamics of this integration are poorly understood. Dynamic updates of LLM-generated reward weights violate PBRS stationarity assumptions and contaminate experience replay buffers.
AI.method: Proposes two stabilization strategies: 1) Phase-Based Freeze Schedule that enforces strict stationarity within training phases, and 2) Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing that bounds per-episode weight drift. Evaluated across three cooperative environments (Simple Spread, Level-Based Foraging, SMAC 3m) with QMIX and exploratory VDN extension over five random seeds.
AI.result: Established a three-regime taxonomy: 1) Augmentative regime (Simple Spread): baseline functional at 74.4%, EMA improves to 86.7% (+12.3pp), naive dynamic updates collapse to 15.2%. 2) Essential regime (Level-Based Foraging): baseline broken at 0.1%, any shaping unlocks task (95.9% under EMA). 3) Supplementary regime (SMAC 3m): baseline near-saturated at 98.8%, stabilized shaping preserves performance (99.9%) while unstabilized adds variance without gain.
AI.conclusion: Reward-signal stationarity is a necessary design constraint for integrating LLM-generated reward shaping in MARL. Regime placement (based on baseline competence) is a practical predictor of whether dynamic LLM shaping helps or harms performance. The proposed stabilization methods effectively control non-stationarity issues.

title: Two Black Boxes, One Solver: Encoder Probing and Decoder Attribution for Neural Multi-Attribute VRP under Hard-Mask and Recourse Decoders
comment: Accepted at the IJCAI-ECAI 2026 Workshop on Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), Bremen, Germany. 8 pages
summary: Neural autoregressive solvers for the Multi-Attribute Vehicle Routing Problem (MAVRP) reach competitive cost but offer no per-step justification, a problem when dispatchers must validate, accept, or compare them. We open two complementary black boxes in one protocol. On the encoder side, linear probes, spontaneous-organization metrics, rank-based richness measures, and discovered-direction analyses with intervention validation characterize how the latent represents constraint families at the graph, node, and edge level. On the decoder side, three attribution methods (gradient, integrated gradients, DeepLIFT) feed three reading angles: abductive, contrastive against the best feasible alternative, and counterfactual (smallest input change that switches the action or restores feasibility). Explanations are scored on fidelity, concentration, stability, sanity, and actionability. Across six variants combining three encoders (Attention baseline, Unimp, UnimpMoe) with two decoders (Hard-Mask, Recourse), we find that graph inductive bias improves both representational predictability and decoder sanity, that the Mixture-of-Experts encoder represents constraints in a distributed rather than axis-aligned way, and that the Recourse training regime, not merely its softer mask, produces policies that represent infeasibility usefully, exposing make-feasible counterfactuals that Hard-Mask policies fail to produce even when fed infeasible alternatives externally.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an explanation framework for neural autoregressive solvers in MAVRP, analyzing encoder representations and decoder decisions through multiple attribution methods to provide justifications that human dispatchers can validate.
AI.motivation: Current neural autoregressive solvers for MAVRP achieve competitive costs but lack per-step justifications, which is problematic when human dispatchers need to validate, accept, or compare solutions. This "black box" nature limits practical deployment where human oversight is required.
AI.method: Two-phase explanation protocol: 1) Encoder analysis using linear probes, spontaneous-organization metrics, rank-based richness measures, and discovered-direction analyses with intervention validation to characterize how latent representations encode constraints. 2) Decoder analysis using three attribution methods (gradient, integrated gradients, DeepLIFT) for three reading angles (abductive, contrastive, counterfactual). Explanations are scored on fidelity, concentration, stability, sanity, and actionability metrics.
AI.result: Across six variants combining three encoders (Attention baseline, Unimp, UnimpMoe) with two decoders (Hard-Mask, Recourse), results show: graph inductive bias improves representational predictability and decoder sanity; Mixture-of-Experts encoder represents constraints in distributed rather than axis-aligned ways; Recourse training produces policies that usefully represent infeasibility and expose make-feasible counterfactuals that Hard-Mask policies cannot.
AI.conclusion: The comprehensive explanation framework successfully opens the black box of neural MAVRP solvers, revealing important architectural insights and enabling actionable justifications that address real-world deployment needs for human validation.

title: Lyapunov-Guided Training for Hardware-Safe Neural Networks Under Fixed-Point Arithmetic
summary: Low-precision neural networks are attractive for resource-constrained hardware, but fixed-point arithmetic introduces failure modes that are often hidden by idealised quantisation models. In particular, two's-complement overflow wrapping can corrupt hidden activations by changing both their magnitude and sign, leading to unstable numerical error propagation and severe accuracy degradation. This paper proposes a Lyapunov-stabilised quantisation framework for low-precision neural networks operating under hardware-style wrapping arithmetic. The hidden-state energy is monitored through a layerwise Lyapunov function, and a monotone projection is applied to enforce bounded and non-increasing state evolution across depth. The method is evaluated on MNIST using a compact patch-based transformer under post-training quantisation and quantisation-aware training with fixed-point bit-widths from 4 to 16 bits. Monte Carlo results show that unconstrained wrapped quantisation-aware training collapses to near-chance accuracy across 6-16 bits, with activation overflow rates exceeding 11%. In contrast, the proposed monotone Lyapunov projection suppresses activation overflow to below 0.012% and restores stable low-precision learning, achieving 86.55% accuracy at 12 bits. These results demonstrate that Lyapunov-based state control can act as a hardware-aware stabilisation mechanism for reliable fixed-point neural inference and training.

AI.tldr: A Lyapunov-stabilized quantization framework prevents catastrophic failures in low-precision neural networks by enforcing bounded activation evolution through monotone projections, enabling reliable fixed-point training and inference.
AI.motivation: Low-precision neural networks are desirable for resource-constrained hardware, but fixed-point arithmetic introduces critical failure modes like two's-complement overflow wrapping that corrupt hidden activations. This leads to unstable error propagation and severe accuracy degradation, which are often hidden by idealized quantization models.
AI.method: The paper proposes a Lyapunov-stabilized quantization framework where hidden-state energy is monitored through layerwise Lyapunov functions. A monotone projection is applied to enforce bounded and non-increasing state evolution across network depth, preventing activation overflow and stabilizing numerical error propagation.
AI.result: Evaluation on MNIST using a compact patch-based transformer shows: unconstrained wrapped quantization-aware training collapses to near-chance accuracy (10%) across 6-16 bits with activation overflow rates >11%. The proposed monotone Lyapunov projection suppresses overflow to below 0.012% and restores stable learning, achieving 86.55% accuracy at 12 bits.
AI.conclusion: Lyapunov-based state control serves as an effective hardware-aware stabilization mechanism for reliable fixed-point neural inference and training, addressing critical overflow issues in low-precision networks using wrapping arithmetic.

title: Auto: The AGI Compiler
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables, 1 algorithm. Code: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/auto
summary: Every LLM agent run re-derives its behavior token by token on a frontier model: brilliant, expensive, slow, and unbounded. We present Auto, a compiler that records live agent behavior, measures which parts are secretly deterministic, extracts them into verified programs or distilled specialists, and emits cognition binaries: WebAssembly artifacts whose manifests carry measured guarantees and whose declared capabilities are physically enforced by the sandbox. A tiered runtime executes compiled behavior behind conformally calibrated guards; guard trips deopt to the reference agent, and the captured trace recompiles back down, so nothing is figured out twice. We use "AGI compiler" in one narrow, testable sense: a system that autonomously converts novel experience into permanent, verified, near-free skill while measuring what it does not know. On AUTO-BENCH, a benchmark we introduce and pre-register, 87.1% of 560 recorded frontier-agent spans are witnessed-deterministic (three of the four censused task families measure 100.0%). On a 300-item stream with three scheduled distribution shifts, the closed loop compiles three artifact generations and drives marginal cost from 59 to 2 micro-dollars per item (6.4x end-to-end) at 96.9% parity on witnessed inputs with zero errors. The same stream also quantifies the failure modes: a loose guard silently mislabels 48.9% of compiled answers, and an unfaithful deopt reference causes the verification gate to refuse recompilation. Calibration and reference fidelity, not model capability, decide whether cheap stays correct. Code: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/auto

AI.tldr: Auto is an AGI compiler that records LLM agent behavior, identifies deterministic patterns, compiles them into verified WebAssembly binaries, and creates a runtime system that efficiently executes compiled behavior while maintaining accuracy through guard mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents re-derive behavior token by token on expensive frontier models, making them slow, costly, and unbounded. There's a need to capture and compile deterministic agent behavior into efficient, verified programs that can be executed cheaply while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: Auto records live agent behavior, measures deterministic parts, extracts them into verified WebAssembly programs or distilled specialists. Creates a tiered runtime with calibrated guards that monitor execution - guard trips fall back to the reference agent, and captured traces recompile to improve. The system autonomously converts experience into permanent, verified skills while measuring uncertainty.
AI.result: On AUTO-BENCH (560 recorded frontier-agent spans), 87.1% were witnessed-deterministic (three of four task families measured 100%). On a 300-item stream with distribution shifts, the closed loop compiled three artifact generations, reducing marginal cost from 59 to 2 micro-dollars per item (6.4x end-to-end improvement) with 96.9% parity on witnessed inputs and zero errors.
AI.conclusion: Auto demonstrates that agent behavior can be compiled into efficient, verified binaries, dramatically reducing costs while maintaining accuracy. The key challenges are calibration and reference fidelity, not model capability. This represents a novel approach to "AGI compilation" - autonomously converting experience into permanent, near-free skills while quantifying uncertainty.

title: LeukocyteCount: Automatic Identification and Counting for leukocytes using Deep Learning
summary: Diagnosing and monitoring diseases frequently involves the analysis of human biological samples, with blood analysis being pivotal. Specifically, leukocytes, or white blood cells (WBCs), are essential markers for evaluating the body's defense mechanisms against infections. Traditional methods for WBC counting and classification are labor-intensive and prone to inaccuracies, primarily due to human error. The conventional processes for blood cell analysis, especially those concerning WBCs, are beset with difficulties. These include the laborious nature of manual counting and the susceptibility to errors, which can significantly impact the accuracy and reliability of disease diagnosis and monitoring. This study proposes an automated, machine learning-based solution aimed at mitigating the identified challenges. By employing a hybrid model that integrates Yolov5 for the detection of WBCs, coupled with a finely tuned, pre-trained MobileNetV2 model and a Logistic Regression classifier, the study innovates in the accurate identification, counting, and classification of WBCs into four distinct types. The methodology leverages the BCCD dataset for training and validation purposes. The application of the proposed hybrid machine learning model has yielded remarkable results, demonstrating a detection accuracy rate of 98\% through the Yolov5 stage, and an unparalleled classification accuracy of 99.04\% in subsequent stages utilizing MobileNetV2 and Logistic Regression. Additionally, Our proposed YOLOv5-based RBC detection module achieves an F1 score of 99.73\%, which outperforms the baseline. These findings underscore the model's potential in transforming traditional laboratory practices for WBC analysis, offering a path towards more accurate, efficient, and reliable disease diagnostics and monitoring.

AI.tldr: A hybrid ML model combining YOLOv5 for WBC detection and MobileNetV2+Logistic Regression for classification achieves 98% detection accuracy and 99.04% classification accuracy for white blood cell analysis.
AI.motivation: Traditional white blood cell (WBC) counting and classification methods are labor-intensive, prone to human error, and can significantly impact disease diagnosis accuracy. There's a need for automated solutions to improve reliability in medical diagnostics.
AI.method: A hybrid machine learning approach using YOLOv5 for WBC detection, followed by fine-tuned MobileNetV2 for feature extraction and Logistic Regression for classification into four WBC types. The BCCD dataset was used for training and validation.
AI.result: Achieved 98% detection accuracy with YOLOv5 and 99.04% classification accuracy with MobileNetV2+Logistic Regression. The RBC detection module also achieved 99.73% F1 score, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed automated system offers a highly accurate, efficient alternative to manual WBC analysis, with potential to transform traditional laboratory practices and improve disease diagnosis and monitoring reliability.

title: Predicting Therapeutic Outcome via Aligning Patient-Specific Knowledge Graph and Gene-Level Perturbation Representations
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables. Accepted at BIOKDD 2026, held in conjunction with ACM SIGKDD 2026
summary: Accurate prediction of patient-specific therapeutic response from pre-treatment transcriptomes is hindered by the scarcity of matched clinical response labels and post-treatment molecular profiles. Preclinical transfer-learning models can simulate drug-induced expression changes but are often hard to interpret and unstable, whereas knowledge-graph methods provide mechanistic context yet remain static and fail to capture drug-induced transcriptomic perturbation dynamics. We propose PREDIKTOR, a patient-centered multi-view framework that aligns a personalized network view with a transferable transcriptomic perturbation view to predict clinical drug response. For each patient, we construct an individualized gene regulatory network from tumor expression using DysRegNet and augment it with drug-target links from DrugBank; a graph neural encoder yields a drug-centric, mechanistically grounded embedding. In parallel, a frozen condition-specific gene-gene attention model pretrained on LINCS L1000 generates a simulated post-perturbation transcriptomic profile for the same patient-drug pair. We align the two views in a shared latent space via a CLIP-style contrastive objective with drug-context hard negatives, then concatenate the representations for end-to-end response classification. On TCGA, PREDIKTOR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under patient-, drug-, and tissue-split evaluations, and transfers zero-shot to the I-SPY2 trial, improving AUROC by 5.6% over competing methods. The aligned embeddings yield stable gene and pathway attributions that recover known mechanisms, supporting actionable and interpretable precision oncology.

AI.tldr: PREDIKTOR is a patient-centered multi-view framework that aligns personalized gene regulatory networks with simulated drug-induced transcriptomic changes to predict clinical drug response, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on TCGA and I-SPY2 trial data.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for predicting therapeutic response face limitations: matched clinical response labels and post-treatment molecular profiles are scarce; preclinical transfer-learning models that simulate drug effects are often hard to interpret and unstable; while knowledge-graph methods provide mechanistic context, they remain static and can't capture drug-induced transcriptomic perturbation dynamics.
AI.method: PREDIKTOR aligns two views: (1) A personalized gene regulatory network constructed from patient tumor expression using DysRegNet, augmented with drug-target links from DrugBank, encoded with a graph neural encoder for mechanistically grounded embeddings; (2) A simulated post-perturbation transcriptomic profile generated by a frozen condition-specific gene-gene attention model pretrained on LINCS L1000 data. The two views are aligned in a shared latent space using CLIP-style contrastive learning with drug-context hard negatives, then concatenated for end-to-end response classification.
AI.result: PREDIKTOR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on TCGA under patient-, drug-, and tissue-split evaluations, and transfers zero-shot to the I-SPY2 trial with a 5.6% AUROC improvement over competing methods. The aligned embeddings provide stable gene and pathway attributions that recover known mechanisms, supporting interpretable precision oncology.
AI.conclusion: PREDIKTOR successfully integrates mechanistic network biology with transcriptomic perturbation modeling to provide accurate, interpretable, and actionable drug response predictions, addressing key limitations of existing approaches through multi-view alignment that captures both personalized networks and drug-induced dynamics.

title: A Few Teacher Steps Go a Long Way: Cost-Efficient On-Policy Data Augmentation for Agent Post-Training
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026 Workshop: RLxF: Reinforcement Learning from World Feedback
summary: For LLM agents, supervised fine-tuning is not only about teacher labels' quality, but also about which interaction contexts those labels condition on. Pure behavioral cloning uses full teacher demonstrations, creating a mismatch between teacher-induced contexts seen in training and student-induced contexts encountered at test time. Recent work addresses this mismatch by querying a teacher at contexts reached by the student, often with increasingly elaborate filtering of the teacher's continuations. We instead frame on-policy data construction as a budget-allocation problem: under matched supervision resources, should teacher output be spent on more start-to-finish demos, longer continuations, outcome filtering, or broader coverage of learner-induced contexts? We formalize this design space through the rollout policy, switch-time distribution, continuation horizon, filtering rules, and two complementary costs: teacher inference generated before filtering and teacher supervision retained for SFT. Across HotpotQA, ALFWorld, and Terminal-Bench-Dev, bounded unfiltered teacher continuations at learner-induced contexts improve over pure behavioral cloning at matched budgets. On HotpotQA and ALFWorld, where we run the full comparison, few-step continuations match or exceed success-filtered and critical-context-filtered alternatives. Our findings suggest that a few teacher steps, placed at learner-induced contexts, can be a more cost-efficient supervision allocation than longer or more heavily curated teacher completions.

AI.tldr: On-policy data construction for LLM agents: under matched supervision budgets, unfiltered few-step teacher continuations at learner-induced contexts outperform pure behavioral cloning and filtered alternatives.
AI.motivation: Supervised fine-tuning for LLM agents suffers from a context mismatch - behavioral cloning uses full teacher demonstrations creating a discrepancy between teacher-induced contexts seen in training and student-induced contexts encountered at test time. Recent approaches address this by querying teachers at student contexts but involve increasingly complex filtering of teacher outputs.
AI.method: Frames on-policy data construction as a budget-allocation problem considering five design dimensions: rollout policy, switch-time distribution, continuation horizon, filtering rules, and two cost metrics (teacher inference before filtering and teacher supervision retained for SFT). Compares different supervision strategies under matched resources across HotpotQA, ALFWorld, and Terminal-Bench-Dev.
AI.result: Bounded unfiltered teacher continuations at learner-induced contexts improve over pure behavioral cloning at matched budgets. On HotpotQA and ALFWorld, few-step continuations match or exceed success-filtered and critical-context-filtered alternatives.
