In multi-GPU heterogeneous routing, however, where fast and slow requests compete for shared resources, the agent discovers a workload-segregation policy that eliminates Head-of-Line blocking, yielding a 3.5x (348%) improvement over Round-Robin and a 48% improvement over the strongest heuristic baseline (Shortest-Queue), with 60% higher throughput and 25% lower latency while respecting SLA constraints. The policy generalizes to unseen bursty and real-world traffic despite training only on synthetic Poisson arrivals and an attention-augmented policy network converges roughly 20% faster than an MLP baseline.
  These results suggest RL's advantage over engineered heuristics concentrates in combinatorial, multi-resource decisions rather than single-resource temporal scheduling, a practical distinction for deciding where learned policies justify their engineering cost in production inference infrastructure.

AI.tldr: RL-based adaptive batching/routing policies outperform static heuristics in multi-GPU inference serving but offer only marginal gains in single-GPU scenarios, showing RL's value concentrates in combinatorial multi-resource decisions.
AI.motivation: Current inference serving systems use static batching policies requiring manual tuning that cannot adapt to bursty, heterogeneous workloads. The paper investigates whether RL can learn better adaptive batching and routing policies.
AI.method: Train REINFORCE and PPO agents on a discrete-event simulator validated against queuing theory and production traces (Azure Functions, BurstGPT). Formulate as MDP over queue state, request type, and GPU availability. Evaluate across Poisson traffic, extreme bursts, real-world traces, and heterogeneous multi-GPU routing.
AI.result: In single-GPU: RL offers only marginal gains (+0.1% to +1.0%) as static batching is near-optimal. In multi-GPU heterogeneous routing: RL discovers workload-segregation policy eliminating Head-of-Line blocking, achieving 3.5x improvement over Round-Robin, 48% improvement over Shortest-Queue baseline, with 60% higher throughput and 25% lower latency while respecting SLA constraints.
AI.conclusion: RL's advantage over engineered heuristics concentrates in combinatorial, multi-resource decisions rather than single-resource temporal scheduling. This practical distinction helps decide where learned policies justify their engineering cost in production inference infrastructure.

title: Air Quality Downscaling with Station-Guided Pseudo-Supervision
summary: Super-resolving coarse atmospheric fields to local PM$_{2.5}$ variations is uniquely challenged by a mismatch in spatial support: while pixels represent regional averages, ground-truth observations are discrete, unaligned samples of a continuous spatial signal. To bridge this gap, we present a station-guided framework for high-resolution PM$_{2.5}$ downscaling over Europe. Taking coarse CAMS atmospheric composition fields alongside heterogeneous side information (i.e., human activity, land cover, elevation, satellite aerosol observations, and wind fields) our framework jointly super-resolves ($\times 40$, $\approx$ 1 km) and bias-corrects CAMS rasters, without relying on temporal sequence modelling. To address the challenge of densely supervising our multi-scale transformer network with sparse in-situ data, we introduce a time-agnostic propagation strategy that utilises spatial Gaussian blending of interpolated OpenAQ observations. Extensive qualitative and station-level evaluations across Europe demonstrate that our model recovers fine-grained spatial structures and effectively mitigates localised CAMS biases.

AI.tldr: A station-guided framework that super-resolves coarse atmospheric PM2.5 data (×40, ≈1 km) using multi-scale transformers with spatial Gaussian blending of sparse ground observations, enabling bias-corrected high-resolution PM2.5 mapping over Europe without temporal modeling.
AI.motivation: The core challenge is the spatial support mismatch between coarse atmospheric fields (regional averages) and sparse ground-truth observations (discrete samples), which prevents direct high-resolution PM2.5 downscaling. There's a need to bridge this gap for accurate local air quality assessment.
AI.method: Proposes a station-guided framework using multi-scale transformer networks to jointly super-resolve and bias-correct coarse CAMS atmospheric composition fields. The approach incorporates heterogeneous side information (human activity, land cover, elevation, satellite aerosol observations, wind fields) and addresses sparse supervision through time-agnostic spatial Gaussian blending of interpolated OpenAQ observations.
AI.result: The model successfully recovers fine-grained spatial structures and effectively mitigates localized CAMS biases across Europe, as demonstrated through extensive qualitative and station-level evaluations. It achieves 40× super-resolution (≈1 km resolution) without relying on temporal sequence modeling.
AI.conclusion: The presented framework successfully addresses the spatial support mismatch problem in PM2.5 downscaling, providing an effective method for generating high-resolution, bias-corrected air quality maps over Europe using sparse ground observations and coarse atmospheric data.

title: MARLIN: De Novo Molecular Structure Elucidation from Tandem Mass Spectra without a Ground-Truth Formula
summary: Untargeted tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) detects thousands of small molecules per biological sample, yet most go unidentified because they are absent from spectral libraries. These uncharacterized metabolites and natural products are precisely the compounds that matter for drug discovery, biomarker research, and exposomics. Computational de novo structure elucidation could close this gap, but almost all state-of-the-art methods assume the ground-truth molecular formula is known, an oracle that does not exist for genuinely novel compounds and is itself predicted with substantial error. We present MARLIN, a de novo method that elucidates structures directly from a spectrum with no molecular formula at any stage. A self-supervised encoder predicts a molecular fingerprint from the raw peaks, and a block-diffusion language model generates candidate structures conditioned only on the fingerprint and the instrument-measured precursor mass. A provably safe mass-shell constraint keeps every candidate consistent with the measured mass without fixing the atom inventory, and candidates are accepted by exact parts-per-million mass agreement. A symmetric noise objective absorbs encoder error, and a candidate-diversity mechanism keeps the candidates from collapsing to a single structure. On the NPLIB1 benchmark, MARLIN is the strongest method evaluated without a ground-truth formula across exact-match accuracy, structural distance, and fingerprint similarity, and it recovers the correct molecular formula as a byproduct about as often as a dedicated predictor without ever using one. MARLIN enables reliable de novo structure elucidation in the realistic discovery regime where the molecular formula is unavailable.

AI.tldr: MARLIN is a de novo structure elucidation method that predicts molecular structures directly from MS/MS spectra without needing molecular formulas, using self-supervised fingerprint prediction and block-diffusion generation with safety constraints.
AI.motivation: Current computational de novo methods assume known molecular formulas (which doesn't exist for novel compounds), creating a bottleneck in identifying uncharacterized metabolites and natural products crucial for drug discovery, biomarker research, and exposomics.
AI.method: MARLIN uses: 1) self-supervised encoder to predict molecular fingerprint from raw MS/MS peaks, 2) block-diffusion language model to generate structures conditioned only on fingerprint and precursor mass, 3) provable mass-shell constraint for consistency without fixing atom inventory, 4) symmetric noise objective to absorb encoder error, 5) candidate-diversity mechanism to prevent structural collapse.
AI.result: On NPLIB1 benchmark, MARLIN outperforms all methods without ground-truth formulas across exact-match accuracy, structural distance, and fingerprint similarity. It recovers correct molecular formulas as a byproduct at rates comparable to dedicated predictors without ever using formulas.
AI.conclusion: MARLIN enables reliable de novo structure elucidation in realistic discovery settings where molecular formulas are unavailable, closing the gap in identifying novel compounds from MS/MS data.

title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is effective when the current policy already samples useful reasoning trajectories, but it stalls on hard prompts whose correct solution modes lie outside the student's on-policy support. We propose TREK (Teacher-Routed Exploration via Forward KL), a simple staged procedure that uses distillation not for imitation but for exploration support expansion. A key advantage of TREK is its generality: because it only consumes verified output trajectories, it can use an external black-box teacher, a white-box teacher, or the same model given additional inference-time context, and it can efficiently identify which hard-prompt samples are most worth consolidating even when teacher internals are unavailable. TREK first identifies prompts where the unaided student has very low pass rate, queries a proposal source to produce verified candidate solutions, keeps the top-$r$ proposals ranked by current student likelihood, applies a short forward-KL phase to pull those verified modes into the student's support, and then returns to standard on-policy GRPO refinement. On mathematical reasoning, TREK with DeepSeek-V4 proposals improves Qwen3 models across all tested scales on AIME 2024 and AIME 2025; for Qwen3-8B, it improves AIME 2025 from 36.9 to 40.3 and AIME 2024 from 47.9 to 51.1 (avg@16), while the self-context variant reaches 38.5 and 49.6 without an external teacher. On agentic tasks, TREK raises ALFWorld success rate from 75.8 to 82.8 and ScienceWorld success rate from 12.5 to 26.7; notably, on the hardest task types, TREK achieves high success rates early in training while unaided GRPO requires substantially more optimization steps to reach comparable levels.

AI.tldr: TREK (Teacher-Routed Exploration via Forward KL) is a staged method that uses verified solutions from a teacher model to expand the student policy's exploration support for hard prompts, improving performance on reasoning and agentic tasks.
AI.motivation: GRPO stalls on hard prompts where correct solutions lie outside the student policy's on-policy support, needing a way to expand exploration without requiring access to teacher internals.
AI.method: TREK first identifies low-success prompts, queries verified solutions from any source (black-box teacher, white-box, or self-context), ranks them by student likelihood, applies forward-KL to pull verified modes into student's support, then returns to standard GRPO refinement.
AI.result: Improves Qwen3 models on AIME 2024/2025 math reasoning (e.g., Qwen3-8B: AIME 2025 from 36.9 to 40.3; AIME 2024 from 47.9 to 51.1); self-context variant reaches 38.5 and 49.6. On agentic tasks: ALFWorld from 75.8 to 82.8, ScienceWorld from 12.5 to 26.7, achieving high success rates earlier than GRPO.
AI.conclusion: TREK effectively addresses GRPO's exploration limitation by using verified trajectories to expand policy support, achieving significant improvements across reasoning and agentic tasks with broad applicability to different teacher types.

title: Towards Personalized Differentially Private Learning for Decentralized Local Graphs
summary: Graph-structured data is increasingly generated and stored in decentralized environments, such as social platforms, mobile applications, and edge networks, where users maintain control over their local graph data. However, collecting and analyzing such decentralized graph data for downstream learning tasks raises significant privacy concerns, as nodes and their attributes often contain sensitive personal information. Local Differential Privacy (LDP) has emerged as a promising solution for privacy-preserving data collection without relying on trusted servers. Nevertheless, existing LDP-based graph learning methods typically assume uniform privacy requirements across users, ignoring the heterogeneous and personalized privacy preferences commonly observed in real-world systems. This uniform treatment leads to inflexible noise injection at the data collection stage, resulting in substantial distortion of graph data and degraded utility in subsequent analysis. To address this limitation, we propose PPGNN, a personalized differentially private framework for decentralized graph data. PPGNN enables user-specific privacy budgets during local perturbation while preserving analytical utility. To handle heterogeneous privacy levels and noise distortion, we design a two-stage solution consisting of a Personalized Perturbation Mechanism (PPM) and a weighted calibration strategy, FlexProp. Extensive experiments on six real-world graph datasets demonstrate that PPGNN effectively balances personalized privacy protection and data utility in decentralized graph learning scenarios.

AI.tldr: PPGNN is a personalized differentially private framework for decentralized graph data that allows user-specific privacy budgets, addressing heterogeneous privacy preferences while maintaining data utility through personalized perturbation and weighted calibration.
AI.motivation: Decentralized graph data (from social platforms, mobile apps, edge networks) raises privacy concerns as nodes contain sensitive personal information. Existing LDP-based methods assume uniform privacy requirements, ignoring heterogeneous/personalized privacy preferences in real systems, leading to excessive noise and degraded utility.
AI.method: PPGNN framework with two-stage solution: 1) Personalized Perturbation Mechanism (PPM) for user-specific privacy budgets during local perturbation, and 2) FlexProp weighted calibration strategy to handle heterogeneous privacy levels and noise distortion.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six real-world graph datasets demonstrate that PPGNN effectively balances personalized privacy protection and data utility in decentralized graph learning scenarios.
AI.conclusion: PPGNN addresses the limitation of uniform privacy assumptions in existing LDP-based graph learning methods by enabling personalized privacy protection that adapts to heterogeneous user preferences while maintaining analytical utility in decentralized environments.

title: What Does a Discrete Diffusion Model Learn?
summary: What does a discrete diffusion model learn: a denoiser, a score ratio, or a bridge plug-in predictor? At the level of jump rates, these are one object in different coordinates, and reading a neural network in the wrong coordinate changes the process being trained and sampled. Starting with a rigorous derivation of the continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) ELBO for any noising process, boundary terms included, we prove the \emph{Oracle Distance} theorem: the negative ELBO is exactly equal to the data entropy plus the path KL from the oracle reverse process to the learned one, not merely a bound. Its unique optimizer is therefore the conditional expectation of the true reverse jump rate given the current noisy state, and its irreducible cost is the rate at which the forward process $Z_t$ destroys information about the clean data $Z_0$, $-\tfrac{d}{dt}I(Z_0; Z_t)$, so every noising process shares the same best achievable negative ELBO: the data entropy. For sequences with token-factorizing noise, the oracle projection yields three exact coordinates for the optimizer: denoiser, cavity (bridge plug-in), and score, with closed-form conversions among them. This framework identifies which law each loss in the literature actually optimizes, recovering MDM, UDM, SEDD, and GIDD as special cases; explains why denoiser and cavity coincide for masked diffusion but not for uniform diffusion; proves that a denoiser parameterization makes the uniform ELBO diverge at initialization while the bridge plug-in stays finite; and calibrates ELBO implementations exactly at initialization. Every identity is verified numerically, without approximation, on an exactly solvable model.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that discrete diffusion models optimize to match the true reverse jump rates, and different parameterizations (denoiser, score, bridge plug-in) are equivalent coordinates of the same optimal solution. It proves that the negative ELBO equals data entropy plus a KL divergence to the oracle reverse process.
AI.motivation: To understand what discrete diffusion models actually learn and how different parameterizations (denoiser, score ratio, bridge plug-in predictor) relate to each other. The motivation is to provide a rigorous theoretical framework that unifies different discrete diffusion approaches and explains their mathematical relationships.
AI.method: Derives a rigorous continuous-time Markov chain ELBO with boundary terms, proves the "Oracle Distance" theorem showing negative ELBO equals data entropy plus path KL divergence, analyzes different parameterizations of the optimizer (denoiser, cavity, score), and provides exact coordinate conversions. Validates identities on exactly solvable models.
AI.result: 1) The optimal learned reverse process equals the conditional expectation of the true reverse jump rate. 2) Different parameterizations (denoiser, cavity, score) are equivalent coordinates. 3) Framework unifies MDM, UDM, SEDD, and GIDD as special cases. 4) Explains differences between masked and uniform diffusion. 5) Proves initialization issues with denoiser parameterization for uniform diffusion.
AI.conclusion: Discrete diffusion models learn the conditional expectation of the true reverse jump rates, and different parameterizations are mathematically equivalent representations. The paper provides a unified theoretical framework that clarifies what discrete diffusion models optimize and how different implementations relate.

title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization via Direct On-Policy Distillation
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is a powerful recipe for improving language-model reasoning, but it is expensive to repeat on every new strong model because the target model must generate many rollouts during training. As models scale, post-training itself becomes a bottleneck. We study a weak-to-strong alternative: run RL on a smaller model where rollouts are cheaper, then reuse what that RL run learned to improve a stronger target model. Directly distilling the post-RL weak teacher is not enough, because the teacher's final policy mixes useful RL gains with the limitations of the smaller model. We propose Direct On-Policy Distillation (Direct-OPD), which transfers the teacher's RL-induced policy shift instead. Direct-OPD compares the post-RL teacher with its own pre-RL reference and treats their log-ratio as a dense implicit reward for the student. In plain terms, the checkpoint pair tells us which actions RL made the weak model more or less likely to take, and Direct-OPD applies that signal on the stronger student's own on-policy states. This directly reuses the weak model's RL supervision signal without training an explicit reward model or running sparse-reward RL on the target model. Empirically, Direct-OPD consistently leverages weaker teachers to improve stronger target models; notably, it boosts Qwen3-1.7B from 48.3% to 62.4% on AIME 2024 in just 4 hours on 8 A100 GPUs. It outperforms step-matched direct RL and enables the sequential composition of multiple policy shifts. Our results show that RL outcomes can be reused across model scales as implicit reward signals, not merely as final models to imitate.

AI.tldr: Direct-OPD transfers RL-induced policy shifts from weak models to stronger ones by using log-ratio of post-RL vs pre-RL teacher policies as implicit dense reward, avoiding expensive RL retraining on large models.
AI.motivation: RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is expensive to repeat on every new strong model due to costly rollouts during training, creating a post-training bottleneck as models scale up.
AI.method: Propose Direct On-Policy Distillation (Direct-OPD): uses checkpoint pair (post-RL weak teacher and its pre-RL reference), computes their log-ratio as dense implicit reward, applies this signal to stronger student model's on-policy states to transfer RL-induced policy shift.
AI.result: Consistently improves stronger target models using weaker teachers; boosts Qwen3-1.7B from 48.3% to 62.4% on AIME 2024 in 4 hours on 8 A100 GPUs; outperforms step-matched direct RL; enables sequential composition of multiple policy shifts.
AI.conclusion: RL outcomes can be effectively reused across model scales as implicit reward signals rather than just final models to imitate, providing efficient weak-to-strong transfer.

title: Compressed Computation under $L^4$ Loss is likely Computation in Superposition
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, 1 table + 2 pages, 5 figures, 1 table appendix
summary: Neural networks are thought to represent concepts as directions in their activation space, and superposition lets them encode more concepts than they have dimensions. It is natural to ask whether they can also compute more functions than they have neurons, i.e., perform computation in superposition. In this regime many functions of sparse inputs are evaluated by a layer with fewer neurons than there are functions to compute. Representation in superposition is by now fairly well understood, but computation in superposition is not, and there are few toy models of it arising through training rather than being hand designed. As a toy model of computation in superposition we study the compressed-computation setup: a single-hidden-layer ReLU network with 50 neurons that must compute the ReLU of each of 100 sparse input features. We show that training it under an $L^4$ loss (the mean fourth power of the error), rather than the usual $L^2$, elicits a solution that appears to compute all features in superposition. We then reverse-engineer this solution. We find that the network assigns each feature a sparse binary codeword over neurons and decodes it with a pseudoinverse of the encoder. Given these codewords, a description with only three scalars recovers most of the network's performance, and we validate it by building equivalent networks from hand-designed codes.

AI.tldr: Training neural networks under L4 loss instead of L2 loss enables computation in superposition, allowing them to compute more functions than they have neurons by using sparse binary codewords.
AI.motivation: To understand whether neural networks can perform computation in superposition - evaluating more functions than they have neurons - similar to how they represent more concepts than dimensions through superposition. Current understanding of representation in superposition exists but computation in superposition is not well understood.
AI.method: Studied compressed-computation setup: single-hidden-layer ReLU network with 50 neurons that must compute ReLU of each of 100 sparse input features. Trained network under L4 loss (mean fourth power of error) rather than standard L2 loss. Reverse-engineered the solution to understand computation mechanisms.
AI.result: L4 loss elicits solution that appears to compute all features in superposition. Network assigns each feature sparse binary codewords over neurons and decodes with pseudoinverse of encoder. Solution can be described with only three scalars while maintaining most performance, validated by building equivalent networks from hand-designed codes.
AI.conclusion: Computation in superposition is achievable through specific training objectives (L4 loss), using sparse binary codewords and pseudoinverse decoding, enabling networks to compute more functions than their neuron count would suggest.

title: Layer-Parallel Inference Reduces Encrypted Nonlinear Depth in Transformers
summary: Fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) enables computation on encrypted data, but practical encrypted Transformer inference is bottlenecked by the sequential composition of many nonlinear blocks. We study whether Structured Newton Layer Parallelism (SNLP) can make this inter-layer composition more FHE-friendly: each Transformer block still requires polynomial approximations for operations such as softmax and RMSNorm, but SNLP reduces the layerwise sequential nonlinear depth from L stages to a small number of solver iterations plus linear structured corrections. Using a simulation framework based on Chebyshev polynomial approximations, we measure error accumulation under sequential versus SNLP inference across 8 models and 4 architecture families. On a 0.5B IDN-trained model, SNLP reduces symbolic bootstraps from 53 to 20 (2.65x) with only +1.2% perplexity degradation, while lowering error amplification (1.36x vs. 1.42x). Across all tested models, SNLP has lower amplification than sequential inference. Ablations show that softmax approximation dominates the error budget and CKKS arithmetic noise is negligible in our setting, suggesting that SNLP is complementary to block-level FHE-friendly operator design rather than a replacement for it.

AI.tldr: SNLP reduces FHE inference bottlenecks by converting sequential nonlinear Transformer layers into parallel structured Newton iterations, cutting bootstraps 2.65x with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Fully homomorphic encryption enables computation on encrypted data, but practical encrypted Transformer inference is bottlenecked by the sequential composition of many nonlinear blocks that require polynomial approximations, creating significant computational overhead.
AI.method: Structured Newton Layer Parallelism (SNLP) reduces layerwise sequential nonlinear depth from L stages to a small number of solver iterations plus linear structured corrections. The method was tested using a simulation framework based on Chebyshev polynomial approximations across 8 models and 4 architecture families.
AI.result: On a 0.5B IDN-trained model, SNLP reduces symbolic bootstraps from 53 to 20 (2.65x improvement) with only +1.2% perplexity degradation. Error amplification is lower (1.36x vs 1.42x). Across all tested models, SNLP consistently shows lower amplification than sequential inference.
AI.conclusion: SNLP is complementary to block-level FHE-friendly operator design rather than a replacement for it, as softmax approximation dominates the error budget while CKKS arithmetic noise is negligible. The approach effectively reduces FHE inference bottlenecks while maintaining accuracy.

title: KinEMbed: Decoding Kinematics from Electromyography via Cross-Modal Contrastive Learning
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Structured Data for Health, Seoul, South Korea
summary: Decoding hand kinematics from surface electromyography (EMG) is a core challenge in wearable biosignal processing with clinical relevance for prosthetic control and motor rehabilitation. Most representation learning approaches for EMG focus on discrete gesture classification, and few focus on continuous regression. We present KinEMbed, a cross-modal contrastive learning framework for hand kinematics regression that jointly trains dual encoders -- one for windowed EMG features and one for kinematic (joint angle) targets. The resulting embeddings inherit the geometric structure of the kinematic space without requiring kinematic signals at inference time. Evaluating on the NinaPro DB8 dataset that includes both able-bodied users and subjects with limb difference (N=11), KinEMbed outperforms PCA, PLS, autoencoder and contrastive (CEBRA) baselines on held-out sessions, with largest gains on the most challenging thumb degrees of articulation. We position this work as a first step toward contrastive representation learning for regression of hand kinematics from structured wearable biosignals.

AI.tldr: KinEMbed: A cross-modal contrastive learning framework that learns joint embeddings from EMG signals and kinematic targets to enable continuous hand kinematics regression without requiring kinematic signals at inference time.
AI.motivation: Most EMG representation learning approaches focus on discrete gesture classification, while continuous regression of hand kinematics is under-explored despite its clinical relevance for prosthetic control and motor rehabilitation.
AI.method: Uses cross-modal contrastive learning with dual encoders - one for windowed EMG features and one for kinematic (joint angle) targets. The framework learns embeddings that inherit the geometric structure of kinematic space without requiring kinematic signals during inference.
AI.result: On the NinaPro DB8 dataset (N=11, including both able-bodied users and subjects with limb difference), KinEMbed outperforms PCA, PLS, autoencoder and CEBRA baselines on held-out sessions, with the largest performance gains observed on the most challenging thumb degrees of articulation.
AI.conclusion: This work represents a first step toward contrastive representation learning for regression of hand kinematics from structured wearable biosignals, demonstrating improved performance for continuous motion decoding from EMG signals.

title: Probably Correct Optimal Stable Matching under Two-Sided Uncertainty
summary: We study a sequential learning problem for stable matchings in two-sided markets where preferences on both sides are initially unknown. We focus on a centralized setting where an algorithm matches agents at each time step and receives noisy rewards that reflect the preferences of the matched agents, following a semi-bandit feedback structure. We adopt a pure exploration perspective, aiming to efficiently identify the optimal stable matching with high probability. Our work extends prior results by handling \emph{two-sided uncertainty} and by exploiting \emph{partial preference} information. A central ingredient is the notion of \textbf{pervasive stable matching}, which enables the identification of optimal stable matchings under partial preferences. We propose elimination-based algorithms whose stopping criteria exploit the structure of the learned partial preferences, and provide a refined sample-complexity analysis. Beyond pure exploration, we extend our approach to regret minimization and establish regret bounds with respect to the \emph{optimal} stable matching that avoid dependence on the minimum reward gap $Δ_{\min}$.

AI.tldr: Pure exploration algorithms for learning optimal stable matchings in two-sided markets with unknown preferences using semi-bandit feedback, with extensions to regret minimization.
AI.motivation: Most prior work on learning stable matchings assumes preferences are known on at least one side, but in real-world scenarios, preferences on both sides are often unknown. The paper aims to handle this two-sided uncertainty while exploiting partial preference information that can be learned during the process.
AI.method: Uses elimination-based algorithms with semi-bandit feedback (receiving noisy rewards for matched pairs). Introduces the concept of "pervasive stable matching" to identify optimal stable matchings under partial preferences. The algorithms stop when they can confidently identify the optimal matching based on learned partial preferences.
AI.result: Develops sample-complexity bounds for pure exploration that leverage the structure of partial preferences. For regret minimization, establishes regret bounds that avoid dependence on the minimum reward gap Δ_min, providing better performance guarantees than traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully addresses the challenging problem of learning optimal stable matchings with two-sided uncertainty, offering both sample-efficient pure exploration algorithms and improved regret bounds for online learning settings.

title: Representing and Detecting Label Ambiguity in IMU-Based Exercise Evaluation
summary: Home-based physiotherapy is performed without supervision, which leads to incorrect execution and motivates systems that assess movement automatically from inertial measurement units (IMUs). Such systems assign each repetition to a category, yet a relevant share of repetitions falls near a class boundary, where even trained raters disagree. Classifiers trained with one-hot labels collapse these borderline repetitions onto a single class and discard this ambiguity. We address this with a method that automatically generates a label distribution per repetition without a large rater pool. We train a network to reproduce the full distribution with a Kullback-Leibler objective, the ambiguity approach, and compare it against a one-hot cross-entropy baseline on four IMU exercise datasets. From the network output we further determine whether a repetition is ambiguous and which classes are relevant to it. The ambiguity approach matched or exceeded the baseline classification on all four datasets, and detected ambiguity and the relevant classes more reliably. Representing the label distribution in the training target therefore adds information about ambiguity at no cost to classification.

AI.tldr: A method to handle ambiguous exercise repetitions in IMU-based movement assessment by generating label distributions instead of one-hot labels, improving both classification and ambiguity detection.
AI.motivation: Home-based physiotherapy lacks supervision, leading to incorrect exercise execution. Current systems using IMUs classify repetitions into categories, but borderline repetitions near class boundaries create ambiguity where even trained raters disagree. One-hot labels discard this ambiguity information.
AI.method: Proposed method automatically generates a label distribution per repetition without needing a large rater pool. Trains a network with Kullback-Leibler divergence objective to reproduce full label distributions (ambiguity approach), compared against one-hot cross-entropy baseline. Network output identifies ambiguous repetitions and relevant classes.
AI.result: The ambiguity approach matched or exceeded baseline classification on all four IMU exercise datasets. It also detected ambiguity and relevant classes more reliably than the baseline method.
AI.conclusion: Representing label distributions in training targets adds information about ambiguity without compromising classification performance, providing more informative assessments for unsupervised exercise execution.

authors: Wil M. P. van der Aalst
title: Framework for Grouping Local Process Models
summary: Local Process Models (LPMs) are an underexplored concept in process mining. LPMs describe patterns in event data considering sequence, choice, concurrency, and loop. In recent years, process mining has proved successful in the analysis and improvement of operational processes. More often than not, surprising findings are found when one does not consider the full process, making LPMs and their discovery highly valuable. However, similar to other pattern mining approaches, LPM discovery algorithms face the problems of model explosion and model repetition, i.e., the algorithms may create hundreds if not thousands of LPMs, and subsets of them are close in structure or behavior. Practically, no analyst would be able to comb through thousands of LPMs leading to using a sample of LPMs that are easily accessible. The current sentiment is that the top-scoring LPMs form the optimal sample to be presented. However, different applications should demand a different optimal sample. With this work, we show that if the goal of the mined LPMs is to understand a process, using the top-scoring LPMs as an optimal sample is a poor choice because of high repetition. We propose a framework for grouping LPMs and creating an optimal sample by taking one representative LPM for each group. We measure similarity between models via established process model similarity measures or by comparing the context in which an LPM appears. The context is formed using data attributes available in the underlying event logs. We demonstrate the usefulness of grouping on multiple event logs by comparing repetition and coverage between samples comprised of the top-scoring models and the representatives of discovered groups.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework for grouping Local Process Models (LPMs) to create optimal representative samples, addressing the problem of model explosion and repetition in LPM discovery algorithms.
AI.motivation: LPM discovery algorithms produce hundreds to thousands of models with high structural/behavioral repetition, making practical analysis impossible. Current practice uses top-scoring LPMs as samples, but this leads to poor understanding of processes due to high repetition and may not be optimal for different applications.
AI.method: Proposes a framework for grouping LPMs and creating optimal samples by taking one representative LPM per group. Measures similarity between models using established process model similarity measures or by comparing context in which LPM appears (using data attributes from event logs).
AI.result: Demonstrates usefulness of grouping on multiple event logs by comparing repetition and coverage between samples of top-scoring models versus representatives of discovered groups. Shows that using top-scoring LPMs as optimal samples is poor for process understanding due to high repetition.
AI.conclusion: Grouping LPMs and selecting representatives provides better sample quality than using top-scoring LPMs alone, addressing model explosion and repetition problems in LPM discovery while improving practical usability for process analysis.

title: Enhancing the Forecasting Capability of Multi-Model Blending Algorithms for Extreme Precipitation via Joint Use of Station and Gridded Observations
summary: Accurate extreme precipitation forecasting is critical for disaster mitigation but remains challenging for numerical weather prediction (NWP) models due to systemic intensity underestimation and spatial displacement. Traditional precipitation multi-model blending algorithms perform pixel-by-pixel blending on the forecast field based on weights, which may lead to the expansion of precipitation areas and the smoothing of extreme values. This study proposes an U-Net based two-stage framework: probability classification followed by value reconstruction, to blend forecasts from six major NWP models. A novel station-grid joint supervision mechanism is introduced by integrating observations from 2411 national meteorological stations in China into the loss function, simultaneously constraining spatial structures and peak intensities. Evaluations using independent samples from the 2025 flood season demonstrate that our model significantly outperforms both individual NWPs and current operational products. For rainstorms (>=50 mm), the Threat Score (TS) improved by 38.4% compared to the best NWP. Notably, for extreme events (>=100 mm) driven by extratropical cyclones and the subtropical high, the model successfully elevated the TS to above 0.1, transforming forecasts from having negligible reference value into those with certain operational utility. Furthermore, the model exhibits data-driven spatial correction capabilities, effectively realigning systematic rainbelt displacements with actual precipitation centers. The inclusion of station observations specifically enhanced the TS for rainstorms by 10.4% and effectively balanced the Bias. These results highlight the efficacy of multi-source joint supervision in enhancing the capture of extreme precipitation events.

AI.tldr: A two-stage U-Net framework improves extreme precipitation forecasting by blending multiple NWP models with station-grid joint supervision, significantly enhancing performance for heavy rainfall events.
AI.motivation: Current NWP models systematically underestimate extreme precipitation intensity and misplace precipitation areas. Traditional multi-model blending methods cause precipitation area expansion and extreme value smoothing.
AI.method: Proposes a U-Net based two-stage framework: probability classification followed by value reconstruction to blend forecasts from six NWP models. Introduces station-grid joint supervision by integrating 2411 station observations into loss function to constrain spatial structures and peak intensities.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms individual NWP models and operational products. For rainstorms (>=50 mm), TS improved by 38.4% vs best NWP. For extreme events (>=100 mm), TS elevated above 0.1, transforming forecasts from negligible to operationally useful. Station observations improved rainstorm TS by 10.4% and balanced Bias.
AI.conclusion: The station-grid joint supervision framework effectively enhances extreme precipitation capture, demonstrates data-driven spatial correction capabilities, and shows efficacy of multi-source supervision for operational forecasting improvement.

title: Active Learning on Adversarially Corrupted Graphs
summary: Motivated by real-world scenarios where malicious entities tamper with existing networks, we define a model where an adversary seeks to hide a set of \emph{corrupted vertices} inside a graph $G^*$. To this end, the adversary can add edges between the corrupted vertices, as well as edges between the corrupted vertices and $G^*$, and its power is then measured by the size of the \emph{neighborhood} of the corrupted vertices in $G^*$. Our goal is to design an active learning algorithm that efficiently finds the subset of corrupted vertices using a small number of label queries. We devise an efficient algorithm that approximately recovers the corrupted vertices with a query complexity that depends polynomially on both the power of the adversary and the \emph{vertex expansion} of $G^*$, a fundamental measure of graph connectivity. At the heart of this result is a polynomial-time algorithm, obtained by carefully adapting sum-of-squares algorithms for approximating minimum expansion, that finds a set with small vertex expansion subject to cardinality constraints. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the vertex expansion is shown to play a key role in determining the query complexity of active learning algorithms robust to structural adversarial attacks.

AI.tldr: An active learning algorithm that efficiently detects corrupted vertices in graphs under adversarial manipulation using limited label queries, with query complexity depending on adversary power and vertex expansion.
AI.motivation: Addresses real-world scenarios where malicious actors tamper with existing networks by hiding corrupted vertices. The adversary can add edges between corrupted vertices and to the original graph, making detection challenging.
AI.method: Designs an active learning algorithm that finds corrupted vertices with limited label queries. The approach adapts sum-of-squares algorithms to find sets with small vertex expansion subject to cardinality constraints, using polynomial-time algorithms that approximate minimum expansion.
AI.result: Develops an efficient algorithm that approximately recovers corrupted vertices with query complexity polynomial in both adversary power and vertex expansion of the original graph. Shows vertex expansion is crucial for determining query complexity in robust active learning against structural adversarial attacks.
AI.conclusion: Vertex expansion plays a key role in determining query complexity for active learning algorithms robust to structural adversarial attacks, enabling efficient corruption detection with polynomial query complexity based on adversary power and graph connectivity.

title: RL-Ballast: Ship Ballast Water Path Planning and Clog Prediction via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Under the Shipping 4.0 paradigm, autonomous and reduced-crew vessels require intelligent internal systems to maintain operational safety and structural stability. Ballast-water control is essential for ship trim and integrity, but conventional rule-based or manual approaches have limited adaptability to hydraulic anomalies such as valve failures and pipe blockages, and often depend on dense pressure or flow sensors for diagnosis. To address these limitations, this paper proposes RL-Ballast, a graph-based deep reinforcement learning framework for adaptive ballast-water path planning and sensor-frugal blockage candidate scoring. The valve-permutation problem is transformed into 54 feasible fluid-transfer routes generated using graph theory and depth-first search. The partially observable ballast environment is approximated with frame-stacked tank levels and action outcomes, allowing the agent to infer hidden blockage effects without explicitly modeling a high-dimensional POMDP. During deterministic inference, episode-level failed-action memory and dynamic action masking prevent repeated ineffective actions and support immediate rerouting. Failed transfer histories are further accumulated to rank suspicious valves or pipe segments without dense instrumentation. Monte Carlo simulations show that RL-Ballast completes all unexpected single-blockage scenarios and reduces average decision steps from 61.0 to 41.5 compared with a Dijkstra rule-based baseline. For diagnostic support, the failure-history scoring scheme achieves a 100% Top-3 hit rate, a 66.7% strict Top-1 hit rate, and an 83.3% Top-1 tie-hit rate under serially indistinguishable blockage conditions. These results suggest that RL-Ballast enables adaptive rerouting and maintenance-oriented blockage diagnosis under limited sensing conditions.

AI.tldr: RL-Ballast: A graph-based deep reinforcement learning framework for adaptive ballast-water path planning and blockage diagnosis in autonomous ships, enabling efficient rerouting and sensor-frugal fault identification.
AI.motivation: Shipping 4.0 requires intelligent internal systems for autonomous vessels. Conventional rule-based or manual ballast-water control has limited adaptability to hydraulic anomalies (valve failures, pipe blockages) and often depends on dense sensor arrays for diagnosis, which is impractical and expensive.
AI.method: Transform valve-permutation problem into 54 feasible fluid-transfer routes using graph theory and depth-first search. Use frame-stacked tank levels and action outcomes to approximate partially observable environment. Implement episode-level failed-action memory and dynamic action masking to prevent repeated ineffective actions and support immediate rerouting. Accumulate failed transfer histories to rank suspicious valves/pipe segments without dense instrumentation.
AI.result: RL-Ballast completes all unexpected single-blockage scenarios in Monte Carlo simulations, reducing average decision steps from 61.0 to 41.5 compared with Dijkstra rule-based baseline. Failure-history scoring achieves 100% Top-3 hit rate, 66.7% strict Top-1 hit rate, and 83.3% Top-1 tie-hit rate under serially indistinguishable blockage conditions.
AI.conclusion: RL-Ballast enables adaptive rerouting and maintenance-oriented blockage diagnosis under limited sensing conditions, demonstrating effectiveness for intelligent ballast-water management in autonomous vessels and addressing key limitations of conventional approaches.

title: When Do Foundation Models Pay Off? A Break-Even Analysis of Pretrained Time Series Forecasters
summary: Deploying a time series foundation model requires GPU infrastructure, engineering overhead, and carries no guarantee of improvement over XGBoost. We provide the first systematic break-even analysis answering when this investment pays off. Across 30 benchmark datasets, we compare zero-shot and LoRA fine-tuned foundation models (Chronos, Moirai, Lag-Llama) against classical baselines (Naive, ETS, ARIMA, XGBoost) at six training set sizes from 2% to 100% of available data. Foundation models outperform classical methods at every evaluated training fraction on 15 of 30 datasets -- GPU deployment is unconditionally justified on these regardless of data volume. On 6 datasets, classical methods surpass zero-shot foundation models with as little as 2% of training data (21-2,768 samples); on the remaining 9, break-even ranges from 24 to 8,361 samples. One robust deployment rule requires no model training: if n_train < 700 and seasonality is non-negligible, use FM zero-shot and skip fine-tuning -- this resolves 10 of 30 deployment decisions immediately. Contrary to common practice, LoRA fine-tuning can actively degrade performance on short series. We operationalise these findings as a two-step decision framework -- compute dataset length and seasonality strength, run a brief 5-10% pilot only if needed -- enabling practitioners to make the FM-versus-classical decision before committing to full infrastructure. Four dataset features motivate mechanistic hypotheses for the remaining cases, though reliable automated prediction at this benchmark scale remains an open problem. Code, benchmark, and decision tools are available at https://github.com/nicolaisi/fm-breakeven.

AI.tldr: Foundation models don't always beat classical time series methods like XGBoost - they only outperform on 15/30 datasets, while classical methods can be better with small datasets. A simple decision framework using dataset size and seasonality can guide when to use foundation models vs traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Deploying time series foundation models requires significant GPU infrastructure and engineering overhead, but there's no guarantee they'll outperform simpler classical methods like XGBoost. The paper addresses when this investment actually pays off through systematic break-even analysis.
AI.method: Conducted systematic comparison across 30 benchmark datasets, evaluating zero-shot and LoRA fine-tuned foundation models (Chronos, Moirai, Lag-Llama) against classical baselines (Naive, ETS, ARIMA, XGBoost) at six training set sizes from 2% to 100% of available data.
AI.result: Foundation models outperform classical methods at all training fractions on 15 of 30 datasets (justifying GPU deployment unconditionally on these). Classical methods beat zero-shot foundation models with as little as 2% training data on 6 datasets. LoRA fine-tuning can degrade performance on short series. Developed a two-step decision framework based on dataset length and seasonality strength.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models don't always beat classical methods - the decision depends on dataset characteristics. The proposed decision framework allows practitioners to make informed FM-vs-classical decisions before committing to infrastructure. Simple rules like "if n_train < 700 and seasonality is non-negligible, use FM zero-shot" can resolve many deployment decisions.

title: Lightweight ML-Based Automatic Sleep Staging Framework with Constrained CNN and Mamba for Small-Sample EEG Datasets
summary: Automatic sleep staging is a key technology for precise diagnosis and treatment of sleep disorders as well as long-term home sleep monitoring. Portable electroencephalogram (EEG) devices have become the focus of research due to their convenience in data collection. However, current methods still face three major challenges: large parameter sizes that easily lead to overfitting on small datasets, low accuracy in classifying difficult stages such as N1 and REM, unclear optimal training dataset size, and difficulty in deployment. This paper proposes GamSleepNet, a lightweight and low-latency automatic sleep staging framework for single-channel EEG. The framework features the FEB module, which combines improved Gabor kernels with learnable filters for feature extraction, uses the Mamba architecture to build a temporal classification network, introduces a novel contrastive loss and a two-stage training strategy, and experimentally validates the optimal dataset size for single-channel EEG sleep staging models. On the Sleepedf dataset, this model achieves an overall accuracy of 87.86 percent with only 30.86 thousand parameters, with all metrics reaching SOTA levels and significantly improving the identification accuracy of challenging sleep stages.

AI.tldr: GamSleepNet is a lightweight, low-latency automatic sleep staging framework for single-channel EEG that achieves SOTA performance with only 30.86K parameters, using Gabor filters, Mamba architecture, contrastive loss, and optimized training strategy.
AI.motivation: Current automatic sleep staging methods face challenges: large parameter sizes causing overfitting on small datasets, low accuracy for difficult stages (N1 and REM), unclear optimal training dataset size, and deployment difficulties. Portable EEG devices need lightweight, accurate solutions for home sleep monitoring and clinical diagnosis.
AI.method: Proposes GamSleepNet framework with: 1) FEB module combining improved Gabor kernels with learnable filters for feature extraction, 2) Mamba architecture for temporal classification network, 3) Novel contrastive loss and two-stage training strategy, 4) Experimental validation of optimal dataset size for single-channel EEG sleep staging.
AI.result: Achieves 87.86% overall accuracy on Sleepedf dataset with only 30.86 thousand parameters, reaching SOTA levels on all metrics. Significantly improves identification accuracy of challenging sleep stages (N1 and REM).
AI.conclusion: GamSleepNet provides an effective lightweight solution for automatic sleep staging that addresses key challenges in parameter efficiency, difficult stage classification, and deployment practicality for single-channel EEG applications.

title: Sensitivity Sampling with Predictions for k-Means Clustering
summary:
We study the problem of k-means clustering on large datasets. The state-of-the-art for the problem is given by coresets-based approaches, which build small weighted summaries of the input and derive approximate solutions with rigorous quality guarantees from them. One of the most popular and advanced approaches to derive coresets for k-means is sensitivity sampling. However, sensitivity sampling requires to compute the importance of each input point with respect to the whole dataset over all possible choices of centers. Since the exact computation of such quantities is unfeasible, current approaches work by approximating the sensitivity values. Nevertheless, the runtime of such approaches is still impractical for large datasets.
  In this work, we propose to reduce the runtime of sensitivity-based approaches for k-means by leveraging predictions to approximate the importance of input points. We first formally prove that current theoretical results on coresets construction via sensitivity sampling hold for coarser approximations of sensitivities compared to the one required by existing approaches. This implies that even fairly noisy predictors can be leveraged for sensitivity-sampling approaches. We then propose a natural predictor, which applies to the common scenario where clustering is performed (over time) on a sequence of datasets from the same problem. We prove that when the datasets in the sequence come from the same (unknown) distribution, centers resulting in a low error on one dataset can be used as predictions for sensitivity sampling in subsequent datasets, with guarantees on their quality. We perform an extensive experimental evaluation showing that our approach significantly improves, in terms of clustering cost vs runtime, over uniform sampling and state-of-the-art sensitivity sampling approaches when applied to sequences of datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a faster sensitivity-based coreset construction for k-means clustering by using predictors to approximate importance scores, particularly effective for sequences of related datasets.
AI.motivation: Current sensitivity sampling approaches for k-means coresets require computing importance scores for each data point, which is computationally expensive and impractical for large datasets, especially when dealing with sequences of related datasets over time.
AI.method: The method uses predictions to approximate sensitivity values instead of exact computations, proving theoretically that coarser approximations still maintain coreset guarantees. Specifically, it leverages centers from previous clusterings in a sequence of related datasets as predictors for sensitivity sampling in subsequent datasets.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that noisy predictors can be used successfully, and when datasets come from the same distribution, previous clustering centers make effective predictors. Experimental evaluation demonstrates significant improvements in clustering cost vs runtime compared to uniform sampling and state-of-the-art sensitivity sampling methods.
AI.conclusion: Using predictions to approximate sensitivities enables faster coreset construction for k-means clustering while maintaining theoretical guarantees, particularly beneficial for sequences of related datasets where previous clustering results can inform current computations.

title: Train Smarter, Not Longer: Memorization-Guided Data Reuse for Efficient LLM Training
comment: Published as a paper at 3rd DATA-FM workshop @ ICLR 2026, Brazil
summary: The training paradigm of large language models has shifted from traditional one-pass training to multi-epoch training, as reasonable reuse of limited high-quality data can improve both model performance and sample efficiency. Meanwhile, excessive repetition introduces the risk of overfitting and diminishing returns. Determining when and how to reuse data effectively thus emerges as a natural but under-explored question. Through a novel observation of model's "Memorization Window" signals derived from loss retention dynamics and downstream evaluation scores, we propose "Memorization-guided Data Reuse", a training paradigm that adaptively determines when and how data should be reused, enabling principled decisions on the number of training epochs and the scheduling of data replays. Our preliminary experiments reveal a consistent memorization-driven regime: performance continues to improve with repetition far beyond current practice (e.g., the commonly cited four-epoch limit). While a full scheduler remains future work, these insights provide a foundation for memorization-aware training schedules, helping to determine reuse budgets and move toward training LLMs smarter rather than longer with limited high-quality data.

AI.tldr: Multi-epoch LLM training with data reuse can improve performance beyond current limits using memorization window signals to guide when and how to repeat data.
AI.motivation: Current shift to multi-epoch training reuses limited high-quality data but risks overfitting and diminishing returns. Need principled methods to determine optimal data reuse timing and scheduling for better performance without excessive repetition.
AI.method: Proposes "Memorization-guided Data Reuse" paradigm using novel "Memorization Window" signals from loss retention dynamics and downstream evaluation scores to adaptively determine when and how data should be reused, enabling principled decisions on training epochs and data replay scheduling.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments reveal consistent memorization-driven regime where performance continues improving with repetition far beyond current practice (e.g., beyond four-epoch limit). Found that current practice may be too conservative with data reuse.
AI.conclusion: Insights provide foundation for memorization-aware training schedules to determine optimal reuse budgets, enabling smarter LLM training rather than just longer training with limited high-quality data. Full scheduler remains future work.

title: Geometry-Aware Bayesian Quantification via Compositional Data Analysis
summary: Accurately estimating the unknown target label distribution is the critical first step for adapting to label shift. This task, widely known as quantification or class prevalence estimation, has recently seen significant advances through continuous KDE-based methods which model the density of multiclass classifier posteriors. Posterior vectors might be regarded as compositional data, since they lie on the probability simplex. However, existing KDE-based quantifiers typically rely on Euclidean Gaussian kernels, which ignore simplex geometry and incorrectly assign probability mass outside its boundaries. We introduce a geometry-aware KDE model for multiclass quantification based on log-ratio representations and Aitchison geometry, together with a shrinkage regularization that improves robustness near the simplex boundary. Combined with a maximum-likelihood interpretation of KDE-based quantification, we derive both point-estimation and Bayesian inference procedures for class prevalences. Experiments on 42 datasets across tabular, text, and image domains show that the proposed method is competitive with state-of-the-art quantifiers, often improving over standard KDE-based baselines, while also yielding strong results among Bayesian quantification methods.

AI.tldr: A novel geometry-aware KDE method for class prevalence estimation that uses log-ratio representations and Aitchison geometry to properly handle probability simplex structure, with shrinkage regularization and Bayesian inference capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing KDE-based quantifiers for label shift adaptation use Euclidean Gaussian kernels that ignore the simplex geometry of posterior probability vectors, incorrectly assigning probability mass outside the probability simplex boundaries.
AI.method: Introduces a geometry-aware KDE model using log-ratio representations and Aitchison geometry for compositional data on the probability simplex, with shrinkage regularization for boundary robustness. Derives both point-estimation and Bayesian inference procedures from a maximum-likelihood interpretation of KDE-based quantification.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on 42 datasets across tabular, text, and image domains shows the method is competitive with state-of-the-art quantifiers, often outperforming standard KDE baselines, and yields strong results among Bayesian quantification methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed geometry-aware approach effectively addresses the limitations of Euclidean KDE methods by properly modeling the simplex structure of classifier posteriors, leading to improved quantification performance while also enabling Bayesian inference capabilities.

title: Qantara: Bridge-Flow Training for Multi-Paradigm JEPA Control
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables. Project page: https://corl-team.github.io/qantara
summary: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) underpin a growing family of latent world models for control from raw pixels, but every existing JEPA world model commits at training time to a single inference paradigm: either trajectory optimisation in a learned dynamics model, or direct behaviour cloning. A single checkpoint that serves both would defer this choice to inference, when deployment constraints (rollout cost, observation accessibility) determine which path wins. We present Qantara, an end-to-end JEPA whose joint training objective pairs a Brownian-bridge interpolant between consecutive clean latents on the state axis with noise-to-data flow matching on the action axis. The same checkpoint serves three inference paradigms without retraining: latent planning, behaviour-cloning action sampling, and inverse dynamics, which we query through a video-inverse composition that first predicts the next latent without action conditioning, then extracts the action. Training concentrates mass on the edges of the (action-time, state-time) noise square, where inference queries the predictor: replacing it with uniform interior sampling drops Push-T planning from 90.1 to 53.3 SR at matched compute. On the LeWM control suite, Qantara reaches a 91.2 SR three-train-seed average and sets new SOTA on OGBench-Cube (+7.7 SR over DINO-WM, +19.7 over LeWM). From the same weights, the behaviour-cloning and video-inverse paths reach 82-83 SR on Push-T and 71-73 SR on Cube. These results move JEPA world models from single-paradigm planners to multi-paradigm controllers.

AI.tldr: Qantara is a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) world model that enables three inference paradigms from a single checkpoint: latent planning, behavior cloning, and inverse dynamics, making it a versatile multi-paradigm controller.
AI.motivation: Existing JEPA world models commit to either trajectory optimization or behavior cloning at training time, but deployment constraints (rollout cost, observation accessibility) vary. A single checkpoint serving multiple inference paradigms would allow flexibility at inference time.
AI.method: Qantara uses an end-to-end JEPA with joint training that pairs a Brownian-bridge interpolant between consecutive clean latents on the state axis with noise-to-data flow matching on the action axis. Training concentrates mass on the edges of the (action-time, state-time) noise square.
AI.result: Achieves 91.2 SR three-train-seed average on LeWM control suite, sets new SOTA on OGBench-Cube (+7.7 SR over DINO-WM, +19.7 over LeWM). From same weights: behavior cloning and video-inverse paths reach 82-83 SR on Push-T and 71-73 SR on Cube. Latent planning on Push-T reaches 90.1 SR.
AI.conclusion: Qantara moves JEPA world models from single-paradigm planners to multi-paradigm controllers, enabling flexible inference based on deployment constraints while maintaining strong performance across multiple benchmarks.

title: Data-Driven Soft Labeling Scales DNA Read Classification to Whole-Body Cell-Type Deconvolution
summary: Cell-type deconvolution, the task of estimating the proportions of constituent cell types in a heterogeneous biological sample, is a core problem in computational biology. Methods that rely on epigenetic marks such as DNA methylation typically operate on aggregated methylation estimates, discarding the pattern-level information carried by individual DNA reads. Existing read-level approaches that exploit this information are scarce, and all remain restricted to few-class settings; scaling them further is an open problem because, at scale, non-discriminative reads dominate and hard labels conflict with the many-to-many mapping between methylation patterns and cell types, preventing classifier convergence. To overcome this, we propose data-driven soft labels that estimate the conditional cell-type distribution for each read, and integrate this scheme into Syto, a new modular framework for read-level classification-based deconvolution. On a whole-body atlas of 39 human cell types, Syto reduces MSE by 2.56$\times$ over SoTA, with gains transferring to an out-of-distribution dataset spanning 16 tissues. Syto lays the foundation for modeling increasingly large cell-type panels, with improved applications in biology and healthcare. The proposed soft-labeling scheme is further translatable to any setting with a many-to-many signal-to-label mapping.

AI.tldr: Systo is a new framework for cell-type deconvolution that uses read-level classification with data-driven soft labels to handle the many-to-many mapping between methylation patterns and cell types, achieving 2.56× better MSE than state-of-the-art methods on a 39-cell-type whole-body atlas.
AI.motivation: Current cell-type deconvolution methods using epigenetic marks like DNA methylation either work on aggregated methylation estimates (losing pattern-level information) or use read-level approaches that struggle to scale beyond few-class settings. The challenges include non-discriminative reads dominating at scale and hard label conflicts due to many-to-many mapping between methylation patterns and cell types, preventing classifier convergence.
AI.method: Syto is a modular framework for read-level classification-based deconvolution that introduces data-driven soft labels. These soft labels estimate the conditional cell-type distribution for each DNA read, overcoming the limitations of hard labels. The approach preserves pattern-level information from individual DNA reads while handling the many-to-many mapping between methylation patterns and cell types.
AI.result: On a whole-body atlas of 39 human cell types, Syto reduces mean squared error (MSE) by 2.56× compared to state-of-the-art methods. The performance gains also transfer to an out-of-distribution dataset spanning 16 tissues, demonstrating robustness and generalization capability.
AI.conclusion: Syto successfully addresses the scaling limitations of read-level deconvolution methods by using soft labels that capture the probabilistic relationship between methylation patterns and cell types. The framework enables modeling of large cell-type panels with improved performance, laying foundation for better applications in biology and healthcare. The soft-labeling scheme is also generalizable to other settings with many-to-many signal-to-label mappings.

title: Non-Convex Sparse Reinforcement Learning via Non-Monotone Inclusions
summary:
This work delivers two key contributions: one to efficient feature selection in reinforcement learning
  (RL), the other to the theory of non-monotone inclusions. On the RL side, the estimation bias inherent
  in conventional regularization schemes is addressed by augmenting classical least-squares
  temporal-difference (LSTD) policy evaluation with the sparsity-inducing, non-convex projected minimax
  concave (PMC) penalty. Because the PMC penalty is weakly convex, the resulting fixed-point problem is
  no longer monotone; instead, it falls under a broader class of non-monotone inclusions involving the
  sum of a monotone Lipschitz operator and a hypomonotone operator. On the theory side, novel convergence
  conditions are developed for the forward-reflected-backward splitting (FRBS) method applied to this
  broader class of non-monotone inclusion problems. Under mild conditions, Lyapunov stability and the
  existence of a limit point of the sequence of FRBS iterates are established; alternatively, under the
  weak Minty variational inequality assumption, exact convergence is guaranteed. Numerical tests on
  benchmark datasets show that the proposed FRBS iterates, applied to the non-convexly regularized LSTD
  problem, substantially outperform state-of-the-art feature-selection methods, especially when many
  noisy features are present.

AI.tldr: Non-convex PMC penalty for sparse RL feature selection, with novel FRBS convergence theory for non-monotone inclusions
AI.motivation: Address estimation bias in conventional regularization for RL feature selection, and extend theory to handle non-monotone inclusion problems arising from non-convex penalties
AI.method: Augment LSTD policy evaluation with sparsity-inducing non-convex PMC penalty, then apply forward-reflected-backward splitting (FRBS) method with novel convergence conditions for the resulting non-monotone inclusion problem
AI.result: Established Lyapunov stability and limit point existence under mild conditions, with exact convergence under weak Minty variational inequality; numerical tests show FRBS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art feature selection methods, especially with noisy features
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully addresses RL feature selection bias through non-convex regularization, while advancing theoretical understanding of non-monotone inclusion problems with practical applications in sparse reinforcement learning

title: Beyond Modality Fusion: Deep Ensembles for Multimodal Classification
summary:
In multimodal classification, late-fusion approaches classify concatenated modality-specific features extracted by unimodal neural networks.
  When modality imbalance is pronounced, various regularization techniques have been proposed to balance the learning process and overcome the inferior performance of late-fusion networks.
  In contrast, this work demonstrates that multimodal data can be effectively classified without any explicit modality fusion, using deep ensembles of unimodal networks.
  We systematically compare deep ensembles to late-fusion networks at equal parameter count and show that ensembles consistently outperform state-of-the-art late-fusion methods designed to address modality imbalance.
  This advantage also holds over intermediate-fusion techniques we evaluated and over hybrid methods that combine unimodal and multimodal predictions.
  We propose and empirically validate a method for selecting the number of models per modality in an ensemble, avoiding computationally expensive exhaustive search.
  Under extreme modality imbalance and small ensemble sizes, the heuristic indicates that ensembles of unimodal models trained solely on the stronger modality are preferable; as the ensemble scales up, incorporating models from the weaker modality becomes beneficial.
  Both predictions align with our empirical findings.
  To systematically explore the challenges of optimizing multimodal models, we propose a synthetic multimodal framework that allows control over both the number of modalities and their predictive strength; our findings are consistent across synthetic and real-world datasets.
  Finally, by fitting scaling laws to bimodal datasets, we estimate the asymptotic performance of ensembles.

AI.tldr: Late-fusion multimodal classification struggles with modality imbalance; deep ensembles of unimodal networks outperform fusion methods without explicit modality fusion, with proposed heuristics for ensemble composition based on modality strength.
AI.motivation: Late-fusion approaches in multimodal classification suffer from modality imbalance issues, leading to inferior performance despite various regularization techniques. The paper challenges the necessity of explicit modality fusion for effective multimodal classification.
AI.method: Proposes using deep ensembles of unimodal networks instead of fusion methods. Introduces a heuristic method for selecting optimal number of models per modality in ensembles without exhaustive search. Also creates a synthetic multimodal framework for controlled experimentation and fits scaling laws to estimate asymptotic performance.
AI.result: Deep ensembles consistently outperform state-of-the-art late-fusion methods, intermediate-fusion techniques, and hybrid methods at equal parameter count. Under modality imbalance: small ensembles benefit from using only stronger modality; larger ensembles benefit from including weaker modality models. Findings validated across synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal classification can be effectively performed without explicit modality fusion using deep ensembles. The proposed ensemble approach and composition heuristics provide a practical solution to modality imbalance problems, with predictable scaling behavior and superior performance over fusion-based methods.

title: Uncertainty-aware damage identification in short-span bridges via physics-informed variational autoencoder
summary: Vibration-based damage identification in civil infrastructure is a challenging, ill-posed inverse problem due to measurement noise, sparse sensor arrays, and environmental variability. While deep learning is powerful for system identification, deterministic approaches lack reliable uncertainty quantification and can yield physically inconsistent results. This work proposes a robust probabilistic Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) framework: a physics-informed Gaussian copula variational autoencoder (PI-GCVAE) for structural health monitoring (SHM). First, we eliminate the need for data-driven surrogates by embedding a differentiable numerical eigenvalue solver directly into the VAE architecture. This ensures that latent space samples satisfy the governing equations of structural dynamics, reducing the trainable parameter space and improving generalization. Second, we replace the conventional independence assumption of latent variables with a Gaussian copula. This model captures complex, physics-dependent spatial cross-correlations between adjacent structural elements, defining feasible solutions while accounting for inherent system variability and measurement errors. Third, compared with alternatives such as Gaussian mixtures, our copula-based VAE provides an efficient distributional model for high-dimensional, strongly correlated latent spaces. We validate the approach using a synthetic dataset of a simply supported bridge subjected to various damage scenarios and corrupted with stochastic Gaussian noise. Synthetic data enables exhaustive validation against ground-truth stiffness values unavailable in practice. Results demonstrate that the PI-GCVAE accurately recovers the true posterior distribution, achieving 77.2% coverage. The proposed framework provides a reliable, scalable tool for early-stage damage diagnosis in operating bridges.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed Gaussian copula variational autoencoder (PI-GCVAE) framework for robust structural health monitoring that combines physics constraints with probabilistic modeling for reliable damage identification with uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Vibration-based damage identification faces challenges as an ill-posed inverse problem due to measurement noise, sparse sensors, and environmental variability. Traditional deep learning approaches lack reliable uncertainty quantification and can produce physically inconsistent results.
AI.method: The PI-GCVAE framework embeds a differentiable numerical eigenvalue solver directly into the VAE architecture, eliminating need for data-driven surrogates. It replaces conventional independence assumption of latent variables with a Gaussian copula to capture complex spatial cross-correlations between structural elements. This approach defines feasible solutions while accounting for system variability and measurement errors.
AI.result: Validated using synthetic dataset of a simply supported bridge with various damage scenarios and stochastic Gaussian noise. The method accurately recovers the true posterior distribution, achieving 77.2% coverage, and successfully identifies ground-truth stiffness values unavailable in practice.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a reliable, scalable tool for early-stage damage diagnosis in operating bridges, offering improved generalization, physical consistency, and better uncertainty quantification compared to deterministic deep learning approaches.

title: CollabEval: Statistically Efficient Collaborative Model Evaluation via Matrix Completion
summary: Evaluating generative AI models is a routine, but resource-intensive, process that is conducted over and over again during the course of model development. In this work, we propose Collaborative Evaluation (CollabEval), a simple, effective, and principled method for exploiting dependencies between historical runs of different models on the same tasks to improve statistical efficiency. Specifically, our approach treats model evaluation as a matrix completion problem over an $M \times N$ matrix of evaluation scores, where $M$ is the total number of models and $N$ is the total number of evaluation prompts. We assume that a subset of these $M$ models are targeted for evaluation. For these target models only a small fraction, $p$, of prompts has been annotated with evaluation scores.
