Leveraging recent results in prediction-powered inference, we build a low-rank approximation of the score matrix, and use the reconstructed values as control variates in a manner that guarantees unbiased estimates of the true evaluation metric mean, in addition to statistically valid confidence intervals. Empirically, across a wide range of datasets, models, and sparsity levels $p$, we find that CollabEval substantially reduces the mean confidence interval size, and the mean squared error of the point estimate, compared to baseline methods at the same annotation budget.

AI.tldr: CollabEval is a method that treats model evaluation as a matrix completion problem to improve statistical efficiency by leveraging dependencies between historical model evaluations, reducing annotation costs while maintaining statistical validity.
AI.motivation: Evaluating generative AI models is resource-intensive and repeatedly conducted during model development. There's a need to reduce annotation costs while maintaining statistical validity in model evaluation processes.
AI.method: Treats model evaluation as a matrix completion problem over an M×N matrix (models × evaluation prompts). Uses prediction-powered inference to build low-rank approximations of the score matrix and uses reconstructed values as control variates to guarantee unbiased estimates and valid confidence intervals.
AI.result: Across various datasets, models, and sparsity levels, CollabEval substantially reduces mean confidence interval size and mean squared error of point estimates compared to baseline methods at the same annotation budget.
AI.conclusion: CollabEval provides a simple, effective, and principled method for improving statistical efficiency in model evaluation by exploiting dependencies between historical runs, offering practical benefits for resource-constrained AI development.

title: KVpop -- Key-Value Cache Compression with Predictive Online Pruning
summary: Key-value (KV) cache growth is a major bottleneck in autoregressive decoding, as memory and bandwidth scale linearly with context length. Existing KV eviction methods often rely on static heuristics or proxy scores, which poorly track future token utility and cause brittle eviction as relevance shifts. To address this, we introduce KVpop, which learns a fixed-budget KV eviction policy by directly supervising the keep-or-drop decision. The scorer is trained against a novel future-attention target, computed efficiently without materializing dense attention maps. We further introduce a delayed memory-based scorer that, uniquely among learned eviction methods, defers scoring for a fixed number of steps to exploit near-future context. On AIME and HMMT mathematical reasoning, KVpop retains 98% of full-attention performance on Qwen3-4B at 75% KV cache compression and 97% at 88% compression, consistently outperforming established eviction baselines. Qwen3-8B shows even stronger results, reaching near-full teacher performance. These results show that supervising eviction with future-attention signals cuts memory costs while maintaining quality.

AI.tldr: KVpop learns fixed-budget KV cache eviction by supervising keep/drop decisions with future-attention targets, achieving near-full attention performance with high compression.
AI.motivation: KV cache growth is a major bottleneck in autoregressive decoding, with existing eviction methods relying on static heuristics or proxy scores that poorly track future token utility and cause brittle eviction as relevance shifts.
AI.method: KVpop learns a fixed-budget KV eviction policy by directly supervising keep-or-drop decisions against a novel future-attention target computed efficiently without materializing dense attention maps. It also introduces a delayed memory-based scorer that defers scoring for fixed steps to exploit near-future context.
AI.result: On AIME and HMMT mathematical reasoning, KVpop retains 98% of full-attention performance on Qwen3-4B at 75% KV cache compression and 97% at 88% compression, consistently outperforming established eviction baselines. Qwen3-8B shows even stronger results, reaching near-full teacher performance.
AI.conclusion: Supervising eviction with future-attention signals effectively cuts memory costs while maintaining quality, showing that learned eviction policies can significantly improve KV cache efficiency in autoregressive decoding.

title: Computing Monetary Risk Measures in Linear Time
summary: Monetary risk measures have gained popularity for expressing decision-makers' risk aversion. Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Conditional-Value-at-Risk (CVaR), in particular, are used commonly for this purpose. This paper proposes new efficient algorithms to compute these risk measures for a discrete random variable in expected linear time with respect to the size of its domain. First, we propose a QuickVaR algorithm that computes the VaR of a discrete random variable. Then, we leverage QuickVaR to propose QuickDivergence, an algorithm for computing a class of $\varphi$-divergence risk measures, including the popular CVaR risk measure. The QuickVaR algorithm adapts the well-known Quickselect algorithm, while QuickDivergence builds on polymatroid optimization algorithms. Numerical results show that our new algorithms offer an order-of-magnitude speedup for large domains, and a library implementation of the algorithms is available at https://github.com/RiskAverseRL/RiskMeasures.jl.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes new efficient algorithms (QuickVaR and QuickDivergence) for computing VaR and φ-divergence risk measures (including CVaR) for discrete random variables in expected linear time.
AI.motivation: Monetary risk measures like VaR and CVaR are popular for expressing risk aversion, but efficient computation methods are needed for discrete random variables with large domains.
AI.method: Developed QuickVaR algorithm based on Quickselect algorithm for computing VaR, then extended to QuickDivergence algorithm using polymatroid optimization to compute φ-divergence risk measures including CVaR.
AI.result: The proposed algorithms achieve expected linear time complexity relative to domain size, with numerical results showing order-of-magnitude speedup for large domains. Implementation available as open-source library.
AI.conclusion: The new algorithms provide efficient computational methods for risk measures, enabling faster risk assessment in applications requiring VaR and CVaR calculations for discrete random variables.

title: FAST: A Holistic Framework for Optimizing Memory-I/O, Computation, and Sampling in Temporal GNN Training
summary: Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) are widely used for learning from dynamic graphs in applications such as recommendation, social network analysis, and traffic forecasting. However, scaling TGNN training to large dynamic graphs remains challenging due to three intertwined bottlenecks: memory I/O, irregular computation, and temporal neighbor sampling. Existing systems often optimize these stages in isolation, leaving substantial performance headroom on the table. We present FAST, a holistic framework that accelerates end-to-end TGNN training by jointly optimizing sampling, memory I/O, and computation. FAST introduces SlimCache, which exploits within-batch compression and cross-batch caching to reduce host-device data movement under limited GPU memory budgets. It further designs thread-efficient graph operators tailored to sparse temporal subgraphs, improving GPU cache locality and reducing the latency of aggregation and edge softmax. In addition, FAST employs a topology-aware sampling strategy that improves CPU cache locality and accelerates temporal neighbor sampling. Extensive experiments on real-world large dynamic graphs show that FAST achieves an average of 2.1x (up to 4.7x) speedup over state-of-the-art systems without sacrificing model accuracy.

AI.tldr: FAST is a holistic framework that accelerates Temporal Graph Neural Network training by jointly optimizing sampling, memory I/O, and computation, achieving up to 4.7x speedup over state-of-the-art systems.
AI.motivation: Scaling Temporal Graph Neural Network (TGNN) training to large dynamic graphs is challenging due to three intertwined bottlenecks: memory I/O, irregular computation, and temporal neighbor sampling. Existing systems optimize these stages in isolation, leaving substantial performance headroom.
AI.method: FAST introduces three key components: 1) SlimCache for memory I/O optimization using within-batch compression and cross-batch caching, 2) Thread-efficient graph operators for sparse temporal subgraphs to improve GPU cache locality, and 3) Topology-aware sampling strategy to improve CPU cache locality for temporal neighbor sampling.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world large dynamic graphs show that FAST achieves an average of 2.1x speedup (up to 4.7x) over state-of-the-art systems without sacrificing model accuracy.
AI.conclusion: FAST provides a holistic solution to accelerate end-to-end TGNN training by jointly optimizing the three key bottlenecks, demonstrating significant performance improvements while maintaining model accuracy for large dynamic graph applications.

title: Functional Bilevel Optimization for Predictive Fairness
summary: When sensitive attributes are continuous and high-dimensional $-$ demographic score vectors, posteriors over attributes, age or income profiles $-$ enforcing full statistical independence is often too restrictive, and existing relaxations rely on indirect dependence penalties or adversarial schemes that do not directly target the fairness-accuracy trade-off. We instead consider mean demographic parity through DPVar, the variance of the conditional-mean prediction given the sensitive attribute, and show that optimizing it yields a functional bilevel problem. We propose two algorithms for this problem: FBO, which uses a closed-form adjoint we derive for the squared-loss case to obtain an exact hypergradient, and ITD, which differentiates through unrolled inner steps and extends beyond squared loss. On synthetic data and a new semi-synthetic benchmark built from 60 tabular regression datasets, both methods achieve the lowest or near-lowest aggregate fairness-accuracy regret, and consistently match or outperform strong HSIC, adversarial, linear-dependence, and generalized-DP baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces two algorithms (FBO and ITD) for enforcing mean demographic parity fairness constraints in regression with continuous sensitive attributes, achieving better fairness-accuracy trade-offs than existing methods.
AI.motivation: When sensitive attributes are continuous and high-dimensional (like demographic score vectors, posteriors over attributes, age or income profiles), enforcing full statistical independence is too restrictive. Existing relaxations rely on indirect dependence penalties or adversarial schemes that don't directly target the fairness-accuracy trade-off.
AI.method: The authors consider mean demographic parity through DPVar (variance of conditional-mean prediction given sensitive attribute). They propose two algorithms: 1) FBO - uses a closed-form adjoint derived for squared-loss case to obtain exact hypergradient, 2) ITD - differentiates through unrolled inner steps and works beyond squared loss. Both optimize a functional bilevel problem.
AI.result: On synthetic data and a new semi-synthetic benchmark built from 60 tabular regression datasets, both methods achieve the lowest or near-lowest aggregate fairness-accuracy regret. They consistently match or outperform strong baselines including HSIC, adversarial, linear-dependence, and generalized-DP methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods provide effective solutions for fairness in regression with continuous sensitive attributes by directly optimizing the fairness-accuracy trade-off through mean demographic parity constraints, outperforming existing approaches that rely on indirect dependence measures.

title: Counterfactual Methods for Detecting Unfairness in Anti-Money Laundering Algorithms
summary: The application of machine learning-based predictive algorithms to Anti-Money Laundering (AML) has grown rapidly, driven by the vast volume of financial transaction data available to banks. These algorithms are typically trained not only on transactional data but also on sensitive client information, which may raise fairness concerns. Despite this, AML detection systems remain largely underexplored from a fairness perspective, even though deeper analytical methods based on counterfactuals are now available. Such techniques enable the decomposition of the direct and indirect effects of potentially sensitive features on model predictions, thereby supporting the evaluation of whether their influence is acceptable from a fairness perspective. Closing this gap, we consider the synthetic IBM AMLSim transaction dataset and construct additional features of the country of an account and its average behaviour. This improves the predictive performance of diverse machine learning models, ranging from baseline decision trees to state-of-the-art graph neural networks. We assess the potential unfairness associated with these features through a counterfactual, path-specific effect analysis. This reveals that fairness violations tend to be more pronounced for models whose predictive performance benefits the most from the extended features. Such a finding highlights a concrete instance of the trade-off between predictive accuracy and fairness in AML applications, thus underscoring the urgency of a systematic fairness analysis in such critical domains.

AI.tldr: Machine learning in AML creates fairness concerns when using sensitive client data. Using IBM AMLSim dataset with country and behavioral features improves model performance but reveals fairness violations, showing accuracy-fairness trade-off.
AI.motivation: AML detection systems use sensitive client data but remain underexplored from fairness perspective. There's a need to evaluate whether influence of sensitive features is acceptable from fairness viewpoint.
AI.method: Use synthetic IBM AMLSim transaction dataset with added features (country of account and average behavior). Train diverse ML models from decision trees to graph neural networks. Apply counterfactual, path-specific effect analysis to assess potential unfairness.
AI.result: Extended features improve predictive performance across all models. Fairness violations are more pronounced for models that benefit most from extended features. Reveals concrete instance of trade-off between predictive accuracy and fairness.
AI.conclusion: There's a trade-off between accuracy and fairness in AML applications. Models with better predictive performance tend to have worse fairness outcomes. Highlights urgency of systematic fairness analysis in critical financial domains.

title: Choosing a parallel heterogeneous ensemble method for tabular classification
summary:
Parallel ensemble methods were compared on $56$ small-to-medium tabular classification tasks drawn from OpenML CC18. A set of ``best practice'' recommendations on the use of ensemble methods was derived from these observations. It was later validated on 28 additional tasks using TabArena's precomputed data, where the recommendation set significantly outperformed Single Best and matched or exceeded individual ensemble methods.
  Two key observations were made. First, Blending and Stacking are inconsistent, but their inconsistencies are independent and happen on different tasks. Second, while Hard Voting's probabilistic classification is rather weak, a consequence of using vote proportions as posterior estimates, Robust Soft Voting's probabilistic classification is particularly successful, especially in the multiclass case.

AI.tldr: A study comparing parallel ensemble methods on tabular classification tasks found that combining multiple ensembles outperforms individual methods, with Robust Soft Voting showing strong probabilistic classification performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to establish evidence-based "best practice" recommendations for using ensemble methods on tabular classification tasks, particularly small-to-medium sized datasets from OpenML CC18 benchmark.
AI.method: The study compared parallel ensemble methods on 56 small-to-medium tabular classification tasks from OpenML CC18, then validated findings on 28 additional tasks using TabArena's precomputed data.
AI.result: Two key findings: 1) Blending and Stacking are inconsistent but their inconsistencies occur on different tasks (making them complementary), 2) While Hard Voting has weak probabilistic classification, Robust Soft Voting has particularly successful probabilistic classification, especially for multiclass problems. The derived recommendation set significantly outperformed Single Best methods and matched or exceeded individual ensemble methods.
AI.conclusion: Combining multiple ensemble methods (recommendation set) yields superior performance to using any single ensemble method alone. Robust Soft Voting is particularly effective for probabilistic classification in multiclass problems, while Blending and Stacking can be complementary despite their individual inconsistencies.

title: Physiological Noise Augmentation Improves Non-Invasive Brain-to-Speech
summary: Non-invasive brain-to-speech decoding aims to restore communication to patients suffering from neurodegenerative disease, without the risks of neurosurgery. Existing MEG- and EEG-based methods, while scalable, continue to suffer from high word error rates driven by relatively low signal-to-noise ratios compared to invasive recordings. We propose physiological noise augmentation (PNA), a data augmentation method that explicitly trains decoders to become invariant to task-agnostic artifacts (e.g. ocular and cardiac activity). PNA draws inspiration from automatic speech recognition systems, where environmental noise (e.g. dogs barking, city traffic) is added to clean speech to improve robustness. Analogously, we decompose brain recordings into clean data and noise artifacts using independent component analysis (ICA), before scaling and remixing to generate biophysically realistic, label-preserving training examples. We show that PNA approximates anisotropic regularization, penalizing decoder sensitivity along artifact-dominated directions. On MegNIST, a 12k-trial imagined-digit MEG dataset, PNA with 10-trial averaging improves EEGNet decoding accuracy by 4.7 percentage points (absolute) over training on real data alone. Our results suggest that artifact-aware augmentation and trial averaging are complementary tools for improving robustness in non-invasive speech BCIs.

AI.tldr: PNA improves non-invasive brain-to-speech decoding by using physiological noise augmentation to train decoders to be invariant to task-agnostic artifacts, achieving 4.7% accuracy improvement on MEG digit decoding.
AI.motivation: Non-invasive brain-to-speech decoding suffers from high word error rates due to low signal-to-noise ratios compared to invasive methods. Existing MEG/EEG methods need improvement to restore communication for neurodegenerative disease patients without surgical risks.
AI.method: Physiological Noise Augmentation (PNA) - decomposes brain recordings using ICA into clean data and noise artifacts, then scales and remixes them to generate realistic training examples. Inspired by ASR noise augmentation, it explicitly trains decoders to become invariant to ocular/cardiac artifacts, approximating anisotropic regularization that penalizes sensitivity along artifact-dominated directions.
AI.result: On MegNIST (12k-trial imagined-digit MEG dataset), PNA with 10-trial averaging improves EEGNet decoding accuracy by 4.7 percentage points (absolute) over training on real data alone.
AI.conclusion: Artifact-aware augmentation and trial averaging are complementary tools for improving robustness in non-invasive speech BCIs. PNA effectively mitigates physiological artifacts to enhance non-invasive brain-to-speech decoding performance.

title: MeGA-MP: Metric Graph Advection Message Passing -- A Physics-Informed Message Passing Operator for Advection-Dominated Metric Graphs
summary: Many real-world systems are organized as networks where spatio-temporal dynamics unfold along connections and not discretely between nodes. Examples include utility networks such as water distribution systems or gas networks, electrical grids, and traffic flow networks. Such systems are naturally modeled as metric graphs, where edges correspond to one-dimensional Euclidean subspaces connected at vertices. Metric graphs are independent of an underlying global Euclidean space, limiting direct application of typical PINNs and operator-learning methods. Especially transport dynamics like advection require a methodology able to capture antisymmetric and long-range dependencies on graphs, which is itself a challenge. We propose a novel physics-informed message passing operator that encodes linear advection on metric graphs as an inductive bias. In the purely advective setting, the operator provably recovers the exact dynamics up to a theoretically derived discretization error without any training. Combined with trainable components like MLPs, our message passing operator extends to realistic advection-reaction dynamics in water distribution systems, where we achieve superior performance compared to baselines and zero-shot generalization across different graph topologies.

AI.tldr: A novel physics-informed message passing operator for modeling advection dynamics on metric graphs (networks like water/gas systems) that achieves exact recovery in pure advection settings without training and generalizes well to realistic systems.
AI.motivation: Many real-world systems (water distribution, gas networks, electrical grids, traffic flows) are organized as networks with spatio-temporal dynamics unfolding along connections. These systems are naturally modeled as metric graphs, but existing methods like PINNs and operator-learning approaches are limited because metric graphs lack an underlying global Euclidean space. Transport dynamics like advection require capturing antisymmetric and long-range dependencies on graphs, which poses a significant challenge.
AI.method: Proposed a novel physics-informed message passing operator that encodes linear advection on metric graphs as an inductive bias. The operator can recover exact dynamics up to discretization error without training in purely advective settings. Combined with trainable components like MLPs, it extends to realistic advection-reaction dynamics.
AI.result: The operator achieves superior performance compared to baselines for realistic advection-reaction dynamics in water distribution systems. It demonstrates zero-shot generalization across different graph topologies. In pure advection settings, it provably recovers exact dynamics up to a theoretically derived discretization error without any training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed physics-informed message passing operator effectively addresses the challenge of modeling transport dynamics on metric graphs, providing a theoretically sound approach that combines physical principles with trainable components for practical applications in utility networks and similar systems.

title: Platonic Projection Structures: Operator-Induced Observability in Representation Learning
summary: We characterize observability in representation learning through Platonic Projection Structures (PPS), an operator-theoretic framework for analyzing representation accessibility under partial observation. Rather than treating observable outputs as direct reflections of latent representations, PPS models observation through a self-adjoint positive semidefinite operator acting on a latent representation space. A system is represented as a triple $(H, Π, O)$, where $H$ is a latent representation space, $Π\succeq 0$ is an observation operator, and $O(v)=\langle v,Πv\rangle$ defines an induced scalar observable. Observability is characterized by the quotient geometry $H/\ker(Π)$, representing equivalence classes of latent states indistinguishable under observation. We show that quantum measurement and representation inference under linear observation models share this operator-theoretic structure while differing in the algebraic properties of their observation operators; the correspondence is structural rather than physical. Representation transfer and knowledge distillation can likewise be interpreted as approximate preservation of observable geometry through $ΦΠ_T \approx Π_S Φ$. PPS also reveals a structural limitation of output-based interpretability: latent components in $\ker(Π)$ are inaccessible from induced observables, imposing intrinsic constraints on attribution and explanation methods. Controlled empirical validations demonstrate kernel-invariant observability, projection-induced attribution gaps, and rank-controlled observable geometry in latent representation spaces. PPS thus provides an explicit characterization of observability through operator-induced quotient geometry and a unified perspective on representation accessibility, interpretability, and projection-mediated inference.

AI.tldr: PPS framework characterizes representation observability via operator-induced quotient geometry, revealing intrinsic limitations in interpretability methods.
AI.motivation: To formally characterize observability in representation learning and understand the structural limitations of output-based interpretability methods.
AI.method: Platonic Projection Structures (PPS) framework using operator theory - modeling systems as triples (H, Π, O) where Π is a self-adjoint positive semidefinite operator acting on latent space H.
AI.result: Shows quantum measurement and representation inference share this structural framework; identifies kernel-invariant observability, projection-induced attribution gaps, and rank-controlled observable geometry; reveals latent components in ker(Π) are inaccessible to interpretability methods.
AI.conclusion: PPS provides a unified operator-theoretic characterization of observability that reveals fundamental constraints on representation accessibility and interpretability through projection-mediated inference.

title: SMART: A Machine Learning and Monte Carlo Framework for Rapid Analysis of Stochastic Transistor Aging and Process Variation in Digital Circuits
comment: Submitted to Engineering Reports, Under Review
summary: As CMOS technology scales into the deep nanometer regime, digital circuit reliability is increasingly threatened by the combined stochastic effects of Bias Temperature Instability (BTI) and Process Variation (PV). Traditional reliability analysis methods, which rely on computationally intensive simulations or extensive lookup tables, fail to scale efficiently for large designs, creating a critical bottleneck in design space exploration. To address this, we propose SMART, a novel framework that integrates Machine Learning (ML) with Monte Carlo simulation to enable rapid, high-fidelity reliability analysis. SMART employs Random Forest regression to predict gate delay distributions directly, bypassing time-consuming atomic model parameter extractions. Crucially, the model utilizes Bayesian Optimization for automated hyperparameter tuning, ensuring maximum predictive robustness across diverse libraries. Experimental validation on ISCAS85 benchmark circuits demonstrates that SMART achieves a 94.54% reduction in analysis time compared to state-of-the-art methods, while maintaining a remarkable average accuracy error of just 1.63%. By shifting computational complexity to an offline training phase, the proposed framework offers a scalable, accurate solution for designing resilient, reliability-aware digital systems.

AI.tldr: SMART: A machine learning framework using Random Forest regression and Bayesian Optimization for rapid reliability analysis of digital circuits under BTI and process variation effects, achieving 94.54% faster analysis with 1.63% error.
AI.motivation: As CMOS technology scales to deep nanometer levels, digital circuit reliability is threatened by combined stochastic effects of Bias Temperature Instability (BTI) and Process Variation (PV). Traditional reliability analysis methods using intensive simulations or lookup tables fail to scale for large designs, creating bottlenecks in design space exploration.
AI.method: Proposes SMART framework integrating Machine Learning (ML) with Monte Carlo simulation. Uses Random Forest regression to predict gate delay distributions directly, bypassing atomic model parameter extractions. Employs Bayesian Optimization for automated hyperparameter tuning to ensure predictive robustness across diverse libraries.
AI.result: Experimental validation on ISCAS85 benchmark circuits shows SMART achieves 94.54% reduction in analysis time compared to state-of-the-art methods while maintaining average accuracy error of just 1.63%.
AI.conclusion: SMART framework offers a scalable, accurate solution for designing resilient, reliability-aware digital systems by shifting computational complexity to an offline training phase, enabling rapid, high-fidelity reliability analysis.

title: Latent Programming Horizons in Coding Agents
summary: A coding agent solving a software-engineering task spends dozens of steps reasoning, editing code, and running tests, yet little is known about what the underlying language model internally represents about the program it is working on. We show that the residual streams of language models under coding agents linearly encode properties of the evolving program: a logistic-regression probe on hidden states is able to decode whether the current code parses, passes its test suite, reduces the number of failing tests, and introduces regressions, reaching AUC up to 0.83 for correctness across two models and two benchmarks. Our second finding is more surprising: these representations run ahead of the agent's own edits. Probes trained to predict the outcome of future edits (before they are materialized and written on disk) achieve performance above chance up to roughly 25 steps in advance. We call this the agent's latent programming horizon. As a proof of external validity, we show that the probes transfer across benchmarks without retraining. Our positive results open calls for more research in mechanistic interpretability of coding agents.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that language models' internal representations while acting as coding agents linearly encode properties of the evolving program, including future edits up to 25 steps ahead (latent programming horizon).
AI.motivation: Coding agents spend many steps reasoning, editing code, and running tests, but little is known about what language models internally represent about the programs they're working on during this process.
AI.method: Use logistic-regression probes on hidden states (residual streams) of language models to decode program properties like parsing status, test suite results, failing test reduction, and regressions. Also train probes to predict outcomes of future edits before they're materialized.
AI.result: Probes can decode current program properties with AUC up to 0.83 for correctness across two models and two benchmarks. More surprisingly, probes can predict future edit outcomes above chance up to roughly 25 steps in advance (latent programming horizon). Probes also transfer across benchmarks without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Language models under coding agents develop linear representations of program properties that even run ahead of actual edits. These positive results call for more research in mechanistic interpretability of coding agents.

title: FlatManifold: Robust Continual Learning under Severe Label Noise and Domain Shifts via Intrinsic Manifold Flattening
summary:
In non-stationary streaming environments, simultaneously adapting to complex, non-linear domain shifts via continual learning while mitigating the catastrophic effects of severe, uncalibrated label noise poses a fundamental mathematical challenge. In this paper, we propose \FlatManifold{}, a novel, streamlined robust continual learning framework that utilizes a Nyström manifold flattening map based on the kernel trick and projection onto an orthogonalized Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS).
  Unlike traditional methods that rely on complex, error-prone sample-filtering pipelines, the proposed approach exploits the intrinsic mathematical robustness of the flattened space itself. By mapping feature distributions onto a fixed orthogonal target topology with a ridge regularizer, the framework naturally smoothes and counteracts the influence of extreme label noise during the optimization process. Concurrently, catastrophic forgetting is prevented via a continual topology brake term that leverages the covariance matrix of past experiences.
  Extensive evaluation on real-world multi-session robotics datasets demonstrates that even under severe conditions featuring 40\% symmetric label noise, \FlatManifold{} successfully mitigates gradient corruption. Under extreme cross-session domain shifts spanning various seasons and lighting conditions, the proposed framework establishes high generalization capabilities, significantly outperforming standard sequential optimization baselines and proving that structural linearization itself serves as a powerful mathematical barrier against distributed label corruption.

AI.tldr: FlatManifold is a robust continual learning framework that uses Nyström manifold flattening to handle severe label noise and non-linear domain shifts simultaneously in streaming environments.
AI.motivation: Addressing the fundamental challenge of adapting to complex, non-linear domain shifts via continual learning while mitigating the catastrophic effects of severe, uncalibrated label noise in non-stationary streaming environments.
AI.method: Proposes FlatManifold framework using a Nyström manifold flattening map based on kernel trick and projection onto orthogonalized RKHS. Maps feature distributions onto a fixed orthogonal target topology with ridge regularizer, and prevents catastrophic forgetting via a continual topology brake term leveraging covariance matrix of past experiences.
AI.result: Successfully mitigates gradient corruption even under 40% symmetric label noise. Shows high generalization capabilities under extreme cross-session domain shifts spanning various seasons and lighting conditions. Significantly outperforms standard sequential optimization baselines.
AI.conclusion: Structural linearization serves as a powerful mathematical barrier against distributed label corruption, offering a streamlined approach that exploits the intrinsic robustness of flattened spaces rather than complex sample-filtering pipelines.

title: Video-based detection of cessation of breathing in pre-term infants using machine learning
comment: Paper submitted to Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine (CMPB)
summary:
Pre-term infants are susceptible to potentially harmful apnoea-related cessations of breathing due to immature respiratory control. However, reliable respiratory monitoring in the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) remains challenging because motion artefacts, sensor displacement, and skin fragility can compromise contact-based measurements. Non-contact video monitoring offers a complementary approach that does not depend on adhesive sensors while providing additional respiratory information.
  We investigated whether camera-based signals can detect apnoea-related cessation of breathing (COBE) and provide complementary information to routinely acquired physiological signals. Using video and clinical recordings from 30 pre-term infants, respiratory motion was extracted from dynamically tracked torso regions to generate camera-derived time-series signals. Camera-only models were trained using residual network (ResNet) architectures, while hybrid models combined video-derived signals with impedance pneumography (IP), ECG-derived respiration (EDR), and the PPG-derived respiratory envelope.
  Camera-only models achieved a balanced accuracy of 76.9%, demonstrating the feasibility of non-contact COBE detection. Combining video-derived features with IP improved balanced accuracy to 90.6%, outperforming either modality alone and indicating that video provides respiratory information beyond standard physiological signals.
  These findings show that video-derived signals contain clinically relevant respiratory features and enhance COBE detection when combined with conventional physiological signals. This supports non-contact video as a complementary modality for automated COBE detection and highlights its potential to improve the robustness of neonatal respiratory monitoring.

AI.tldr: Video-based monitoring can detect apnoea-related cessations of breathing in preterm infants, and combining video with conventional physiological signals (impedance pneumography) improves detection accuracy beyond either modality alone.
AI.motivation: Preterm infants are vulnerable to harmful apnoea events due to immature respiratory control. Current contact-based monitoring in NICUs has limitations including motion artefacts, sensor displacement, and skin fragility. Non-contact video monitoring could provide a complementary approach without adhesive sensors while offering additional respiratory information.
AI.method: Used video and clinical recordings from 30 preterm infants. Extracted respiratory motion from dynamically tracked torso regions to generate camera-derived time-series signals. Developed camera-only models using ResNet architectures, and hybrid models that combined video-derived signals with impedance pneumography (IP), ECG-derived respiration (EDR), and PPG-derived respiratory envelope.
AI.result: Camera-only models achieved 76.9% balanced accuracy for COBE detection. Hybrid models combining video with IP achieved 90.6% balanced accuracy, outperforming either modality alone. This indicates video provides respiratory information beyond standard physiological signals.
AI.conclusion: Video-derived signals contain clinically relevant respiratory features and enhance COBE detection when combined with conventional physiological signals. Non-contact video shows promise as a complementary modality for automated COBE detection and could improve robustness of neonatal respiratory monitoring.

title: FUSE: FK-Steered Multi-Modal Flow Matching for Efficient Simulation-Based Posterior Estimation
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 22 pages, 5 figures
summary: Simulation-Based Inference (SBI) is critical for scientific discovery, with generative models offering a promising path toward efficient inference. However, existing methods struggle with effective multimodal modeling. They often rely on brute-force fusion strategies that ignore the structural disparities between parameters and observations, thus limiting estimation fidelity. In this work, we introduce FUSE (Feynman-Kac steered mUlti-modal flow matching for efficient Simulation-based posterior Estimation). Unlike prior work, FUSE employs a dual-track architecture that preserves the distinct features of multimodal inputs while facilitating dynamic interaction. Additionally, we propose an FK-steered sampling strategy that leverages intermediate observation likelihoods to guide the generative trajectories, effectively improving the sample quality during inference. Our approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard SBI benchmarks, producing posteriors that closely match ground-truth MCMC. Furthermore, in a real-world exoplanet orbital estimation task, FUSE successfully resolves complex parameter degeneracies that challenge existing methods, highlighting its potential to accelerate complex scientific discoveries in astrophysics and beyond.

AI.tldr: FUSE introduces a dual-track architecture with FK-steered sampling for multimodal simulation-based inference, outperforming existing methods on SBI benchmarks and successfully resolving complex parameter degeneracies in real-world exoplanet orbital estimation.
AI.motivation: Existing simulation-based inference methods struggle with effective multimodal modeling and often use brute-force fusion strategies that ignore structural disparities between parameters and observations, limiting estimation fidelity.
AI.method: FUSE employs a dual-track architecture that preserves distinct features of multimodal inputs while enabling dynamic interaction, along with an FK-steered sampling strategy that uses intermediate observation likelihoods to guide generative trajectories and improve sample quality.
AI.result: FUSE outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard SBI benchmarks, producing posteriors that closely match ground-truth MCMC. In real-world exoplanet orbital estimation, it successfully resolves complex parameter degeneracies that challenge existing methods.
AI.conclusion: FUSE demonstrates significant improvements in multimodal simulation-based inference and shows potential to accelerate complex scientific discoveries in astrophysics and other fields by effectively handling parameter degeneracies.

title: GeoFlow: Geo-Aware Modeling of Inter-Area Relationships in Origin-Destination Flow Prediction and Generation
summary: Origin-destination (OD) flow modeling underpins urban planning and mobility analysis, but prevailing graph-based methods often neglect salient geographic attributes, limiting their ability to model long-range and multi-area dependencies. In this paper, we introduce GeoFlow, a novel framework that (i) augments area representations with geospatial attributes, including relative positions, k-hop and geodesic distances, (ii) employs a specialized geometric-intrinsic fusion encoder design that combines graph attention for intrinsic area signals with coordinate-aware encoders for global structure, and (iii) adopts an axial-global attention decoder to capture OD-specific competitive dependencies. For OD flow generation, GeoFlow is paired with flow matching models to produce more authentic and diverse mobility samples. Empirically, GeoFlow achieves superior performance in predictive accuracy, while substantially improving generative fidelity and diversity. Ablation and analytical studies confirm the contribution of each component. Code is available at https://github.com/ZheruiHuang/GeoFlow.

AI.tldr: GeoFlow: A novel framework that integrates geospatial attributes into origin-destination flow modeling using geometric-intrinsic fusion encoding and axial-global attention decoding, outperforming graph-based methods in predictive accuracy and generative diversity.
AI.motivation: Current graph-based methods for origin-destination (OD) flow modeling fail to incorporate important geographic attributes, which limits their ability to capture long-range and multi-area dependencies in urban mobility patterns.
AI.method: 1) Augment area representations with geospatial attributes (relative positions, k-hop and geodesic distances); 2) Use geometric-intrinsic fusion encoder combining graph attention for local signals and coordinate-aware encoders for global structure; 3) Employ axial-global attention decoder to capture OD-specific competitive dependencies; 4) Pair with flow matching models for generation.
AI.result: GeoFlow achieves superior predictive accuracy compared to existing methods and substantially improves generative fidelity and diversity when producing mobility samples. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component.
AI.conclusion: GeoFlow successfully addresses the limitations of graph-based OD flow modeling by effectively integrating geographic attributes, enabling better capture of spatial dependencies and producing more authentic mobility patterns for urban planning applications.

title: Target-Guided Selective Reweighting for Physics-Informed Neural Network Inverse Problems: A Transfer Learning Approach
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) encounter ill-posed optimization, loss competition, and parameter compensation in partial differential equation (PDE) inverse problems. Transfer learning can reuse representations from source tasks, but direct fine-tuning may introduce negative transfer when dominant physical mechanisms, governing parameters, or observation noise differ between source and target domains: the model achieves low field error yet recovers incorrect target physical parameters. To mitigate, we propose Target-Guided Selective Reweighting PINN (TGSR-PINN), a target-evidence-driven representation correction method for PINN inverse transfer learning. TGSR-PINN transfers only the weights and biases from the source PINN, while target physical parameters are independently initialized; after a short target-adaptation phase, the method computes neuron target scores using first-order Taylor sensitivity and pre-activation variance on fixed scoring batches, and converts evidence associated with low-scoring neurons into continuous weak-adaptation signals via a Gaussian mixture model (GMM) with rank fallback. TGSR-PINN then applies selective soft decay to input weight rows and biases of low-scoring neurons instead of hard pruning or random resetting. In experiments, TGSR-PINN improves target parameter recovery while maintaining comparable field accuracy in the high-Péclet 2D advection-diffusion task and in the Allen--Cahn to Burgers cross-PDE-family transfer task; a 5%-noise reaction--diffusion case provides supplementary evidence under milder source-target mismatch. Ablation studies suggest that neuron target scoring, weak-adaptation signal estimation, layer protection, and selective soft decay jointly contribute to the benefits.

AI.tldr: TGSR-PINN addresses negative transfer in PINN inverse problems by selectively adapting low-scoring neurons using target-evidence-driven representation correction instead of direct fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: PINNs face ill-posed optimization, loss competition, and parameter compensation in PDE inverse problems. Direct transfer learning can cause negative transfer when source/target domains differ in physical mechanisms, governing parameters, or observation noise, leading to correct field predictions but incorrect physical parameters.
AI.method: Target-Guided Selective Reweighting PINN (TGSR-PINN): transfers only weights/biases from source PINN, independently initializes target parameters. After target-adaptation, computes neuron target scores using first-order Taylor sensitivity and pre-activation variance, converts low-scoring neuron evidence into weak-adaptation signals via Gaussian mixture model with rank fallback, then applies selective soft decay to input weight rows/biases of low-scoring neurons.
AI.result: TGSR-PINN improves target parameter recovery while maintaining comparable field accuracy in high-Péclet 2D advection-diffusion and Allen-Cahn to Burgers cross-PDE-family transfer tasks. Supplementary evidence from 5%-noise reaction-diffusion case under milder mismatch. Ablation studies confirm contributions from neuron scoring, weak-adaptation signals, layer protection, and selective soft decay.
AI.conclusion: TGSR-PINN effectively mitigates negative transfer in PINN inverse problems through selective, target-evidence-driven representation correction, balancing parameter recovery with field accuracy while avoiding issues of direct fine-tuning or hard pruning methods.

title: Advances in Neural Controlled Differential Equations
comment: DPhil thesis, University of Oxford, 188 pages, 17 figures
summary:
Many real-world systems evolve continuously, yet most machine learning models interpret time series as discrete sequences. Continuous-time approaches instead treat time series as samples from an underlying input path, a formulation that naturally accommodates irregularly sampled or oversampled data. Among these, Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs) are a maximally expressive class of models that parametrise a vector field using a neural network and evolve their hidden state by solving a dynamical system driven by the input path. NCDEs typically use a non-linear vector field, so their expressive power and continuous-time flexibility come at the cost of a forward pass that is both computationally expensive and inherently sequential, limiting their scalability and practical applicability.
  This thesis advances the training and scalability of NCDEs through three complementary contributions. First, building on neural rough differential equations, Log-NCDEs apply the Log-ODE method to efficiently approximate an NCDE's solution during training, improving both computational speed and empirical performance. Second, Linear NCDEs replace the non-linear vector field with a linear one, enabling closed-form solutions and parallel-in-time computation without sacrificing theoretical expressivity. Third, Structured Linear NCDEs use structured linear vector fields to further enhance efficiency while maintaining theoretical expressiveness and empirical performance.
  Collectively, these methods reduce the time per training step for an NCDE by up to three orders of magnitude while achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse time series benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This thesis improves Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs) by introducing three methods that dramatically reduce computational costs while maintaining performance, making continuous-time models more practical for real-world time series.
AI.motivation: Real-world systems evolve continuously, but most ML models treat time as discrete. NCDEs offer continuous-time modeling but suffer from computational expense and sequential limitations due to their nonlinear vector fields, hindering scalability.
AI.method: Three complementary approaches: (1) Log-NCDEs use the Log-ODE method for efficient approximation during training, (2) Linear NCDEs replace nonlinear with linear vector fields enabling closed-form solutions and parallel computation, (3) Structured Linear NCDEs use structured linear fields for further efficiency gains.
AI.result: Methods reduce training time per step by up to three orders of magnitude while achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse time series benchmarks, making NCDEs much more practical and scalable.
AI.conclusion: The thesis successfully addresses the computational limitations of NCDEs through innovative modifications that preserve theoretical expressiveness while dramatically improving efficiency, advancing the practical applicability of continuous-time models for real-world systems.

title: Learning Only What Valid Adapters Can Express: Subspace-Constrained Adaptation Against Fine-Tuning Poisoning
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/infinition/z-manifold
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning still leaves a broad space of behavior-changing updates reachable, so a poisoned objective can be represented and optimized. We study an alternative: adaptation constrained to the subspace estimated from a trusted pool of existing task adapters. On flan-t5-large with 196 public LoRA adapters, we show that (1) the functionally relevant content of an adapter lies in a low-dimensional shared subspace, 30 to 38 percent of its weight norm being redundant under the evaluated task distributions; (2) gradient adaptation restricted to 128 coordinates on this subspace matches full LoRA fine-tuning on clean classification data, while under targeted label inversion LoRA collapses to 3-26 percent exact match and the constrained learner keeps 62-96 percent on the tasks the pool covers; (3) the constrained learner cannot fit corrupted data, its adaptation loss separating clean from garbage by two orders of magnitude (120x), an out-of-distribution signal without an extra detector; and (4) against an adaptive backdoor attacker who optimizes within the subspace, the attack is blocked (8 percent success versus 100 for LoRA) on the task where its target behavior is unlike anything in the pool, and only partially blocked (85 percent) when the target coincides with a common pool behavior. On these two tasks the outcome is consistent with how close the target is to the pool's directions, which suggests but does not establish a pool-relative boundary. The mechanism trades peak plasticity for these properties: on tasks the pool covers poorly, unconstrained fine-tuning wins, and the protection assumes the pool itself is trusted. Code and data are public.

AI.tldr: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning can be secured by constraining adaptations to a trusted subspace from existing task adapters, blocking backdoor attacks while maintaining performance on clean data.
AI.motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (like LoRA) leaves enough space for poisoned objectives/backdoors to be optimized, creating security vulnerabilities in model adaptation.
AI.method: Constrained adaptation to a low-dimensional subspace estimated from a trusted pool of existing task adapters, with gradient adaptation restricted to 128 coordinates on this subspace.
AI.result: (1) Adapters have 30-38% redundant weight norm; (2) Constrained adaptation matches full LoRA on clean data while resisting label inversion attacks (62-96% vs 3-26% exact match); (3) Adaptation loss clean/garbage separation of 120x; (4) Blocks adaptive backdoor attacks (8% success vs 100% for LoRA) when target behavior differs from pool.
AI.conclusion: Constraining adaptation to trusted subspaces provides security benefits against backdoor attacks while maintaining clean-data performance, but trades plasticity for protection and assumes the pool itself is trusted.

title: Biologically Informed Deep Neural Networks for Multi-Omic Integration, Pathway Activity Inference and Risk Stratification in Cancer
summary: Integrating complex, multi-omics data presents significant challenges. Existing approaches often face a trade-off between model interpretability and representational capacity, with most either relying on post-hoc interpretation or use linear models that may overlook complex interactions. We report Pathway Activity Autoencoders for the multi-omics setting, which embed prior knowledge via pathway-informed architectural constraints, fostering interpretability, while preserving representational power. Our multi-omic framework is applied in the context of breast cancer and is evaluated in survival prediction and subtype classification with results indicating a positive effect of integration. We conduct analysis of individual omics layer impact on end-task performance, revealing that gene, protein, and microRNA expression layers provide the strongest contribution. Repeatability studies indicate that, while dropout improves model robustness and consistency, excessive regularisation can reduce predictive performance. Finally, visualizations of the learned feature space illustrate the framework's intrinsic transparency and clinical relevance. The results underscore the value of multi-omic integration and delineate the impact of individual omics layers, establishing practical guidelines for integration within our framework. Overall, our pathway activity autoencoder frameworks yield superior latent representations that are biologically meaningful and are directly translatable into clinically relevant insights.

AI.tldr: A pathway-informed autoencoder framework for multi-omics integration that balances interpretability and representational power, applied to breast cancer for survival prediction and subtype classification.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-omics integration methods face a trade-off between interpretability and representational capacity, often relying on post-hoc interpretation or linear models that miss complex interactions.
AI.method: Pathway Activity Autoencoders that incorporate prior biological knowledge through pathway-informed architectural constraints, enabling interpretability while preserving representational power for multi-omics data integration.
AI.result: Successful application to breast cancer with positive integration effects for survival prediction and subtype classification; gene, protein, and microRNA expression layers contribute most; dropout improves robustness but excessive regularization harms performance; learned features show clinical relevance.
AI.conclusion: The pathway activity autoencoder framework provides superior biologically meaningful latent representations that translate into clinically relevant insights, establishing practical guidelines for multi-omic integration.

title: How Far is Too Far? Defining the Distance Threshold for Verification Siamese Networks
summary: Siamese verification networks are widely used to compare items such as faces, cars, or signatures. In these scenarios, the network is trained to learn an embedding space in which similar objects are mapped closer together, while dissimilar objects are mapped further apart. Two objects are considered to belong to the same class (e.g., the same person in two different images) when the distance between their embeddings falls below a predefined threshold. Defining this threshold, however, is a non-trivial task and typically requires labeled data. In this work, we assume that the distribution of distances produced by a siamese verification network can be approximated by a bimodal function. Based on this assumption, we propose an unsupervised method to determine the verification threshold by identifying the minimum point between the two modes. The proposed approach does not require annotated samples, enabling the verification threshold to be updated directly in the deployment environment without the cost of manual labeling. We evaluate our method on four datasets: MNIST, CIFAR-10, LFW, and PKLot. The results indicate that the proposed approach achieves an average verification accuracy of 94%, comparable to the Equal Error Rate method, while eliminating the need for labeled data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an unsupervised method to determine verification thresholds for siamese networks by modeling distance distributions as bimodal functions and finding the minimum point between the two modes, eliminating the need for labeled data.
AI.motivation: Defining verification thresholds for siamese networks typically requires labeled data, which is costly and impractical for deployment environments. The authors aim to develop an unsupervised approach that can determine thresholds without manual annotation.
AI.method: The method assumes the distribution of distances produced by a siamese verification network can be approximated by a bimodal function. The verification threshold is determined by identifying the minimum point between the two modes of this distribution, corresponding to the natural separation between similar and dissimilar pairs.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves an average verification accuracy of 94% across four datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, LFW, and PKLot), which is comparable to the supervised Equal Error Rate method while completely eliminating the need for labeled data.
AI.conclusion: The unsupervised threshold determination method successfully enables verification threshold updates directly in deployment environments without manual labeling costs, maintaining performance comparable to supervised approaches while offering practical deployment advantages.

title: CompactionRL: Reinforcement Learning with Context Compaction for Long-Horizon Agents
summary: Long-horizon agentic LLMs are increasingly limited by finite context windows, as extended interaction trajectories can exceed the maximum context length before a task is completed. Context compaction offers a natural solution by summarizing previous interaction states and continuing the rollout under a compressed context, but incorporating compaction into reinforcement learning remains underexplored. We propose CompactionRL, a reinforcement learning strategy to train long-horizon agentic LLMs with context compaction. Our approach jointly optimizes task execution and summary generation with token-level loss normalization and cross-trajectory generalized advantage estimation. This design enables the LLM agents to learn from compacted long-horizon trajectories. We train CompactionRL on top of open models and observe consistent performance gains on agentic coding tasks. CompactionRL enables the open GLM-4.5-Air model (106B-A30B) to achieve Pass@1 scores of 66.8% on SWE-bench Verified and 24.5% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, with absolute gains of 7.0 and 3.1 points, respectively. Built upon GLM-4.7-Flash (30B-A3B), CompactionRL improves Pass@1 by 5.5 and 6.8 points, reaching 56.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 20.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, respectively. CompactionRL is thus deployed in the RL pipeline for training the open GLM-5.2 model (750B-A40B).

AI.tldr: CompactionRL is a reinforcement learning method that trains long-horizon agentic LLMs with context compaction, enabling them to learn from compressed interaction histories and achieve significant performance gains on agentic coding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon agentic LLMs face context window limitations as extended interaction trajectories can exceed maximum context lengths before task completion. While context compaction offers a solution by summarizing previous states, incorporating it into reinforcement learning remains underexplored.
AI.method: CompactionRL is a reinforcement learning strategy that jointly optimizes task execution and summary generation using token-level loss normalization and cross-trajectory generalized advantage estimation. This enables LLM agents to learn from compacted long-horizon trajectories.
AI.result: CompactionRL achieves consistent performance gains on agentic coding tasks: GLM-4.5-Air (106B-A30B) achieved 66.8% Pass@1 on SWE-bench Verified (+7.0 points) and 24.5% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 (+3.1 points). GLM-4.7-Flash (30B-A3B) improved by 5.5 and 6.8 points, reaching 56.0% and 20.2% respectively.
AI.conclusion: CompactionRL successfully addresses context window limitations for long-horizon agentic LLMs through reinforcement learning with context compaction, demonstrating substantial performance improvements on coding benchmarks and being deployed in the training pipeline for GLM-5.2.

title: TabPack: Efficient Hyperparameter Ensembles for Tabular Deep Learning
summary: In deep learning for tabular data, efficient ensembles of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) have recently emerged as effective and practical architectures. Existing methods of this kind use the same hyperparameters for all underlying MLPs, which requires hyperparameter tuning for achieving the best performance. In this work, we introduce TabPack, an efficient MLP ensemble with strong out-of-the-box performance and reduced reliance on traditional tuning. In a single run, TabPack samples and trains many MLPs with different hyperparameters efficiently in parallel and selects ensemble members on the fly during training. Thus, TabPack only requires specifying ranges from which to sample MLP hyperparameter rather than exact hyperparameter values, which naturally demands less precision for good performance. In experiments on medium-to-large public datasets, TabPack with default settings performs on par with extensively tuned prior methods, thus substantially reducing effort and compute resources needed to achieve competitive results on tabular tasks. Notably, running the default TabPack configuration on a modern MacBook took less time than tuning some baselines on an industry-grade GPU.

AI.tldr: TabPack is an efficient MLP ensemble for tabular data that achieves strong out-of-the-box performance by training many MLPs with different hyperparameters in parallel and selecting ensemble members during training, eliminating the need for extensive hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing MLP ensemble methods for tabular data require all underlying MLPs to use the same hyperparameters, which necessitates extensive hyperparameter tuning to achieve optimal performance. This tuning process demands significant computational resources and human effort.
AI.method: TabPack samples and trains multiple MLPs with different hyperparameters efficiently in parallel within a single run. It automatically selects ensemble members during training rather than requiring all MLPs to share the same hyperparameters. Users only need to specify ranges for hyperparameter sampling rather than exact values.
AI.result: In experiments on medium-to-large public datasets, TabPack with default settings performs on par with extensively tuned prior methods. Remarkably, running default TabPack on a modern MacBook took less time than tuning some baselines on an industry-grade GPU, demonstrating significant efficiency gains.
AI.conclusion: TabPack provides an efficient MLP ensemble approach that substantially reduces the computational resources and tuning effort needed for competitive tabular data performance, offering a practical alternative to traditional hyperparameter-intensive methods.
